blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ وَٱلنُّورَ ۖ ثُمَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ (١)
All praise belongs to Allah who created the heavens and the earth and made the darkness and the light. Yet the faithless equate [others] with their Lord. (1)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن طِينٍ ثُمَّ قَضَىٰٓ أَجَلًا ۖ وَأَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى عِندَهُۥ ۖ ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تَمْتَرُونَ (٢)
It is He who created you from clay, then ordained the term [of your life]—the specified term is with Him—and yet you are in doubt. (2)
وَهُوَ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يَعْلَمُ سِرَّكُمْ وَجَهْرَكُمْ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْسِبُونَ (٣)
He is Allah in the heavens and on the earth: He knows your secret and your overt [matters], and He knows what you earn. (3)

(Commentary)

The purpose of this Surah is to establish His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — in its broadest sense. By this, I mean that for man there is a Lord who is the Lord of all the worlds (العالمين) — all of creation; from Him everything begins, and to Him everything ends and returns. He sent messengers as bearers of good news and as warners, through whom He guides His subject servants to His true religion (الدين). For this reason, most of its verses were revealed in the form of argumentation (الحجاج) against the polytheists regarding monotheism, the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and life after death, and prophethood (النبوة) — the office of divine messengership. It also includes a summary of legal obligations and religious prohibitions.

Its context (السياق) — as reflection (التدبر) suggests — is a single, connected flow, containing no evidence of a separation that would imply it was revealed piecemeal (نجوما).

This indicates that it was revealed all at once and that it is Meccan (مكية), as is evident from its context, which directs speech in most or all of it toward the polytheists.

The commentators and narrators have agreed on its being Meccan, except for six verses which some have narrated to be Medinan (مدنية). These are His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا قَدَرُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ "They did not regard Allah with the regard due to Him" (6:91) until the completion of three verses; and His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ تَعَالَوْا أَتْلُ مَا حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ "Say, ‘Come, I will recite what your Lord has forbidden you’" (6:151) until the completion of three verses.

It has also been said that it is entirely Meccan except for two verses revealed in Medina, which are His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ تَعَالَوْا أَتْلُ "Say, ‘Come, I will recite’" and the one following it.

It has further been said that Surah al-An‘am was revealed entirely in Mecca except for two verses revealed in Medina concerning a man from the Jews, who was the one who said: مَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ عَلَى بَشَرٍ مِنْ شَيْءٍ "Allah has not sent down anything to any human" (6:91).

And it has been said: "It is entirely Meccan except for one verse revealed in Medina, which is His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّنَا نَزَّلْنَا إِلَيْهِمُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ 'Even if We had sent down the angels to them'" (6:111).

There is no evidence for any of these views from the perspective of the linguistic context, given the aforementioned unity of context and the interconnectedness of the Surah's verses, as we shall clarify to the best of our ability. It has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), as well as from Ubayy, ‘Ikrimah, and Qatadah, that it was revealed all at once in Mecca.

His saying, the Exalted: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ الظُّلُمَاتِ وَالنُّورَ "All praise belongs to Allah who created the heavens and the earth and made the darkness and the light" (6:1). He opened with praise (الثناء) for Allah, which serves as an introduction to the meaning of monotheism intended for clarification. This is achieved by embedding within the praise the core objective of the Surah, using it as a means to argue for it in detail. It also embeds wonder at them and blame for their equating others with Him and their doubting His unity, serving as a preamble to the forthcoming sections of exhortation, warning, and intimidation.

In this praise, situated in the first three verses, He has pointed to the sum of what the religious call relies upon regarding the true gnosis (المعارف الحقيقية) — the essential spiritual and metaphysical truths — which are like the substance of the Law (الشريعة). These resolve into three systems:

The system of the general cosmos (الكون العام), which the first verse points to; the system of man in terms of his existence, which the second verse contains; and the system of human action, which the third verse alludes to.

The result of the three verses combined is praise for Him, the Exalted, for creating the macrocosm (العالم الكبير) in which man lives; for creating a microcosm (عالم صغير), which is the limited existence of man from his beginning with clay to his end with the decreed term (الأجل المقضي); and for His knowledge of man's secret and manifest states and what he earns.

What is in the third verse: وَهُوَ اللَّهُ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَفِي الْأَرْضِ "He is Allah in the heavens and on the earth" (6:3), serves as a clarification of the content of the two preceding verses and a preamble to the explanation of His knowledge of man's secret and manifest states and what his soul earns.

His saying: خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ الظُّلُمَاتِ وَالنُّورَ "created the heavens and the earth and made the darkness and the light" is an allusion to the general cosmic system upon which the governance of things depends, despite their multiplicity and dispersion in our world. In its firm, ongoing system, the world of the earth is surrounded by the world of the heavens in their vastness, which then acts upon it through light and darkness. Upon these two, the millstone of the witnessed world turns in its transformation and perfection; thus, one thing continues to be born from another, one thing turns into another, one appears and another is hidden, the new comes into being and the old decays. From the meeting of these diverse movements, despite their scattered nature, the great universal movement is organized, carrying the burdens of things and driving them toward their resting place.

The making (الجعل) in His saying: وَجَعَلَ الظُّلُمَاتِ "and made the darkness" etc., is in the sense of creation (الخلق), except that creation, being originally derived from the "creation of a garment" (خلق الثوب), implies the composition (التركيب) of various parts in its meaning, unlike "making" (الجعل). Perhaps this is the reason for specifying "creation" for the heavens and the earth due to the composition within them, unlike darkness and light; hence, they were specified with the use of "making." And Allah knows best.

He used "darkness" (الظلمات) in the plural form while using "light" (النور) in the singular. This is perhaps because darkness is realized in relation to light, for it is the absence (عدم) of light in that which has the potential to be illuminated; thus, it multiplies according to the degrees of its proximity to or distance from light. This is unlike light, which is an existential (وجودي) matter that is not realized by comparison to darkness, which is privative (عدمية). Multiplying it conceptually by comparing it to darkness does not necessitate its actual multiplicity or plurality.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ "yet the faithless equate [others] with their Lord" (6:1) is phrased to express wonder mixed with blame. That is, Allah, the Glorified, by His creation of the heavens and the earth and His making of darkness and light, is unique in divinity (الألوهية) and solitary in Lordship (الربوبية); nothing is like Him or shares with Him. It is a wonder that the disbelievers, despite their admission that creation and governance belong to Allah in the true sense of ownership (الملك) — unlike the idols they have taken as gods — equate others from among their idols with Allah and level their statues with Him, making for Him peers (أندادا) that equate to Him in their claim. Thus, they are blamed for that.

From this, the reason for using then (ثم), which indicates delay and distance (التراخي), becomes clear. It is as if the Speaker, after describing His uniqueness in fashioning and origination and His oneness in divinity and Lordship, mentioned the claim of the polytheists and idolaters that these stones and woods made into idols equate to the Lord of all the worlds. Wonder occupied Him for a time and held Him back from speaking, then He proceeded in His speech and pointed to the reason for His silence — that the bewilderment of wonder was what prevented the flow of His speech — so He said: الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ "the faithless equate [others] with their Lord."

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ قَضَى أَجَلًا "It is He who created you from clay, then He ordained a term" (6:2) points to His creation of the human microcosm after the allusion to the creation of the macrocosm. He explains that Allah, the Glorified, is the one who created man and governed his affair by setting a term for his worldly survival. Thus, his existence is limited between the clay from which the creation of his species began — even if the survival of his progeny follows the law of mating and intercourse, as He, the Exalted, said: وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسَانِ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلَالَةٍ مِنْ مَاءٍ مَهِينٍ "He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid" (32:7-8) —

And between the decreed term (الأجل المقضي) which coincides with death, as He, the Exalted, said: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا تُرْجَعُونَ "Every soul shall taste death, then you shall be brought back to Us" (29:57). It is possible that the "term" refers to what coincides with the return to Allah, the Glorified, through the Resurrection (البعث), for the Quran seems to count the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — as part of the world, as suggested by the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ قَالُوا لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ فَاسْأَلِ الْعَادِّينَ قَالَ إِنْ لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا لَوْ أَنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "He will say, ‘How many years did you tarry on earth?’ They will say, ‘We tarried a day, or part of a day; as those who keep count.’ He will say, ‘You tarried only a little, if you only knew’" (23:112-114). He also said: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ مَا لَبِثُوا غَيْرَ سَاعَةٍ كَذَلِكَ كَانُوا يُؤْفَكُونَ وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَالْإِيمَانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ فَهَذَا يَوْمُ الْبَعْثِ وَلَكِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ "The day the Hour sets in, the guilty will swear they tarried but an hour. Thus were they used to being perverted. But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you have tarried in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection. So this is the Day of Resurrection, but you did not know’" (30:55-56).

He left the matter of the term ambiguous by mentioning it in the indefinite form in His saying: ثُمَّ قَضَى أَجَلًا "then He ordained a term," to indicate that it is unknown to man, who has no way of knowing it through ordinary sciences.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ "and a specified term is with Him" (6:2). Specifying the term (تسمية الأجل) means defining it. Custom in contracts, debts, and the like involves mentioning the term (الأجل) — which is the set duration or the end of the duration — by its name, which is the "specified term" (الأجل المسمى). He, the Exalted, said: إِذَا تَدَايَنْتُمْ بِدَيْنٍ إِلَى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى فَاكْتُبُوهُ "when you contract a debt for a stated term, write it down" (2:282), where the term means the end of the set duration. Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كَانَ يَرْجُو لِقَاءَ اللَّهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللَّهِ لَآتٍ "Whoever expects to encounter Allah [should know that] Allah’s [appointed] time will indeed come" (29:5). He, the Exalted, said in the story of Moses and Shu‘ayb: قَالَ إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُنْكِحَكَ إِحْدَى ابْنَتَيَّ هَاتَيْنِ عَلَى أَنْ تَأْجُرَنِي ثَمَانِيَ حِجَجٍ فَإِنْ أَتْمَمْتَ عَشْرًا فَمِنْ عِنْدِكَ — until He said — قَالَ ذَلِكَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ أَيَّمَا الْأَجَلَيْنِ قَضَيْتُ فَلَا عُدْوَانَ عَلَيَّ "He said, ‘I desire to marry you to one of these two daughters of mine, on condition that you hire yourself to me for eight years. And if you complete ten, that will be of your own accord’... He said, ‘This is [a contract] between me and you; whichever of the two terms I complete, let there be no injustice to me’" (28:27-28), where the term means the completion of the set duration.

It appears that "term" in the sense of the end of the duration is a derivative of "term" in the sense of the full duration in usage. That is, "the decreed term" (الأجل المقضي) was used frequently, then the adjective was omitted and the noun sufficed, so "term" gave the meaning of the decreed term. Al-Raghib said in his Mufradat: "The duration set for a human's life is called an 'term' (أجل); thus it is said: 'his term has drawn near,' meaning death is near, and its origin is the fulfillment of the term." End quote.

In any case, the apparent meaning of His speech, the Exalted, is that what is meant by "term" and "specified term" is the end of the life span for the completion of the duration, as suggested by His saying: فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللَّهِ لَآتٍ "Allah’s [appointed] time will indeed come."

Thus, it becomes clear that there are two terms: the term in its ambiguity, and the specified term which is with Allah, the Exalted. This latter one is that in which no change occurs because of its being restricted by His saying عِنْدَهُ "with Him," and He, the Exalted, has said: وَمَا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ بَاقٍ "and what is with Allah is lasting" (16:96). This is the inevitable term (الأجل المحتوم) which does not change or alter. He, the Exalted, said: إِذَا جَاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ "When their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it" (10:49).

The relationship of the specified term to the non-specified term is like the relationship of the absolute and finalized (المطلق المنجز) to the conditional and suspended (المشروط المعلق). It is possible for the conditional and suspended to fail to manifest due to the non-manifestation of the condition upon which it was suspended, unlike the absolute and finalized, which has no way of failing to manifest at all.

Reflection on the preceding verses, joined with His saying, the Exalted: لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتَابٌ يَمْحُو اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتَابِ "Every time has a Book. Allah effaces and confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book" (13:38-39), suggests that the specified term is what is placed in the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب), while the non-specified term is what is written in what we call the Tablet of Effacement and Confirmation (لوح المحو والإثبات). It will be explained, Allah willing, that the Mother of the Book is applicable to events fixed in reality — that is, events from the perspective of their attribution to general causes (الأسباب العامة) that do not fail in their effect. The Tablet of Effacement and Confirmation is applicable to events from the perspective of their attribution to incomplete causes (الأسباب الناقصة), which we might call predispositions (المقتضيات) that may be joined by obstacles (موانع) preventing their effect.

Consider what has been mentioned regarding the complete and incomplete cause (السبب التام والناقص) through the example of the sun's illumination. We know that this night will end after some hours and the sun will rise and illuminate the face of the earth. However, it is possible for this to be joined by the intervention of a cloud, the moon, or any other obstacle that prevents the illumination. But if the sun is above the horizon and no assumed obstacle exists between the earth and it, it will inevitably illuminate the face of the earth.

The rising of the sun alone, in relation to illumination, is like the Tablet of Effacement and Confirmation. Its rising at its proper time without any assumed barrier between it and the earth, in relation to illumination, is like the Mother of the Book, called the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ).

Thus, the specific composition of this human individual's constitution, with the limited predisposition in its elements, requires that he live a natural life span, which they might define as a hundred or a hundred and twenty years. This is what is written in the Tablet of Effacement and Confirmation, for example. However, all parts of the cosmos have a connection and influence on human existence; thus, causes and obstacles that we cannot count may interact in a way we cannot encompass, leading to the arrival of his term before the natural span ends, which is called "extirpative death" (الموت الاخترامي).

By this, it becomes easy to conceive the need — according to how Allah has organized existence — for both the specified and non-specified terms. The ambiguity regarding the non-specified term does not contradict the determination regarding the specified term. The non-specified and specified terms may coincide or they may differ, and what occurs in that case is certainly the specified term.

This is what reflection on His saying: ثُمَّ قَضَى أَجَلًا وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ "then He ordained a term, and a specified term is with Him" yields. The commentators have strange interpretations for the two terms mentioned in the verse:

Among them: that the first term is what is between creation and death, and the second is what is between death and resurrection. This was mentioned by several of the early scholars and is sometimes narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas.

Among them: that the first term is the term of the people of the world until they die, and the second is the term of the Hereafter which has no end. This is attributed to Mujahid, al-Jubba’i, and others.

Among them: that the first term is the term of those who have passed, and the second is the term of those who remain and are yet to come. This is attributed to Abu Muslim.

Among them: that the first term is sleep, and the second is death.

Among them: that the two terms are one, and the estimation of the noble verse is: "then He ordained a term, and this is a specified term with Him."

I do not see that engaging in the investigation of the correctness or infirmity of these views and their like is justified by time in its tightness, nor does life in its brevity permit it.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَنْتُمْ تَمْتَرُونَ "yet you doubt" (6:2) is from al-miryah (المرية) meaning doubt and suspicion. An apostrophe (التفات) — a rhetorical shift in person — has occurred in the verse from the third person (غيبة) to the second person (حضور). The reason for this seems to be that the first verse mentions a general creation and governance, the result of which is that it was not fitting for the disbelievers to equate others with Allah, the Glorified. Mentioning them in the third person was sufficient for that. However, the second verse mentions the creation and governance occurring specifically in man. Thus, it was appropriate for the Speaker, who is expressing wonder and blame, to confront them directly and blame them to their faces, as if He is saying: "This is the creation of the heavens and the earth and the making of darkness and light; We excused you for being heedless of its wisdom because it is a general matter that one might possibly overlook. But what is your excuse for your doubting Him when He is the one who created you and ordained a term for you, and a specified term is with Him?"

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ اللَّهُ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَفِي الْأَرْضِ "He is Allah in the heavens and on the earth" (6:3). The two preceding verses mention creation and governance in the worlds generally and in man specifically. This is sufficient to realize that Allah, the Glorified, is the one God who has no partner in His creation and governance.

Yet, despite this, they established other gods and various intercessors for different aspects of governance, such as a god of life, a god of sustenance, a god of the land, a god of the sea, and so on. Likewise for different species, peoples, and scattered nations, such as the god of the sky, the god of this group, and the god of that group. He negated this by His saying: وَهُوَ اللَّهُ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَفِي الْأَرْضِ.

The verse is similar to His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي فِي السَّمَاءِ إِلَهٌ وَفِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَهٌ وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ "It is He who is God in the heaven and God on the earth, and He is the All-wise, the All-knowing" (43:84). Its meaning is the pervasiveness of the rule of His divinity, the Exalted, in the heavens and on the earth without variation or limitation. It is a clarification of what preceded and a preamble for the speech that follows.

His saying, the Exalted: يَعْلَمُ سِرَّكُمْ وَجَهْرَكُمْ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْسِبُونَ "He knows your secrets and what you reveal, and He knows what you earn" (6:3). Secret (السر) and manifest (الجهر) are opposites, and they are descriptions of actions. Their secret is what they have done secretly, and their manifest is what they have done openly without concealment.

As for "what they earn" (ما يكسبون), it is the psychological state (الحال النفساني) that man earns through his secret and manifest actions, whether good or evil. The mentioned secret and manifest — as you have seen — are formal descriptions of the core of external actions, while what they earn is a spiritual, non-material state residing in the souls. Thus, they differ in terms of form and meaning. Perhaps the difference between the two known things in themselves is what necessitated the repetition of the mention of knowledge in His saying: يَعْلَمُ سِرَّكُمْ وَجَهْرَكُمْ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْسِبُونَ.

The verse is like a preamble for what it will address regarding the matter of the Message and the Return. For since Allah, the Glorified, is knowledgeable of what man brings of action secretly or manifest, and is knowledgeable of what he earns for his soul by his action of good or evil, and since the reins of upbringing and governance are His, it was for Him to send a messenger with a religion He legislates to guide people, despite the insistence of the idolaters on the lack of need for prophethood, as He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا لَلْهُدَى "Indeed, it is up to Us to guide" (92:12).

Likewise, since He, the Exalted, is knowledgeable of actions and their consequences in the human soul, it was for Him to call them to account on a day when He leaves out none of them, as He, the Exalted, said: أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ "Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (38:28).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali ibn Abi Hamzah, who said: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Indeed, Surah al-An‘am was revealed all at once; seventy thousand angels escorted it until it was revealed to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) — so they magnified it and honored it — for the name of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is in it in seventy places. If people knew what is in its recitation, they would not leave it."

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it from him (peace be upon him) without a chain (مرسلا).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, he said: My father narrated to me from al-Husayn ibn Khalid from al-Rida (peace be upon him), who said: "Al-An‘am was revealed all at once, seventy thousand angels escorting it — they had a clamor (زجل) of glorification (التسبيح), proclamation of oneness (التهليل), and magnification (التكبير). Whoever recites it, they seek forgiveness for him until the Day of Resurrection."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Majma‘ from al-Husayn ibn Khalid from him (peace be upon him), except that he said: "They glorify for him until the Day of Resurrection."

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, who said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: "Indeed, Surah al-An‘am was revealed all at once — and seventy thousand angels escorted it when it was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) — so they magnified it and honored it — for the name of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is in it in seventy places. If people knew what merit is in its recitation, they would not leave it," the hadith.

In Jawami‘ al-Jami‘ by al-Tabarsi, he said: In the hadith of Ubayy ibn Ka‘b from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he said: "Al-An‘am was revealed to me all at once — seventy thousand angels escorting it, having a clamor of glorification and praise. Whoever recites it, those seventy thousand angels pray for him — according to the number of every verse of al-An‘am, for a day and a night."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from him through several paths.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Ibn Mahbub from Abu Ja‘far al-Ahwal from Salam ibn al-Mustanir from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah created Paradise before He created the Fire, and He created obedience before He created disobedience, and He created mercy before wrath, and He created good before evil — and He created the earth before the heaven, and He created life before death, and He created the sun before the moon, and He created light before darkness."

I say: The creation of light before darkness, considering that darkness is privative (عدميا) in relation to light, is clear in meaning. As for the attribution of creation to obedience and disobedience, it does not necessitate the invalidity of free will (الاختيار), for its invalidity would necessitate the invalidity of obedience and disobedience themselves, leaving no valid reason for their attribution to creation. Rather, the meaning is that He, the Exalted, owns them just as He owns everything that falls within His kingdom. How could anything fall within His kingdom that is outside His encompassment and authority, and isolated from His will and permission?

There is no evidence for restricting "creation" (الخلق) to origination and fashioning without an intermediary, such that He, the Exalted, would be independent in originating everything whose creation is attributed to Him. Thus, if it is said: "Allah created justice or killing," for example, it would not mean He invalidated the will of the just person or the killer and independently performed the justice or killing by removing the intermediary. Understand this. The full discussion of this meaning has already preceded in the first volume of the book.

By a similar explanation, the meaning of attributing creation to good and evil also becomes clear, whether they are good and evil in cosmic matters or in actions.

As for obedience being created before disobedience, and likewise good before evil, its explanation follows what preceded regarding light being before darkness — that the relationship between them is that of privation and possession (العدم والملكة), and privation depends for its realization upon possession. By this, it also appears that the creation of life was before death.

By this, it is clarified that the creation of mercy was before wrath, for mercy is connected to obedience and good, while wrath is connected to disobedience and evil; and obedience and good are before disobedience and evil.

As for the creation of the earth before the heaven, it is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: خَلَقَ الْأَرْضَ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ — until He said — ثُمَّ اسْتَوَى إِلَى السَّمَاءِ وَهِيَ دُخَانٌ فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا قَالَتَا أَتَيْنَا طَائِعِينَ فَقَضَاهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَاوَاتٍ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ "created the earth in two days... Then He settled to the heaven when it was smoke, and said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come obediently.’ So He completed them as seven heavens in two days" (41:9-12).

As for the creation of the sun being before the moon, it is not at all far-fetched that it be derived from His saying, the Exalted: وَالشَّمْسِ وَضُحَاهَا وَالْقَمَرِ إِذَا تَلَاهَا "By the sun and her brightness, and the moon when he follows her" (91:1-2). Natural scientific research today has favored the view that the earth is derived from the sun and the moon is derived from the earth.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Ja‘far ibn Ahmad from al-‘Amraki ibn ‘Ali from al-‘Ubaydi from Yunus ibn ‘Abd al-Rahman from ‘Ali ibn Ja‘far from Abu Ibrahim (peace be upon him), who said: "For every prayer there are two times, and the time for Friday is the zenith of the sun." Then he recited this verse: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ الظُّلُمَاتِ وَالنُّورَ ثُمَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ — he said: "They equate (يعدلون) between darkness and light, and between injustice and justice."

I say: This is another meaning for the verse, based on making His saying بِرَبِّهِمْ "with their Lord" connected to His saying كَفَرُوا "disbelieved" rather than يَعْدِلُونَ "equate."

In al-Kafi, from Muhammad ibn Yahya from Ahmad ibn Muhammad from Ibn Faddal from Ibn Bukayr from Zurarah from Humran from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: I asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: قَضَى أَجَلًا وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ "He ordained a term, and a specified term is with Him." He said: "They are two terms: a finalized term (أجل محتوم) and a suspended term (أجل موقوف)."

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Humran, who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: قَضَى أَجَلًا وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى. He said: "They are two terms — a suspended term in which Allah does what He wishes, and a finalized term."

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Mas‘adah ibn Sadaqah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: ثُمَّ قَضَى أَجَلًا وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ. He said: "The term which is not specified is suspended; He advances from it what He wishes. As for the specified term, it is that which descends from what He intends to be from one Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر) to the next." He said: "That is the saying of Allah: فَإِذَا جَاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ 'When their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it.'"

In the same source, from Humran from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I asked him about the saying of Allah: أَجَلًا وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ. He said: "The specified is what is named for the Angel of Death on that night, and it is that about which Allah said: فَإِذَا جَاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ. It is what is named for the Angel of Death on the Night of Ordainment. Regarding the other, He has the will (المشية); if He wishes He advances it, and if He wishes He delays it."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام). What they indicate regarding the meaning of the specified term and others is what was previously derived from the noble verses.

In Tafsir ‘Ali ibn Ibrahim, he said: My father narrated to me from al-Nadr ibn Suwayd from al-Halabi from ‘Abdullah ibn Muskan from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The decreed term (الأجل المقضي) is the finalized one which Allah has decreed and finalized. The specified (المسمى) is that in which there is changeability (البداء) — the manifestation of a divine decree after it was previously hidden — He advances what He wishes and delays what He wishes. In the finalized, there is no advancing or delaying."

I say: Some of those in the chain of narration have erred and reversed the meaning, interpreting each of the "specified" and the "other" with the meaning of the other. Furthermore, the narration does not address the interpretation of the verse, so there is not much harm in accepting it.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from al-Husayn from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: قَضَى أَجَلًا وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ. Then Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "The first term is what He cast (نبذه) to the angels, the messengers, and the prophets. The specified term with Him is what Allah has veiled from the creatures."

I say: The content of the narration apparently contradicts the preceding narrations. However, it is possible to derive from his saying "He cast" that the meaning is that He, the Exalted, gave them the principle from which the non-specified terms are derived. As for the specified term, He did not give anyone mastery over its knowledge in the sense of casting to them a light by which every specified term is disclosed whenever that is desired, even if He, the Exalted, names it for the Angel of Death or for His prophets and messengers when He wishes. This is like the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge; its knowledge is exclusive to Him, the Exalted, yet He discloses something of it to whom He is pleased with from among the messengers when He so wishes.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babawayh with his chain of narration to Muthanna al-Hannat from Abu Ja‘far — I think he is Muhammad ibn al-Nu‘man — who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَهُوَ اللَّهُ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَفِي الْأَرْضِ. He said: "Likewise, He is in every place." I said: "By His essence (بذاته)?" He said: "Woe to you! Places are measures (أقدار). If you say: 'in a place by His essence,' it would necessitate you saying: 'in measures' and so forth. But He is distinct (بائن) from His creation, encompassing what He created in knowledge (علما), power (قدرة), encompassment (إحاطة), and authority (سلطانا). His knowledge of what is on the earth is not less than what is in the heaven; nothing is distant from Him. All things are equal to Him in knowledge, power, authority, kingdom, and will."

Ayahs

وَمَا تَأْتِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ (٤)
There did not come to them any sign from among the signs of their Lord, but that they used to disregard it. (4)
فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِيهِمْ أَنۢبَـٰٓؤُا۟ مَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٥)
They have certainly denied the truth when it came to them, but soon there will come to them the news of what they have been deriding. (5)
أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ مَّكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَا لَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّكُمْ وَأَرْسَلْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْهِم مِّدْرَارًا وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمْ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَأَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا ءَاخَرِينَ (٦)
Have they not regarded how many a generation We have destroyed before them whom We had granted power in the land in respects that We did not grant you, and We sent abundant rains for them from the sky and made streams run for them? Then We destroyed them for their sins, and brought forth another generation after them. (6)
وَلَوْ نَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ كِتَـٰبًا فِى قِرْطَاسٍ فَلَمَسُوهُ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ لَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧)
Had We sent down to you a Book on paper so they could touch it with their [own] hands, [still] the faithless would have said, ‘This is nothing but plain magic.’ (7)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ ۖ وَلَوْ أَنزَلْنَا مَلَكًا لَّقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ثُمَّ لَا يُنظَرُونَ (٨)
They say, ‘Why has not an angel been sent down to him?’ Were We to send down an angel, the matter would surely be decided, and then they would not be granted any respite. (8)
وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ مَلَكًا لَّجَعَلْنَـٰهُ رَجُلًا وَلَلَبَسْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّا يَلْبِسُونَ (٩)
Had We made him an angel, We would have surely made him a man, and We would have surely confounded them just as they confound [the truth now]. (9)
وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَحَاقَ بِٱلَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (١٠)
Apostles were certainly derided before you. Then those who ridiculed them were besieged by what they used to deride. (10)
قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١١)
Say, ‘Travel over the land, and then observe how was the fate of the deniers.’ (11)

(Commentary)

The verses point toward their denial of the Truth (الحق) with which the Messenger was sent, their persistence in rejecting the Truth, and their mockery of the signs (آيات) of Allah, the Glorified. This is followed by an exhortation to them, a warning (تخويف), and an admonition (إنذار), as well as a response to some of the nonsensical arguments they raised in denying the manifest Truth.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَما تَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كانُوا عَنْها مُعْرِضِينَ» — “There does not come to them any sign from the signs of their Lord but that they are disregardful of it.” (6:4). This is an indication that the disposition of arrogance (الاستكبار) has become firmly rooted in their souls, resulting in an aversion to the signs that point toward the Truth. Thus, they pay no heed to any sign whatsoever, without distinction between one sign and another, because they have denied the fundamental objective, which is the Truth. This is as He, the Exalted, says: «فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ» — “They certainly denied the truth when it came to them.” (6:5).

His saying, the Exalted: «فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِيهِمْ أَنْباءُ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ» — “Soon there will come to them the news of what they have been deriding.” (6:5). This is a warning and an admonition, for that which they mock is the Truth, and the Truth must inevitably manifest one day, moving from the realm of report (النبأ) to the realm of direct observation (العيان). He, the Exalted, says: «وَيَمْحُ اللهُ الْباطِلَ وَيُحِقُّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ» — “Allah effaces falsehood and confirms the truth by His words.” (42:24). And He says: «يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِؤُا نُورَ اللهِ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ وَاللهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْكافِرُونَ. هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهدى وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُشْرِكُونَ» — “They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah shall perfect His light though the faithless should be averse. It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse.” (61:8-9). And He says in a parable He struck: «كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْباطِلَ فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفاءً وَأَمَّا ما يَنْفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ» — “As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits mankind, it stays on the earth.” (13:17).

It is well known that when the Truth manifests, the believer (المؤمن) and the disbeliever (الكافر), the humble (الخاضع) and the mocker (المستهزئ), are not equal in how they are affected by it. He, the Exalted, says: «وَلَقَدِ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ وَإِنَّ جُنْدَنا لَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى حِينٍ وَأَبْصِرْهُمْ فَسَوْفَ يُبْصِرُونَ أَفَبِعَذابِنا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ فَإِذا نَزَلَ بِساحَتِهِمْ فَساءَ صَباحُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ» — “Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors. So leave them alone for a while, and watch them; soon they will see [the truth]. Do they seek to hasten Our punishment? But when it descends in their courtyard, evil will be the morning of those who were warned.” (37:171-177).

His saying, the Exalted: «أَلَمْ يَرَوْا كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ» — “Have they not regarded how many a generation We have destroyed before them...” (6:6) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: The generation (القرن) refers to a people joined together (المقترنون) in a single period of time, and its plural is qurun (قرون).

He also said regarding the saying of the Exalted: «وَأَرْسَلْنَا السَّماءَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِدْراراً» — “and We sent the sky for them in abundant rains” (6:6) and «يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً» — “He will send for you the sky in abundant rains” (11:52): Its root is from al-darr (الدر) and al-dirrah (الدرة), meaning milk. This is used metaphorically for rain, just as the names and attributes of camels are used metaphorically. Thus it is said: "To Allah belongs his milk (لله دره)" [a phrase of praise], and "May your milk be abundant (در درك)." From this is borrowed their saying: "A flowing of its milk (غسوق دره)," meaning its abundance.

In the saying of the Exalted: «مَكَّنَّاهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ ما لَمْ نُمَكِّنْ لَكُمْ» — “whom We had established in the earth as We have not established you” (6:6), there is a shift (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the second person (الحضور). The apparent reason for this is to remove ambiguity regarding the referent of the pronoun. Had there not been a shift to the second person in His saying: «ما لَمْ نُمَكِّنْ لَكُمْ» — “as We have not established you”, the context might have suggested that it referred back to the same group as the pronoun in «مَكَّنَّاهُمْ» — “whom We had established”. Otherwise, the original context at the opening of the Surah is in the third person. We have previously discussed the shift occurring in His saying: «هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ طِينٍ» — “It is He who created you from clay.” (6:2).

In His saying: «فأَهْلَكْناهُمْ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ» — “so We destroyed them for their sins” (6:6), there is an indication that evil deeds and sins (الذنوب) have an influence on general calamities and trials. Regarding this meaning, as well as the influence of good deeds (الحسنات) and acts of obedience (الطاعات) in the outpouring of blessings (النعم) and the descent of benedictions (البركات), there are many verses.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَوْ نَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ كِتاباً فِي قِرْطاسٍ فَلَمَسُوهُ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ» — “Had We sent down to you a manuscript on parchment and they had touched it with their hands...” (6:7) until the end of the verse. This is an indication that their arrogance has reached a level where even if We were to send down a book on parchment and they touched it with their hands—such that their senses of sight and hearing were confirmed by the sense of touch—it would be of no use. They would inevitably say at that point: "This is nothing but manifest magic." Therefore, no weight should be given to their nonsensical demand: «وَلَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ» — “and we will not believe your ascension until you bring down to us a book that we may read.” (17:93).

The word "book" (كتاباً) was used in the indefinite form in His saying: «كِتاباً فِي قِرْطاسٍ» — “a book on parchment” because this Book was revealed in a specific manner of revelation (تنزيل) that is only accepted as being revealed in stages (نجوماً) and gradually (تدريجاً). He conditioned it as being "on parchment" (في قرطاس) to be closer to what they suggested, and further from what might occur to their hearts—that the verses revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) were of his own invention without the Trustworthy Spirit (الروح الأمين) bringing them down, as Allah, the Glorified, mentions: «نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُنْذَرِينَ بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ» — “The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart, so that you may be one of the warners, in a clear Arabic language.” (26:193-195).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقالُوا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ وَلَوْ أَنْزَلْنا مَلَكاً لَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ ثُمَّ لا يُنْظَرُونَ» — “They say, ‘Why has not an angel been sent down to him?’ Had We sent down an angel, the matter would have been decided, and then they would not have been granted any respite.” (6:8). Their saying «لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ» — “Why has not an angel been sent down to him?” is a challenge (تحضيض) intended to express incapacity (التعجيز). The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had already informed them, through the signs of Allah he recited to them, that what he brought to them was from a noble angel descending from Allah, as in His saying: «إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ ، ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ ، مُطاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ» — “it is indeed the speech of a noble messenger, powerful and established with the Lord of the Throne, obeyed there and trustworthy.” (81:19-21), among other verses.

Their request for the descent of an angel was for one of two reasons, based on what Allah relates from them in His speech:

The first: That the angel should bring them the punishment the Prophet promised them, as He, the Exalted, says: «فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا فَقُلْ أَنْذَرْتُكُمْ صاعِقَةً مِثْلَ صاعِقَةِ عادٍ وَثَمُودَ» — “But if they turn away, say, ‘I warn you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of Ad and Thamud.’” (41:13). And He said: «قُلْ هُوَ نَبَأٌ عَظِيمٌ» — “Say, ‘It is a great news’” until He said: «إِنْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ إِلَّا أَنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ» — “Nothing is revealed to me except that I am a manifest warner.” (38:67-70).

Since the descent of an angel constitutes a transformation of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — into the manifest (الشهادة), and there is no further goal beyond it, it would result—if they did not believe, and they would never believe due to the entrenched nature of arrogance within them—in the matter being decided between them with justice. At that point, there would be no escape from their destruction, as He, the Exalted, says: «وَلَوْ أَنْزَلْنا مَلَكاً لَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ ثُمَّ لا يُنْظَرُونَ» — “Had We sent down an angel, the matter would have been decided, and then they would not have been granted any respite.” (6:8).

Furthermore, the souls of people deeply immersed in the material world (عالم المادة) and residing in the realm of nature (دار الطبيعة) cannot endure the sight of angels if they were to descend upon them and mingle with them, because their sphere of existence (ظرف) is different. If people were to enter the angels' sphere, it would only be by their transition from the depths of matter to the peak of what lies beyond it, which is death. As He, the Exalted, says: «وَقالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرى رَبَّنا لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيراً ، يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلائِكَةَ لا بُشْرى يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْراً مَحْجُوراً» — “Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why have the angels not been sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?’ Certainly they are too proud of themselves and have become terribly defiant. The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day, and they will say, ‘Keep off!’” (24:21-22). This is the day of death or what follows it, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: «أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلاً» — “The inhabitants of paradise will be in a better abode on that day and a better resting place.” (25:24).

He, the Exalted, says afterward—and the apparent context suggests it is another day: «وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّماءُ بِالْغَمامِ وَنُزِّلَ الْمَلائِكَةُ ، تَنْزِيلاً الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمنِ وَكانَ يَوْماً عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ عَسِيراً» — “The day when the sky is rent asunder with clouds and the angels are sent down in a majestic descent. On that day true sovereignty will belong to the All-beneficent, and it will be a hard day for the faithless.” (25:25-26). Perhaps this is what they meant by their saying: «أَوْ تَأْتِيَ بِاللهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةِ قَبِيلاً» — “or bring Allah and the angels face to face [with us].” (17:92).

In short, His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَوْ أَنْزَلْنا مَلَكاً لَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ» etc., is a response to their suggestion that an angel descend to punish them. Based on this, one should add what Allah promised this nation—that He would delay the punishment for them, as indicated by the verses in Surah Yunus: «وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ ، وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ ، قُلْ لا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللهُ لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ» — “There is an apostle for every nation; so when their apostle comes, judgement is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged. They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ Say, ‘I have no power to benefit or harm myself, except what Allah wills. There is a time for every nation...’” until He said: «وَيَسْتَنْبِئُونَكَ أَحَقٌّ هُوَ قُلْ إِي وَرَبِّي إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ» — “They ask you to inform them: ‘Is it true?’ Say, ‘Yes! By my Lord, it is indeed true, and you cannot frustrate [Him].’” (10:47-53). There are many other verses regarding this meaning, which we shall explore fully in another Surah, if Allah wills.

And He, the Exalted, says: «وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنْتَ فِيهِمْ وَما كانَ اللهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ» — “But Allah would not punish them while you were among them, nor will Allah punish them while they plead for forgiveness.” (8:33).

The conclusion from the verse is that they ask for the descent of an angel, but We do not grant what they ask, because if the angel were to descend, the matter would be decided between them and they would not be granted respite. Allah has willed to grant them respite for a time, so let them indulge in what they indulge in until they meet their day; and what they asked for will surely come to them, and Allah will judge between them.

It is possible to establish the meaning of the verse in another way: that their intention was for the angel to descend as a sign (آية), not to bring punishment. The meaning of the response would then be that if an angel were to descend upon them, they would not believe in him due to the vice of obstinacy (العناد) and arrogance (الاستكبار) firmly established within them. At that point, the matter would be decided between them and they would not be granted respite, and they do not desire that.

The second: That the angel should descend upon them to carry the burdens of the message (الرسالة) and call to Allah in place of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), or be a messenger like him, confirming his call and witnessing to his truthfulness. This is as in their saying, which Allah relates: «وَقالُوا ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ وَيَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْواقِ لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيراً» — “And they say, ‘What sort of apostle is this who eats food and walks in the marketplaces? Why has not an angel been sent down to him to be a warner along with him?’” (25:7). They mean that one who is a messenger from Allah's side should not, in his status, share in the common habits of people like eating food and earning a living by walking in the markets. Rather, he should be distinguished by a heavenly life and a celestial existence, unmixed with the toil of striving and the hardship of material life, so that he may be manifest in his call; or a heavenly angel should descend with him to be a warner with him, so that there remains no doubt regarding the truth of his call and the reality of his message.

This is what the following verse responds to: «وَلَوْ جَعَلْناهُ مَلَكاً» etc.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَوْ جَعَلْناهُ مَلَكاً لَجَعَلْناهُ رَجُلاً وَلَلَبَسْنا عَلَيْهِمْ ما يَلْبِسُونَ» — “Had We made him an angel, We would surely have made him a man, and We would have surely confused for them what they are [themselves] confusing.” (6:9). Al-Labs (اللبس) with a fatha [on the lam] means covering with a veil that which must be covered due to its ugliness or its need for that. Al-Labs (اللبس) with a damma means obscuring the truth (التغطية على الحق). It seems the meaning is metaphorical, and the root is the same.

Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "He wore the garment (لبس الثوب)" means he covered himself with it, and "he clothed another (ألبسه غيره)"... until he said: "The root of al-lubs (with a damma on the lam) is covering a thing. This is said regarding abstract meanings; it is said: 'I confused his matter for him (لبست عليه أمره).'" He said: «وَلَلَبَسْنا عَلَيْهِمْ ما يَلْبِسُونَ» — “and We would have surely confused for them what they are [themselves] confusing” (6:9), and He said: «وَلا تَلْبِسُوا الْحَقَّ بِالْباطِلِ» — “Do not mix the truth with falsehood” (2:42), «لِمَ تَلْبِسُونَ الْحَقَّ بِالْباطِلِ» — “Why do you mix the truth with falsehood” (3:71), «الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ» — “Those who have faith and do not sully their faith with wrongdoing” (6:82). And it is said: "In the matter there is lubsa (لبسة)," meaning confusion (التباس).

The object of "they confuse" (يلبسون) is omitted. Perhaps from this, generality (العموم) is inferred, and the estimation is: the disbelievers confuse for themselves, which is broader than some confusing for themselves and some confusing for others.

As for their confusing for others, it is like when evil scholars mix the truth with falsehood for their ignorant followers, or when tyrants who are followed mix the truth with falsehood for their weak followers. This is like the saying of Pharaoh, which Allah relates to his people: «يا قَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهذِهِ الْأَنْهارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي أَفَلا تُبْصِرُونَ أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْ هذَا الَّذِي هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلا يَكادُ يُبِينُ ، فَلَوْ لا أُلْقِيَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ أَوْ جاءَ مَعَهُ الْمَلائِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ ، فَاسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُ فَأَطاعُوهُ» — “‘O my people! Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me and these rivers that flow at my feet? Do you not perceive? Am I not better than this one who is a lowly wretch and can hardly speak clearly? Why have not gold bracelets been cast upon him, or angels come with him in procession?’ So he fooled his people, and they obeyed him.” (43:51-54). And his saying: «ما أُرِيكُمْ إِلَّا ما أَرى وَما أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ» — “I only show you what I see, and I only guide you to the way of rectitude.” (40:29).

As for their confusing for themselves, it is by imagining to themselves that the truth is falsehood and that falsehood is truth, and then persisting in falsehood. For although man distinguishes truth from falsehood by the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and his soul is inspired with its vice and its godwariness, his strengthening of the side of whim (الهوى) and his support for the spirit of lust and anger within himself generates in his soul the habit of arrogance toward the truth and superiority over reality. Thus, his soul becomes attracted to it, deceived by its own deed, and does not allow him to heed the truth or listen to its call. At that point, his deed is made alluring to him, and he mixes the truth with falsehood while he knows, as He, the Exalted, says: «أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ وَخَتَمَ عَلى سَمْعِهِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَجَعَلَ عَلى بَصَرِهِ غِشاوَةً» — “Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly, and set a seal upon his hearing and his heart, and put a blindfold on his sight?” (45:23). And He said: «قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمالاً الَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعاً» — “Say, ‘Shall we inform you who are the biggest losers in their works? Those whose endeavours go awry in the life of the world, while they suppose they are doing good.’” (18:103-104).

This is what justifies the depiction of a human being's straying in a matter despite his knowledge of it. Thus, the objection does not hold that a person's confusing the truth with falsehood for himself is an undertaking of certain harm, which is irrational.

Furthermore, if we were to look deeply into the states of our own souls and then act with fairness, we would find evil habits whose badness we acknowledge, yet we do not abandon them due to the rootedness of the habit. This is nothing but straying despite knowledge, confusing the truth with falsehood for the soul, and being distracted by imaginary pleasure and infatuated with it away from remaining firm upon the truth and acting by it. May Allah help us toward His pleasure.

In any case, His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَوْ جَعَلْناهُ مَلَكاً لَجَعَلْناهُ رَجُلاً» etc., is the response to their request for the descent of an angel to be a warner so that they might believe in him.

The gist of it is that this world is the realm of choice (الاختيار), in which man's true felicity is not achieved except by his following the path of choice, and his acquiring for himself or against himself what benefits him in his felicity or harms him. Whichever of the two paths he chooses for himself, Allah, the Glorified, carries that out for him.

He, the Exalted, says: «إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً» — “Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful.” (76:3). It is only guidance and showing the way so that he may choose what he chooses for himself of following the path or rebelling, without being compelled to either of the two paths or forced to follow it. Rather, he sows for himself and then reaps what he sowed. He, the Exalted, says: «وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى وَأَنَّ سَعْيَهُ سَوْفَ يُرى ثُمَّ يُجْزاهُ الْجَزاءَ الْأَوْفى» — “and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for, and that he will soon be shown his endeavour, then he will be rewarded for it with the fullest reward.” (53:39-41). Thus, man has nothing but the consequence of his striving; if it is good, Allah shows him that, and if it is evil, He carries it out for him. He, the Exalted, says: «مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ الْآخِرَةِ نَزِدْ لَهُ فِي حَرْثِهِ وَمَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ الدُّنْيا نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها وَما لَهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنْ نَصِيبٍ» — “Whoever desires the tillage of the Hereafter, We will enhance his tillage for him; and whoever desires the tillage of the world, We will give him [some] of it, and he will have no share in the Hereafter.” (42:20).

In short, this divine call cannot be established unless it is placed upon human choice without compulsion or forcing. Therefore, it is inevitable that the Messenger carrying the messages of Allah be one of the people, speaking to them in their language, so that they may choose for themselves felicity through obedience or wretchedness through opposition and disobedience, without Allah forcing them to accept the call by a heavenly sign that compels them to it, even though He is capable of that. As He said: «لَعَلَّكَ باخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ ، إِنْ نَشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ آيَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْناقُهُمْ لَها خاضِعِينَ» — “You might kill yourself [with grief] that they will not believe. If We wish, We will send down to them a sign from the sky before which their heads will remain bowed in humility.” (26:3-4).

So if Allah were to send down to them an angel as a messenger, it would be a requirement of wisdom (الحكمة) to make him a man like them. Thus, the winners would win by their acquisition, and the losers would lose by confusing the truth with falsehood for themselves and for their followers, just as they confuse it with the human Messenger. Then Allah would carry that out and confuse for them as they confused. He, the Exalted, says: «فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ» — “So when they swerved, Allah made their hearts swerve.” (61:5).

Thus, the descent of an angel as a messenger would not result in any benefit or effect greater than what results from sending a human messenger; it would then be futile. So the saying of those who disbelieve: «لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ» is nothing but a request for a futile matter that would not result in any specific new effect as they hoped. This is the meaning of His saying: «وَلَلَبَسْنا عَلَيْهِمْ ما يَلْبِسُونَ» — “and We would have surely confused for them what they are [themselves] confusing.” (6:9).

From the preceding explanation, several points have become clear:

First: That the necessary connection between making the Messenger an angel and making him a man is only from the perspective of the requirement of wisdom to preserve human choice in the divine religious call. For if the angel were sent in his heavenly form, turning the Unseen into the manifest, it would be a form of compulsion (الإلجاء) with which the elective call (الدعوة الاختيارية) could not be established.

Second: That what the verse indicates is the angel becoming a man, while remaining silent as to whether this is by the transformation of the angelic essence (الماهية الملكية) into the human essence—which many researchers consider impossible—or by his appearing in a human likeness (التمثيل), like the Spirit appearing to Mary as a well-proportioned human, and the noble angels appearing to Abraham and Lot (peace be upon them) in the form of human guests.

Although most verses regarding angels support the second of the two views, His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَجَعَلْنا مِنْكُمْ مَلائِكَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ يَخْلُفُونَ» — “Had We wished, We would have made angels from among you to be [Our] successors on the earth” (43:60) is not devoid of some indication toward the first view. This discussion has further details that should be sought elsewhere.

Third: That His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَلَبَسْنا عَلَيْهِمْ ما يَلْبِسُونَ» is of the same nature as His saying: «فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ» — “So when they swerved, Allah made their hearts swerve.” (61:5). Thus, it is a divine leading astray (إضلال) of them after they preferred straying for themselves, without it being an initial leading astray, which would be unbecoming of His holy presence, the Glorified.

Fourth: That the omitted object of "they confuse" (يلبسون) is general, including their confusing for themselves and the confusion of some of them for others.

Fifth: That the gist of the verse is an argument against them: that if an angel were sent to them with the message, it would not benefit them in removing their confusion. For Allah would then make the angel a man resembling the human Messenger, and they would confuse for themselves with him and be skeptical. They only desire by this request to be rid of the human Messenger who is in the form of a man, in order to replace their doubt with certainty; but if the angel were to become of this description—as he inevitably must—they would not benefit from that at all.

Sixth: That in the expression: «لَجَعَلْناهُ رَجُلاً» — “We would surely have made him a man”, and He did not say: "We would have made him a human (بشراً)" to include both man and woman, there is an indication—as has been said—that the Messenger can only be a man. Similarly, the expression is not devoid of an indication that this making is by the appearance of the angel in the form of a human, rather than the transformation of his identity into the identity of a human, as has been said.

Most commentators have explained the verse by saying the meaning is: since they could not endure seeing the angel in his original form due to their immersion in the material world, if an angel were sent to them, it would be necessary for him to appear to them as a well-proportioned human. At that point, there would appear to them the same confusion and doubt that appears with a messenger from among humans, and they would not benefit from it at all.

This explanation is insufficient for the soundness of the response, even if we grant that an ordinary human cannot witness angels in their original forms based on verses like His saying: «يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلائِكَةَ لا بُشْرى يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ» — “The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day.” (25:22).

This is because if witnessing the angel in his original form were impossible for a human, the state of human individuals would not differ in terms of possibility and impossibility. Yet it has been narrated in the reports of both groups [Sunni and Shi'a] that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw Gabriel in his original form twice. It is within the power of Allah to strengthen other people for what He strengthened His Prophet for, so they might behold them and believe in them. There is no hindrance to this according to wisdom except the hindrance of compulsion (الإلجاء); that is the hindrance that must be refuted by the verse, as mentioned before.

Similarly, witnessing the angel in the form of humans does not necessitate the possibility of doubt and confusion. For Allah, the Glorified, informs regarding Abraham and Lot (peace be upon them) that they beheld the angels in the form of humans, then recognized them and did not doubt their matter. Likewise, He informed regarding Mary that she witnessed the Spirit, then recognized him and did not doubt him, nor was his matter confused for her. Why then would it not be possible for the state of other people to be like their state (peace be upon them) in beholding the angel in the form of a human, then recognizing him and having certainty in his matter? Were it not that making the souls of all people like the souls of Abraham, Lot, and Mary would necessitate the erasure of their instincts and primordial natures and their replacement with pure, holy souls—and in that is the hindrance of compulsion. Thus, compulsion is the hindrance with which the subject of trial (الامتحان) would no longer remain, and it is what must be refuted by the verse, as mentioned before.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَقَدِ اسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ» — “Apostles were certainly derided before you...” (6:10) until the end of the two verses. Al-Hayq (الحيق) means descending and befalling. In the Mufradat of al-Raghib: It is said its root is haqqa (حق), so it was inverted like zalla (زل) and zala (زال). It has been recited: «فَأَزَلَّهُمَا الشَّيْطانُ» — “Then Satan caused them to slip” (2:36) and fa-azalahuma (فأزالهما) [caused them to move away]. Based on this are dhammahu (ذمه) and dhamahu (ذامه).

Their mockery of the messengers was by mocking the punishment they were warned would descend and befall them. Thus, the very thing they mocked befell them. In the first verse, there is a solace for the soul of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and a warning to the polytheists; and in the second verse, a command to take heed and an exhortation.

Ayahs

قُل لِّمَن مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ قُل لِّلَّهِ ۚ كَتَبَ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةَ ۚ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٢)
Say, ‘To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘To Allah. He has made mercy incumbent upon Himself. He will surely gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt. Those who have ruined their souls will not have faith.’ (12)
۞ وَلَهُۥ مَا سَكَنَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (١٣)
To Him belongs whatever abides in the night and the day, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (13)
قُلْ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَلَا يُطْعَمُ ۗ قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ ۖ وَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٤)
Say, ‘Shall I take for guardian [anyone] other than Allah, the originator of the heavens and the earth, who feeds and is not fed?’ Say, ‘I have been commanded to be the first of those who submit [to Allah], and [told,] “Never be one of the polytheists.” ’ (14)
قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّى عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٥)
Say, ‘Indeed, should I disobey my Lord, I fear the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (15)
مَّن يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُۥ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٦)
Whoever is spared of it on that day, He has certainly been merciful to him, and that is manifest success. (16)
وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ ٱللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلَا كَاشِفَ لَهُۥٓ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٧)
Should Allah visit you with some distress there is no one to remove it except Him; and should He bring you some good, then He has power over all things. (17)
وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١٨)
And He is the All-dominant over His servants, and He is the All-wise, the All-aware. (18)

(Commentary)

A group of verses argues against the polytheists (المشركين) regarding monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the Return (المعاد) — the restoration of life after death for judgment. The first two verses contain the proof for the Return, while the remaining five verses are directed toward monotheism, establishing the proof for it from two perspectives, as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قُلْ لِلَّهِ — "Say, ‘To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘To Allah.’" (6:12). This is the commencement of the demonstration for the Return. Its summary is that Allah, the Exalted, is the Owner (مالك) of all that is in the heavens and the earth; He has the right to dispose of them however He wills and desires. He, the Glorified, has characterized Himself with the attribute of mercy (الرحمة) — the lifting of the need of every needy being and the bringing of everything to what it deserves, bestowing it upon them. A number of His servants, including human beings, are fit for eternal life and prepared to be felicitous therein. Therefore, by the requirement of His ownership and His mercy, He will certainly dispose of them by gathering them and giving them what they deserve.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (Say, ‘To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?’) etc., contains one of the premises of the argument. His saying: كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ (He has prescribed mercy for Himself) contains another premise. And His saying: وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ (To Him belongs whatever dwells in the night and the day) (6:13) etc., is a third premise serving as a part of the argument.

So, His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ etc., commands the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to ask them about who owns the heavens and the earth and has the right to dispose of them as He wills without any preventer hindering Him. This is Allah, the Glorified, without doubt, because anything other than Him—even the idols and the lords of the idols whom the polytheists invoke—are like the rest of creation; their creation and their command terminate in Him, the Exalted. Thus, He is the Owner of all that is in the heavens and the earth.

Because the subject of the question is known and clear to both the asker and the asked, and the opponent (الخصم) acknowledges it, there was no need for the answer to be issued by the opponent or for his verbal admission. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded to state the answer himself and take charge of it so that the proof would be completed without waiting for their response.

Asking the opponent and the questioner himself undertaking the answer are both among the exquisite stylistic devices (السلائق البديعة) used in the presentation of arguments. A benefactor says to the one he has bestowed favors upon, who then showed ingratitude for his blessing: "Who is it that fed you, gave you drink, and clothed you? It is I who did that for you and favored you with it, yet you repay me with ingratitude."

In short, it is established by this question and answer that Allah, the Glorified, is the Absolute Owner (المالك على الإطلاق). He has the right to dispose of things as He wills—of giving life, providing sustenance, causing death, and resurrecting after death—without any preventer hindering Him, such as precision in work, death, absence, disorder, or otherwise. With this, one of the premises of the argument is completed, so He joined it with the other premise, which is His saying: كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ (He has prescribed mercy for Himself).

His saying, the Exalted: كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ — "He has prescribed mercy for Himself." Writing (الكتابة) is the act of establishing and the final decree (القضاء الحتم). Since mercy (الرحمة) — the bestowing of grace upon its deserving recipient and bringing a thing to the felicity that befits it — is among His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية), it is correct to attribute it to His writing. The meaning is: He has made mercy, the bestowing of blessings, and the sending down of good mandatory upon Himself for those who deserve it.

Similar to this in attributing an act to "writing" and the like is His saying, the Exalted: كَتَبَ اللهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي — "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles.’" (58:21), and His saying: فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ — "By the Lord of the sky and the earth, it is indeed the truth" (51:23). As for the attributes of the essence (صفات الذات) such as life (الحياة), knowledge (العلم), and power (القدرة), it is not correct to attribute them to "writing" and the like at all; it is not said: "He wrote life, knowledge, and power upon Himself."

The necessary consequence of His prescribing mercy for Himself—as previously mentioned—is that He completes His blessing upon them by gathering them for the Day of Resurrection to recompense them for their words and deeds, whereby the believers (المؤمنون) succeed and others lose.

Therefore, He concluded with His saying, which is like the conclusion (النتيجة) of the argument: لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ — "He will surely gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt." (6:12). He emphasized the meaning with the most eloquent emphasis: the lam of the oath, the nun of emphasis, and His saying: لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ (in which there is no doubt).

Then He pointed out that the profit on this day is for the believers and the loss is upon others, saying: الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Those who have ruined their souls will not have faith." (6:12).

The argument established in this verse for the Return is different from the two arguments established for it in His saying, the Exalted: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ ، أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ — "We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of the faithless; so woe to the faithless from the Fire! Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (38:27-28). Those two verses establish the proof for the Return from the perspective that His act, the Exalted, is not in vain (باطل) but has a purpose (غاية), and from the perspective that equating the believer with the disbeliever and the God-conscious (المتقي) with the vicious (الفاجر) is an injustice (ظلم) that does not befit Him, the Exalted. Since they are not distinguished in this world, there must be another realm (نشأة أخرى) in which they are distinguished by felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة). This is different from what is in this verse, which proceeds toward the objective through the path of mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ — "To Him belongs whatever dwells in the night and the day, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing." (6:13). Dwelling (السكون) in the night and the day is to occur within the vessel of this natural world (العالم الطبيعي), whose affairs are managed by night and day. Its system (نظام) runs by the covering of the pouring light from a luminous sun, and the operation of light transformations within it through proximity and distance, abundance and scarcity, presence and absence, alignment, and other factors.

The night and the day are the general cradle in which the universal elements (العناصر الكلية) and their offspring are nurtured—a nurturing that drives every part of its parts and every individual of its individuals toward the goal (غاية) destined for it, completing it in spirit and body.

Just as the general and specific dwelling place (المسكن) has a complete influence on the being of the dweller (الساكن)—for example, a human being dwells on a land, roaming it in search of sustenance, being nourished by the grains and fruits it produces and the animals raised upon it, drinking its water, breathing its air, and being affected by the qualities of its region, with all parts of his body growing according to its measure—likewise, the night and the day have a complete influence on the formation of everything that comes into being within them.

The human being is among the things dwelling in the night and the day. He came into being by the will of Allah from the combination of simple and compound parts into a specific form. His existence is distinguished in its inception and survival by a life based on intellectual perception (شعور فكري) and will (إرادة), which are prepared for him by internal emotional powers that command him to attract benefits and repel harms. These powers call him to establish a structured society (مجتمع) in which we see the details of communication through languages, the agreement to follow norms, laws, and customs in social interactions and transactions, and the respect for general opinions and beliefs regarding beauty and ugliness (الحسن والقبح), justice and injustice (العدل والظلم), obedience and disobedience (الطاعة والمعصية), and reward, punishment, recompense, and pardon.

Since Allah, the Glorified, is the Creator of the night and the day and what dwells within them, being unique in bringing them into existence, then to Him belongs whatever dwells in the night and the day. He is the True Owner (المالك الحق) of all the night and the day, their inhabitants, and the events, actions, and words that follow their existence. To Him belongs the system running within them in its wondrous vastness. Thus, He is the All-hearing (السميع) of our words, including sounds and signs, and the All-knowing (العليم) of our deeds and actions with their attributes of beauty and ugliness, justice and injustice, benevolence and wrongdoing, and what the souls acquire of felicity and wretchedness.

How could ignorance of that be possible for Him when everything has originated in His kingdom and by His permission?
