The mode of existence of this type of matter—I mean beauty and ugliness, justice and injustice, obedience and disobedience, as well as the languages indicating mental meanings—all of these are cognitive matters (أمور علمية) that have no reality except within the vessel of knowledge (ظرف العلم). Therefore, we see that an act does not occur from us as beautiful or ugly, nor as obedience or disobedience, and a composed sound is not called "speech" unless we know it and intend its purpose.

How is it possible for something that is cognitive (علمي) in itself, by virtue of being so, to be owned, and then its owner be ignorant of it and not know it? (Reflect deeply on this).

Allah, the Glorified, is the One who brought this world into existence in its wondrous vastness—in its simple, elemental, and compound parts—according to a system that dazzles the intellect. Then He created us and settled us in the night and the day, then multiplied us and ran among us the system of human society. Then He guided us to the formulation of languages, the consideration of norms, and the establishment of conventional concepts (الاعتبارات). He has never ceased to accompany us and all other causes (الأسباب) step by step, running with us and them in the course of night and day moment by moment, driving innumerable events, one after another.

When one of us speaks a word, He places the meaning in his heart by His inspiration (إلهام), runs the word on his tongue by His making it known, makes the sound heard by the listener by His making him hear, carries its meaning to his mind by His preservation, and makes the meaning understood by his thinking faculty by His teaching. Then He motivates him to agree with what the speaker conveyed to him or prevents him from that by a will that drives him toward it or a dislike that repels him from it. In all these stages, which the fingers of counting and enumeration fail to encompass, He is the Leader, the Driver, the Guide, the Preserver, and the Watcher (الرقيب)! How can a speaker say anything except that He, the Exalted, is All-hearing, All-knowing? "There is no secret talk between three but He is their fourth, nor between five but He is their sixth, nor less than that nor more but He is with them wherever they may be. Then He will inform them of what they did on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things." (58:7).

Likewise, when one of us performs a deed, whether good or evil, it is born of a father and a mother who give birth to it under the supervision of choice (الاختيار) and will (الإرادة). It has transitioned to them after traversing a long path and a lengthy period in the loins of its active causes and the wombs of other passive causes, until it terminates in that which Allah knows best. He, the Glorified, has never ceased to move it by His will from one lap to another—the earth is His handful and the heavens are in His right hand—until it descended into the station of choice. He accompanied it station after station by His permission until it rose from the horizon of external reality (العين) and took its place in the dwelling of night and day. Then it continues to run through the parts of the cosmos that are affected by it as one of the running causes, while Allah, the Glorified, is a Witness (شهيد) over it and Encompassing (محيط) it. How is it possible for Him, the Glorified, to be heedless of that which is of this nature? "Does He who created not know, while He is the All-attentive, the All-aware?" (67:14).

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ (and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing) is in the position of a conclusion (النتيجة) for His saying: وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ (To Him belongs whatever dwells in the night and the day).

Hearing (السمع) and knowledge (العلم), although counted among His essential attributes (صفاته الذاتية) which are identical to the Transcendent Essence (عين الذات) without being branched from anything else, there is a type of knowledge—as well as hearing and sight—that is an active attribute (صفة فعلية) external to the Essence. Its establishment depends on the realization of an object (متعلق) other than the Holy Essence, such as creating (الخلق), providing (الرزق), giving life (الإحياء), and causing death (الإماتة), which depend on the existence of a creature, a recipient of sustenance, a living being, and a dead being.

Since things in their very selves and essences are owned and encompassed by Him, the Exalted, then if they are sounds, they are "heard" and "audible" to Him, the Exalted; if they are lights and colors, they are "seen" and "visible" to Him, the Exalted; and all of them, whatever they may be, are "knowledge" and "known" to Him, the Exalted. This type of knowledge is among His active attributes that are realized upon the realization of the act from Him, the Exalted, not before that. Their establishment after they were not does not necessitate change in His Essence, the Exalted and Sanctified, because they do not exceed the station of the act (مقام الفعل) and do not enter the realm of the Essence (عالم الذات). Thus, the verse, in its derivation of knowledge from ownership, intends to establish active knowledge (العلم الفعلي); so understand that.

The verse—I mean His saying: وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ etc.—is like one of the premises of the argument explained in the previous verse. For although the argument for the Return was completed by His saying: قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قُلْ لِلَّهِ كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ, a simple, initial glance might overlook the fact that His ownership of things necessitates His knowledge of them and His hearing of what is heard from them, such as sounds and words.

Therefore, He drew attention to it by repeating the ownership of the heavens and the earth and branching hearing and knowledge upon it, saying: وَلَهُ ما سَكَنَ فِي اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ —which is in the meaning of His saying: "To Him belongs what is in the heavens and the earth"— وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ. Thus, this verse was, for that reason, in the position of a premise completing the argument presented in the previous verse.

The verse—though we have not exhausted its right, nor will it ever be exhausted—is among the most delicate of Quranic verses in meaning, the most precise in indication and proof, and the most eloquent in logic.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَلا يُطْعَمُ — "Say, ‘Shall I take for guardian anyone other than Allah, the Originator of the heavens and the earth, who feeds and is not fed?’" (6:14). This is the commencement of the argumentation for His uniqueness, the Exalted, and that He has no partner.

What is gathered from the history of paganism (الوثنية) and the taking of idols and deities is that they only followed that and submitted to the deities for one of two reasons: Either they found themselves in need of many causes for the preservation of life, such as nourishment through food, clothing, shelter, spouses, children, kin, and the like—the most central of which is food, for which the human need is more intense than the need for anything else according to a simple view. They believed that every category of these needs was connected to a cause (سبب) that bestowed upon them the enjoyment of the means to lift that need, such as the cause that makes the sky rain so that the pasture and fodder grow for their beasts and provides fertility for themselves, or the cause that manages the affairs of the plain and the mountain, or inspires love and intimacy, or to whom the affair of the sea and the ships running therein belongs.

Then they found that their power was insufficient to master those necessary needs, so they were forced to submit to the cause linked to their need, taking it as a god and then worshipping it.

Or because they found this defenseless human being to be a target for the arrows of events, surrounded by great general harms and evils that he cannot resist, such as floods, earthquakes, deluges, famine, and plague, and by other innumerable particular afflictions and trials, such as diseases, pains, falls, poverty, infertility, enemies, the envious, the hater, and others. Then they assigned to these things powerful causes that send these afflictions to them, breaking their backs with them and darkening the purity of their lives. These are celestial creatures such as the lords of the species (أرباب الأنواع) and the spirits of the stars and celestial bodies. They took them as gods out of fear of their wrath and punishment, and worshipped them to win them over through worship and please them through submission and humility, thereby escaping from harms and calamities and being safe from their evils and the injuries descending from them.

The verse—I mean His saying: قُلْ أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا etc.—and the verses following it argue against the polytheists by turning their own two arguments back upon them. That is, it accepts the basis of the argument and considers it true, but explains that its necessary consequence is to worship Allah, the Glorified, alone, and to negate every posited partner from Him.

So His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ يُطْعِمُ وَلا يُطْعَمُ is an indication of the argument from the first path, which is the path of hope (الرجاء)—that the deity is worshipped because He is a Benefactor (منعم), so worshipping Him is gratitude for His favor and a cause for its increase.

He, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to explain to them in the form of an inquiry and a question that Allah, the Glorified, alone is the Guardian (الولي) of the blessing that the human being and others enjoy, because He is the Provider (الرازق) who does not need anyone else to provide for Him; He feeds and is not fed. The proof for this is that He, the Exalted, is the One who originated (فطر) the heavens and the earth, bringing them out from the darkness of non-existence into the light of existence, and favored them with the blessing of realization and stability. Then He bestowed upon them blessings that none can count but He for the preservation of their existence, among which is the feeding of the human being and others. For all these blessings prepared for the preservation of the existence of the human being and others, and the causes that drive those blessings to the places of desert, all of that terminates in His origination and His bringing into existence of things, causes, and their effects altogether as His handiwork.

Thus, to Him, the Glorified, returns the provision (الرزق), of which the most important manifestation for the human being is feeding. Therefore, it is mandatory to worship Allah alone because He is the One who feeds us without needing to be fed by anyone else.

It has become clear from what we have explained: First, that the expression of servitude (العبودية) and deification (التأله) as "taking a guardian" (اتخاذ الولي) in His saying: أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَتَّخِذُ وَلِيًّا is only because the argument is directed from the perspective of His favor, the Exalted, in feeding.

Second, that the reliance upon His saying: فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (the Originator of the heavens and the earth) is only to explain the reason for the confinement of feeding to Him, the Exalted, as previously established. Perhaps this is also understood from the allusion (التعريض) in His saying: وَلا يُطْعَمُ (and is not fed), for in it is an allusion to the fact that all others whom they have taken as gods are in need—like Jesus and others—of feeding or what serves its purpose.

It is possible that it be understood from its mention in the argument that it is an indication of another path in establishing the proof for His uniqueness, the Exalted, which is nobler than both previous paths. Its summary is that Allah, the Glorified, is the Originator of this world, and to His origination (فطرة) everything terminates; therefore, submission to Him is mandatory.

The reason this path is nobler is that the other two paths, although they result in the monotheism of the deity from the perspective that He is the Worshipped, they are not free from something—namely, that they result in the obligation of worshipping Him out of greed for blessing or fear of punishment. Thus, the primary objective is the acquisition of blessing or safety from punishment rather than Him, the Exalted and Sanctified. As for this path, it results in the obligation of worshipping Allah because He is Allah, the Glorified.

Third, that the specific mention of feeding (الإطعام) from among His blessings, the Exalted, despite their abundance, is due to the consideration that feeding, according to a simple view, is the most obvious of the needs of a living animal, including the human being.

Then He, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله after the completion of the argument to mention to them what supports this rational proof (الحجة العقلية)—which is that Allah commanded him through revelation (الوحي) to proceed in taking a deity upon the path to which the intellect guides, which is monotheism, and explicitly forbade him from overstepping it to join the polytheists, saying: قُلْ إِنِّي أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ — "Say, ‘I have been commanded to be the first of those who submit [to Allah],’" then He said: وَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — "and [I was told], ‘Do not be one of the polytheists.’" (6:14).

Two matters remain here:

One: That His saying: أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ (the first of those who submit), if the meaning is the first of those who submit from among you, then it is apparent, for he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله submitted before his nation. If the meaning is the first of those who submit without restriction, as is the apparent sense of the absolute expression, then his priority in that is according to rank (الرتبة) rather than time (الزمان).

Two: That since the result of the argument was servitude (العبودية), which is a type of humility and submission (تسليم), the use of the word "Islam" (الإسلام) in this context was more appropriate than the word "faith" (الإيمان) because of its indication of the purpose of worship, which is humility.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنِّي أَخافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ — "Say, ‘I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a tremendous day.’" (6:15). This is the second path of the two paths previously mentioned that the polytheists relied upon in taking deities—namely, that the worship of their deities secures them from the encompassment of their wrath and the descent of their punishment.

He, the Glorified, has taken in the argument the most fearful thing that must be feared among the types of punishment—and He commanded it—which is the punishment of the Hour that weighs heavily in the heavens and the earth, just as He took in the first argument the most needed thing that the human being requires according to the first glance among blessings, which is feeding.

It was said: إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي (if I should disobey my Lord) rather than saying "if I should associate partners with my Lord," as an indication of what was in His saying, the Exalted, in the previous verse: وَلا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (Do not be one of the polytheists), regarding the prohibition of him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from polytheism (الشرك). Thus, the verse conveyed that it is rationally mandatory for me to worship Allah alone to be safe from what I fear of the punishment of a tremendous day, and that which the intellect indicated, the revelation from my Lord has guided me to.

By this, this verse corresponds to the previous verse from the perspective of establishing the rational proof first, then supporting it with revelation from Allah, the Glorified; so understand that. This is among the subtleties of the Quran's conciseness, for it sufficed in conveying this meaning in its vastness by merely placing His saying: عَصَيْتُ (I disobey) in the place of "I associate partners."

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُ — "Anyone from whom it is averted on that day, He has indeed had mercy on him." (6:16) etc. The meaning is clear. The verse completes the argument presented in the previous verse. For the apparent sense of the previous verse, according to a simple view, is the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله establishing the proof for the obligation of monotheism upon himself, in that Allah forbade him from polytheism, so monotheism is mandatory upon him to be safe from the punishment of the Hereafter.

It might occur to the view of the heedless one who does not reflect to counter the argument by saying that since the prohibition was specific to you—as you claim—then the fear is specific, and then the obligation of monotheism and the negation of a partner is also specific to you. Thus, the argument would be against you and not against others.

So He conveyed by His saying: مَنْ يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُ that His punishment is looming over everyone and encompassing all; there is no escape from it except by His mercy. Therefore, every human being must fear for himself from the punishment of that day what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله fears for himself. Thus, the argument is general, established against all people, not specific to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ اللهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلا كاشِفَ لَهُ إِلَّا هُوَ — "Should Allah visit you with any distress, there is no one to remove it except Him" (6:17) to the end of the verse. The two arguments mentioned in the previous verses had taken a sample of what the human being hopes for, which is feeding, and a sample of what he fears, which is the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, and completed the explanation with them. They did not address other types of distress (الضر) and categories of good (الخير) with which Allah, the Glorified, visits the human being, and all of it is from Allah, exalted is His Name.

So the verse clarifies by explicit statement that there is distress other than the punishment of the Day of Resurrection with which Allah, the Glorified, visits the human being, and one must turn to Him, the Exalted, for its removal. And that there is good with which Allah visits the human being, and there is none to repel His grace (فضله) nor any preventer to hinder its bestowal, due to His power over all things. The hope for good makes it mandatory for the human being to take Him, the Glorified, as a worshipped God.

Since it might be imagined that His visiting the human being with distress or good only necessitates that He be taken as a worshipped one—and the opponent does not deny that (1)—but as for the confinement of divinity (الألوهية) and worshipfulness (المعبودية) to Him, the Exalted, it does not, because what they have taken as gods are intermediate causes (أسباب متوسطة) and powerful intercessors (شفعاء أقوياء) that have effects in the cosmos of evil or good, which makes it mandatory for the human being to seek proximity to them out of fear of their evil or hope for their good.

He refuted this by stating that Allah, the Glorified, is the All-conquering (القاهر) above His servants; none of them surpasses Him nor equals Him. They themselves are under His conquest, as are their actions and effects. They perform no deed of good or evil except by His permission and His will, not being independent in any matter at all, nor possessing for themselves any harm or benefit or anything else. So whatever effect of good or evil arises from the horizon of their essences terminates in His command, His will, and His permission, being attributed to Him in a manner that befits the courtyard of His sanctity and His might.

Thus, both verses together complete a single meaning: that whatever reaches the human being of good or evil is from Allah, according to the attribution that befits His courtyard. So Allah, the Glorified, is the One unique in divinity and the One solitary in worshipfulness; there is no god but He, and no worshipped one besides Him.

He expressed the occurrence of distress and good as "touching" (المس), which indicates insignificance, in His saying: إِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ (should He visit/touch you) and وَإِنْ يَمْسَسْكَ, to indicate by it that what reaches the human being of distress or good is a small thing from what is carried by the infinite power (القدرة غير المتناهية) which nothing can withstand, nor can any limited creature endure or bear it.

It is as if His saying, the Exalted, regarding the side of good: فَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (He has power over all things) was placed in the position of something like our saying: "there is no preventer to hinder Him," to indicate that He, the Exalted, is powerful over every assumed good just as He is powerful over every assumed distress. By this, the reason for His saying: فَلا كاشِفَ لَهُ إِلَّا هُوَ (there is no one to remove it except Him) is revealed; for if anyone other than Him, the Exalted, were to remove anything of the distress He visited, that would repel His power over it. Likewise, His power over all things necessitates that nothing can repel what He visits of good.

The specification of the distress or good visited upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in this verse is similar to the specification occurring in His saying: قُلْ إِنِّي أَخافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ. And His saying: وَهُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ (He is the All-conquering, above His servants) provides a generalization similar to what His saying: مَنْ يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُ provided.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ — "He is the All-conquering, above His servants, and He is the All-wise, the All-aware." (6:18). Conquest (القهر) is a type of overcoming (الغلبة); it is for a thing to prevail over another thing, forcing it to comply with an effect from the overcomer that opposes the effect the overcome has by nature or the like—such as water prevailing over fire, conquering it into extinction, and fire conquering water, evaporating it or drying its moisture. Since the cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية) were only manifested by Allah, the Glorified, to be intermediaries in the occurrence of events, placing their effects in their results, and they—whatever they may be—are forced to comply with what Allah, the Glorified, intends in them and through them, it is true of them generally that they are conquered (مقهورة) by Allah, the Glorified; so Allah is the All-conquering (قاهر) over them.

Thus, "the All-conquering" (القاهر) is among the names that are true of Him, the Exalted, as they are true of others, except that between His conquest, the Exalted, and the conquest of others, there is a difference. The difference is that others besides Him, the Exalted, among things, only conquer one another while they are joined in the rank of their existence and the degree of their being—meaning that fire conquers wood into burning and igniting, and both together are natural existents, one of which naturally requires the opposite of what the other requires, but the fire is stronger in imposing its effect upon the wood than the wood is upon the fire, so it prevails over it in its influence with its effect upon it.

But Allah, the Glorified, is All-conquering, not like the conquest of fire over wood; rather, He is All-conquering by superiority (التفوق) and absolute encompassment (الإحاطة على الإطلاق). This means that if we attribute the burning and igniting of a body like wood, for example, to Allah, the Glorified, then He, the Glorified, is All-conquering over it by the limited existence with which He brought it into being; All-conquering over it by the properties and qualities He gave it and equipped it with by His hand; All-conquering over it by the fire He kindled for its burning and igniting—and He is the Owner of all that the fire possesses of essence and effect; All-conquering over it by cutting off the gift of resistance from the wood and placing burning and igniting in its stead. Thus, there is no resistance, no disobedience, no rebellion, nor anything similar before His will and His desire, because they are from a higher horizon.

So He, the Exalted, is All-conquering over His servants, but He is "above them" (فَوْقَهُمْ), not like the conquest of one thing over another while they are peers. The Quran has confirmed this discussion with its conclusion, mentioning it as a name for Him, the Exalted, in two places in this Surah: this verse and verse 61.

He restricted the name in both places with His saying: فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ (above His servants). The majority of preserved instances of the use of "conquest" (القهر) is that the overcome be among the possessors of intellect (أولي العقل), unlike "overcoming" (الغلبة). For this reason, al-Raghib explained it as "subjugation" (التذليل), and submissiveness (الذلة) is more apparent in the possessors of intellect. This does not prevent the correctness of its application in cases other than the possessors of intellect according to usage or by figurative extension.

Allah, the Glorified, is the All-conquering above His servants; He visits them with distress and with good and subjugates them to His compliance. He is the All-conquering above His servants in what they do and the effects they produce, because He is the Owner of what He made them own and the One powerful over what He empowered them with.

Since in the two verses He attributed to Himself the visiting with distress and good, and they might be attributed to others, He distinguished His station from the station of others by His saying at the end of the verse: وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ (and He is the All-wise, the All-aware). So He is the All-wise (الحكيم) who does not do what He does haphazardly or out of ignorance, and the All-aware (الخبير) who does not err or make mistakes like others.

(1) The elite among the pagans, although they might permit His worship based on the fact that He is of unlimited existence and worshipful focus cannot attach to Him, the commoners among them perhaps worshipped Him alongside other deities, as appears from the talbiyah of the polytheists of Mecca during Hajj: "At Your service, You have no partner, except a partner who is Yours; You own him and what he owns."

Ayahs

قُلْ أَىُّ شَىْءٍ أَكْبَرُ شَهَـٰدَةً ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ شَهِيدٌۢ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۚ وَأُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ لِأُنذِرَكُم بِهِۦ وَمَنۢ بَلَغَ ۚ أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً أُخْرَىٰ ۚ قُل لَّآ أَشْهَدُ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ وَإِنَّنِى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ (١٩)
Say, ‘What thing is greatest as witness?’ Say, ‘Allah! [He is] witness between me and you, and this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn thereby you and whomever it may reach.’ ‘Do you indeed bear witness that there are other gods besides Allah?’ Say, ‘I do not bear witness [to any such thing].’ Say, ‘Indeed He is the One God, and I indeed disown what you associate [with Him].’ (19)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَعْرِفُونَهُۥ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَآءَهُمُ ۘ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٠)
Those whom We have given the Book recognize him just as they recognize their sons. Those who have ruined their souls will not have faith. (20)

(Commentary)

This is an argumentation for monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — by way of revelation (الوحي). Although the oneness of the Deity and the negation of any partner (الشريك) are matters that the intellect can attain through various modes of perception, there is nothing to prevent establishing them through the path of explicit revelation in which there is no doubt. The objective is to attain certainty (اليقين) that He, the Exalted, is one God with no partner. If such certainty is assumed to be achieved through the path of revelation—which is free from any doubt regarding its status as divine revelation, such as the Quran which relies upon the challenge of inimitability (التحدي)—then there is no obstacle to relying upon it.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَيُّ شَيْءٍ أَكْبَرُ شَهادَةً قُلِ اللهُ شَهِيدٌ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ "Say, ‘What thing is greatest as witness?’ Say, ‘Allah is witness between me and you.’" (6:19). He commanded His Prophet to ask them about the greatest of things in terms of witnessing (الشهادة). Witnessing consists of bearing a report based on a type of direct observation (العيان), such as sight and the like, and the performance (الأداء) of what was thus borne by reporting and informing. Since bearing and performing—especially bearing—are matters that vary drastically according to the perception of the witnesses, the clarity of the report they bear, and the strength or weakness of the performance in its exposition (البيان), they differ immensely.

A witness whose temperament is dominated by oversight, forgetfulness, or heedlessness is not like one who preserves what his ear encompasses and what his eye falls upon. The sober person is not like the intoxicated, nor is the specialized expert in a matter like the unequipped outsider.

Since the matter is thus, there is no doubt that Allah, the Glorified, is greater than everything in witnessing (الشهادة). For He is the One who brought into existence everything, whether minute or magnificent; to Him every matter and creation returns. He encompasses everything and is with everything; not even the weight of an atom in the heavens or the earth escapes His knowledge, nor anything smaller than that or greater. He neither errs nor forgets.

Because the matter is self-evident and no doubt occurs within it, there was no need to mention the answer in the wording by saying: "Say, Allah is greatest as witness," as was said in: قُلْ لِمَنْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قُلِ اللهِ "Say, ‘To whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘To Allah.’" (6:12), or as it was said: "They will say, 'To Allah,'" as in: قُلْ لِمَنِ الْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيها إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ "Say, ‘To whom belongs the earth and whoever is in it, if you know?’ They will say, ‘To Allah.’" (23:84-85).

Furthermore, His saying: "Say, ‘Allah is witness between me and you’" indicates this and stands in its place. It is not far-fetched that the word "witness" (شهيد) is the predicate (الخبر) of a deleted subject (المبتدأ), which is the pronoun referring back to Allah; the estimation being: "Say, Allah, He is witness between me and you." Thus, the sentence would include the answer to the question as well as the newly commenced speech.

His saying: "Say, ‘Allah is witness between me and you’"—while it includes the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) informing of the testimony of Allah—is itself a testimony by virtue of the command "Say" (قل). For His commanding him to inform them of His testimony regarding the prophethood is inseparable from the testimony itself. Accordingly, there is no need to cling to the various types of testimony found in the Noble Quran regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prophethood and the descent of the Quran from Him, such as His saying: وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُ "and Allah knows that you are indeed His Apostle" (63:1), or His saying: لكِنِ اللهُ يَشْهَدُ بِما أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكَ أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ "But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you—He sent it down with His knowledge" (4:166), and other verses indicating this explicitly or implicitly, using the word "testimony" or otherwise.

The restriction of His testimony by the phrase "between me and you" (بيني وبينكم) indicates the mediation of the Exalted between two disputing parties: the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his people. The Prophet was not isolated from them nor distinguished from them except in the claim of prophethood (النبوة), messengership (الرسالة), and the claim of the Quran's descent. However, the descent of the Quran through revelation (الوحي) is mentioned afterward in His saying: "And this Quran has been revealed to me." Therefore, what is meant by His testimony between him and them is His testimony to his prophethood. This is also supported by His saying in the following verse: "Those to whom We have given the Book recognize him just as they recognize their own sons," as will be discussed, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ "and this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach." (6:19). This is part of the quoted speech and is coordinated with His saying: "Allah is witness...", etc. Making warning (الإنذار) the objective of the descent of the Noble Quran adopts the path of fear (الخوف) in the prophetic call (الدعوة), which is more effective in the understanding of the general public. For although the path of hope (الرجاء) and promise (الوعد) is one of the two ways of the call—and the Mighty Book has used it in general—the hope for good does not incite one to seek it with an obligatory impulse; rather, it bequeaths longing and desire. This is in contrast to fear, due to the intellectual necessity of repelling potential harm.

Furthermore, the call of Islam is a call to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — which is stored and treasured within the nature of people. They are only veiled from it by the polytheism (الشرك) and disobedience they have been afflicted with, which necessitates the dominance of wretchedness and the descent of divine wrath upon them. Thus, it is closer to wisdom (الحكمة) and prudence in calling them to begin with warning. For all these reasons, the status of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is sometimes restricted to warning, as in His saying: إِنْ أَنْتَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ "You are but a warner" (35:23) and His saying: وَإِنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ "and I am only a manifest warner" (24:50).

This applies to the general public. As for the elite among the servants of Allah—those who worship Him out of love for Him, not out of fear of Fire nor out of greed for Paradise—they receive from the call of fear and hope something else. They perceive the Fire as an abode of distance and wrath, so they fear it for that reason; and they perceive Paradise as a courtyard of proximity and pleasure, so they long for it for that reason.

The apparent meaning of His saying: "that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach" is that it is an address to the polytheists of Mecca, or the Quraysh, or the Arabs in general. However, the contrast between the pronoun of address ("you") and "whomever it may reach" (من بلغ)—the latter referring to those whom the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did not address directly during his lifetime or after him—indicates that the intended audience in His saying "that I may warn you" are those whom the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) addressed directly, whether their call preceded the revelation of the verse, coincided with it, or followed it.

Thus, His saying: "and this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach" indicates the universality of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) messengership through the Quran for everyone who hears it from him or hears it from someone else until the Day of Resurrection. Or if you wish, you could say: the verse indicates that the Noble Quran is a proof (حجة) from Allah and His Book that speaks the truth to the people of the world from the time of its revelation until the Day of Resurrection.

It was said: "that I may warn you thereby (به)" and He did not say: "by its recitation." Thus, the Quran is a proof against anyone who hears its wording, knows its meaning, and is guided to its objectives, or for whom its wording is explained and its contents reach his ears. It is not a condition for a book written to a people that it be in their tongue, but rather that its proof be established against them and its contents encompass them. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called through his letters Egypt, Abyssinia, Rome, and Iran, while their tongues were other than the tongue of the Quran. Among those who believed in him during his lifetime and whose faith he accepted were Salman the Persian, Bilal the Abyssinian, Suhayb the Roman, and a number of Jews whose tongue was Hebrew; all of this is beyond doubt.

His saying, the Exalted: أَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ مَعَ اللهِ آلِهَةً أُخْرى قُلْ لا أَشْهَدُ "Do you really bear witness that there are other gods with Allah?’ Say, ‘I do not bear witness [to any such thing].’" (6:19) to the end of the verse. When He mentioned the testimony of Allah—which is the greatest testimony—to his messengership, and he was only sent to call them to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), it is not for anyone after the testimony of Allah, the Glorified, that there is no partner to Him in His divinity, to testify that there are other gods with Allah. He commanded His Prophet to ask them a question of wonder and denunciation: Do they testify to a plurality of gods? This is what is indicated by the emphasis of the subject of the question with "inna" (إن) and the "lam" (اللام), as if the soul cannot accept that they would testify to it after hearing the testimony of Allah, the Exalted.

Then He commanded him to oppose them in testimony by negating from himself the testimony to what they testified to, saying: "Say, ‘I do not bear witness,’" meaning to what you have testified to, by the context of the situation. Then He said: قُلْ إِنَّما هُوَ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ وَإِنَّنِي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ "Say, ‘He is only the One God, and I am indeed quit of what you ascribe as partners [to Him].’" (6:19). This is a testimony to His oneness, the Exalted, and a disavowal of the partners they claim for Him.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَعْرِفُونَهُ كَما يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْناءَهُمُ "Those to whom We have given the Book recognize him just as they recognize their own sons." (6:20). This is an informing of what Allah, the Glorified, testified to in the Books revealed to the People of the Book, and what the scholars of the People of the Book knew from the books of the prophets they possessed, consisting of glad tidings after glad tidings regarding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), describing him with descriptions that admit no doubt and are not subject to uncertainty.

Thus, by what they held present of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) description, they recognized him personally just as they recognize their own sons. The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَالْإِنْجِيلِ "those who follow the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel" (7:157). And the Exalted said: مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ تَراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلاً مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْواناً سِيماهُمْ فِي وُجُوهِهِمْ مِنْ أَثَرِ السُّجُودِ ذلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِي الْإِنْجِيلِ "Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in worship], seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure. Their mark is on their faces from the trace of prostration. That is their description in the Torah and their description in the Evangel" (48:29). And the Exalted said: أَوَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ آيَةً أَنْ يَعْلَمَهُ عُلَماءُ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ "Is it not a sign for them that the learned of the Children of Israel know him?" (26:197).

Since some of their scholars were concealing the glad tidings and descriptions of him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that they possessed, and were too proud to believe in him, Allah, the Exalted, clarified their loss in their affair, saying: الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ "Those who have ruined their souls will not have faith." (6:20).

Some discussion has already passed in the commentary (تفسير) of the counterpart of this verse in Surah al-Baqarah (verse 146), and we explained there the reason for the transition (الالتفات) from presence to absence. The complete discussion will come in Surah al-A'raf (verse 156), Allah willing.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babawayh with his chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Isa ibn Ubayd, who said: Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) said to me: "What do you say if it is said to you: 'Inform me about Allah, the Mighty and Majestic—is He a thing (شيء) or no-thing?'" He said: I replied: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has established Himself as a thing, where He says: قُلْ أَيُّ شَيْءٍ أَكْبَرُ شَهادَةً قُلِ اللهُ شَهِيدٌ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ (Say, ‘What thing is greatest as witness?’ Say, ‘Allah is witness between me and you.’). And I say: He is a thing unlike other things, for in the negation of 'thingness' from Him is His negation and nullification." He said to me: "You have spoken the truth and done well."

Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: "People have three doctrines regarding monotheism (التوحيد): negation (نفي), anthropomorphism (تشبيه), and affirmation without anthropomorphism (إثبات بغير تشبيه). The doctrine of negation is not permissible, and the doctrine of anthropomorphism is not permissible, because nothing resembles Allah, the Blessed and Exalted. The path is the third way: affirmation without anthropomorphism."

I say: What is meant by the doctrine of negation (مذهب النفي) is the negation of the meanings of the attributes from Him, the Exalted, as the Mu'tazilites held. In the same vein is the reduction of the positive attributes to the negation of their opposites, such as saying that the meaning of "Powerful" is that He is not "incapable," and the meaning of "Knowing" is that He is not "ignorant," unless this is returned to what he (peace be upon him) mentioned as the third doctrine.

What is meant by the doctrine of anthropomorphism (مذهب التشبيه) is to liken Him, the Exalted, to others—while "nothing is like Him"—meaning to establish for Him an attribute with its limited meaning that exists within us, which is distinct from other attributes, such that His power would be like our power and His knowledge like our knowledge, and so on. If His attribute were like our attribute, He would be in need as we are in need, and thus He would not be the Necessary Being (الواجب); the Exalted is far above that.

What is meant by the doctrine of affirmation without anthropomorphism (مذهب الإثبات من غير تشبيه) is to establish for Him the essential meaning of the attribute while negating from Him the specificity that accompanies it in contingent (الممكنات) created beings; that is, the attribute is affirmed and the limit (الحد) is negated.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): قُلْ أَيُّ شَيْءٍ أَكْبَرُ شَهادَةً قُلِ اللهُ شَهِيدٌ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ "That was because the polytheists of the people of Mecca said: 'O Muhammad, did Allah find no messenger to send other than you? We do not see anyone who believes you in what you say'—that was at the beginning of his call to them while they were in Mecca at that time—they said: 'We have asked the Jews and Christians about you, and they claimed that there is no mention of you with them. So bring us someone who testifies that you are the Messenger of Allah.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: اللهُ شَهِيدٌ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ (Allah is witness between me and you)."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Bukayr from Muhammad from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ (that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach), he said: "Ali (peace be upon him) is among those whom it reached."

I say: Its apparent meaning is that "whomever it may reach" (مَنْ بَلَغَ) is coordinated with the pronoun in "between you" (بَيْنَكُمْ). It has been mentioned in some narrations that what is meant by "whomever it may reach" is the Imam, which would necessitate coordinating "whomever it may reach" with the implicit subject of "that I may warn you." However, the apparent meaning of the verse is the former.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babawayh with his chain of narration from Yahya ibn Imran al-Halabi from his father from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: He was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ (and this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach). He said: "In every tongue."

I say: The basis for deriving this from the verse has already passed.

In Tafsir al-Manar: Abu al-Shaykh extracted from Ubayy ibn Ka'b who said: "Prisoners were brought to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and he said to them: 'Were you called to Islam?' They said: 'No.' So he let them go, then he recited: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ (and this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach). Then he said: 'Let them go until they reach their place of safety—because they had not been called.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Umar ibn al-Khattab said to Abdullah ibn Salam: "Do you recognize Muhammad in your Book?" He said: "Yes, by Allah, we recognize him—by the description with which Allah described him to us when we saw him among you—just as one of us recognizes his son when he sees him with the boys."

And that by which Ibn Salam swears: "I am more certain in my recognition of this Muhammad than I am of my own son."

Ayahs

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢١)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies His signs? Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous. (21)
وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآؤُكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ (٢٢)
On the day when We gather them all together, We shall say to those who ascribed partners [to Allah] ‘Where are your partners that you used to claim?’ (22)
ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُن فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ وَٱللَّهِ رَبِّنَا مَا كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ (٢٣)
Then their only excuse will be to say, ‘By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.’ (23)
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢٤)
Look, how they forswear themselves, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them! (24)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَجَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا ۚ وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كُلَّ ءَايَةٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُوكَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَكَ يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٢٥)
There are some of them who prick up their ears at you, but We have cast veils on their hearts lest they should understand it, and a deafness into their ears; and though they should see every sign, they will not believe in it. When they come to you, to dispute with you, the faithless say, ‘These are nothing but myths of the ancients.’ (25)
وَهُمْ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ وَيَنْـَٔوْنَ عَنْهُ ۖ وَإِن يُهْلِكُونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٢٦)
They dissuade [others] from [following] him, and [themselves] avoid him; yet they destroy no one except themselves, but they are not aware. (26)
وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ وُقِفُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ فَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰلَيْتَنَا نُرَدُّ وَلَا نُكَذِّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّنَا وَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢٧)
Were you to see when they are brought to a halt by the Fire, whereupon they will say, ‘If only we were sent back [into the world]! Then we will not deny the signs of our Lord, and we will be among the faithful!’ (27)
بَلْ بَدَا لَهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُخْفُونَ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلَوْ رُدُّوا۟ لَعَادُوا۟ لِمَا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (٢٨)
Indeed, what they used to hide before has now become evident to them. But were they to be sent back they would revert to what they were forbidden, and they are indeed liars. (28)
وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ (٢٩)
They say, ‘There is nothing but our life of this world, and we shall not be resurrected.’ (29)
وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ وُقِفُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ قَالَ أَلَيْسَ هَـٰذَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا ۚ قَالَ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٣٠)
Were you to see when they are brought to a halt before their Lord. He will say, ‘Is this not a fact?’ They will say, ‘Yes, by our Lord!’ He will say, ‘So taste the punishment because of what you used to deny.’ (30)
قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً قَالُوا۟ يَـٰحَسْرَتَنَا عَلَىٰ مَا فَرَّطْنَا فِيهَا وَهُمْ يَحْمِلُونَ أَوْزَارَهُمْ عَلَىٰ ظُهُورِهِمْ ۚ أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَزِرُونَ (٣١)
They are certainly losers who deny the encounter with Allah. When the Hour overtakes them suddenly, they will say, ‘Alas for us, for what we neglected in it!’ And they will bear their burdens on their backs. Look! Evil is what they bear! (31)
وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ ۖ وَلَلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٣٢)
The life of the world is nothing but play and diversion, and the abode of the Hereafter is surely better for those who are Godwary. Do you not exercise your reason? (32)

(Commentary)

The verses return to the original context, which is the Divine Presence (الحضور), turning the discourse toward the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They recount the injustices (مظالم) of the polytheists regarding the foundations of pure creed—namely, monotheism (التوحيد), belief in prophethood (النبوة), and the Return (المعاد). This is found in His saying, the Exalted: "Who is a greater wrongdoer..." [6:21], and His saying: "There are some of them who listen to you..." [6:25], and His saying: "They say, ‘There is nothing but our life of this world...’" [6:29].

Then, the verses clarify that this conduct constitutes the most severe wrongdoing (الظلم) and the destruction and loss of their own souls. They explain how these injustices will recoil upon them and confront them on the Day of Resurrection, where they will lie against themselves by denying what they said in the world, wishing to return to the world to perform righteous deeds (الصالحات), and expressing deep regret (التحسر) over what they neglected regarding the side of Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِآياتِهِ — "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies His signs?" (6:21). Wrongdoing (الظلم) is among the most heinous of sins; indeed, a precise analysis dictates that all other sins are only heinous and blameworthy to the extent that they contain the meaning of wrongdoing, which is deviation (الانحراف) and departing from the balanced center of justice (العدل).

Just as wrongdoing increases or decreases in magnitude based on the specific characteristics of the one from whom it originates, its status also varies in greatness or smallness based on the one against whom the wrongdoing is committed or intended. Thus, the more exalted the position and the greater the status of the victim, the larger and more immense the wrongdoing becomes. There is no status more cherished nor any presence more noble than that of Allah, the Glorified, nor His signs (آياته) which point toward Him. Therefore, none is a greater wrongdoer than one who wrongs this sanctified Presence or that which is attributed to It in any way—though he only wrongs himself.

Allah, the Glorified, has confirmed this rational perspective with His saying: "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies His signs?" As for fabricating a lie against Him, the Exalted, it includes affirming a partner for Him—while He has no partner—or claiming prophethood, or attributing a ruling to Him through falsehood and innovation (ابتداع). As for denying His signs that point toward Him, it includes denying a truthful Prophet in his claim that is accompanied by divine signs, or rejecting the true religion (الدين الحق), which includes the fundamental denial of the Creator.

The verse applies to the polytheists (المشركين), the idolaters toward whom the argument is directed, because they affirmed partners for Allah, the Glorified, under the pretext that they are intercessors and sources of cosmic affairs, and that the management of the world's affairs ends with them independently. It also applies because they denied His signs, the Exalted, which point toward prophethood and the Return.

Some have perhaps extended this to those who believe in the permissibility of the intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the pure ones from his progeny, or the noble saints (الأولياء) of his community. They judged that seeking intercession through them for any worldly or otherworldly need is a form of polytheism (شرك) covered by this verse and similar noble verses.

It is as if it was hidden from them that He, the Exalted, affirmed intercession in His speech when it is accompanied by His permission, without restricting it to this world or the hereafter. The Mightiest of Speakers said: مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ — "Who is it that may intercede with Him except with His permission?" (2:255).

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, said: وَلا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ — "Those whom they invoke besides Him do not possess [the power of] intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know [it]." (43:86). Thus, He affirmed intercession as a right for the scholars who bear witness to the truth. The most certain instance of these are the Prophets, including the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Allah, the Glorified, affirmed his witness-bearing with His saying: وَجِئْنا بِكَ عَلى هؤُلاءِ شَهِيداً — "and We bring you as a witness against them?" (4:41). He explicitly mentioned his knowledge where He said: وَنَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ تِبْياناً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "and We have sent down the Book to you as an exposition of all things" (16:89), and said: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — "The Trustworthy Spirit brought it down to your heart" (24:193-194). Is it conceivable that the Book, which is an exposition of all things, would descend upon a heart without knowledge of it? Or that He, the Exalted, would send him as a witness when he is not a witness to the truth? Allah, the Exalted, also said: لِتَكُونُوا شُهَداءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ — "that you may be witnesses to mankind" (2:143), and said: وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنْكُمْ شُهَداءَ — "and that He may take witnesses from among you" (3:140). He, the Exalted, also said: وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثالُ نَضْرِبُها لِلنَّاسِ وَما يَعْقِلُها إِلَّا الْعالِمُونَ — "We draw these parables for mankind, but no one grasps them except the learned." (29:43). Thus, He affirmed that in this community there are witnesses who are scholars, and He only affirms the truth.

Regarding the People of his House (عليه‌السلام), He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ اللهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنْكُمُ الرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيراً — "Indeed Allah desires to repel all impurity from you, O People of the Household, and purify you with a thorough purification." (33:33). He clarified that they are purified through His purification. Then He said: إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ ، فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ ، لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ — "This is indeed a noble Quran, in a guarded Book—no one touches it except the pure ones." (56:77-79). Thus, He counted them as the scholars of the Quran, which is an exposition of all things. The pure ones (المطهرون) are the most certain instance of this community in bearing witness to the truth, a state to which neither vain talk nor sinfulness can find a path. We have discussed the meaning of intercession extensively in the first volume of this book, so let it be referred to.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ — "Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous." (6:21). Felicity (الفلاح), success (الفوز), prosperity (النجاح), triumph (الظفر), and happiness (السعادة) are terms close in meaning. For this reason, al-Raghib interpreted falah as the attainment of one's desire, which is approximately the meaning of happiness. He said in al-Mufradat: "Falh (الفلح) means splitting; it is said 'iron splits (yaflahu) iron,' meaning it cleaves it. The farmer is called fallah for this reason. Falah is triumph and the attainment of one's desire, and it is of two types: worldly and otherworldly.

The worldly type is the attainment of the felicities that make the life of this world pleasant, which are survival, wealth, and honor. This is what the poet intended by saying:

Prosper (aflah) with whatever you wish, for it may be attained through weakness, and the clever one may be deceived.

The otherworldly felicity consists of four things: survival without annihilation, wealth without poverty, honor without humiliation, and knowledge without ignorance." (End of quote). Thus, it is possible to say that falah is happiness (السعادة), named so because it involves triumph and attaining one's desire by splitting (شق) the barriers that stand between the seeker and the goal.

This is a comprehensive meaning that applies to various instances of usage, such as His saying: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ — "Certainly, the faithful have attained felicity" (23:1), and His saying: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها — "one who purifies it is certainly felicitous" (91:9), and His saying: إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الْكافِرُونَ — "Indeed the faithless will not be felicitous" (23:117), among other instances.

Thus, His saying: "Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous"—where wrongdoing is taken as a descriptive attribute—means that the wrongdoers will not attain the desire they clung to by the means they employed. For wrongdoing does not guide the wrongdoer to the happiness and triumph he seeks through his wrongdoing.

This is because happiness can only be happiness if it is a desire and a goal according to the reality of things and external existence. In such a case, the entity seeking this desire and happiness must, by its very nature, be equipped with the causes and tools appropriate for that sought happiness. For example, an individual whose sought happiness is to survive by replacing what has decomposed from his structure is equipped with a precise digestive system suitable for that, along with the appropriate tools and causes. Furthermore, there exists in external matter that which agrees with the temperament of his structure; he takes it using the prepared causes and tools, then refines it and changes its form into what resembles the decomposed parts of his body, then attaches it to his body, so the body returns to completeness after its deficiency. This is a general law applicable to all external species perceived by our senses and encompassed by our induction, without any deviation or variation whatsoever.

The system of the universe proceeds upon this course in its movement: for every sought end and intended happiness, there is a specific path that can only be traversed through it. If one were to reach it through something other than its cause, which the system is accustomed to, it would necessitate the dysfunction of the cause and the nullification of the path. In its dysfunction and nullification lies the corruption of everything connected to and dependent upon it of causes and relations. For instance, if an individual were supposed to achieve the preservation of existence without the path of consuming, swallowing, and digesting, it would lead to the dysfunction of his nutritive power, and in its dysfunction, the deviation of both his growth and reproductive powers, for example.

Divine Providence has necessitated that these species, which live by consciousness and will, live by applying their actions to the knowledge they have acquired of the external world. If they were to deviate from the external reality due to some interference, it would result in the nullification of the action; and if this were repeated, it would result in the nullification of the essence—like a person intending to eat who errs and thinks poison is food or clay is bread, and the like.

Man possesses general creeds and views generated from the external system of the universe, which he establishes as a foundation and applies his actions to, such as creeds related to the Origin and the Return, and practical rulings he sets as measures for his actions in worship and transactions.

These are paths to human felicities according to their nature; there is no path to them without these. If a person traverses them, he attains his desire and triumphs in his happiness. But if he deviates from them to something else—which is wrongdoing (الظلم)—it will not lead him to his desire. Even if it were to lead him to it, it would not remain stable for him, nor would it last. This is because all other paths and ways are linked to it, so they contend with him for it, opposing and contradicting him with all their capacity and power. Furthermore, the parts of the external universe, which are the cause for the emergence of these views and rulings, do not harmonize with his action, and they continue in this state until they overturn the matter for him, corrupting his happiness and embittering his life.

Thus, a wrongdoer may be driven by the tyranny of greed to use his sinful might and false power to obtain a desire and happiness through an illegitimate path. He may oppose the true creed of the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — of Allah, the Glorified, or contest legitimate rights, encroaching upon people's wealth by seizing it unjustly, or upon people's honor by violating their sanctity by force, or upon their blood and lives by disposing of them without right. Or he may disobey any of the laws of servitude to Allah, the Glorified, regarding prayer, fasting, pilgrimage, or others, or commit any of the sins related to that, such as lying, fabrication, and deception.

He may perform some of this and perhaps attain what he intended, feeling pleased with what he has triumphed in according to his claim, while he has actually lost his bargain and failed in his endeavor in both his world and his hereafter.

As for his world, it is because the path he traversed is only the path of chaos, disorder, and the breakdown of the system. For if it were a true path, it would be universal; and if it were universal, it would nullify the system. If the system were nullified, the life of human society would be nullified. Thus, the system that guarantees the survival of the human species, whatever it may be, contends with him for what he acquired through his illegitimate action, and it continues this contention until it corrupts the implication of his action and the result of his ill-fated endeavor, whether sooner or later. His wrongdoing shall never endure.

As for the hereafter, it is because his wrongdoing is written in the record of his deeds and engraved upon the tablet of his soul by the effect it brings upon it. Then he is recompensed for it, living according to its pattern: "Whether you disclose what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it."

Allah, the Exalted, said: أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ الْكِتابِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ فَما جَزاءُ مَنْ يَفْعَلُ ذلِكَ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا خِزْيٌ فِي الْحياةِ الدُّنْيا وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يُرَدُّونَ إِلى أَشَدِّ الْعَذابِ وَمَا اللهُ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ — "What! Do you believe in part of the Book and defy another part? So what is the requital of those of you who do that except disgrace in the life of this world? And on the Day of Resurrection, they shall be delivered to the severest punishment. And Allah is not oblivious of what you do." (2:85). And He said: كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتاهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَشْعُرُونَ ، فَأَذاقَهُمُ اللهُ الْخِزْيَ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَلَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ — "Those who were before them denied; so the punishment came on them from whence they were not aware. So Allah made them taste disgrace in the life of this world, and the punishment of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known." (39:25-26). And He said: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُجادِلُ فِي اللهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَلا هُدىً وَلا كِتابٍ مُنِيرٍ ، ثانِيَ عِطْفِهِ لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُ فِي الدُّنْيا خِزْيٌ وَنُذِيقُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ ، ذلِكَ بِما قَدَّمَتْ يَداكَ وَأَنَّ اللهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ — "Among the people is he who disputes about Allah without any knowledge or guidance, or an illuminating Book, turning his side to lead [people] astray from the way of Allah. For him there is disgrace in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall make him taste the punishment of the Burning. ‘That is for what your hands have sent ahead, and because Allah is not unjust to [His] servants.’" (22:8-10), among many other verses.

The verses—as you see—encompass social and individual injustices, confirming the preceding discussion. The most comprehensive of them in content is the verse under discussion: "Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous."

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً — "On the day We shall muster them all together..." (6:22) until the end of the two verses. The adverbial phrase is connected to an omitted verb, the estimation being: "And remember the day..." etc. The emphasis in the speech is placed on the word "all together" (جَمِيعاً) to indicate that knowledge and power do not fail to reach any one of them. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, encompasses all of them in knowledge and power; He will count them and muster them, leaving not one of them behind.

The sentence is in the position of explaining His saying: "Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous." It is as if when it was said: "Indeed the wrongdoers will not be felicitous," it was asked: "How is that?" So it was said: "Because Allah will muster them and ask them about their partners, so they will lose them and miss them, then they will deny their polytheism and swear by Allah to that effect falsely. Had these wrongdoers been felicitous in their taking of partners for Allah, their partners would not have been lost to them, nor would they have lied against themselves; rather, they would have found them as they claimed of partnership and intercession and would have attained their intercession."

Regarding His saying: ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ — "Then their plea will be nothing..." (6:23) etc., it is said: the meaning of fitnah (فتنة) is the answer, meaning their answer was nothing but that they swore by Allah that they were not polytheists. It is also said: the speech assumes an omitted word, meaning: the consequence of their being tested (افتتانهم) by idols was nothing but that they said... etc. It is also said: the meaning of fitnah is the excuse. Each of these views has its merit.

His saying, the Exalted: انْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ — "Look how they lie against themselves, and that which they used to fabricate has forsaken them!" (6:24). This is an exposition of the point of evidence in what was recounted of their state on the Day of Resurrection. The meaning is that they will lie against themselves and lose what they fabricated. Had they been felicitous in their wrongdoing and happy in what they sought, their affair would not have led to the loss of that and their denial of it against themselves.

As for their lying against themselves, it is because when they swore by Allah that they were not polytheists, they denied what they claimed in the world—that Allah, the Glorified, has partners. They used to insist upon this and turn away from every clear proof and manifest sign out of wrongdoing and rebellion. This is a lie from them against themselves.

As for the forsaking (ضلال) of what they used to fabricate, it is because that Day is a day in which it becomes manifest to the sight that command, sovereignty, and power belong entirely to Allah; nothing belongs to anyone else except the humiliation of servitude, poverty, and need, without any independence whatsoever. The Exalted said: وَلَوْ يَرَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِذْ يَرَوْنَ الْعَذابَ أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً وَأَنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعَذابِ — "That if the wrongdoers were to see—when they face the punishment—that power belongs entirely to Allah, and that Allah is severe in punishment" (2:165). And He said: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ — "‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’" (40:16). And He said: يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً ، وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ — "The day when no soul will be of any avail to another soul and all command that day will belong to Allah." (82:19).

At that time, they will witness with their own eyes that divinity belongs to Allah alone, without partner. Their idols and partners will appear to them, possessing neither harm nor benefit for themselves or others. They will find that the attributes they affirmed for them—of lordship, intercession, and others—belong to Allah alone. The matter had been confused for them, so they imagined them for others, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them.

If they seek help from them, they will be met with a rejection that leaves no room for hope thereafter. The Exalted said: وَإِذا رَأَى الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا شُرَكاءَهُمْ قالُوا رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ شُرَكاؤُنَا الَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِكَ فَأَلْقَوْا إِلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ ، وَأَلْقَوْا إِلَى اللهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ السَّلَمَ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ — "When the polytheists see their partners, they will say, ‘Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You.’ But they will retort at them, ‘You are indeed liars!’ And they will offer submission to Allah on that day, and that which they used to fabricate will forsake them." (16:86-87). And He, the Exalted, said: ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَالَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ ما يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْ قِطْمِيرٍ ، إِنْ تَدْعُوهُمْ لا يَسْمَعُوا دُعاءَكُمْ وَلَوْ سَمِعُوا مَا اسْتَجابُوا لَكُمْ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ — "That is Allah, your Lord; to Him belongs the kingdom; and those whom you invoke besides Him do not own [even] a date-stone’s skin. If you invoke them, they will not hear your invocation, and even if they heard, they would not respond to you. On the Day of Resurrection they will deny your polytheism." (41:13-14). And He, the Exalted, said: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَشُرَكاؤُكُمْ فَزَيَّلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ وَقالَ شُرَكاؤُهُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ إِيَّانا تَعْبُدُونَ ، فَكَفى بِاللهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ إِنْ كُنَّا عَنْ عِبادَتِكُمْ لَغافِلِينَ ، هُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ — "On the day We shall muster them all together, then We shall say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’ Then We shall set them apart from one another, and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you used to worship. Allah suffices as a witness between us and you: we were indeed unaware of your worship.’ There every soul will examine what it has sent ahead, and they will be returned to Allah, their real master, and that which they used to fabricate will forsake them." (10:28-30).

By contemplating these verses, it becomes clear that the meaning of the forsaking (ضلال) of what they fabricated is the appearance of the reality of their partners as devoid of the attribute of partnership and intercession, and their realization that what appeared to them of that in the world was nothing but a mirage-like appearance, as the Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَعْمالُهُمْ كَسَرابٍ بِقِيعَةٍ يَحْسَبُهُ الظَّمْآنُ ماءً حَتَّى إِذا جاءَهُ لَمْ يَجِدْهُ شَيْئاً وَوَجَدَ اللهَ عِنْدَهُ فَوَفَّاهُ حِسابَهُ — "As for the faithless, their works are like a mirage in a plain, which the thirsty man supposes to be water. When he comes to it, he finds it to be nothing; but there he finds Allah, who pays him his full account." (24:39).

If you say: The verses dealing with the description of the Day of Resurrection—as previously mentioned—are manifest in the emergence of realities and their departure from the place of concealment and ambiguity, which is among the necessities of the first worldly life, as the Exalted said: يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ — "The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah." (40:16). What benefit, then, is there in their lying? And how can they lie when the contrary of the lie they report is witnessed by the eyes? For He, the Exalted, said: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ — "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done..." (3:30).

I say: Their lying and their swearing upon the lie on the Day of Resurrection is something that has occurred in His speech, the Exalted, more than once. Similar to this verse is His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَحْلِفُونَ لَهُ كَما يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ — "The day Allah will raise them all together, they will swear to Him as they swear to you" (58:18). Their lying and swearing upon it is not to achieve corrupt goals or to hide the truth as is done through lying in the world, for the hereafter is the abode of recompense, not the abode of action and acquisition.

However, because they were accustomed to escaping dangers and perils and attracting benefits to themselves through false oaths and fabricated reports out of deception and delusion, the habit (ملكة) of lying became firmly rooted in their souls. When a habit becomes rooted in the soul, the soul is compelled to respond to what it calls for. This is just as the foul-mouthed person, once the habit of insulting is established in his soul, is unable to refrain from it even if he resolves to do so. Similarly, the arrogant, stubborn, and obstinate person does not possess the power over himself to be humble; even if he humbles himself in a position of peril and humiliation at times, he only humbles himself outwardly and with his tongue. As for his inner self and his heart, he remains in his state, unchanged and never to change.

This is the secret behind their lying on the Day of Resurrection, because it is a day in which the secrets are tested (تبلى فيه السرائر). A secret that is knotted upon lying contains nothing but lying, so what is established within it appears, as the Exalted said: وَلا يَكْتُمُونَ اللهَ حَدِيثاً — "and they will not be able to conceal any matter from Allah." (4:42). A parallel to this is the quarreling that takes place among the people of the world; it appears exactly as it is on the Day of Resurrection among them. Allah, the Glorified, has recounted this in many places of His speech, and summarized it in His saying: إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَحَقٌّ تَخاصُمُ أَهْلِ النَّارِ — "That is indeed true: the jangling of the inmates of the Fire." (38:64). This is regarding the people of punishment. As for the people of forgiveness and Paradise, there appears from them there what was in their souls here of purity and peace. The Exalted said: لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً وَلا تَأْثِيماً ، إِلَّا قِيلاً سَلاماً سَلاماً — "They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech, but only the word, ‘Peace! Peace!’" (56:25-26). So understand that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ — "There are some of them who listen to you..." (6:25) until the end of the verse. Akinna (الأكنة) is the plural of kinn (كن), which is the covering in which a thing is kept and covered. Waqr (الوقر) is heaviness in hearing. Asatir (الأساطير) is the plural of usturah (أسطورة), meaning lies and falsehood, according to what is reported from al-Mubarrad. Its origin was satr (السطر), which is a line of writing, trees, or people; its usage became dominant for what is collected, organized, and arranged of false reports.

The apparent context would have suggested saying: "They say, 'This is nothing but myths of the ancients.'" Perhaps the use of the explicit noun [the faithless] was to indicate the cause of this accusation, which is disbelief (الكفر).

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُمْ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ وَيَنْأَوْنَ عَنْهُ وَإِنْ يُهْلِكُونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ — "They forbid [others] from it and keep away from it [themselves]; and they do not destroy any but themselves, and they are not aware." (6:26). "They forbid from it," meaning from following it. N'ay (النأي) is to keep a distance. The restriction in His saying: "and they do not destroy any but themselves" is a restriction of reversal (قصر القلب), for they used to think that forbidding from it and keeping away from it was a destruction of it and a nullification of the divine call. But Allah refuses except to complete His light; thus, they are the ones who are destroyed from where they do not perceive.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ تَرى إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلَى النَّارِ — "Were you to see when they are made to stand by the Fire..." (6:27) until the end of the two verses. This is an exposition of the consequence of their rejection and their insistence upon disbelief and turning away from the signs of Allah, the Exalted.

Regarding His saying: يا لَيْتَنا نُرَدُّ وَلا نُكَذِّبَ بِآياتِ رَبِّنا — "‘Would that we were sent back, and [then] we would not deny the signs of our Lord...’" (6:27) etc., according to the reading of the subjunctive (نُكَذِّبَ and نَكُونَ), it is a wish from them to return to the world and join the ranks of the believers so that they may be saved from the punishment of the Fire on the Day of Resurrection. This saying of theirs is similar to their denial of polytheism and their swearing by Allah to that effect falsely, as part of the emergence of their psychological habits on the Day of Resurrection. For they were accustomed to wishing for the goods and benefits that have passed them by and which they have no way of acquiring, especially if their loss was based on their poor choice and deficient management in action. A parallel to this is also what will come regarding their regret over what they neglected in the matter of the Hour.

Furthermore, wishing (التمني) is valid for impossible things that are unattainable, just as it is valid for possible things that are difficult, such as wishing for the return of past days and the like. The poet said:

Would that (layta)—and does "would that" benefit anything? Would that youth were sold, so I could buy it.

Regarding His saying: بَلْ بَدا لَهُمْ ما كانُوا يُخْفُونَ مِنْ قَبْلُ — "Rather that which they used to conceal before has become manifest to them."
