(6:28) etc., the apparent meaning of the speech is that the referent of the pronouns—namely the pronouns in "to them" (لَهُمْ), "they used to" (كانُوا), and "they conceal" (يُخْفُونَ)—is one, which is the polytheists mentioned previously. The "before" (القبل) refers to the world. Thus, the meaning is that there appeared to these polytheists, when they were made to stand by the Fire, what they themselves used to conceal in the world. The appearance of that prompted them to wish for a return to the world, belief in the signs of Allah, and entry into the community of believers.

Nothing appeared to them except the Fire by which they were made to stand on the Day of Resurrection; they had concealed it in the world through disbelief, hiding the truth, and covering it after it had appeared to them, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — "‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have suspended your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today.’" (50:22).

As for the truth itself which they disbelieved in the world despite its appearance to them, it was already manifest to them before. The context refuses that the mere appearance of the truth to them, while ignoring the appearance of the Fire and the horror of the Day of Resurrection, would be the cause for this wish.

This is suggested by some of what is found in similar contexts of His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: وَإِذا قِيلَ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ وَالسَّاعَةُ لا رَيْبَ فِيها قُلْتُمْ ما نَدْرِي مَا السَّاعَةُ إِنْ نَظُنُّ إِلَّا ظَنًّا وَما نَحْنُ بِمُسْتَيْقِنِينَ ، وَبَدا لَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — "When it was said, ‘Allah’s promise is indeed true, and there is no doubt about the Hour,’ you said, ‘We do not know what the Hour is. We only think it to be a conjecture, and we are not certain.’ The evils of what they had done will appear to them, and they will be besieged by what they used to mock." (45:32-33). And His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً وَمِثْلَهُ مَعَهُ لَافْتَدَوْا بِهِ مِنْ سُوءِ الْعَذابِ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَبَدا لَهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ ما لَمْ يَكُونُوا يَحْتَسِبُونَ ، وَبَدا لَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما كَسَبُوا وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — "Even if the wrongdoers possessed all that is on the earth, and as much of it besides, they would surely offer it to redeem themselves from the terrible punishment on the Day of Resurrection. There will appear to them from Allah what they had never reckoned. The evils of what they had earned will appear to them, and they will be besieged by what they used to mock." (39:47-48).

Regarding the verse: "Rather that which they used to conceal before has become manifest to them," many views have been mentioned, which al-Manar brought to nine. It said: (And in it are opinions: First, that it is their evil deeds and shameful abominations that appeared to them in their records, and their limbs bore witness against them.

Second: that it is their deeds through which they used to fabricate and think their happiness lay therein, as Allah, the Exalted, makes them scattered dust.

Third: that it is their disbelief and denial which they concealed in the hereafter before they were made to stand by the Fire, as previously recounted from them in His saying, the Exalted: "Then their plea will be nothing but that they say, ‘By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.’"

Fourth: that it is the truth or the faith which they used to keep secret and conceal by manifesting disbelief and denial out of obstinacy toward the Messenger and arrogance against the truth. This only applies to the most severe of the faithless among the obstinate and arrogant, about some of whom He said: "And they denied them—though they were convinced of them in their hearts—out of wrongdoing and haughtiness."

Fifth: that it is what the leaders used to conceal from their followers of the truth brought by the Messengers; it appeared to the followers who used to imitate them. This includes the concealment by some of the People of the Book of the message of our Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), his attributes, and the glad tidings of their Prophets regarding him.

Sixth: that it is what the hypocrites used to conceal in the world of the secrets of disbelief while manifesting faith and Islam.

Seventh: that it is the Resurrection and the Recompense, including the punishment of Hell, and that their concealing it is an expression of their denial of it, which is the original meaning of the root of kufr (disbelief).

Eighth: that there is an omitted word in the speech, meaning: the consequence of what they used to conceal of disbelief and evil deeds appeared to them, and its punishment descended upon them, so they became weary and distressed and wished to escape it by returning to the world and leaving the denial of the signs and lack of faith that led to it—just as one who is pained by an incurable disease wishes for death because it saves him from pains, not because it is beloved in itself. We do not see the preponderance of any of these opinions; rather, the correct view in our opinion is another, which is:

Ninth: that there appears on that day to each of those about whom the speech was revealed and their likes among the faithless what they used to conceal in the world of what is shameful in their view or the view of those from whom they concealed it.) End of quote. Then he generalized the speech to the leaders of the faithless and their imitating followers, and to the hypocrites and the transgressors among those who commit indecencies and hide them from people or leave obligations and offer false excuses while hiding the reality of the situation, in a long discourse.

By returning to the view we presented and contemplating it, the flaws in each of these opinions become apparent, so we shall not prolong the discussion.

Regarding His saying: وَلَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ — "and were they sent back, they would surely go back to what they were forbidden" (6:28). This is a reminder of the action of what has been established in their souls of vile habits in the worldly life. For that which prompted them to wish for a return to the world and to believe therein in the signs of Allah and enter the community of believers is only the appearance of the neglected truth with all its consequences of punishment on the Day of Resurrection. This is among the requirements of the otherworldly life, which necessitates the appearance of the unseen realities as a manifest witnessing.

If they were to return to the world, the law of that life would apply to them, the veils of the unseen would be drawn over them, and they would return to their choice, along with the desire of the soul, the whispering of Satan, the natural dispositions of the servants, arrogance, and rebellion. Thus, they would return to their previous polytheism and obstinacy against the truth. For that which called them while they were in the world to oppose the truth and deny the signs of Allah, the Exalted, remains as it is, assuming their return to the world after the Resurrection. Thus, its ruling is its ruling, without difference.

Regarding His saying: وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ — "and they are indeed liars" (6:28). Meaning in their saying: "Would that we were sent back, and [then] we would not deny the signs of our Lord..." etc. Although a wish (التمني) is a performative utterance (إنشاء) in which truth and falsehood do not occur, when they said: "we were sent back, and [then] we would not deny," meaning "Allah sent us back to the world, and if He sent us back we would not deny"—and they did not say "we return and we do not deny"—their speech was implicit of a request and a promise. Namely, the request for return and the promise of faith and righteous deeds, as explicitly stated in His saying: وَلَوْ تَرى إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ — "Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [saying], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard; so send us back [that] we may act righteously. Indeed we are [now] convinced.’" (32:12). And His saying: وَهُمْ يَصْطَرِخُونَ فِيها رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً غَيْرَ الَّذِي كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ — "They will cry out therein, ‘Our Lord! Bring us out, so that we may act righteously—different from what we used to do!’" (35:37).

In short, their saying: "Would that we were sent back, and [then] we would not deny..." etc., is in the meaning of their saying: "Our Lord, return us to the world, we will not deny Your signs and we will be among the believers." In this respect, it admits of truth and falsehood, and it is correct to count them as liars.

Perhaps the attribution of lying to them in their wish is explained by the fact that it means the lying of hope and wishing, which is its non-realization externally, as it is said: "Your hope lied to you," to one who wishes for what he cannot attain.

It is also said: the meaning is their lying in all else they report about themselves regarding hitting the mark and believing the truth. This is as you see.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا — "They say, ‘There is nothing but our life of this world...’" (6:29) until the end of the two verses. This is a mention of their explicit denial of the Gathering (الحشر) and what it entails on the Day of Resurrection of witnessing and taking confession for what they denied. Paganism (الوثنية) used to deny the Return (المعاد), as Allah has recounted from them in His speech more than once. Their belief in the intercession of partners was only in worldly matters, such as attracting benefits to themselves and repelling harms and fears from them.

Thus, His saying: "They say, ‘There is nothing...’" etc., is a recounting of their denial, meaning: there is no life except our life of this world, no life after it, and we are not to be resurrected after death. His saying: "Were you to see when they are made to stand..." is like the answer, and it is an exposition of what their saying "There is nothing but..." etc., entails for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the form of a wish because of His saying: "Were you to see." This is that they will confirm what they rejected and confess to what they denied by their saying: "and we are not to be resurrected," when they are made to stand before their Lord. They will witness with their own eyes this station which they were informed of in the world—namely, that they are resurrected after death—so they will confess to that after they denied it in the world.

From here it becomes clear that Allah, the Glorified, interpreted the Resurrection in His saying: "Were you to see when they are made to stand before their Lord" as the meeting with Allah (لقاء الله). This is also supported by His saying in the following verse: "They are certainly losers who deny the encounter with Allah until the Hour comes upon them..." etc., where He replaced the Gathering, Resurrection, and Rising mentioned in the previous speech with "encounter" (لقاء), then mentioned the Hour, meaning the hour of the encounter.

And His saying: أَلَيْسَ هذا — "‘Is this not...’" meaning: is this Resurrection which you denied in the world, which is the encounter with Allah, "‘...the truth?’ They will say, ‘Yes, by our Lord!’ He will say, ‘Then taste the punishment because of what you used to defy’" and conceal.

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ خَسِرَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِلِقاءِ اللهِ — "They are certainly losers who deny the encounter with Allah..." (6:31) until the end of the verse. It is said in al-Majma': everything that comes suddenly has baghata (بغت); it is said: the matter baghatahu (surprised him) baghtatan (suddenly). End of quote. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: al-Hasr (الحسر) is uncovering the garment from what is upon it; it is said: I uncovered (hasartu) the forearm. The hasir is one who has no armor or helmet. The mihasrah is the broom—until he said—and the hasir is the exhausted one because his powers are uncovered—until he said—and al-Hasrah (الحسرة) is grief over what has passed him and regret over it, as if the ignorance that drove him to what he committed has been uncovered from him, or his powers have been uncovered from excessive grief, or exhaustion has overtaken him from rectifying what he neglected. (End of the relevant part).

He said: al-Wazar (الوزر) (with two fathas) is the refuge one resorts to in a mountain; He said: "No indeed! There is no refuge (wazar). With your Lord is the settling-place on that day." And al-Wizr (الوزر) (with kasra then sukun) is the weight, by way of comparison to the weight of the mountain. It is used to express sin just as it is expressed by weight; He said: "that they may bear their burdens (awzarahum) in full"—the verse—like His saying: "They will surely carry their own loads (athqalahum), and [other] loads along with their own loads." End of quote.

The verse clarifies another consequence of their denial of the Resurrection, which is that the Hour will surprise them, so they will call out in regret (الحسرة) over their neglect regarding it. Their burdens (أوزارهم) and sins will be personified for them, and they will carry them on their backs, which is the most difficult and worst of human states. "Behold, evil is that which they bear (yazirun)" and carry of weight, or of sin, or of the consequence of sin.

The verse—namely His saying: "They are certainly losers who deny the encounter with Allah"—is in the position of a conclusion drawn from His saying: "They say, ‘There is nothing but our life of this world...’" until the end of the two verses. It is that by exchanging the comfort of the hereafter and the spirit of the encounter with Allah for the denial of the Resurrection and its subsequent painful punishment, they have lost the bargain.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ وَلَلدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ — "The life of this world is nothing but play and diversion, and the abode of the Hereafter is surely better..." (6:32) etc. This is a completion of the speech, containing an exposition of the state of the two lives: the world and the hereafter, and a comparison between them. The life of this world is nothing but play (لعب) and diversion (لهو), for it revolves around a series of conventional creeds and illusory goals, just as play revolves around them; thus it is play. Furthermore, it distracts man from what concerns him of the other, real, and permanent life; thus it is diversion. The life of the hereafter, being real and stable, is better; and it is only attained by the God-conscious (المتقون) — those who possess the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — so it is better for them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Hisham ibn Salim from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah will forgive on the Day of Resurrection with a forgiveness that does not occur to anyone's mind—until the people of polytheism say: 'By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.'"

In al-Majma': regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Then their plea will be nothing but that they say..." the verse: that the meaning is: "Their excuse was nothing but that they said..." etc. He said: "And it is narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: "They forbid [others] from it and keep away from it [themselves]..." the verse. He said: "The Banu Hashim used to support the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and prevent Quraysh [from harming him], and they 'keep away' (yan'awn), meaning they distance themselves and do not believe."

I say: The narration is close to what was narrated from Ata and Muqatil: "That the intended person is Abu Talib, the uncle of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), for he used to forbid Quraysh from [harming] the Prophet and keep away from the Prophet and not believe in him."

The context refuses this, for the apparent meaning of the verse is that the pronoun refers to the Quran, not the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Furthermore, the narrations from the paths of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام) are concurrent regarding his faith.

It is said in al-Majma': "The consensus of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام) has been established regarding the faith of Abu Talib, and their consensus is a proof because they are one of the Two Weighty Things (الثقلين) which the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded to hold fast to by his saying: 'That which if you hold fast to both, you shall never go astray.'"

This is also indicated by what was narrated by Ibn Umar: "That Abu Bakr brought his father, Abu Quhafah—on the day of the Conquest—to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He said: 'Why did you not leave the old man so I could go to him?'—and he was blind. Abu Bakr said: 'I wanted Allah, the Exalted, to reward him. By the One who sent you with the truth, I was more joyful at the Islam of Abu Talib than I was at the Islam of my father—seeking thereby the delight of your eye.' He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'You have spoken the truth.'"

Al-Tabari narrated with his chain: "That when the leaders of Quraysh saw Abu Talib's defense of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), they gathered before him and said: 'We have brought you the youth of Quraysh in beauty, generosity, and bravery—Umarah ibn al-Walid. We hand him over to you, and you hand over to us your nephew who has divided our community and mocked our intellects, so we may kill him.' Abu Talib said: 'You have not been fair to me—you give me your son so I may feed him, and I give you my son so you may kill him? Rather, let every man of you bring his son so I may kill him.' And he said:

We defended the Messenger, the Messenger of the King With white [swords] shining like the flash of lightning I repel and protect the Messenger of the King The protection of a compassionate, affectionate protector.

His sayings and poems indicating his Islam are many, famous, and innumerable. Among them is his saying:

Did you not know that we found Muhammad A Prophet like Moses, written in the first of Books? Is not our father Hashim who strengthened his back And enjoined his sons to the spear-thrust and to war?

And his saying from a poem:

And they said to Ahmad: You are a man Of a failing tongue and weak means Behold, Ahmad has come to them With truth, and he did not come to them with lies.

And his saying regarding the account of the Parchment (الصحيفة), which is among the miracles of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله):

In the matter of the Parchment there was a lesson Whenever the absent of the people are informed, they wonder Allah erased from it their disbelief and their disobedience And what they resented from a speaker of truth, an expositor And the son of Abdullah became among us confirmed Despite the wrath of our people, not blamed.

And his saying in a poem urging his brother Hamzah to follow the Prophet and be patient in his obedience:

Patience, O Abu Ya'la, upon the religion of Ahmad And be a manifestor of the religion; you were guided as a patient one It gladdened me when you said: 'I am a believer' So be a helper to the Messenger of Allah for the sake of Allah.

And his saying from a poem:

I remain upon the support of the Prophet Muhammad Fighting for him with spears and battalions.

And his saying urging the Negus (النجاشي) to support the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله):

Know, O King of the Abyssinians, that Muhammad Is a vizier to Moses and the Messiah son of Mary He came with guidance like that which they both brought And each, by the command of Allah, guides and protects And you recite him in your Book With truthful speech, not the speech of a conjecturer So do not set up a peer for Allah and submit (aslimu) For the path of truth is not dark.

And his saying in his will when death approached him:

I enjoin the support of the Prophet, the best of presence Ali my son, and the elder of the people, Abbas And Hamzah the lion, the protector of his reality And Ja'far, that they repel the people from him Be my ransom—my mother and what she bore— In the support of Ahmad, shields against the people.

Examples of these verses, which are found in his famous poems, wills, and sermons, would make the book long." End of quote.

The main reliance of those who say he did not embrace Islam is some narrations reported through the paths of the general public (الجمهور) regarding that. On the other side is the consensus of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام), some narrations from the paths of the general public, and his poems transmitted from him. Every man has what he chooses.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Khalid from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "'and were they sent back, they would surely go back to what they were forbidden'—they are cursed in the origin."

In the same source, narrated from Uthman ibn Isa from some of his companions from him (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah said to the water: 'Be sweet and fresh, I shall create from you My Paradise and the people of My obedience.' And He said to the water: 'Be salty and bitter, I shall create from you My Fire and the people of My disobedience.' So He made the two waters flow upon the clay. Then He took a handful with His hand—and it was the right—and created them as a creation like atoms (الذر). Then He made them bear witness against themselves: 'Am I not your Lord and is not My obedience upon you?' They said: 'Yes.' Then He said to the Fire: 'Be a fire'—and behold, it was a blazing fire. He said to them: 'Fall into it.' Some of them hastened, some of them slowed in their pace, and some of them did not leave their place. When they felt its heat, they returned, and not one of them entered it.

Then He took a handful with His hand and created them as a creation like atoms like those first ones. Then He made them bear witness against themselves like He made the others bear witness. Then He said to them: 'Fall into this fire.' Some of them slowed, some of them hastened, and some of them passed like the blink of an eye, so they all fell into it. He said: 'Come out of it safely,' so they came out and nothing had touched them.

The others said: 'O our Lord, excuse us, we will do as they did.' He said: 'I have excused you.' Some of them hastened in their pace, and some of them did not leave their place—just as they did the first time. That is His saying: 'and were they sent back, they would surely go back to what they were forbidden, and they are indeed liars.'"

I say: This narration and the one before it are among the narrations of the Atom (الذر), and the full discussion of them will come in Surah al-A'raf in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ ، قالُوا بَلى — "When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness about themselves: ‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes...’" (7:172).

The summary of it is that just as the system of reward and punishment in the hereafter has a complete connection to another life before it—which is the life of the world in terms of obedience and disobedience—similarly, obedience and disobedience in the world have a complete connection to another life before it in rank, which is the world of the Atom (عالم الذر).

Thus, the meaning of his saying in the narration: "That is His saying, the Exalted: 'and were they sent back...'" etc., is that the meaning of the verse is: if they were returned from the arenas of the Gathering to the world, they would return to what they were forbidden, and they are indeed liars from the world of the Atom since they lied to Allah therein. This is exactly what is intended by his saying (peace be upon him) in the first narration: "and were they sent back, they would surely go back to what they were forbidden—they are cursed in the origin," meaning in the world of the Atom because of their lying therein.

Accordingly, the two narrations contain a fourth view in interpreting the verse, other than the three views previously mentioned in the commentary.

In al-Majma', narrated from al-A'mash from Abu Salih from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا حَسْرَتَنا عَلى ما فَرَّطْنا فِيها — "‘Alas for us, for what we neglected in it!’" (6:31). He said: "The people of the Fire will see their stations in Paradise, so they will say: 'Alas for us!'" End of quote.

Ayahs

قَدْ نَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيَحْزُنُكَ ٱلَّذِى يَقُولُونَ ۖ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ (٣٣)
We certainly know that what they say grieves you. Yet it is not you that they deny, but it is Allah’s signs that the wrongdoers impugn. (33)
وَلَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَصَبَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا كُذِّبُوا۟ وَأُوذُوا۟ حَتَّىٰٓ أَتَىٰهُمْ نَصْرُنَا ۚ وَلَا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكَ مِن نَّبَإِى۟ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٣٤)
Apostles were certainly denied before you, yet they patiently bore being denied and tormented until Our help came to them. Nothing can change the words of Allah, and there have certainly come to you some of the accounts of the apostles. (34)
وَإِن كَانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْرَاضُهُمْ فَإِنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتَ أَن تَبْتَغِىَ نَفَقًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَوْ سُلَّمًا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتَأْتِيَهُم بِـَٔايَةٍ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَمَعَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۚ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٣٥)
And should their aversion be hard on you, find, if you can, a tunnel into the ground, or a ladder into sky, that you may bring them a sign. Had Allah wished, He would have brought them together on guidance. So do not be one of the ignorant. (35)
۞ إِنَّمَا يَسْتَجِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ ۘ وَٱلْمَوْتَىٰ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ (٣٦)
Only those who listen will respond [to you]. As for the dead, Allah will resurrect them, then they will be brought back to Him. (36)

(Commentary)

This is a consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the lapses of the polytheists (المشركين) in the matter of his mission, and a heartening of his soul with the promise of inevitable victory. It is also an explanation that the religious mission (الدعوة الدينية) is situated within the sphere of human choice (الاختيار الإنساني); thus, whoever wills may believe, and whoever wills may disbelieve. The decisive Divine Power and Will do not intervene in this to the extent of compelling them to accept, for had Allah willed, He would have gathered them all upon guidance (الهدى).

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ نَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُ لَيَحْزُنُكَ الَّذِي يَقُولُونَ — "We certainly know that what they say grieves you" until the end of the verse. The particle قَدْ is a particle of realization (تحقيق) when used with the past tense, while with the present tense, it usually denotes paucity (التقليل), though it is sometimes used for realization as well, which is the intended meaning in this verse. The terms حزنه كذا and أحزنه (it grieved him) carry the same meaning, and the verse has been recited using both forms.

Regarding His saying: فَإِنَّهُمْ لا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ — "for it is not you that they cry lies to," it has been recited with the doubled consonant (التشديد) from the form taf‘īl (meaning to call someone a liar), and with the lightened form (التخفيف) (meaning to find someone to be a liar). It appears that the particle fā’ in His saying فَإِنَّهُمْ is for derivation (التفريع). The meaning is as if to say: We know that their speech grieves you, but it should not grieve you, for their denial does not actually return to you. You only call them toward Us, and your only role is the delivery of the message (الرسالة); rather, by doing so, they wrong Our signs and reject them.

Thus, what is in this verse, along with His saying at the end of the verses: ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ — "then to Him they shall be returned," is similar in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَلا يَحْزُنْكَ كُفْرُهُ إِلَيْنا مَرْجِعُهُمْ فَنُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا إِنَّ اللهَ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ — "As for him who is faithless, let his faithlessness not grieve you. To Us will be their return, and We will inform them of what they have done. Indeed Allah knows best what is in the breasts" (Luqman: 23), and His saying: فَلا يَحْزُنْكَ قَوْلُهمْ إِنَّا نَعْلَمُ ما يُسِرُّونَ وَما يُعْلِنُونَ — "So let not their speech grieve you. Indeed We know what they hide and what they expose" (Ya-Sin: 76), and other verses revealed to console him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This applies to the recitation with the doubled consonant.

As for the recitation with the lightened form, the meaning is: Do not grieve, for they do not prevail over you by proving you a liar in what you call them to, nor do they invalidate your proof with any counter-argument; they only wrong the signs of Allah by rejecting them, and to Him is their return.

His saying: وَلكِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بِآياتِ اللهِ يَجْحَدُونَ — "but the wrongdoers deny the signs of Allah." The apparent context would suggest saying "but they," yet the shift to the explicit noun "the wrongdoers" (الظالمين) is to indicate that their rejection (الجحد) stems from their own wrongdoing (ظلم), not from incapacity or ignorance. It is nothing but insolence, resentment, and rebellion, and Allah will resurrect them, then to Him they shall return.

For this reason, a transition (الالتفات) occurred in the speech from the first person to the third person, as it says: بِآياتِ اللهِ — "the signs of Allah," rather than "Our signs." This is to indicate that their action is a confrontation with the station of Divinity (الألوهية) and an attempt to exalt themselves over it—a station against which nothing can stand.

Other views have been mentioned in the interpretation of this verse:

First: The view of the majority, that the meaning is: They do not deem you a liar in their hearts out of conviction, but they manifest denial with their mouths out of stubbornness.

Second: That they do not cry lies to you, but rather they cry lies to Me, for your denial returns to Me and is not exclusive to you. This view is distinct from the one we previously mentioned, though close to it. Both of these views apply to the recitation with the doubled consonant.

Third: That they do not find you to be a liar. The Arabs say: "We fought them but did not find them cowardly" (ما أجبناهم), meaning we did not find them to be cowards. However, the correct view is what was mentioned first.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فَصَبَرُوا عَلى ما كُذِّبُوا — "Apostles were certainly denied before you, yet they patiently endured being denied" until the end of the verse. This is a guidance for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to the path of the prophets who preceded him, which is the path of patience (الصبر) for the sake of Allah. He, the Exalted, has said: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللهُ فَبِهُداهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ — "They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance" (Al-An'am: 90).

His saying: حَتَّى أَتاهُمْ نَصْرُنا — "until Our help came to them," is an explanation of the beautiful end-result of their patience and an allusion to the Divine promise of victory. In His saying: وَلا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِ اللهِ — "and there is no changer of Allah’s words," there is a reinforcement of the promise alluded to in the preceding speech and an affirmation of its inevitability. It refers to what He mentioned in His saying: كَتَبَ اللهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي — "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles’" (Al-Mujadila: 21), and His saying: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ ، إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they shall indeed be the victorious ones" (Al-Saffat: 171-172).

The occurrence of "changer" (المبدل) in His saying وَلا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِ اللهِ within a negative context negates any supposed changer, whether from His side—the Exalted—by His will changing regarding a specific word such that He erases it after establishing it or annuls it after confirming it, or from the side of someone other than Him—the Exalted—by prevailing over Him and forcing Him contrary to what He willed, thereby changing what He had made firm or altering it in any way.

From this, it becomes clear that these "words" (الكلمات) which He, the Glorified, informed are not subject to change, are matters outside the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment (لوح المحو والإثبات). The "Word of Allah" (كلمة الله) and His "Speech" (قوله), as well as His "Promise" (وعده) in the terminology of the Quran, refer to the Final Decree (القضاء الحتم) in which there is no hope for alteration or change. He, the Exalted, said: قالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ — "He said, ‘This is the truth, and the truth I say’" (Sad: 84), and He, the Exalted, said: وَاللهُ يَقُولُ الْحَقَّ — "and Allah speaks the truth" (Al-Ahzab: 4), and He, the Exalted, said: أَلا إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ — "Look! Allah’s promise is indeed true" (Yunus: 55), and He, the Exalted, said: لا يُخْلِفُ اللهُ الْمِيعادَ — "Allah does not break His promise" (Az-Zumar: 20). A comprehensive discussion on the meaning of the "Words of Allah" and their synonymous terms in the terminology of the Quran has already passed under the commentary of His saying: مِنْهُمْ مَنْ كَلَّمَ اللهُ — "of them were those to whom Allah spoke" (Al-Baqarah: 253).

His saying at the end of the verse: وَلَقَدْ جاءَكَ مِنْ نَبَإِ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — "and there have already come to you some of the accounts of the apostles," is a confirmation and a citation of evidence for His saying: وَلَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ — "Apostles were certainly denied before you..." etc. It can be inferred from this that this Surah was revealed after some of the Meccan Surahs that narrate the stories of the prophets, such as Surah al-Shu'ara, Maryam, and their like. These Surahs were certainly revealed after the likes of Surah al-Alaq and al-Muddaththir. Thus, Surah al-An'am falls into the third layer of Surahs revealed in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة), and Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْراضُهُمْ — "And if their aversion is hard on you" until His saying فَتَأْتِيَهُمْ بِآيَةٍ — "and bring them a sign." Al-Raghib said: النفق (the tunnel) is a through-way or an underground passage. He said: فَإِنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ أَنْ تَبْتَغِيَ نَفَقاً فِي الْأَرْضِ — "then if you can, seek a tunnel into the earth." From this comes the nāfiqā’ of the jerboa (one of its burrow holes). The jerboa is said to nāfaqa or nafaqa (entered its hole). From this also comes hypocrisy (النفاق), which is entering the Sharia through one door and exiting it through another; Allah pointed this out by saying: إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ — "Indeed the hypocrites are the transgressors" (At-Tawbah: 67), meaning those who exit the Sharia. Allah made the hypocrites worse than the disbelievers, saying: إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ فِي الدَّرْكِ الْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ النَّارِ — "Indeed the hypocrites will be in the lowest reach of the Fire" (An-Nisa: 145). The nayfaq of trousers is well-known. (End of quote).

He also said: السلم (the ladder) is that by which one reaches high places, hoping for safety (سلامة). Then it was used as a name for anything by which one reaches something lofty, like a "means" (السبب). The Exalted said: أَمْ لَهُمْ سُلَّمٌ يَسْتَمِعُونَ فِيهِ — "Or do they have a ladder whereby they listen?" (At-Tur: 38), and He said: أَوْ سُلَّماً فِي السَّماءِ — "or a ladder into the sky." A poet said: "Even if he reached the means of the sky with a ladder." (End of quote).

The apodosis (جواب الشرط) of the conditional sentence in the verse is omitted because it is known. The estimation, as has been said, is: "If you are able to seek such-and-less, then do so."

The intended meaning of "sign" (آية) in His saying فَتَأْتِيَهُمْ بِآيَةٍ is a sign that would compel them to believe. The address in His saying: وَإِنْ كانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْراضُهُمْ — "And if their aversion is hard on you" etc., was delivered to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by way of the Quran, which is the greatest Divine sign indicating the truth of his mission. Its miraculous nature (إعجازه) is close to their understanding, as they are eloquent and rational people. The meaning is that their aversion should not be hard or difficult for you, for this world is the abode of choice (الاختيار), and the call to truth and its acceptance proceed according to the course of choice. You are not able to obtain a sign that would necessitate belief for them or compel them to it, for Allah, the Glorified, did not intend for them to believe except by their own choice. Thus, He did not create a sign that forces people into faith and obedience. Had Allah willed, all people would have believed, and these disbelievers would have joined the believers with you. So do not be distressed or grieved by their aversion, lest you be among those ignorant of Divine knowledge.

As for what some have conjectured—that the meaning is: "bring them a sign that is better than the sign with which We sent you, i.e., the Quran"—this does not fit the context of the verse, especially His saying وَلَوْ شاءَ اللهُ لَجَمَعَهُمْ عَلَى الْهُدى — "Had Allah wished, He would have gathered them on guidance," for it clearly points to compulsion (الاضطرار).

From here it becomes clear that the intended "Will" (المشية) is that Allah would will for them to be guided to faith such that they would be compelled to accept, thereby nullifying their choice. This is what the apparent context of the noble verse requires.

However, in similar passages of His speech, He, the Glorified, did not base His lack of such a will solely on the necessity of compulsion. For instance, His saying: وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَآتَيْنا كُلَّ نَفْسٍ هُداها ، وَلكِنْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِنِّي لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ — "Had We wished, We would have given every soul its guidance, but My word has become due: ‘I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together’" (As-Sajdah: 13). He refers there to something like His saying: قالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "He said, ‘This is the truth, and the truth I say: I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together’" (Sad: 84-85). Thus, He explained that the non-realization of His will to guide them all is due to His decree, which He decreed in response to Iblis's oath that he would surely lead them all astray except for His devoted servants among them.

In another place, He attributed the decree to their own deviance (غوايتهم). He said in the story of Adam and Iblis: قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ ، إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ قالَ هذا صِراطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ ، إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ ، وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them all, except Your exclusive servants among them.’ He said, ‘This is a straight path [leading] to Me. Indeed, as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you. Indeed hell is the tryst of them all’" (Al-Hijr: 39-43). Iblis also attributed this to them in what Allah narrated of his speech to them on the Day of Resurrection: وَقالَ الشَّيْطانُ لَمَّا قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ إِنَّ اللهَ وَعدَكُمْ وَعْدَ الْحَقِّ وَوَعَدْتُكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُكُمْ — "When the matter is decided, Satan will say, ‘Indeed Allah made you a true promise. I [too] made you promises, but I failed you...’" until he said: إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ بِما أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِنْ قَبْلُ — "I disavow your having staggered me [with Him] before" (Ibrahim: 22).

These verses explain that sins, including polytheism (الشرك), end in human deviance (غواية), and deviance ends in the human soul itself. This does not contradict what appears in other verses—that man cannot will unless Allah wills the "willing" from him—such as His saying: إِنَّ هذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً ، وَما تَشاؤُنَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ — "This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take the way to his Lord. But you do not wish unless Allah wishes" (Al-Insan: 29-30), and His saying: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ ، لِمَنْ شاءَ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ يَسْتَقِيمَ ، وَما تَشاؤُنَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ — "It is just a reminder for all the nations, for those of you who wish to walk straight. But you do not wish unless it is wished by Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (At-Takwir: 27-29).

Thus, although the realization of man's will depends on the will of Allah, the Glorified, Allah does not will the "willing" from him unless he is prepared for it by the goodness of his inner self (سريرته) and exposes himself to His mercy. He, the Exalted, said: وَيَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنابَ — "and He guides to Himself those who turn [to Him] penitently" (Ar-Ra'd: 27), meaning those who incline and return to Him. As for the transgressor (الفاسق) whose heart has swerved, who clings to the earth and inclines toward deviance, Allah does not will his guidance nor does He cover him with His mercy, as He said: يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ — "He misleads many thereby, and He guides many thereby; and He misleads no one thereby except the transgressors" (Al-Baqarah: 26), and He said: فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ — "So when they swerved, Allah made their hearts swerve" (As-Saff: 5), and He said: وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَرَفَعْناهُ بِها وَلكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ — "Had We wished, We would have surely exalted him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his low desires" (Al-A'raf: 176).

In short, the religious mission only follows the path of choice (الاختيار), and Divine signs are only revealed with due regard for choice. Allah, the Glorified, only guides to Himself those who expose themselves to His mercy and prepare themselves for His guidance through the path of choice.

By this, another somewhat difficult problem is resolved: We concede that His sending a sign that compels them to believe and forces them to accept the religious mission contradicts the foundation of choice upon which the structure of the religious mission is built. However, why is it not permissible for Allah to will the faith of all people just as He willed the faith of those who believed—by willing that all of them "will" [to believe], just as He willed specifically from the believers that they "will"—and then reveal a sign that drives them toward guidance and clothes them in faith without thereby nullifying their choice and freedom of action?

The answer is that while this is possible in itself, it contradicts the universal law (الناموس العام) in the world of causes (عالم الأسباب) and the system of preparation and emanation (نظام الاستعداد والإفاضة). Guidance is only emanated upon those who are God-conscious (اتقى الله) and purify their souls—"He who purifies it is indeed felicitous"—and error only afflicts those who turn away from the remembrance of their Lord and stunt their souls—"and he who stunts it is indeed a failure." His being afflicted by error means that the human being is denied guidance. He, the Exalted, said: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنا لَهُ فِيها ما نَشاءُ لِمَنْ نُرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنا لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلاها مَذْمُوماً مَدْحُوراً ، وَمَنْ أَرادَ الْآخِرَةَ وَسَعى لَها سَعْيَها وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ كانَ سَعْيُهُمْ مَشْكُوراً ، كُلًّا نُمِدُّ هؤُلاءِ وَهَؤُلاءِ مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً — "Whoever desires this transitory life, We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire. Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blamed and spurned. Whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with an effort worthy of it, should he be faithful—the endeavour of such will be well-appreciated. To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not barred" (Al-Isra: 18-20), meaning it is not withheld. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, extends to every soul from His bounty that which it deserves; if it desires good, it is given it, and if it desires evil, it is given it—meaning it is barred from good. Had Allah willed for every soul, whether righteous or wicked, to "will" good and devote itself to faith and godwariness through the path of choice, that would have entailed the nullification of the universal system and the corruption of the order of causes.

This is supported by the following verse, namely His saying: إِنَّما يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ — "Only those who listen will respond" until the end of the verse, according to the meaning that will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ وَالْمَوْتى يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ — "Only those who listen will respond. As for the dead, Allah will resurrect them, then to Him they will be returned." The verse is like an explanation for His saying: وَإِنْ كانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْراضُهُمْ — "And if their aversion is hard on you" until the end of the verse. Its summary is that you cannot turn them away from this aversion, nor obtain a sign that drives them to faith. He explained in this verse that they are like the dead (الموتى); they have no consciousness nor hearing such that they might perceive the meaning of the religious mission and hear the call of the caller, who is the Prophet (ص).

These visible human forms are of two types: one type is alive and hears—and indeed, only those who hear respond—and the other type is dead and does not hear, even if they appear in the form of the living. Their hearing of the speech depends on Allah resurrecting them, and He will surely resurrect them, and they will hear what they were unable to hear in the world, as Allah narrated from them in His saying: وَلَوْ تَرى إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عندَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ — "Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [confessing], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard; so send us back [to the world] that we may act righteously. Indeed [now] we have certainty’" (As-Sajdah: 12).

The speech is delivered by way of metonymy (الكناية). The intended meaning of "those who listen" (الذين يسمعون) is the believers, and by "the dead" (الموتى) is meant those among the polytheists and others who turn away from responding to the call. The description of believers as having life (الحياة) and hearing (السمع), and the description of disbelievers as having death (الموت) and deafness (الصمم), is repeated in His speech, the Exalted, as He said: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (Al-An'am: 122), and He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّكَ لا تُسْمِعُ الْمَوْتى وَلا تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ الدُّعاءَ إِذا وَلَّوْا مُدْبِرِينَ ، وَما أَنْتَ بِهادِي الْعُمْيِ عَنْ ضَلالَتِهِمْ إِنْ تُسْمِعُ إِلَّا مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِآياتِنا فَهُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ — "You cannot make the dead hear, nor can you make the deaf hear the call when they turn their backs [and] flee. Nor can you lead the blind out of their error. You can make only those hear who believe in Our signs and have submitted" (An-Naml: 80-81), among many other verses.

In some previous discussions, another meaning for these attributes—which the majority of commentators take as metonymy and metaphor—has been repeated, suggesting they possess a literal (الحقيقة) sense; so let it be referred to.

In the verse, there is an indication that Allah will make the disbelievers and polytheists understand the truth and hear His call in the Hereafter, just as He made the believers understand and hear in this world. Thus, man, whether a believer or a disbeliever, has no escape from understanding the truth, sooner or later.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, it is stated: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to love that al-Harith ibn 'Amir ibn Nawfal ibn 'Abd Manaf would embrace Islam. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called him and strove with him to submit, but wretchedness (الشقاء) overcame him. This was hard on the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so Allah revealed: وَإِنْ كانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْراضُهُمْ — 'And if their aversion is hard on you' until His saying نَفَقاً فِي الْأَرْضِ — 'a tunnel into the earth,' meaning a burrow (سربا)."

I say: The narration, despite its weakness and interrupted chain (إرسال), does not align with the outward sense of the numerous narrations indicating that the Surah was revealed all at once. However, it is possible to reconcile it by suggesting that the cause (السبب) occurred before the revelation of the Surah, and then the verse alluded to the realized cause by way of application (الانطباق).

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَادِرٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُنَزِّلَ ءَايَةً وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٧)
They say, ‘Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘Allah is indeed able to send down a sign,’ but most of them do not know. (37)
وَمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا طَـٰٓئِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَنَاحَيْهِ إِلَّآ أُمَمٌ أَمْثَالُكُم ۚ مَّا فَرَّطْنَا فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ (٣٨)
There is no animal on land, nor a bird that flies with its wings, but they are communities like yourselves. We have not omitted anything from the Book. Then they will be mustered toward their Lord. (38)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا صُمٌّ وَبُكْمٌ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ ۗ مَن يَشَإِ ٱللَّهُ يُضْلِلْهُ وَمَن يَشَأْ يَجْعَلْهُ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٣٩)
Those who deny Our signs are deaf and dumb, in a manifold darkness. Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and whomever He wishes He puts him on a straight path. (39)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتَىٰكُمْ عَذَابُ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَدْعُونَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤٠)
Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah’s punishment overtake you, or should the Hour overtake you, will you supplicate anyone other than Allah, should you be truthful? (40)
بَلْ إِيَّاهُ تَدْعُونَ فَيَكْشِفُ مَا تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِن شَآءَ وَتَنسَوْنَ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ (٤١)
No, Him you will supplicate, and He will remove that for which you supplicated Him, if He wishes, and you will forget what you ascribe [to Him] as [His] partners.’ (41)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَضَرَّعُونَ (٤٢)
We have certainly sent [apostles] to nations before you, then We seized them with stress and distress so that they might entreat [Us]. (42)
فَلَوْلَآ إِذْ جَآءَهُم بَأْسُنَا تَضَرَّعُوا۟ وَلَـٰكِن قَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٤٣)
Why did they not entreat when Our punishment overtook them! But their hearts had hardened, and Satan had made what they had been doing seem decorous to them. (43)
فَلَمَّا نَسُوا۟ مَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَبْوَٰبَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فَرِحُوا۟ بِمَآ أُوتُوٓا۟ أَخَذْنَـٰهُم بَغْتَةً فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْلِسُونَ (٤٤)
So when they forgot what they had been admonished of, We opened for them the gates of all [good] things. When they became proud of what they were given, We seized them suddenly, whereat, behold, they were despondent. (44)
فَقُطِعَ دَابِرُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ۚ وَٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٥)
Thus the wrongdoing lot were rooted out, and all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (45)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ سَمْعَكُمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرَكُمْ وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِكُم مَّنْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُم بِهِ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ثُمَّ هُمْ يَصْدِفُونَ (٤٦)
Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah take away your hearing and your sight and set a seal on your hearts, which god other than Allah can bring it [back] to you?’ Look, how We paraphrase the signs variously; nevertheless they turn away. (46)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتَىٰكُمْ عَذَابُ ٱللَّهِ بَغْتَةً أَوْ جَهْرَةً هَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٤٧)
Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah’s punishment overtake you suddenly or visibly, will anyone be destroyed except the wrongdoing lot?’ (47)
وَمَا نُرْسِلُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ ۖ فَمَنْ ءَامَنَ وَأَصْلَحَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٤٨)
We do not send the apostles except as bearers of good news and warners. As for those who are faithful and righteous, they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (48)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَمَسُّهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (٤٩)
But as for those who deny Our signs, the punishment shall befall them because of the transgressions they used to commit. (49)
قُل لَّآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِندِى خَزَآئِنُ ٱللَّهِ وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ وَلَآ أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّى مَلَكٌ ۖ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ ۚ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ ۚ أَفَلَا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٥٠)
Say, ‘I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say to you that I am an angel. I follow only what is revealed to me.’ Say, ‘Are the blind one and the seer equal? So do you not reflect?’ (50)
وَأَنذِرْ بِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ أَن يُحْشَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۙ لَيْسَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ وَلِىٌّ وَلَا شَفِيعٌ لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (٥١)
And warn by its means those who fear being mustered toward their Lord, besides whom they shall have neither any guardian nor any intercessor, so that they may be Godwary. (51)
وَلَا تَطْرُدِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَدَوٰةِ وَٱلْعَشِىِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُۥ ۖ مَا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ وَمَا مِنْ حِسَابِكَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَتَطْرُدَهُمْ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٢)
Do not drive away those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening desiring His face. Neither are you accountable for them in any way, nor are they accountable for you in any way, so that you may drive them away and thus become one of the wrongdoers. (52)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ فَتَنَّا بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لِّيَقُولُوٓا۟ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنۢ بَيْنِنَآ ۗ أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (٥٣)
Thus do We test them by means of one another so that they should say, ‘Are these the ones whom Allah has favoured from among us?!’ Does not Allah know best the grateful?! (53)
وَإِذَا جَآءَكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَقُلْ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ كَتَبَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةَ ۖ أَنَّهُۥ مَنْ عَمِلَ مِنكُمْ سُوٓءًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ ثُمَّ تَابَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَصْلَحَ فَأَنَّهُۥ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥٤)
When those who have faith in Our signs come to you, say, ‘Peace to you! Your Lord has made mercy incumbent upon Himself: whoever of you commits an evil [deed] out of ignorance and then repents after that and reforms, then He is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (54)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَلِتَسْتَبِينَ سَبِيلُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٥٥)
Thus do We elaborate the signs, so that the way of the guilty may be exposed. (55)

(Commentary)

Various arguments against the polytheists (المشركين) regarding the matter of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the sign of prophethood (النبوة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ قادِرٌ — They say, ‘Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah is able to send down a sign,’ until the end of the verse. This was an instigation on their part for the sending down of a sign, motivated by a desire to render the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) incapable. Since this statement was issued by them while the greatest of signs was already before them—namely the Noble Quran, which was being revealed to them chapter by chapter and verse by verse, and recited to them time after time—it is certain that the sign they were proposing in their saying, لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ (Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?), was a sign other than the Quran. They did not consider it a sign that satisfied them or that their souls accepted, due to their own recklessness and whims.

Their fanaticism for their deities drove them to sever themselves from Allah, the Glorified, as if He were not their Lord. Thus, they said: لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ (Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?), and they did not say: "from our Lord" or "from Allah" or the like, out of disdain for his affair and to emphasize his perceived inability. That is to say: if what he claims and calls toward were true, then his Lord, whom he calls toward, should show zeal for him, support him, and send down a sign to him indicating the truthfulness of his claim.

What prompted them to make this proposal was their ignorance of two matters: First, the idolaters (الوثنية) believed their deities possessed independence (استقلالاً) in the cosmic affairs referred to them, alongside the station of intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — that they claimed for them. Thus, the god of war or peace would have his own affairs to manage without his management being disrupted by another. Likewise, the god of the land, the god of the sea, the god of love, the god of hate, and all other deities. Consequently, no affair would remain for Allah, the Glorified, to dispose of, as the command had been divided among His associates, even if these were His intercessors and He was the Lord of lords. Therefore, they believed it was not within His power to nullify the command of their deities by sending down a sign that indicated the negation of their divinity.

This delusion was encouraged and reinforced in their hearts by what they received from the Jews of the Hijaz: that the hand of Allah is shackled, and He has no way to change anything of the prevailing system or break the familiar habit in the world of causes (عالم الأسباب).

Second: The signs revealed from Allah, the Glorified, if they are among those He specifically granted to a messenger from among His messengers without the people proposing them, are clear proofs (بينات) indicating the truthfulness of the messenger's claim without entailing a dire consequence for the people called—such as the staff and the white hand for Moses, the reviving of the dead, the healing of the blind and the leper, and the creation of birds for Jesus, and the Noble Quran for Muhammad (ص).

However, if the sign were among those proposed by the people, the sunnah (سنة) — the established way or law — of Allah has proceeded to judge between them upon its revelation: if they believe in it, they are saved; otherwise, punishment descends upon them, and they are given no further respite. This was the case with the signs of Noah, Hud, Salih, and others. In the Noble Quran, there are many verses indicating this, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ وَلَوْ أَنْزَلْنا مَلَكاً لَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ ثُمَّ لا يُنْظَرُونَ — They say, ‘Why has not an angel been sent down to him?’ Had We sent down an angel, the matter would surely have been decided, and then they would not have been granted any respite. (Al-An'am: 8), and His saying: وَما مَنَعَنا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآياتِ إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ وَآتَيْنا ثَمُودَ النَّاقَةَ مُبْصِرَةً فَظَلَمُوا بِها — Nothing kept Us from sending the signs except that the former peoples denied them. We gave Thamud the she-camel as a visible sign, but they wronged her. (Al-Isra: 59).

Both aspects have been alluded to in the noble verse, namely His saying: وَقالُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ قادِرٌ عَلى أَنْ يُنَزِّلَ آيَةً وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — They say, ‘Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah is able to send down a sign,’ but most of them do not know.

He mentioned that Allah is able to send down any sign He wills. How can one be named "Allah" and not possess absolute power (القدرة المطلقة)? In the response, the term "Lord" (الرب) was changed to the name "Allah" to indicate the proof of the ruling; for absolute divinity (الألوهية المطلقة) encompasses every perfection without being limited by any boundary or restricted by any condition, thus possessing absolute power. It was ignorance of the divine station (المقام الألوهي) that prompted them to propose the sign with the intent of proving inability.

Furthermore, they were ignorant that the revelation of the sign they proposed was not in their interest, and that their audacity in proposing it exposed them to the destruction of their collective and the severing of their roots. The evidence that this meaning is intended in some way in the discourse is His saying, the Exalted, at the end of these arguments: قُلْ لَوْ أَنَّ عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ لَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِالظَّالِمِينَ — Say, ‘If what you seek to hasten were with me, the matter would have been decided between me and you, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers.’ (Al-An'am: 58).

In His saying, the Exalted, نُزِّلَ (sent down) and يُنَزِّلَ (send down), the use of the intensified form (التفعيل) indicates that they proposed a gradual sign or many signs revealed one after another. This is supported by what is narrated of their proposals in other places of His speech, such as His saying: وَقالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى تَفْجُرَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ يَنْبُوعاً ، أَوْ تَكُونَ لَكَ جَنَّةٌ — until He said — أَوْ تَرْقى فِي السَّماءِ وَلَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ — They say, ‘We will not believe you until you make a spring gush forth for us from the ground, or until you have a garden... or until you ascend into the sky, and we will not believe your ascent until you bring down to us a book that we may read.’ (Al-Isra: 90-93), and His saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرى رَبَّنا — Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why have the angels not been sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?’ (Al-Furqan: 21), and His saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً — The faithless say, ‘Why has the Quran not been sent down to him all at once?’ (Al-Furqan: 32).

It is narrated from Ibn Kathir that he recited it without the intensification (التخفيف).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا طائِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَناحَيْهِ إِلَّا أُمَمٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ — There is no animal on the earth, nor a bird that flies with its wings, but they are communities like you. until the end of the verse. The animal (الدابة) is every living creature that crawls or walks upon the earth; it is frequently used for the horse. Crawling (الدب) and creeping (الدبيب) refer to a light gait.

The bird (الطائر) is that which swims in the air with its wings; its plural is al-tayr (الطير). The community (الأمة) is a group of people united by a single objective (مقصد) they pursue, such as a single religion (دين), a single law (سنة), a single time, or a single place. The root of its meaning is "intent" (القصد); it is said: amma ya'ummu when one intends something. Resurrection (الحشر) — the gathering of beings before Allah — is the gathering of people by driving them toward war, or for expulsion, and similar social matters.

It appears that describing the bird with the phrase يَطِيرُ بِجَناحَيْهِ (that flies with its wings) is a parallel to describing the animal with فِي الْأَرْضِ (on the earth). It is equivalent to our saying: "There is no terrestrial or aerial animal." Furthermore, this description negates any suspicion of metaphorical usage (التجوز), as "flying" is often used to mean speed of movement, just as "creeping" is a light movement. Thus, it was possible that "flying," when mentioned alongside "creeping," might be understood as rapid movement; this was dispelled by His saying: يَطِيرُ بِجَناحَيْهِ (that flies with its wings).

(A Discourse on Animal Societies)

The address in the verse is to mankind, and it mentions that animals, whether terrestrial or aerial, are communities (أمم) like humans. This does not merely mean that they are groups possessing large numbers, for the term "community" (الأمة) is not applied to a mere large number, but rather when that large number is gathered by a single unifying factor of a necessary or voluntary objective (مقصد) pursued by its members. Nor does it mean merely that they are various species, each species sharing a specific type of life, sustenance, mating, reproduction, shelter, and other vital affairs. While this degree of commonality might justify the judgment of their similarity to humans, His saying at the end of the verse: ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ — then they will be mustered toward their Lord — indicates that the intended similarity is not merely a resemblance in nutrition, mating, and sheltering. Rather, there is another aspect of commonality that makes them like humans in the criterion for being mustered (الحشر) toward Allah.

The criterion for being mustered toward Allah in humans is nothing other than a type of conscious life (الحياة الشعورية) that carves a path for the human toward his happiness or wretchedness. For an individual human may obtain in this world the most delicious food, the most suitable marriage, and the most splendid dwelling, yet not be happy in his life due to the injustice and profligacy he indulges in. Conversely, he may be surrounded by a collection of trials, hardships, and calamities, yet be happy in his life, rejoicing in the perfection of humanity and the light of servitude (العبودية).

Rather, the conscious life of the human—or if you will, the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and what supports it of the prophetic call, prescribes for the human a legislated way (سنة مشروعة) of belief and action. If he adopts it, follows it, and society agrees with him upon it, he is happy in both lives: this world and the Hereafter. If he follows it alone, he is happy in the Hereafter, or in both this world and the Hereafter together. If he does not act upon it and fails to adopt some or all of it, therein lies his wretchedness in this world and the Hereafter.

This prescribed way for him is summarized in two phrases: the urge toward good and obedience, and the deterrent from evil and disobedience. Or if you will: the call to justice and uprightness, and the prohibition of injustice and deviation from the truth. For the human, by his sound primordial nature, deems certain matters good, which constitute justice toward himself or others, and deems certain matters evil, which constitute injustice toward himself or others. Then the divine religion (الدين الإلهي) confirms these and explains their details to him.

This is the summary of what has become clear to us in many previous discussions, and many Quranic verses benefit and support this, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها — by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [discernment between] its virtues and vices: one who purifies it is certainly felicitous, and one who betrays it is certainly a failure. (Ash-Shams: 7-10), and His saying, the Exalted: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ — Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed... (Al-Baqarah: 213).

Deep reflection on the states of the non-human animals that accompany man in many affairs of life, and the conditions of a species among them in the course of its life and livelihood, indicates to us that they, like humans, possess individual and social beliefs and opinions upon which they base their movements and stillness in seeking survival. This is similar to how the human bases his transitions in the stages of worldly life upon a series of beliefs and opinions.

One of us desires food, marriage, children, or the like, or dislikes oppression, poverty, or the like. Thus, an opinion appears to him that it is necessary to seek food, eat it, or store it in his possession, and to marry and reproduce, and so on; and that it is forbidden for him to endure oppression or bear the calamity of poverty, and so on. So he moves and remains still according to the path these opinions appearing to his soul carve for him.

Likewise, an individual animal—as we observe—performs organized movements in the pursuits of its life, by which it schemes to fulfill the needs of its soul for food, mating, and shelter. We do not doubt that it possesses a consciousness (شعور) of its needs and that which fulfills them, and opinions and beliefs by which it is prompted to attract benefits and repel harms, just as in the human. We may even find in them types of ruses and stratagems for obtaining prey and escaping the enemy, through social and individual methods, that the human did not become aware of except after passing through centuries and epochs of his species' life.

Scientists researching animals have discovered in many of their species, such as ants, bees, and termites, wonders of the effects of civilization and society, subtleties of craft, and refinements of laws and policies, the likes of which are hardly found except in civilized and socialized nations of humans.

The Noble Quran has urged the knowledge of animals and reflection upon their creation and actions in general, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي خَلْقِكُمْ وَما يَبُثُّ مِنْ دابَّةٍ آياتٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ — And in your creation and in the animals He scatters are signs for a people who have certainty. (Al-Jathiyah: 4), and it called for taking a lesson from the affair of many of them, such as cattle, birds, bees, and ants.

These opinions and beliefs, which we see that animals of various species base their actions upon in the affairs and objectives of life, if they are not devoid of prompting and deterring judgments, then they are not devoid of deeming certain matters good and others evil, nor are they devoid of the meaning of justice or injustice.

This is supported by what we observe of some differences among individuals of any species of animal in their character (أخلاق). How much difference is there between one horse and another, one ram and another, or one rooster and another, for example, in terms of sharpness of temper or ease of disposition and gentleness of nature.

Similarly, it is supported by other particulars of love, hate, affection, mercy, cruelty, or transgression, and other things we find among individuals of a species, just as we have found their counterparts among individual humans. We have found them influential in the belief in the beauty (الحسن) and ugliness (القبح) of deeds, and justice and injustice in actions. Furthermore, they are also influential in the human's otherworldly life, and a criterion for his being mustered (حشر), the accounting of his deeds, and the recompense for them with otherworldly blessing or punishment.

By the research reaching this point, it may appear to us that animals have a resurrection (حشر) just as humans have a resurrection. For Allah, the Glorified, considers the applicability of justice, injustice, godwariness (التقوى), and profligacy (الفجور) to human actions as a criterion for the resurrection and argues for it by this, as in His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ — Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious? (Sad: 28). Rather, He considers the negation of the resurrection in what He created of the heavens, the earth, and what is between them as a negation of His act and its becoming play or futility, as in the verse preceding this one: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ — We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who are faithless. So woe to the faithless from the Fire! (Sad: 27).

So, do animals other than humans have a resurrection toward Allah, the Glorified, just as humans have a resurrection toward Him? Then, if they have a resurrection, does their resurrection resemble the resurrection of humans, such that they are held accountable for their deeds, which are weighed, and they are then blessed in Paradise or punished in Fire according to the obligation (التكليف) they had in the world? And is the establishment of worldly obligation upon them through the sending of messengers and the revelation of laws? And is the messenger sent to the animal of its own kind, or is he a human?

These are several questions that occur to the mind of the researcher at this juncture:

As for the first question (Do animals other than humans have a resurrection?), His saying, the Exalted, in the verse: ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ — then they will be mustered toward their Lord — provides the answer. Close to it is His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا الْوُحُوشُ حُشِرَتْ — when the wild beasts are mustered (At-Takwir: 5).

Indeed, there are very many verses indicating the return of the heavens, the earth, the sun, the moon, the stars, the jinn, stones, idols, and other partners worshipped besides Allah, and gold and silver—where they will be heated in the fire of Hell and the foreheads and flanks of those who withheld zakat will be branded with them—among many other verses that need not be cited. The narrations (الروايات) regarding these meanings are beyond count.

As for the second question—which is whether their resurrection resembles the human resurrection such that they are raised, their deeds are brought forth, and they are held accountable for them, being blessed or punished thereby—the answer is that this is a necessary consequence of "mustering" (الحشر) in the sense of gathering individuals and driving them toward a matter by force. As for things like the heaven and the earth and what resembles them of the sun, moon, stones, and others, the term "mustering" (الحشر) was not used in their case, as in His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّماواتُ وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ — The day when the earth is transformed into another earth and the heavens [as well], and they emerge before Allah, the One, the All-paramount. (Ibrahim: 48), and His saying: وَالْأَرْضُ جَمِيعاً قَبْضَتُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ — The entire earth will be in His fist on the Day of Resurrection, and the heavens, scrolled, in His right hand. (Az-Zumar: 67), and His saying: وَجُمِعَ الشَّمْسُ وَالْقَمَرُ — and the sun and the moon are brought together (Al-Qiyamah: 9), and His saying: إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ أَنْتُمْ لَها وارِدُونَ ، لَوْ كانَ هؤُلاءِ آلِهَةً ما وَرَدُوها — Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are fuel for hell, and you will enter it. Had these been gods, they would not have entered it. (Al-Anbiya: 98-99).

Furthermore, the criterion given by His speech, the Exalted, regarding the mustering of people is the decisive judgment (القضاء الفصل) between them concerning that in which they differed of the truth. He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ — Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (As-Sajdah: 25), and His saying: ثُمَّ إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيما كُنْتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ — Then to Me will be your return, and I will judge between you concerning that about which you used to differ. (Al-Imran: 55), and other verses.

The ultimate return of all things is to the bestowing of favor upon the doer of good and the taking of vengeance (الانتقام) from the wrongdoer for his wrongdoing, as He mentioned in His saying: إِنَّا مِنَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُنْتَقِمُونَ "Indeed We will take vengeance upon the guilty" (As-Sajdah: 22), and His saying: فَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ اللَّهَ مُخْلِفَ وَعْدِهِ رُسُلَهُ ۗ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انتِقَامٍ * يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّمَاوَاتُ ۖ وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ "So do not suppose that Allah will break His promise to His apostles. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, wielder of retribution. The day when the earth is transformed into another earth and the heavens [as well], and they emerge before Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (Ibrahim: 47-48). These two descriptions—I mean doing good (الإحسان) and wrongdoing (الظلم)—are present in the actions of animals in general.

This is supported by the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ اللَّهُ النَّاسَ بِمَا كَسَبُوا مَا تَرَكَ عَلَىٰ ظَهْرِهَا مِن دَابَّةٍ وَلَٰكِن يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلَىٰ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى "Were Allah to take mankind to task for what they have earned, He would not leave any living being on its back. But He respites them until a specified time" (Fatir: 45). Its apparent meaning is that if the wrongdoing of humans necessitated divine punishment, it would be because it is wrongdoing (ظلم), and wrongdoing is common among everything called a "living being" (دابة)—including humans and all other animals. Thus, it would result in Allah, the Exalted, destroying every living being on the earth's back. This is the case even though some have mentioned that "living being" (الدابة) in the verse refers specifically to humans.

It does not follow from the inclusion of all animals in the seizing and vengeance on the Day of Resurrection that they must equal humans in consciousness (الشعور) and will (الإرادة), or that the mute animal (الحيوان العجم) rises to the level of the human in its psychological and spiritual states. Necessity (الضرورة) refutes this, and the manifest effects seen in them and in humans invalidate it.

This is because mere participation in being seized, vengeance, reckoning (الحساب), and reward (الأجر) between humans and others does not dictate equivalence and equality in all respects.
