Similarly, participation in what is closer than that among human individuals themselves does not necessitate that the reckoning of their deeds—in terms of meticulousness and scrutiny—proceed in a single manner, such that the rational (العاقل), the simpleton (السفيه), the mature (الرشيد), and the oppressed/weakened (المستضعف) are made to stand in the same position.

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, has mentioned regarding some animals such subtleties of understanding (الفهم) and intricacies of alertness (النباهة) that are not at all far from the level of an average human in comprehension and reasoning. This is like what He narrated concerning the ant of Sulayman in His saying: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا أَتَوْا عَلَىٰ وَادِ النَّمْلِ قَالَتْ نَمْلَةٌ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّمْلُ ادْخُلُوا مَسَاكِنَكُمْ لَا يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ سُلَيْمَانُ وَجُنُودُهُ وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ "When they came to the Valley of Ants, an ant said, ‘O ants! Enter your dwellings, lest Sulayman and his hosts should trample you while they are unaware’" (An-Naml: 18). And what He narrated of the hoopoe’s speech to him (peace be upon him) in the story of its absence from him: فَقَالَ أَحَطْتُ بِمَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِ وَجِئْتُكَ مِن سَبَإٍ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ * إِنِّي وَجَدتُّ امْرَأَةً تَمْلِكُهُمْ وَأُوتِيَتْ مِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَلَهَا عَرْشٌ عَظِيمٌ * وَجَدْتُهَا وَقَوْمَهَا يَسْجُدُونَ لِلشَّمْسِ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ فَهُمْ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ "But it did not stay for long [before it came] and said, ‘I have come to know what you do not know, and I have brought you a reliable report from Sheba. I found a woman ruling over them, and she has been given everything, and she has a great throne. I found her and her people prostrating to the sun instead of Allah, and Satan has made their deeds seem decorous to them—thus he has barred them from the way [of Allah], so they are not guided’" (An-Naml: 22-24), until the end of the verses. Indeed, the alert researcher, when contemplating these verses and the signs of understanding and consciousness they manifest, and then estimating their weight, will not doubt that the realization of this amount of understanding and consciousness depends upon vast knowledge and many diverse perceptions of both simple and complex meanings.

This is perhaps supported by what experts in animal behavior have gathered through their deep observations and training of various animal species regarding wondrous states that could hardly manifest except from a being possessed of a subtle will (إرادة لطيفة), deep thought (فكر عميق), and sharp consciousness (شعور حاد).

As for the third and fourth questions—namely: does the animal receive its obligation (تكليف) in this world through a messenger sent to it and a revelation (وحي) descended upon it? And is this messenger sent to a species of animal from among the individuals of that very species?—the world of animals remains, until this time, unknown to us, veiled by a curtain. Engaging in this type of research is of no benefit and yields no result except "guessing at the Unseen" (الرجم بالغيب). The Divine Speech, as far as its apparent meanings (ظواهر) show us, does not address the explanation of any of this, nor is there anything reliable to be found in the narrations (الروايات) transmitted from the Prophet and the Imams of his household (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding this matter.

Thus, it has been established that animal societies, like human society, contain the substance of the divine religion (الدين الإلهي) which they draw from their primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — in a manner similar to how religion is drawn from the human primordial nature. This prepares them for the gathering (الحشر) — the assembly of all creatures before Allah on the Day of Judgment — unto Allah, the Glorified, just as the religion of the primordial nature prepares the human for the gathering and recompense (الجزاء). This is so even if what is observed of the animal's state in comparison to the human—supported by the Quranic verses speaking of the subjection of things to the human and his superiority over all animals—is that the animal has not been given the details of human knowledge, nor has it been burdened with the intricacies of the divine obligations with which the human has been charged.

Let us return to the text of the verse. His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا مِن دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا طَائِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَنَاحَيْهِ إِلَّا أُمَمٌ أَمْثَالُكُم "There is no animal on the earth, nor a bird that flies with its wings, but they are communities like yourselves" (Al-An'am: 38) indicates that the animal societies existing within every species of animal are founded upon conscious, specific objectives (مقاصد نوعية شعورية) aimed at by each species of animal, despite their differences, through consciousness and will, just like the human.

This is not limited to natural objectives—meaning the objectives of nutrition, growth, and reproduction limited to this worldly life—but rather its scope extends to what follows death, preparing for another life linked to felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) drawn from the breast of consciousness and will.

It might be objected that people have conceded that animal species other than humans are deprived of the gift of choice (الاختيار); therefore, the actions of animals are considered like the actions of plants—natural and non-voluntary—because of what is observed of their state: that they do not possess themselves to refrain from an action if they encounter what contains their desired benefit, like a cat when it sees a mouse or a lion when it sees its prey, or fleeing when encountering what it fears of a dominant enemy, like a mouse when it sees a cat or a gazelle when it sees a lion. Thus, there is no meaning for voluntary felicity and wretchedness in animals other than humans.

However, reflection upon the meaning of choice (الاختيار) and the psychological states through which a human reaches his voluntary actions refutes this doubt. This is because the consciousness (الشعور) and will (الإرادة) by which the human's voluntary action is actually completed were only placed in the human, for instance, because he is a conscious species that acts upon external matter to benefit from it for the survival of his existence by distinguishing what benefits him from what harms him. Therefore, Divine Care equipped him with consciousness and will; he distinguishes with his living consciousness what harms him from what benefits him. When the benefit is realized, he wills and then acts. Whatever matters are clearly beneficial and do not require more than their presence and the attainment of knowledge of them to be judged as beneficial, he wills immediately and acts upon them without hesitation, as is the case in most instances of deeply rooted habits (الملكات الراسخة) like breathing. As for matters whose benefit is not clear, being marked by a deficiency in causes or surrounded by external or belief-based obstacles, the mere knowledge of their occurrence is not sufficient for his will and action due to the lack of certainty in benefiting from them.

These are the matters whose motivation depends upon reflection (التفكر), for example, regarding the deficiencies and obstacles accompanying them, and deliberation (التروي) to distinguish thereby whether they are of the beneficial or harmful kind. If deliberation results in them being beneficial, the will appears attached to them and acts, just as if they were clearly beneficial without needing deliberation. This is like a hungry person who finds food with which he can satisfy the pangs of hunger; he might doubt whether it is good food suitable for consumption, or unsuitable, spoiled, poisoned, or containing harmful substances. He might also doubt whether it is his own property with no obstacle to using it—such as a pressing future need or fasting—or the property of another which he is not permitted to use. In such a case, he refrains from immediate action and continues to deliberate until he reaches a conclusion on one of the two sides. If he judges it permissible, it becomes an instance of what is beneficial, and it is not expected thereafter that he would do anything but will and then consume it.

If he did not doubt its state and it was clear to him from the very beginning that it was good food suitable for consumption, he would will it as soon as he knew of its existence without deliberation or reflection, and knowledge of it would never be separated from the will to use it.

The summary of the discourse on choice (الاختيار) is that when it is not clear to a human whether some matters he acts upon are beneficial or harmful, he distinguishes that through deliberation and reflection, and thus chooses one of the sides. However, if it is distinguished from the very beginning, he wills and acts without delay and has no need for deliberation at all. Thus, the human chooses what he sees as beneficial, whether through deliberation or without it; deliberation is only for removing obstacles to the judgment.

Furthermore, if you contemplate the state of human individuals who are "choosers" in their actions, you will find them possessing great variation in the principles of their choice—meaning spiritual traits and internal states such as courage, cowardice, chastity, greed, vigor, laziness, dignity, and levity. Likewise, they vary in the strength and weakness of reasoning, and the correctness or error of insight. Often, the greedy person sees himself as compelled and stripped of choice in matters he desires to indulge in, while the chaste and pure person pays them no mind. A coward might see the slightest harm befalling him in a task or a place of danger as an excuse for himself that negates his choice, while the brave, valiant person who refuses injustice does not see "red death" or any physical deterrent as something beyond his capacity, nor does he see any harm in any immense calamity in the path of his objectives. A foolish, light-headed person might choose based on the slightest trivial conception, while the wise, intelligent person does not see the preference of an action based on such motivators as anything but amusement and play. The actions of non-discerning children are voluntary and accompanied by some deliberation, yet the mature, sensible adult pays them no heed. Often in our conversations, we consider one of our actions as "compelled" or "forced" if it is accompanied by social excuses that are not truly binding, like a smoker excusing himself by habit, a sleeper by laziness, or a thief or traitor by poverty.

This vast difference in the principles and causes of choice, and the wide spectrum in the level of voluntary actions, is what prompted religion and other social norms to define the voluntary action (الفعل الاختياري) by what the average individual of human society considers voluntary. They based upon this the validity of command, prohibition, punishment, reward, the legal effect of transactions, and so on. They excuse those in whom the principles and causes that exist in the voluntary action performed by the average human—namely, average capability and understanding—are not realized.

So, this "middle ground" counted as choice, which negates the voluntariness of what is below it, is only so according to the religious or social judgment in which the interest of religion or society is observed, even if the matter according to the ontological/creative view (النظر التكويني) is broader than that.

Careful consideration of what has preceded allows one to conclude with certainty that animals other than humans are not deprived of the gift of choice (الاختيار) in general, even if it is weaker than the meaning of choice found in the average person. This is because of what we observe in many animals, especially domestic ones, of signs of hesitation in certain instances accompanied by obstacles to action, as well as refraining from action due to a deterrent, fear, or training. All of this indicates that in their souls there is a capacity to judge the necessity of action or omission, which is the criterion for the essence of choice, even if deliberation (التروي) is very weak in them and does not reach the level we find in the average human.

If it is correct that animals other than humans are not devoid of the meaning of choice in general, even if weak, then it is possible that Allah, the Glorified, makes the average of the levels of choice present in them a criterion for obligations (تكاليف) suitable for them and specific to them, which we do not comprehend. Or He may deal with them according to the gift of choice they possess in another manner of which we have no knowledge, except that it exists in them in a way that justifies bestowing favor upon them for compliance, and taking them to task and seeking vengeance from them for non-compliance, in a manner that Allah, the Glorified, knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: مَّا فَرَّطْنَا فِي الْكِتَابِ مِن شَيْءٍ "We have not omitted anything from the Book" (Al-An'am: 38) is a parenthetical sentence. Its apparent meaning is that the thing in which there is no omission is "the Book" (الكتاب), and the phrase "anything" (من شيء) is an explanation of the neglect that would constitute an omission. The meaning is: there is nothing whose state must be observed, whose right must be fulfilled, and whose description must be explained in the Book, except that it has been done without omission; thus, the Book is complete and perfect.

If the intended meaning of "the Book" is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) — the celestial record in which Allah has written the decrees of all things — which Allah, the Glorified, calls in various places of His speech a "written book" containing everything that was, is, and will be, then the meaning is that these communal systems (النظامات الأممية) similar to the human system were, by the necessity of Allah's care, the basis upon which He built the creation of animal species. Thus, their creation would not be in vain, nor their existence for naught, and these species would not be denied the gift of perfection to the extent of their capacity to receive it.

The verse, according to this, conveys specifically what is conveyed generally by His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كَانَ عَطَاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا "And the bounty of your Lord is not confined" (Al-Isra: 20), and His saying: مَّا مِن دَابَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ آخِذٌ بِنَاصِيَتِهَا ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ "There is no living being but He holds it by its forelock. Indeed my Lord is on a straight path" (Hud: 56).

If the intended meaning is the Noble Quran—and Allah has called it a "Book" in many places of His speech—then the meaning is that since the Majestic Quran is a book of guidance (هداية) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — leading to a straight path (صراط مستقيم) based on the explanation of the realities of knowledge that are indispensable for guidance to the manifest truth and pure reality, it has not omitted the explanation of everything upon whose knowledge the felicity of people in their world and their hereafter depends, as He, the Exalted, said: وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ تِبْيَانًا لِّكُلِّ شَيْءٍ "We have sent down the Book to you as an exposition of all things" (An-Nahl: 89).

Among the things people must know in the course of understanding the matter of the Return (المعاد) is to clarify how the gathering (الحشر)—which is the resurrection on the Day of Judgment upon a social pattern—is linked to the communal formation in this world. This is what they find among themselves and among all other animal species. Other benefits follow from this, such as gaining insight into the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — of Allah, the Exalted, and His subtle power and care for the matter of creation and the general system prevailing in the world. One of its most important benefits is the knowledge that existence proceeds in its chain from deficiency toward perfection; some of its segments, containing the links of animals—including humans and those below them—are different, ordered levels proceeding from the levels residing in the horizon of plants to the levels adjacent to the level of the human, and then the human.

Allah, the Glorified, has urged people to know the animal and to look into the signs (الآيات) deposited in its existence with the most eloquent urging. He considered this a means to the best of scientific results inseparable from human felicity, which is certainty (اليقين) in Allah, the Glorified, where He said: وَفِي خَلْقِكُمْ وَمَا يَبُثُّ مِن دَابَّةٍ آيَاتٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ "And in your creation and in the animals He scatters are signs for a people who have certainty" (Al-Jathiyah: 4). The verses encouraging the observation of the animal's state are numerous in the Noble Quran.

It is possible that both meanings are pointed to in the verse, such that "the Book" refers to the Book in an absolute sense. The meaning would then be that Allah, the Glorified, does not omit anything in what He writes. As for the Book of Creation (كتاب التكوين), He decrees and measures for every species the perfection of existence it deserves to attain; for instance, He prepared for each of the animal species the felicity of communal social life just as He prepared it for the human, seeing their suitability for that, and thus He did not omit their matter. As for His Book which is His speech revealed to people, He explains therein what contains the good of people and the felicity of their present and future, and He does not omit that. Part of this is that He did not omit the matter of animal communities, and He explained in this verse the reality of the kind of existential felicity He granted them, which made them living communities proceeding by their existence toward Allah, the Glorified, gathered unto Him just like the human.

And His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ "then they will be mustered toward their Lord" (Al-An'am: 38) is an explanation of the universality of the gathering for them, and that the life granted to them is a type of life that entails being gathered unto Allah, just as human life is. Therefore, He used the pronoun used for those possessed of consciousness and intellect, saying: إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ (unto their Lord they shall be gathered), indicating that the fundamental criterion—the matter upon which pleasure, displeasure, reward, and punishment revolve—is present in them.

A shift (التفات) has occurred in the verse from the third person to the first person plural, and then back to the third person in relation to Him, the Exalted. Contemplation of it shows that the original course of the context is the third person, and the context only shifted in His saying: مَّا فَرَّطْنَا فِي الْكِتَابِ مِن شَيْءٍ (We have not omitted anything from the Book) to the first person plural because the parenthetical sentence is a discourse specifically for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). When it was finished, it returned to the original course of the context.

Among the strange things said regarding this verse is the argument some have made for reincarnation (التناسخ) — the belief that the human soul, after departing the body at death, attaches itself to the body of an animal that suits the base character ingrained within it. For instance, the soul of a deceiver might attach to the body of a fox, the soul of a spiteful mischief-maker to the body of a wolf, the soul of one who pursues the lapses and private faults of people to the body of a pig, and the soul of a gluttonous person to the body of a cow, and so on. It continues to move from one body to another, being punished thereby if it was wretched and possessed of base character. If it was felicitous, it would attach after death to a felicitous body, enjoying its happiness among the noble individuals of mankind. According to this view, the meaning of the verse would be: "There is no animal among the animals except that they were human nations like you, who transitioned after death into the forms of animals."

It has become clear from what preceded that the verse is far removed from this meaning. Furthermore, the end of the verse—ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يُحْشَرُونَ "then they shall be mustered toward their Lord" (6:38)—does not suit this interpretation. Moreover, such claims are so patently false that there is no benefit in addressing them or investigating their soundness or infirmity.

Another strange thing said regarding it is that the "mustering" (الحشر) of animals refers to their death, meaning there is no resurrection after that, or that it refers to the combination of death and resurrection. As for the first view, it is refuted by the apparent meaning of His saying: إِلى رَبِّهِمْ "toward their Lord," as death "toward Allah" carries no meaning. As for the second view, it is an instance of committing oneself to what is unnecessary, as there is no reason to combine death and resurrection in the meaning, nor is there anything in the verse that necessitates it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا صُمٌّ وَبُكْمٌ فِي الظُّلُماتِ "Those who deny Our signs are deaf and dumb, in a manifold darkness" (6:39) until the end of the verse. The Exalted intends that those who deny His signs are deprived of the blessings of hearing, speech, and sight, because they are in darknesses (الظلمات) where sight cannot function. Due to their deafness (الصمم), they are unable to hear the word of truth or respond to it; due to their dumbness (البكم), they cannot speak the word of truth or testify to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the message (الرسالة); and because darkness encompasses them, they cannot perceive the path of truth to take it as their way.

In His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ يَشَأِ اللهُ يُضْلِلْهُ "Whomever Allah wishes He leads astray" (6:39) etc., there is an indication that this deafness, dumbness, and falling into darkness are but a punishment (الرجز) that has befallen them from Him, the Exalted, as a recompense for their denial of Allah's signs. For Allah, the Glorified, has made the "leading astray" (الإضلال) attributed to Him a form of recompense, as in His saying: وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ "and He misleads by it only the transgressors" (2:26).

Thus, the denial of Allah's signs is not caused by their being deaf and dumb in darkness; rather, the matter is the reverse. Accordingly, the meaning of "leading astray" (الإضلال), in terms of its application to this context, is His making them deaf and dumb in darkness, and those whom Allah wishes to lead astray are those who denied His signs.

By contrast, it becomes clear that "placing one on a straight path" (الجعل على صراط مستقيم) means giving him hearing by which he hears and thus responds to Allah's caller with his tongue, and giving him insight into the truth with his sight. This is the reward for one who does not deny the signs of Allah, the Glorified. So, whomever Allah wishes He leads astray—and He only wishes to lead astray those who deserve it—and whomever He wishes He places on a straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — and He only wishes that for those who expose themselves to His mercy.

The investigation into the reality of the meaning of what Allah describes them with—deafness, dumbness, blindness, and similar attributes—has already passed. However, another subtle point (النكتة) is intended in this verse, which is what is conveyed by the "joining" (الوصل) and "separation" (الفصل) in His saying: صُمٌّ وَبُكْمٌ فِي الظُّلُماتِ "deaf and dumb, in a manifold darkness." He mentioned deafness (الصمم), which is one of their attributes, then mentioned dumbness (البكم) and joined it to the former with a conjunction, which is a second attribute. Then He mentioned their being in darkness (الظلمات) without a conjunction, which is a third attribute. In short, He joined some attributes and separated others. In a verse similar to this in meaning, He used separation, namely His saying regarding the hypocrites: صُمٌّ بُكْمٌ عُمْيٌ "Deaf, dumb, [and] blind" (2:18). In another verse similar to it, He used conjunction, namely His saying regarding the disbelievers: خَتَمَ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلى سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلى أَبْصارِهِمْ غِشاوَةٌ "Allah has set a seal on their hearts and their hearing, and there is a blindfold on their sight" (2:7).

Perhaps the subtle point in the verse we are considering—namely His saying: صُمٌّ وَبُكْمٌ فِي الظُّلُماتِ—is an allusion to the fact that those who are deaf are different from those who are dumb. The deaf are the ignorant followers who trail after their leaders; this prevents them from having any hearing with which to listen to the true call. The dumb are the great leaders who are followed, who possess knowledge of the correctness of the call to monotheism and the falsehood of polytheism (الشرك), yet due to their stubbornness and rebellion, they are dumb; their tongues will not move to acknowledge the word of truth or testify to it. Both groups share the fact that they have fallen into a darkness in which the truth cannot be perceived, and no one else can make them see anything through signs because of their location in darkness, for signs do not succeed therein.

This is supported by the fact that the discourse narrated in these verses encompasses both groups, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, in the preceding verses: وَهُمْ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ وَيَنْأَوْنَ عَنْهُ "They forbid [others] from it and [themselves] keep away from it" (6:26), and likewise His saying: وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "but most of them do not know" (6:37).

This applies to the verse we are currently discussing. As for the verse regarding the hypocrites: صُمٌّ بُكْمٌ عُمْيٌ, the focus there is on the gathering of all these attributes in them at a single time, due to their being cut off from Allah's mercy from every direction. As for the verse regarding the disbelievers: خَتَمَ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلى سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلى أَبْصارِهِمْ غِشاوَةٌ, the focus there is on the fact that the sealing of the hearing is of a different nature than the sealing of the hearts, as He narrated from them in His saying: وَقالُوا قُلُوبُنا فِي أَكِنَّةٍ مِمَّا تَدْعُونا إِلَيْهِ وَفِي آذانِنا وَقْرٌ وَمِنْ بَيْنِنا وَبَيْنِكَ حِجابٌ "They say, ‘Our hearts are in sheaths from what you invite us to, and there is a deafness in our ears, and there is a curtain between us and you’" (41:5). The verse has also been explained through other perspectives.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُ اللهِ أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ السَّاعَةُ "Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah’s punishment come to you, or should the Hour overtake you" (6:40) until the end of the two verses. The word أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ consists of the interrogative hamza, the singular masculine past tense form of "seeing" (الرؤية), and the plural second-person pronoun. Grammarians take it to mean "tell me." Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "أرأيت (Tell me) functions like 'inform me' (أخبرني); thus the kaf is attached to it while the ta remains in its state regardless of dual, plural, or feminine forms, and the change is applied to the kaf rather than the ta. The Exalted said: أَرَأيتَكَ هَذَا الَّذِي (Tell me, this one whom...) and قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ (Say, tell me...)." End quote.

In this verse, there is a renewal of the argument against the polytheists and the establishment of a proof for the vanity of their polytheism from one perspective. This perspective assumes a punishment coming from Allah's side or the coming of the Hour (الساعة) — the Day of Resurrection — upon them, and then assumes that in such a state they would call upon someone to remove the punishment from them, as is ingrained in the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — of man: that he turns with a request, when distress reaches its peak, toward one who is able to remove it from him.

Then He asks them: who is it that you would call upon and turn to with your request, if you are truthful? Would you call upon other than Allah from among your idols and statues which you have named as gods of your own accord, or would you call upon Him? Far be it that you would call upon anyone else when you would witness then that they are subject to the laws of the cosmos just like you, and calling upon them would avail you nothing.

Rather, you would forget these partners named as gods, because when calamity encompasses a person and tremors shake him, he forgets everything besides himself, except that within himself there is a hope that the calamity will be lifted. The Lifter whom he hopes will remove it is his Lord. Thus, you forget your partners and call upon the One who removes it besides them, and He is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه). Then Allah, the Glorified, removes that which you call for its removal if He wishes to remove it; for He, the Exalted, is not compelled to respond nor forced to remove it when called upon, but rather He is powerful over all things in every state.

If Allah, the Glorified, is the Powerful Lord whom man does not forget even if he forgets everything except himself, and to whom he is compelled to turn by an inner impulse during crushing and devastating hardships to the exclusion of other partners named as gods, then He, the Glorified, is the Lord of people, not they.

So the meaning of the verse is: "Say," O Muhammad, "أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ" (tell me), "إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُ اللهِ أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ السَّاعَةُ" (should Allah’s punishment come to you, or should the Hour overtake you)—assuming the coming of a punishment from Allah, which they do not deny, and assuming the coming of the Hour, not heeding their denial of it due to its manifest nature—"أَغَيْرَ اللهِ تَدْعُونَ" (would you supplicate anyone other than Allah) to remove it? Allah has narrated in His speech their request for the removal of punishment in both this world and the Day of Resurrection, as this is among the innate characteristics of man. "إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ" (if you are truthful) and act with fairness. "بَلْ إِيَّاهُ" (Rather, Him)—Allah the Glorified, to the exclusion of your idols—"تَدْعُونَ فَيَكْشِفُ ما تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ" (you will supplicate, and He will unfold that for which you supplicate Him)—the punishment—"إِنْ شاءَ" (if He wishes) to remove it, just as He removed it for the people of Jonah (Yunus). He is neither compelled nor forced to accept, due to His essential power. "وَتَنْسَوْنَ ما تُشْرِكُونَ" (and you will forget those you associate [with Him])—the idols and statues—according to what is in the instinct of man: that when calamity encompasses him, he becomes preoccupied with himself to the exclusion of everything else. He cares for nothing but himself because the situation is too narrow for him to amuse himself with what does not benefit him. His preoccupation in this state with calling upon Allah, the Glorified, and his forgetting the idols is the clearest proof that He, the Exalted, is Allah; there is no god but He, and no object of worship besides Him.

From the preceding explanation of the verse's meaning, it becomes clear, firstly: that the coming of the punishment or the Hour, as well as the supplication for its removal, are assumed premises in the argument of the verse. The objective is to demonstrate that the one called upon at that time is Allah, exalted is His Name, to the exclusion of the idols. As for the origin of supplication during hardships and calamities—and that man has an innate turning (توجها جبليا) when calamity looms over him and every means is cut off from him toward the One who can remove it—this is another proof distinct from this one. The objective of this proof—which is in this verse—is monotheism (التوحيد), while the objective of that proof is the affirmation of the Creator (إثبات الصانع) without regard to His oneness, even if the two objectives are inseparable.

Secondly, the restriction of His saying: فَيَكْشِفُ ما تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ — "then He will remove that for which you supplicated Him" — by His saying: إِنْ شاءَ — "if He wishes" — is to demonstrate the absolute nature of His power (إطلاق القدرة). For Allah, the Glorified, has the power to remove every hardship, even the Hour (الساعة) — the Day of Resurrection — about which there is no doubt. Indeed, His definitive decree (قضاؤه الحتم) regarding any matter, though He makes it inevitable and necessary, does not strip Him of the power to refrain from it. He possesses absolute power (القدرة المطلقة) over what He has decreed and what He has not. In this regard, the Hour is like every punishment that is not turned back and every inevitable matter: if He wishes, He brings it to pass, and if He does not wish, He does not, even though He always wishes that which He has definitively decreed and firmly promised—and Allah does not fail His promise. Understand this well.

It is within His power, the Glorified, not to answer the supplication (دعوة) of any supplicant who calls upon Him, even though He has identified Himself as the Responder (مجيب). He said: وَإِذا سَأَلَكَ عِبادِي عَنِّي فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ — "When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me" (2:186). He promised the response to His supplicant with a decisive promise, saying: ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ — "Call Me, I will answer you" (40:60). Indeed, the promise of response does not strip Him of the power to not respond, even though He always responds to everyone who calls upon Him with a sincere supplication (حقيقة الدعاء), and His sunnah (سنته) — His established way of acting — proceeds upon this as a straight path (صراطا مستقيما) in which there is no deviation.

From here, the fallacy of the objection raised against the verse becomes apparent—the claim that its meaning contradicts the texts of the Book and the Sunnah (السنة) — the prophetic tradition — which indicate that the Hour is beyond doubt and its occurrence is inescapable, and that the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) cannot be turned back, while the Exalted has said: وَما دُعاءُ الْكافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ — "and the prayer of the faithless only goes wandering" (40:50). The reason this objection is fallacious is that the verse indicates nothing more than that Allah, the Glorified, does what He wishes and that He is powerful over all things. As for Him wishing everything and doing everything, there is no indication of that in the verse. There is no doubt that His definitive decree for the occurrence of the Hour or for the punishment of a people with the punishment of extirpation does not nullify His power over its opposite; He has the power to act otherwise if He wishes, even if He does not fail His promise nor revoke what He has willed.

As for His saying, the Exalted: وَما دُعاءُ الْكافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ — "and the prayer of the faithless only goes wandering" — this refers to their supplication in Hell (جهنم) for the removal of its punishment or its mitigation. It is well known that supplication, when the judgment has become inevitable and the decree has been finalized, does not manifest in its true reality (حقيقته). For asking that Allah not resurrect the creation or not punish the people of Hell therein is, in relation to Allah, the Glorified, equivalent to asking that Allah not be Allah, the Glorified. This is because it is among the necessary implications of the meaning of divinity (الألوهية) that the creation returns to Him according to their deeds. Thus, such supplications possess only the form of a supplication (صورة الدعاء) without the reality of its meaning. However, if a supplication were realized in its true essence—such that it is a true calling and that calling is truly directed toward Allah, as is the apparent meaning of His saying: أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ — "I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me" — then it would never be rejected. A supplication of this description does not leave the disbeliever (الكافر) as a disbeliever, even at the moment of supplication, as the Exalted said: فَإِذا رَكِبُوا فِي الْفُلْكِ دَعَوُا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ فَلَمَّا نَجَّاهُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ إِذا هُمْ يُشْرِكُونَ — "When they board the ship, they invoke Allah putting exclusive faith in Him, but when He delivers them to land, behold, they ascribe partners [to Him]" (29:65).

Thus, what is mentioned in His saying: وَما دُعاءُ الْكافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ — "and the prayer of the faithless only goes wandering" — is a supplication from them while they persist in disbelief (الكفر). For the established disposition (ملكة) of disbelief does not leave them in the Abode of Recompense (دار الجزاء), even though it was possible for it to leave them in the Abode of Action (دار العمل) through repentance (التوبة) and faith (الإيمان).

Their supplication for the removal of punishment from them on the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) or in Hell is like their lying to Allah on the Day of Resurrection when they say—as Allah narrated—وَاللهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ — "By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists." Lying is of no benefit on that day; rather, they had become accustomed to it in the world, and their vice (رذيلتهم) became deeply rooted in their souls, so its effects emerged from them on the day when secrets are examined (يوم تبلى السرائر). This is similar to their eating, drinking, and feuding in the Fire, none of which avails them in the least, as the Exalted said: تُسْقى مِنْ عَيْنٍ آنِيَةٍ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ ضَرِيعٍ لا يُسْمِنُ وَلا يُغْنِي مِنْ جُوعٍ — "They are given to drink from a boiling spring. They will have no food except cactus, which neither fattens nor satisfies hunger" (88:5-7), and the Exalted said: ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ أَيُّهَا الضَّالُّونَ الْمُكَذِّبُونَ لَآكِلُونَ مِنْ شَجَرٍ مِنْ زَقُّومٍ فَمالِؤُنَ مِنْهَا الْبُطُونَ فَشارِبُونَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْحَمِيمِ فَشارِبُونَ شُرْبَ الْهِيمِ — "Then you, O astray deserters, will surely eat from the tree of Zaqqum, and fill your bellies with it, and drink boiling water on top of it, drinking like thirsty camels" (56:51-55), and the Exalted said: إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَحَقٌّ تَخاصُمُ أَهْلِ النَّارِ — "That is indeed a truth: the feuding of the inmates of the Fire" (38:64). All of this is of the nature of the manifestation of dispositions (الملكات) within them.

The context preceding the verse supports what we have mentioned—that their supplication is not in its true reality—which is His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ فِي النَّارِ لِخَزَنَةِ جَهَنَّمَ ادْعُوا رَبَّكُمْ يُخَفِّفْ عَنَّا يَوْماً مِنَ الْعَذابِ ، قالُوا أَوَلَمْ تَكُ تَأْتِيكُمْ رُسُلُكُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ قالُوا بَلى قالُوا فَادْعُوا وَما دُعاءُ الْكافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ — "Those who are in the Fire will say to the keepers of Hell, ‘Supplicate your Lord to ease the punishment for us [at least] for one day.’ They will say, ‘Did not your apostles bring you manifest proofs?’ They will say, ‘Yes.’ They will say, ‘Then supplicate!’ But the prayer of the faithless only goes wandering" (40:49-50). Their asking the keepers of the Fire to supplicate Allah for them to mitigate the punishment clearly indicates that they are despairing of the response to their own supplications. A supplication made while despairing of a response is not a true supplication or request, as a demand (الطلب) does not attach to that which will never be.

Thirdly, the "forgetting" (النسيان) in His saying: وَتَنْسَوْنَ ما تُشْرِكُونَ — "and you will forget what you used to ascribe as partners [to Him]" — carries its literal meaning (حقيقة معناه) according to what is observed of the human condition when overwhelmed by hardships and calamities (الشدائد والخطوب). In such states, one becomes occupied with oneself and forgets everything else besides Allah, the Glorified. Thus, there is no necessity to adhere to what some have mentioned: that the meaning of His saying: وَتَنْسَوْنَ ما تُشْرِكُونَ is that you turn away from them with the turning away of one who has forgotten something.

However, there is no objection to that interpretation, as it is among the common metaphorical uses (المجازات الشائعة) of the word "forgetting." Indeed, "forgetting" has been used in the Quran to mean turning away from something and not paying attention to it frequently, as the Exalted said: وَقِيلَ الْيَوْمَ نَنْساكُمْ كَما نَسِيتُمْ لِقاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هذا — "It will be said, ‘Today We will forget you just as you forgot the encounter of this day of yours’" (45:34), among other verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا إِلى أُمَمٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فَأَخَذْناهُمْ بِالْبَأْساءِ وَالضَّرَّاءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَضَرَّعُونَ — "We have already sent [messengers] to nations before you, and We seized them with misery and hardship so that they might entreat [Us]" (6:42). Al-Ba’sa’ (البأساء), al-ba’s (البأس), and al-bu’s (البؤس) all refer to severity and misfortune (الشدة والمكروه), except that al-bu’s is most frequently used in the context of war and the like, while al-ba’s and al-ba’sa’ are used in other contexts such as poverty, drought, famine, and similar conditions. Al-durr (الضر) and al-darra’ (الضراء) refer to an ill state (سوء الحال) regarding the soul (النفس), such as grief or ignorance; or regarding the body (البدن), such as illness or physical deficiency; or regarding other things, such as loss of status or wealth. Perhaps the purpose of combining al-ba’sa’ and al-darra’ is to indicate the occurrence of external hardships (الشدائد), such as drought, floods, and earthquakes, and the resulting ill states that afflict people, such as fear, poverty, and wretchedness.

Al-dara’ah (الضراعة) is humility (المذلة), and al-tadarru’ (التضرع) is to humble oneself (التذلل). What is meant here is humbling oneself before Allah, the Glorified, to remove the calamities of hardship and misfortune that have descended upon them.

In this verse and the three following it, Allah, the Glorified, mentions to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) His sunnah regarding the nations before him when their messengers came to them with manifest proofs (البينات). He would send messengers to them who would remind them of the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — of Allah, the Glorified, and of entreaty and sincere turning (الإنابة) to Him. Then He would afflict them with various types of hardship and trials, seizing them with misery (البأساء) and hardship (الضراء), but only to a degree that did not compel them to entreaty nor force them into supplication and submission. This was so that they might entreat Him by their own good choice, and so their hearts might soften, causing them to turn away from Satanic embellishments and from clinging to outward causes (الأسباب الظاهرية). However, they did not entreat Him; rather, their preoccupation with the fleeting interests of the world hardened their hearts, and Satan (الشيطان) made their deeds seem fair to them, causing them to forget the remembrance of Allah.

When they forgot the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified, He opened for them the gates of all things and poured upon them His diverse blessings in abundance. This continued until, when they rejoiced in the blessings they had and became deluded, considering themselves independent of Allah, He seized them suddenly (بغتة) from where they did not perceive it. Suddenly, they were despairing (مبلسون) of salvation, witnessing the collapse of the causes they relied upon. Thus, the roots of the people who were unjust (الظالمين) were cut off, and all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين).

This sunnah is the sunnah of gradual entrapment (الاستدراج) and divine maneuvering (المكر) which Allah, the Exalted, summarized in His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ — "As for those who deny Our signs, We will draw them on [to ruin] step by step from whence they do not know. And I will grant them a respite; indeed My stratagem is profound" (7:182-183). By reflecting on the preceding explanation of the verse's meaning and contemplating its context, it becomes clear that the verse does not contradict other verses stating that man is created with a primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — oriented toward monotheism, and is compelled by the requirement of his nature and constitution to acknowledge it and turn toward Him when severed from cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية), as the Exalted said: وَإِذا غَشِيَهُمْ مَوْجٌ كَالظُّلَلِ دَعَوُا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ فَلَمَّا نَجَّاهُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ فَمِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ وَما يَجْحَدُ بِآياتِنا إِلَّا كُلُّ خَتَّارٍ كَفُورٍ — "When waves cover them like canopies, they invoke Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him. But when He delivers them to land, some of them are moderate [in their faith], and none denies Our signs except every ungrateful traitor" (31:32).

This is because the verse intends by "misery" (البأساء) and "hardship" (الضراء) only that which does not reach such a level of severity and awe that they become oblivious to every cause and forget every customary means. Evidence for this is His saying in the verse: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَضَرَّعُونَ — "so that they might entreat [Us]" — for la'alla (لعل) is a word of hope (رجاء), and there is no hope in a state of compulsion (الإلجاء) and necessity (الاضطرار). Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — "and Satan made what they used to do seem fair to them" — for its apparent meaning is that they were deluded by that and sought to remove the misery and hardship through the deeds they performed with their own hands and managed with their own schemes to overcome the obstacles of life and the adversities of living. Thus, they became occupied with natural causes (الأسباب الطبيعية) that distracted them from entreating Allah, the Glorified, and seeking refuge in Him. This is like His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَرِحُوا بِما عِنْدَهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ ، فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا قالُوا آمَنَّا بِاللهِ وَحْدَهُ وَكَفَرْنا بِما كُنَّا بِهِ مُشْرِكِينَ — "When their apostles brought them manifest proofs, they exulted in the knowledge they possessed, and they were besieged by what they used to mock. Then, when they sighted Our punishment, they said, ‘We believe in Allah alone, and we disbelieve in what we used to ascribe as partners to Him’" (40:83-84). The first verse—as you see—narrates about them something similar to what the verse we are discussing narrates regarding the turning away from entreaty and being deluded by deeds, while the second verse narrates what other verses narrate regarding monotheism in the state of necessity.

From here, the fallacy of what appears from some commentators becomes evident—that the apparent meaning of the verse is that the previous nations were disdainful of monotheism and turned away from entreaty even in compelling hardships. He said in the commentary (تفسير) of the verse: "Allah swore to His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that He sent messengers before him to nations before his community, and they were more deeply rooted in polytheism (الشرك) than his people, and more persistent in injustice (الظلم). For his people call upon Allah alone during severe distress and forget those they took as guardians (الأولياء) and peers (الأنداد), whereas those nations—hardships did not soften their hearts, nor did they rectify what Satan had corrupted of their primordial nature (الفطرة)." [End of quote].

The implication of what he mentioned is that monotheism would not be primordial (فطريا), manifesting upon the removal of distracting illusions and the severance from outward causes, or that it would be possible to nullify the judgment of the primordial nature from its root. Yet the Exalted has said: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation" (30:30). Thus, He informed that the religion of monotheism is primordial and that the primordial nature does not accept change by any changer. He supported this with other verses explicitly stating that man, when severed from causes, inevitably turns to his Lord in supplication, making the religion exclusive to Him.

Furthermore, acknowledging the One God (الإله الواحد) during hardship and severance is something we find within ourselves as a necessary intuition (وجدانا ضروريا), and in this, the primitive man and the man of today do not differ at all.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَوْ لا إِذْ جاءَهُمْ بَأْسُنا تَضَرَّعُوا وَلكِنْ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ — "Why did they not entreat when Our punishment came to them? But their hearts had hardened..." (6:43). Lawla (فَلَوْ لا) is for exhortation (التحضيض) or negation (النفي). In any case, it serves the purpose of negation in this context, as evidenced by His saying: وَلكِنْ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ — "But their hearts had hardened." Hardness of the heart (قسوة القلب) is the opposite of its softness; it is when a person is not affected by witnessing what normally affects one, or by hearing speech whose nature is to have an effect.

The meaning is: they did not entreat when the punishment came, nor did they return to their Lord in humility; rather, their souls refused to be affected by it, and they were distracted by their Satanic deeds that diverted them from the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified, and they clung to the outward causes which they viewed as independent (استقلالها) in rectifying their affairs.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا نَسُوا ما ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ فَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ أَبْوابَ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "So when they forgot what they had been reminded of, We opened for them the gates of all things..." (6:44). What is meant by "opening the gates of all things" is giving them every worldly blessing (نعمة) that people compete for to enjoy the advantages of life—wealth, children, physical health, prosperity, fertility, security, longevity, and power. All of this was provided in abundance without stinting or withholding. Just as when a treasury of wealth is opened to give someone a specific and measured amount, its door is opened, the intended amount is given, and then it is closed; but if it is intended to give without measure, its door is opened and not closed in the face of the one seeking it. In short, it is a metaphor (كناية) for giving them various types of blessings without measure, as the context supports.

Furthermore, the "opening of the gate" naturally suits good things and blessings, whereas evil things and punishments are realized through withholding (المنع) and are suited by the "closing of the gate," as hinted at by His saying, the Exalted: ما يَفْتَحِ اللهُ لِلنَّاسِ مِنْ رَحْمَةٍ فَلا مُمْسِكَ لَها وَما يُمْسِكْ فَلا مُرْسِلَ لَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ — "Whatever mercy Allah unfolds for the people, no one can withhold it; and whatever He withholds, no one can release it after Him" (35:2).

Mublisun (مبلسون) is from ablasa (أبلس). Al-Raghib said: Al-iblas (الإبلاس) is the grief that occurs from intense despair (اليأس)—until he said—and since the one who is mublis often remains silent and forgets what concerns him, it is said: "So-and-so ablasa" when he falls silent and when his argument is cut off. [End of quote]. According to this meaning, which is suitable for His saying: فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْلِسُونَ, it means they were silenced and their arguments were cut off.

The meaning of the verse is that when they forgot what they had been reminded of or turned away from it, We gave them every blessing as a gradual entrapment (استدراجا) until, when the blessings were complete for them and they rejoiced in what they were given, We seized them suddenly, and their breaths were extinguished, and they had no argument, for they deserved that.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقُطِعَ دابِرُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَالْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "Thus the roots of the people who were unjust were cut off, and all praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds" (6:45). The dubur (دبر) of a thing is the opposite of its qubul (قبل); they are the two parts: the front and the back of a thing. Therefore, they are used as metaphors for specific organs. Sometimes they are used more broadly to refer to what follows the front or back part, thus becoming separate from the thing. Verbs have been derived from them according to the context, such as aqbala (أقبل) — to approach — and adbara (أدبر) — to retreat. From this is the derivation of dabir (دابر), meaning that which falls behind a thing and follows it from the rear. It is said: "the dabir yesterday," meaning the day that fell behind today, just as it is said: "the coming year." Dabir in this sense is applied to the trace (أثر) of a thing, like the dabir of a person referring to his descendants and all his traces. Thus, His saying: فَقُطِعَ دابِرُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا means that destruction encompassed them such that neither an individual nor a trace remained of them, or that He annihilated them all so that none of them escaped, as the Exalted said: فَهَلْ تَرى لَهُمْ مِنْ باقِيَةٍ — "Do you see any remnant of them?" (69:8).

The explicit noun was placed in the position of the pronoun in His saying: دابِرُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا — "the roots of the people who were unjust" — instead of saying "their roots" (دابرهم), to indicate the cause of the judgment, which is the injustice (الظلم) that annihilated their assembly and cut off their roots. This also paves the way for the mention of His saying: وَالْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "and all praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds."

From this verse, by including their description as unjust and His praise for His Lordship (ربوبيته), the indication is established that the blame and evil in all the punishment of extirpation that befell them returns to them, because they were the people who were unjust. And that nothing returns to Him, the Exalted, except beautiful praise, because He did not act in the management of their affairs except as required by profound wisdom (الحكمة البالغة), and He did not lead them on the path they ended upon except to what they chose for themselves through their own evil choice. Thus, it is established that ignominy and evil are upon the disbelievers, and that praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَخَذَ اللهُ سَمْعَكُمْ وَأَبْصارَكُمْ — "Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah takes away your hearing and your sight..." (6:46) to the end of the verse. Taking away the hearing and sight is the stripping of the powers of hearing and seeing, which is deafness and blindness. The "sealing of the hearts" (الختم على القلوب) is the closing of their gates so firmly that nothing from the outside enters them, such that they might reflect on their affairs and distinguish the necessary deeds from others, and the beneficial good from the harmful evil, while preserving the original faculty, which is the capacity for intellection (التعقل); otherwise, it would be insanity and mental disorder.

Since these polytheists do not hear the true word regarding Allah, the Glorified, nor do they see His signs indicating that He is One without partner, their hearts have become such that nothing from the inputs of hearing and sight enters them to recognize truth from falsehood. Thus, He established the argument through this to invalidate their doctrine regarding the matter of the Deity, the Exalted, and His oneness.

The summary of it is that the claim of the existence of partners for Allah necessitates the claim of its own invalidity. This is because the claim of partners is to establish intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — which is to intercede and mediate in bringing benefits and repelling harms. Since the partners are intercessors by assumption, Allah, the Glorified, has the right to do in His kingdom whatever He wishes without encountering any preventer to prevent Him or any opponent to oppose Him. If Allah were to strip you of your hearing and your sight and seal your hearts, He would do so, and none of your partners would oppose Him, because they are mediating intercessors, not opposing rivals. And if He did that and stripped what He stripped, none of them would be able to bring it back to you, because they are mediating intercessors, not sources of creation and origination (الإيجاد).

If they are unable to bring benefit or remove harm in the face of events, then what is the meaning of their divinity? For the Deity (الإله) is none other than He who brings into existence and brings to naught, and disposes of the cosmos as He wishes. The human primordial nature was only compelled to acknowledge that the world has a Deity for the sake of attaining the source of the events of good and evil witnessed in existence. If something neither harms nor benefits in the face of events, then calling it a Deity is nothing but vain speech.

It is not for a person of sound intellect and discernment to permit that a stone, wooden, or metal image made by human hands and fashioned by human thought could be the Creator of the world or its disposer through origination and annihilation. Likewise, that the lord of the idol could be a worshipped lord who originated the world without a prior model, while admitting that he is a created servant (عبد مربوب).

The argument returns, in another formulation, to the fact that the meaning of divinity (الألوهية) refuses to be true of a partner in the sense of a mediating intercessor. For being the principle of fashioning and origination necessitates independence in disposal (التصرف) and exclusivity in deserving the submission of the fashioned creature. The assumed mediator, if it has independence in action, would be a source and a principle, not a mediator and an intercessor; and if it has no share of independence, it would be a tool and an instrument that has a principle and a Deity.

Therefore, the cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية), whatever they are assumed to be, have no meaning other than that of the instrument and the tool, such as the causality of eating for satiety, drinking for quenching thirst, parents for the child, the pen for writing, and walking for covering distance, and so on.

His saying: انْظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ الْآياتِ ثُمَّ هُمْ يَصْدِفُونَ — "Look, how We dispense the signs in various ways, yet they turn away" (6:46). The "dispensing" (تصريف) of the signs is their transformation into forms suitable for their understanding. Al-suduf (الصدوف) is turning away (الإعراض); it is said: "he sadafa" when he deviates from something.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُ اللهِ بَغْتَةً أَوْ جَهْرَةً — "Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah’s punishment comes to you suddenly or openly..." (6:47) to the end of the verse. Al-jahrah (الجهرة) is complete manifestation that does not accept doubt; therefore, it is contrasted with al-baghtah (البغتة), which is the coming of something suddenly such that it does not appear to the one it comes upon until after it has arrived and overwhelmed him, leaving him no room for caution.

This is an argument in which it is explained in a general manner that the unjust (الظالمين) are in danger of a punishment from Allah—a punishment that will not bypass them, nor will it err in striking them by striking someone else. Then it explains that they are the unjust because of their defiance (فسقهم) of the divine call and their denial of the signs of Allah, the Exalted.

This is because the meaning of punishment (العذاب) is nothing other than striking the criminal with what grieves and destroys him as a recompense for his crime, and there is no crime except with injustice (ظلم). Thus, if a punishment came to them from Allah, the Glorified, none would be destroyed by it except the unjust. This is what the verse indicates, and then the following two verses explain that they are indeed the unjust.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما نُرْسِلُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ — "We do not send the apostles except as bearers of good news and as warners..." (6:48) to the end of the two verses. He explains through these two verses that they are the unjust, and that none is destroyed by Allah's punishment if it comes to them except because of their injustice.

Therefore, He changed the style of the discourse, directing the explanation to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) so that he would be the one informing about the matter of His punishment, which would be more decisive in cutting off excuses. The first-person plural was used to indicate its issuance from the station of Greatness and Majesty.

The summary of the content is His command, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to establish the argument against them: that if Allah's punishment came to them, none would be destroyed except the unjust among them. Then the Exalted says to His Messenger: "We, who bestow the argument upon you and bring the punishment, inform you that Our sending of the messengers is only for bringing good news and warning. So whoever believes and acts righteously (أصلح), no [fear] shall be upon him; but as for him who denies Our signs, he is the one whom Our punishment will touch because of his defiance (فسقه) and his departure from the manner of servitude (العبودية). So let them look into their own affair: to which of the two groups do they belong?"

The discussion regarding the meaning of faith (الإيمان), righteous deeds (الإصلاح), defiance (الفسق), and the meaning of the negation of fear and grief from the believers has already been completed in previous sections.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ — "Say, ‘I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say to you that I am an angel’" (6:50). Perhaps what is meant by "the treasuries of Allah" (خزائِنُ اللهِ) is what He mentioned in His saying: قُلْ لَوْ أَنْتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزائِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّي إِذاً لَأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ الْإِنْفاقِ — "Say, ‘If you possessed the treasuries of my Lord’s mercy, you would surely withhold them for fear of spending’" (17:100). These treasuries of mercy are those whose effect is revealed by His saying, the Exalted: ما يَفْتَحِ اللهُ لِلنَّاسِ مِنْ رَحْمَةٍ فَلا مُمْسِكَ لَها — "Whatever mercy Allah unfolds for the people, no one can withhold it" (35:2). They are the effusion of existence (فائضة الوجود) that flows from Him, the Exalted, upon things—their existence and the effects of their existence. His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is" (36:82) — has explained that the source of this flowing effect is His word, which is the word "Be" (كُنْ) issued from the station of Greatness and Majesty. This is what is reported in another phrasing in His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ — "There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21).

Thus, what is meant by "the treasuries of Allah" is the station from which whatever is willed of a thing is given by its issuance, without being exhausted by the giving of existence, nor being incapacitated by bestowal and generosity. This is something exclusive to Allah, the Glorified. As for anyone else, whoever and whatever they may be, they are limited, and what they possess is measured; if they bestow something from it, it decreases by the amount bestowed. One who is of this description is unable to enrich every poor person, satisfy every seeker, or answer every question.

As for His saying: وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ — "nor do I know the Unseen" — what is intended by "knowledge" is independence in it without being taught by revelation (الوحي). This is because He, the Exalted, establishes revelation at the end of the verse by His saying: إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَ — "I only follow what is revealed to me." He has explained in various places of His speech that some of what He reveals to His messengers is of the Unseen (الغيب), such as His saying, the Exalted: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ — "[He is] the Knower of the Unseen, and He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of" (72:26-27); and His saying after narrating the story of Joseph: ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ وَما كُنْتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ — "That is from the accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you, and you were not with them when they conspired and when they were plotting" (12:102); and His saying in the story of Mary: ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ وَما كُنْتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلامَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ وَما كُنْتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ — "These are from the accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you, for you were not with them when they were casting lots [to see] which of them would take charge of Mary, nor were you with them when they were contending" (3:44); and His saying after the story of Noah: تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيها إِلَيْكَ ما كُنْتَ تَعْلَمُها أَنْتَ وَلا قَوْمُكَ مِنْ قَبْلِ هذا — "These are from the accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you; neither you nor your people knew them before this" (11:49).

Thus, the purpose of negating the knowledge of the Unseen is to negate that he is equipped in his existence by nature with that which would leave nothing hidden from him—things which humans, according to custom, have no way of knowing, such as hidden matters, whatever they may be.

As for His saying: وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ — "nor do I say to you that I am an angel" — it is a metaphor for negating the characteristics of angelhood, such as being beyond the needs of material life like eating, drinking, marriage, and what follows them. He expressed this in other places by His saying: قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ — "Say, ‘I am only a mortal like you, [into whom] it is revealed’" (18:110). He expressed it here by negating angelhood rather than affirming mortality to correspond with what they were suggesting to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), such as their saying: ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ وَيَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْواقِ — "What is with this apostle that he eats food and walks in the marketplaces?" (25:7).

From here, it becomes clear that the verse—with its style of negation after negation—is, as it were, looking toward the answer to what they were suggesting to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by asking for miraculous signs and objecting to the actions he performed, which were like the actions of ordinary people. This is as He narrated from them in His saying: وَقالُوا ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ وَيَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْواقِ لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيراً ، أَوْ يُلْقى إِلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْها — "And they say, ‘What is with this apostle that he eats food and walks in the marketplaces? Why has not an angel been sent down to him so as to be a warner along with him? Or [why is not] a treasure cast to him, or [why does] he [not] have a garden from which he may eat?’" (25:7-8); and His saying: وَقالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى تَفْجُرَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ يَنْبُوعاً ، أَوْ تَكُونَ لَكَ جَنَّةٌ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَعِنَبٍ فَتُفَجِّرَ الْأَنْهارَ خِلالَها تَفْجِيراً ، أَوْ تُسْقِطَ السَّماءَ كَما زَعَمْتَ عَلَيْنا كِسَفاً أَوْ تَأْتِيَ بِاللهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةِ قَبِيلاً ، أَوْ يَكُونَ لَكَ بَيْتٌ مِنْ زُخْرُفٍ — "And they say, ‘We will not believe you until you make a spring gush forth for us from the ground, or until you have a garden of date palms and vines and you make rivers gush through them, or until you make the sky fall upon us in pieces as you claim, or you bring Allah and the angels face to face [with us], or you have a house of gold...’" — until He said — قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولاً — "Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human apostle?’" (17:90-93); and His saying: فَسَيُنْغِضُونَ إِلَيْكَ رُؤُسَهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هُوَ — "Then they will shake their heads at you and say, ‘When will it be?’" (17:51); and His saying: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها — "They ask you about the Hour, when will it set in?" (7:187).

So the meaning of His saying: قُلْ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ — "Say, ‘I do not say to you’" — etc., is: Say, I have not claimed in what I call you to and convey to you anything beyond my state as an ordinary human, such that you might rebuke me by holding me to what you suggest of me. I did not claim that I possess the treasuries of divinity such that you suggest I make rivers gush forth or create a garden or a house of gold. Nor did I claim that I know the Unseen such that I answer you regarding everything that is hidden under the veils of the Unseen, such as the onset of the Hour. Nor did I claim that I am an angel such that you find fault with me and invalidate my word because of my eating food and walking in the marketplaces for livelihood.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَ — "I only follow what is revealed to me" (6:50) — is an explanation of what he truly claims after rejecting the claims they accused him of on account of his claim to be a messenger from Allah to them. That is: the meaning of my saying "I am a messenger of Allah to you" is not that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor that I know the Unseen, nor that I am an angel; rather, it is that Allah reveals to me what He reveals.

He did not state it in the form of a claim, but said: إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ — "I only follow" — etc., to indicate that he is commanded to convey what is revealed to him, having nothing but to follow that. It is as if when he said: "I do not say to you this, nor that, nor that," it was said to him: "If it is so, and you are a mortal like us and as incapable as any of us, you have no merit over us, so what do you want from us?" So he said: "I only follow what is revealed to me—to bring you good news and to warn you, and to call you to the religion of monotheism."

The evidence for this meaning is His saying after that: قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ أَفَلا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ — "Say, ‘Are the blind and the seeing equal? Will you not then reflect?’" (6:50). For its implication, according to what the context provides, is: "Even if I am equal to you in mortality and incapacity, that does not prevent me from calling you to follow me. For my Lord has placed me upon insight (بصيرة) through what He revealed to me, unlike you. So I and you are like the seeing and the blind, and they are not equal in judgment even if they are equal in humanity. Indeed, reflection upon their state guides a person to the judgment that the blind must follow the seeing, and the ignorant must follow the learned."

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْذِرْ بِهِ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ أَنْ يُحْشَرُوا إِلى رَبِّهِمْ — "And warn by it those who fear being mustered toward their Lord..." (6:51) to the end of the verse. The pronoun in "by it" (بِهِ) refers to the Quran, as indicated by His saying in the previous verse: إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَ — "I only follow what is revealed to me." His saying: لَيْسَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ وَلِيٌّ وَلا شَفِيعٌ — "they shall have no guardian nor intercessor besides Him" — is a circumstantial clause (حال), and the governing agent (العامل) in it is "they fear" (يخافون) or "they are mustered" (يحشرون).

What is meant by "fear" (الخوف) is its well-known meaning, not "knowledge" or what is in its sense, as there is no evidence for that according to the apparent meaning understood from the context. The command to warn specifically those who fear being mustered toward their Lord does not contradict the universality of the warning to them and others, as indicated by His saying in the previous verses: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ — "and this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you by it and whomever it may reach" (6:19). Rather, since the fear of being mustered toward Allah helps their souls to accept and brings the call closer to their understanding, the specification of the command to warn them and this description of them served to emphasize their call and to urge him not to be lenient in their affair nor to treat them like others. Instead, he should grant them extra care in their call because their position is closer to the truth and their faith is more hopeful. Thus, the verse, in conjunction with other verses commanding general warning, conveys the meaning: "Warn the people generally, and especially those who fear being mustered toward their Lord."

His saying: لَيْسَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ وَلِيٌّ وَلا شَفِيعٌ — "they shall have no guardian nor intercessor besides Him" — is an absolute negation of the guardianship (ولاية) and intercession (شفاعة) of anyone other than Allah. Thus, it is restricted by other restricting verses, such as His saying: مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ — "Who is it that may intercede with Him except with His permission?" (2:255); and His saying: وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى — "and they do not intercede except for him whom He approves" (21:28); and His saying: وَلا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنْ شَهدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ — "Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and who know [it]" (43:86).

The reason no exception was made in the verse is that the discourse is directed at the idolaters who used to claim the guardianship and intercession of the idols. They did not claim that by permission or appointment; for guardianship and intercession by permission requires knowledge of it, and knowledge requires revelation and prophethood, and they did not believe in prophethood. As for the guardianship and intercession they affirmed, it was as if it were a matter prepared for their guardians and partners by necessity of their nature, not by permission from Allah—as if the powerful beings of existence among the creation have a type of disposal (تصرف) over its weak beings by nature, even if Allah, the Glorified, did not permit it. Or if you wish, you could say: its implication is that their origination was a compulsory permission for disposal in what is below them.

In short, it was said: "they shall have no guardian nor intercessor besides Him," and it was not said: "except by His permission," because the polytheists only said that the idols are guardians and intercessors without restriction. Thus, the negation of what they mentioned of the guardian and intercessor besides Allah was in correspondence with the negation of their affirmation. As for the exception, although it is correct as it occurred in various places of His speech, the purpose here does not relate to it.

It has become clear from what preceded that the verse, in its apparent absolute sense, commands the warning of everyone who is not devoid of a sense of fear of the Mustering (الحشر) in his heart when reminded by the signs of Allah—whether he is among those who believe in the Mustering, like the believers from the People of the Book, or among those who do not believe in it, like the idolaters and others, but considers it possible, so fear overwhelms his soul due to the possibility or the preponderance of probability (المظنة). For the fear of something is realized by the mere possibility of its existence, even if one is not certain of its occurrence.

The views of the commentators have differed regarding the verse. Some say: the verse was revealed concerning the believers who affirm the Mustering, and that they are the ones intended in the following verse by His saying: وَلا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِ — "And do not drive away those who invoke their Lord morning and evening." Others say: it was revealed concerning a group of idolaters who allowed for the possibility of the Mustering after death, even if the existence of one who held this view among the polytheists of Mecca or the Arabs on the day the Surah (السورة) — a chapter of the Quran — was revealed is not established, despite the discourses of the Surah being directed toward the polytheists of Quraysh or the Arabs according to the context. Others say: what is meant by them is everyone who acknowledges the Mustering, whether Muslim or from the People of the Book; these acknowledgers were specifically commanded to be warned, even though the obligation of warning is general to all creation, because the argument is more binding upon them due to their acknowledgment of the Return (المعاد).

However, the verse did not take in their description anything except the fear of the Mustering. Fear of a thing does not depend on knowledge of its realization nor acknowledgment of its existence; rather, doubt (الشك) — the equal probability of two sides — and preponderance of probability (المظنة) — the favored perception with its various levels — are compatible with fear, just like knowledge, and this is evident.

Thus, the verse only urges the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to warn everyone in whose countenance he sees the signs of fear, from whichever group they may be. This is because the foundation of the religious call is based on the Mustering and the establishment of accountability for evil and good deeds and the recompense for them. The minimum that is hoped for from the effect of the religious call in an individual is that he considers it possible and thus fears it. Whenever the probability of its occurrence increases, the fear increases and the effect strengthens until it becomes clothed in certainty (اليقين) and the possibility of the opposite is entirely negated; there, the complete effect is achieved.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ — "And do not drive away those who invoke their Lord morning and evening, desiring His face..." (6:52) to the end of the verse. The apparent context, supported by what is in the following verse: وَكَذلِكَ فَتَنَّا بَعْضَهُمْ بِبَعْضٍ — "Thus do We test them by one another" — etc., is that the polytheists from his people (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) suggested to him that he drive away from himself the weak ones who believed in him, so Allah, the Exalted, forbade him from that in this verse.

This action of theirs is similar to what the arrogant ones from other nations suggested to their messengers: that they drive away from themselves the weak and the poor among the believers out of arrogance and a desire for self-exaltation. Allah, the Exalted, has narrated that from the people of Noah in what He narrated of his (peace be upon him) argument—an argument that resembles what is in these verses.
