The Exalted said: فَقالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا وَما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا بادِيَ الرَّأْيِ وَما نَرى لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا مِنْ فَضْلٍ بَلْ نَظُنُّكُمْ كاذِبِينَ. قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَآتانِي رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِهِ فَعُمِّيَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنُلْزِمُكُمُوها وَأَنْتُمْ لَها كارِهُونَ — "The elite of the faithless from among his people said, ‘We do not see you to be anything but a human being like ourselves, and we do not see anyone following you except the lowest of us, [who are] simple-minded, nor do we see that you have any advantage over us; rather we think you are liars.’ He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and He has granted me a mercy from Himself, and it should be obscured for you, shall we force it upon you while you are averse to it?’" — until He said — وَما أَنَا بِطارِدِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا رَبِّهِمْ — "And I will not drive away those who have faith; they will indeed encounter their Lord" — until He said — وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ إِنِّي مَلكٌ وَلا أَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ تَزْدَرِي أَعْيُنُكُمْ لَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ خَيْراً اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ إِنِّي إِذاً لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say that I am an angel, nor do I say of those whom your eyes despise that Allah will never grant them any good—Allah knows best what is in their souls—[for] then I would indeed be among the wrongdoers" (11:27-31).

The correspondence between these verses and the verses we are discussing requires that what is meant by "those who invoke their Lord morning and evening, desiring His face" be the believers. Their invocation morning and evening—which is their prayer or their general supplication to their Lord—was mentioned to indicate their connection to their Lord, which is not interfered with by anything other than Him, the Exalted, and to clarify what He will mention in His saying: أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِالشَّاكِرِينَ — "Does not Allah know best the grateful?" (6:53).

His saying: يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ — "desiring His face" — meaning the face of Allah. Al-Raghib said in his Mufradat: The origin of "face" (الوجه) is the organ. He said: فَاغْسِلُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ — "wash your faces and your hands"; وَتَغْشى وُجُوهَهُمُ النَّارُ — "and the Fire will envelop their faces." Since the face is the first thing that meets you and the most noble part of the exterior of the body, it was used for the front of everything and for its most noble part and its beginning; thus it is said: "the face of such-and-hers" and "the face of the day." Sometimes the essence (الذات) is expressed by "face" in the word of Allah: وَيَبْقى وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو الْجَلالِ وَالْإِكْرامِ — "Yet there shall remain the Face of your Lord, majestic and munificent." It was said: His essence; and it was said: He meant by "face" here the turning toward Allah with righteous deeds.

And He said: فَأَيْنَما تُوَلُّوا فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ اللهِ — "so whichever way you turn, there is the face of Allah"; كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ — "Everything is to perish except His Face"; يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللهِ — "desiring the face of Allah"; إِنَّما نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللهِ — "We feed you only for the sake of Allah’s face." It was said: The "face" in all of this is His essence, meaning that everything is to perish except Him, and likewise in its sister verses. It was narrated that this was mentioned to Abu Abdillah ibn al-Rida, and he said: "Glory be to Allah! They have said a momentous thing. He only meant the 'face' through which He is approached, and its meaning is: every deed of the servants is perishing and void except what was intended for Allah." Upon this are the other verses, and upon this is His saying: يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ, يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللهِ. [End of quote].

I say: As for the linguistic transition from "face" in the sense of the organ to everything by which a thing meets another as an extension (توسعا), there is no doubt about it according to the well-known evolution of words in their meanings. However, precise scrutiny does not permit the intention of the essence (الذات) from "face." For a thing, whatever it may be, only meets another through something of its own outward aspects—its attributes and names—and these are what knowledge (المعرفة) attaches to. For we only know what we know through one of its attributes or one of its names, then we infer from that its essence, without our touching its essence directly.

For we only attain knowledge of things first through the tools of sense, which only perceive attributes such as shapes, outlines, qualities, and so on, without our perceiving a substantial essence (ذاتا جوهرية); then we infer from that that they have substantial essences which sustain their accidents and attributes, since they need that which supports them and preserves them. In reality, our saying "the essence of Zayd," for example, means the thing whose relation to the attributes and properties of Zayd is like our relation to our own attributes and properties. Thus, the perception of essences through thought is always by a kind of analogy and relation.

If it is not possible to perceive quidditative essences (الذوات الماهوية) with a complete conceptual perception except through their attributes and traces by a kind of analogy and relation, then the matter regarding Allah, the Glorified—where there is no limit to His essence and no end to His existence—is even clearer and more evident. Knowledge does not fall upon anything except with the delimitation of what it possesses; thus, there is no hope for an epistemic encompassment (الإحاطة العلمية) of Him, the Exalted. He said: وَعَنَتِ الْوُجُوهُ لِلْحَيِّ الْقَيُّومِ — "All faces shall be humbled before the Living One, the All-sustainer" (20:111); and He said: وَلا يُحيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْماً — "and they do not comprehend Him in [their] knowledge" (20:110).

However, since the "face" of a thing is that by which it meets another, the "direction" (الجهة) in the sense of the "side"—meaning that to which an indication points—became a "face." For it is, in relation to the thing that limits the indication, like the face in relation to the human: he meets others through it. By this consideration, righteous deeds become a "face" for Allah, just as evil deeds are a "face" for Satan. This is part of what can apply to verses like His saying: يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللهِ and His saying: إِنَّما نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللهِ, and so on. Likewise, the attributes by which Allah, the Glorified, meets His creation—such as mercy, creation, provision, guidance, and similar active attributes (الصفات الفعلية)—rather, even the essential attributes (الصفات الذاتية) by which we know Him, the Exalted, with a type of knowledge, such as life, knowledge, and power—all of that is His "face" (وجهه) by which He meets His creation and toward which one turns from its direction. This is suggested by some indications or the explicit meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَيَبْقى وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو الْجَلالِ وَالْإِكْرامِ — "Yet there shall remain the Face of your Lord, majestic and munificent" (55:27). For the apparent meaning of the verse is that His saying: ذُو الْجلالِ وَالْإِكْرامِ is an attribute of the "Face," not of the "Lord." Understand this well.

If it is correct that His side (ناحيته) is His direction and His face, then it is correct, in general, that everything attributed to Him, the Exalted, with a type of relation of proximity—such as His names and attributes, His religion, and righteous deeds—and likewise everyone who dwells in the courtyard of His proximity—such as the prophets, the angels, the martyrs, and every forgiven believer—is a "face" for Him, the Exalted.

By this, several points become clear. First: the meaning of His saying, the Glorified: وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ — "and what is with Allah is lasting" (16:96); and His saying: وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ — "and those who are with Him are not too proud to worship Him" (21:19); and His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُ — "Indeed those who are with your Lord are not too proud to worship Him, and they glorify Him" (7:206); and His saying regarding those killed in the way of Allah: بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ — "rather they are living with their Lord, provided for" (3:169); and His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ — "There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us" (15:21). These verses, when the first verse is joined to them, indicate that all these matters are lasting by His lasting, the Exalted; there is no way for perishing or ruin to reach them. Then He, the Exalted, said: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ — "Everything is to perish except His Face" (24:88). The restriction in the verse indicates that all of that is a "face" for Allah; in other words, they are all situated in His direction, settled and tranquil in His side and His vicinity.

Second: that which the servant's will attaches to from his Lord is His "face," as in His saying: يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ وَرِضْواناً — "seeking their Lord’s grace and pleasure" (5:2); and His saying: ابْتِغاءَ رَحْمَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "seeking a mercy from your Lord" (17:28); and His saying: وَابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوَسِيلَةَ — "and seek the means of recourse to Him" (5:35). All of that is His "face," the Exalted, because His active attributes—such as mercy, pleasure, grace, and the like—are of His "face," and likewise His path is of His "face," as preceded. And the Exalted said: إِلَّا ابْتِغاءَ وَجْهِ اللهِ — "except to seek Allah’s face" (2:272).

His saying: ما عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَما مِنْ حِسابِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "You are not accountable for them in any way, nor are they accountable for you in any way" (6:52). Al-hisab (الحساب) is the use of numbers through addition, subtraction, and the like. Since the scrutiny of deeds and their estimation for the fulfillment of reward or the taking of a result and the like does not usually occur without the use of numbers through addition or subtraction, this was called the "account" (حساب) of deeds.

Since the accounting of deeds is for the fulfillment of recompense, and recompense is only from Allah, the Glorified, the account is upon Allah—meaning in His charge and His sufficiency—as He said: إِنْ حِسابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلى رَبِّي — "Their reckoning is only with my Lord" (26:113); and He said: ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا حِسابَهُمْ — "then indeed their reckoning will be with Us" (88:26). He reversed it in His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَسِيباً — "Indeed Allah takes account of all things" (4:86), to indicate His authority, the Exalted, and His dominance over all things.

Accordingly, the meaning of negating that their account is upon him or his account is upon them is the negation that he is the one who accounts for their deeds to recompense them—such that if he did not approve of their affair and disliked their proximity, he would drive them away from himself—or that they are the ones who account for his deeds—such that if he feared their scrutiny or an evil recompense, or disliked them out of arrogance and self-exaltation over them, he would drive them away. Thus, each of the two sentences: "You are not accountable..." etc., and "nor are they accountable..." etc., is intended in the discourse independently.

It may be possible to infer from His saying, ما عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ "You are not accountable for them in any way," a negation that their account—meaning their deeds that are to be accounted for—should be borne by him such that he would find it burdensome. This is based on the notion that an action carries a weight upon the one who performs it or the one who bears its responsibility. Thus, the meaning is: "None of the weight of their deeds is upon you." Accordingly, the follow-up with His saying, وَما مِنْ حِسابِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ "nor are they accountable for you in any way"—though the speech would have been complete without it—is merely to complete all sides of the possibility and to emphasize the correspondence of the discourse. It is also possible to say that the combination of the two sentences—namely His saying: ما عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَما مِنْ حِسابِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ—is an allusion (كناية) to the negation of any connection between the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and them in terms of accountability.

It has also been said that "accountability" (الحساب) here refers to the accounting of sustenance (حساب الرزق) rather than the accounting of deeds. The meaning would be: "You are not responsible for the accounting of their sustenance; rather, Allah sustains them and the accounting of their sustenance is His responsibility." His saying, وَما مِنْ حِسابِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ etc., was brought as an emphasis for the correspondence of the discourse, as mentioned in the previous view. Although both views can be justified in some way, the first view is the correct one.

His saying, فَتَطْرُدَهُمْ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ "lest you should drive them away and so become one of the wrongdoers": Joining the group of the wrongdoers (الظالمين) is a consequence of driving them away—that is, driving away those who call upon their Lord. The natural order of the discourse would require that His saying, فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ, be a consequence of His saying at the beginning of the verse: وَلا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ "Do not drive away those..." However, because the speech became lengthy due to the sentences interspersed between the antecedent and the consequent, the word "driving away" (الطرد) was repeated a second time in the form of a consequence so that His saying, فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ, could be connected to it directly, thereby removing any ambiguity.

Thus, the objection that the discourse involves making a thing a consequence of itself does not apply. The summary of the verse is not: "Do not drive away those who call upon their Lord, lest you drive them away." Rather, the repetition of "driving away" was to link the consequence—namely His saying, فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ—to its origin, as you have seen.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ فَتَنَّا بَعْضَهُمْ بِبَعْضٍ لِيَقُولُوا "Thus do We test some of them by others, that they may say..." until the end of the verse. Trial (الفتنة) is examination (الامتحان). The context indicates that the question in His saying, أَهؤُلاءِ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ بَيْنِنا "Is it these whom Allah has favoured from among us?" is for mockery and derision. It is well known that they only mock those whose affairs they deem insignificant and whose social standing they despise. This was only due to their poverty, misery, and low status in the eyes of the powerful and the arrogant among them.

So Allah, the Glorified, informs His Prophet that this disparity and difference is merely a divine trial (محنة إلهية) by which people are tested to distinguish the disbelievers from the grateful (الشاكرين). Thus, the people of disbelief and arrogance say regarding the poor believers: أَهؤُلاءِ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ بَيْنِنا. This is because social norms among people are often characterized by the nobility or baseness of those who practice them; likewise, an action is weighed by the social weight of its doer. Thus, the path followed by the poor, the lowly, and the slaves is despised by the rich and the powerful. An action performed by a destitute person, or a word spoken by a slave or a humiliated captive, is given no weight by those of wealth and power.

Therefore, the poor, the laborers, and the slaves' adherence to the religion, and the Prophet's concern for them and his drawing them close to himself, served as evidence for the arrogant tyrants of society for the insignificance of the religion—that it was beneath the notice of the noble and the powerful.

His saying, the Exalted, أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِالشَّاكِرِينَ "Does not Allah know best the grateful?" is a response to their mockery based on improbability in their saying: أَهؤُلاءِ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ بَيْنِنا. The gist of it is that these people are grateful to Allah while the others are not; therefore, He brought these forward for His favor and left the others behind. The Glorified alluded to this by saying that Allah knows best those who are grateful for His blessing—meaning that they are indeed grateful. It is an accepted principle that the Bestower only bestows and grants favors upon those who show gratitude for His blessing. Allah has called monotheism (التوحيد) and the negation of partners for Him "gratitude" (شكراً) in His saying, narrating the words of Yusuf (peace be upon him): ما كانَ لَنا أَنْ نُشْرِكَ بِاللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ ذلِكَ مِنْ فَضْلِ اللهِ عَلَيْنا وَعَلَى النَّاسِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَشْكُرُونَ "It is not for us to ascribe any partner to Allah. That is by Allah’s grace upon us and upon mankind, but most people do not give thanks." (Yusuf: 38).

The verse explains that out of their ignorance, they base honor and dignity on precedence in the vanities of the world—wealth, children, and status—whereas these have no value or honor with Allah. Rather, the matter revolves around the quality of gratitude (الشكر), and true blessing (النعمة بالحقيقة) is the divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا جاءَكَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِنا فَقُلْ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ "When those who afford faith in Our signs come to you, say, ‘Peace be to you!’" until the end of the verse.

The meaning of "peace" (السلام) has been mentioned previously. What is meant by His "prescribing mercy upon Himself" (كتابته الرحمة على نفسه) is making it obligatory upon Himself—meaning the impossibility of His act being detached from the title of mercy. Rectification (الإصلاح) is to clothe oneself with righteousness (الصلاح); it is an intransitive verb here, though in reality it is transitive, its origin being "rectifying the soul" or "rectifying the deed."

The verse is clearly connected to the verse before it. In it, Allah, the Glorified—after forbidding His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from driving the believers away—commands him to be kind to them, to greet them with peace, and to give glad tidings to those among them who repent for an evil deed with a sincere repentance (توبة نصوحا) of Allah's forgiveness and mercy. By this, their souls are comforted and the agitation of their hearts is stilled.

From this, several points become clear: First: The verse—which is one of the verses of repentance—only addresses repentance from sins and evil deeds, not from disbelief and polytheism, as evidenced by His saying: مَنْ عَمِلَ مِنْكُمْ "Whoever among you [the believers in the signs of Allah] commits..."

Second: What is meant by "ignorance" (الجهالة) is that which stands in contrast to denial (الجحود) and stubbornness (العناد), both of which stem from intentionality (التعمد) as opposed to ignorance. For one who calls upon his Lord morning and evening, seeking His countenance, and is a believer in the signs of Allah, does not disobey Him out of arrogance or seeking superiority over Him, but rather due to an ignorance that overwhelmed him by following a whim in desire or anger.

Third: The qualification of His saying تابَ "repents" with His saying وَأَصْلَحَ "and acts righteously" is to indicate the realization of repentance in its true essence. For a true return to Allah, the Glorified, and seeking refuge in His presence, cannot coexist—given the purity of His station—with the defilement of the sin from which the returning penitent has purified himself. Repentance is not merely saying "I repent to Allah"—a statement that does not transcend the tongue to the heart. The Exalted has said: وَإِنْ تُبْدُوا ما فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحاسِبْكُمْ بِهِ اللهُ "Whether you disclose what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it." (Al-Baqarah: 284).

Fourth: His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية), such as the All-Forgiving (الغفور) and the Most Merciful (الرحيم), can be truly qualified by time. For although Allah, the Glorified, has prescribed mercy upon Himself, it does not manifest nor exert its effect except when some of His servants commit evil out of ignorance and then repent thereafter and rectify themselves.

What pertains to this station has already been discussed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ "Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for..." until the end of the two verses (An-Nisa: 17) in the fourth volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ وَلِتَسْتَبِينَ سَبِيلُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ "Thus do We elaborate the signs, so that the way of the guilty may be exposed." The "elaboration of the signs" (تفصيل الآيات), by the context of the station, is the explanation of divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) and its extraction from ambiguity and condensation. In His saying, وَلِتَسْتَبِينَ سَبِيلُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ, the letter lam (لـ) denotes purpose (للغاية). It is coordinated with an omitted phrase that was left unmentioned to magnify and exalt its matter—a common occurrence in His speech, such as His saying: وَتِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُداوِلُها بَيْنَ النَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "These are the days that We alternate among the people, and so that Allah may know those who believe" (Al-Imran: 140), and His saying: وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude." (Al-An'am: 75).

The meaning is: "Thus do We explain and distinguish the divine gnoses from one another and remove the ambiguity that may occur to them for important purposes, among which is that the way of the guilty (المجرمين) may be exposed, so that those who believe in Our signs may avoid it." Accordingly, what is meant by "the way of the guilty" is the path followed by the criminals in opposition to the signs speaking of the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah, the Glorified, and the true gnoses related to Him—which is the path of denial, stubbornness, turning away from the signs, and ingratitude for blessings.

It has also been said that "the way of the guilty" refers to the way followed against the guilty—which is the sunnah (سنة) of Allah regarding them, such as His cursing them in this world, the descent of punishment upon them, the evil reckoning, and the painful torment in the Hereafter. However, the first meaning is more in accord with the context of the verses narrated in this Surah.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission reaching Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him), he said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, did not take our Prophet until He had completed the religion for him and sent down to him the Qur'an, in which is the clarification of everything—He clarified therein the lawful and the prohibited, the legal punishments (الحدود) and the rulings (الأحكام), and everything that people need in its entirety. And the Mighty and Majestic said: ما فَرَّطْنا فِي الْكِتابِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — 'We have not omitted anything from the Book.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, narrated from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from Kathir ibn 'Ayyash, from Abu al-Jarud, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا صُمٌّ وَبُكْمٌ "Those who deny Our signs are deaf and dumb." He said: "Deaf to guidance, dumb—they do not speak any good. فِي الظُّلُماتِ 'in the darkness'—meaning the darkness of disbelief. مَنْ يَشَأِ اللهُ يُضْلِلْهُ وَمَنْ يَشَأْ يَجْعَلْهُ عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ 'Whomever Allah wishes He leads astray, and whomever He wishes He puts on a straight path.' This is a refutation of the Qadariyyah of this Ummah—Allah will gather them on the Day of Resurrection with the Sabians, the Christians, and the Magians—and they will say: رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ 'Our Lord, we were not polytheists.' Allah says: انْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ 'Look how they lie against themselves, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them.'" He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Behold, for every nation there are Magians, and the Magians of this Ummah are those who say: "There is no decree (قدر)," and they claim that the will (المشية) and the power (القدرة) belong to them and are for them.'"

The author of al-Burhan said when quoting the hadith: "In another manuscript of the Tafsir of 'Ali ibn Ibrahim, the hadith is as follows: 'He said: Behold, for every nation there are Magians, and the Magians of this Ummah are those who say: "There is no decree," and they claim that the will and the power are neither for them nor against them.' In a third manuscript: '...they say: "There is no decree," and they claim that the will and the power are not unto them nor for them.'" End of quote.

I say: The issue of decree (القدر) is one of the matters over which debate occurred in the first century. A group denied the decree—which is that the will of Allah, the Glorified, has some connection to the actions of the servants—and they affirmed independent will and power for the human being in his action, and that he is its creator and independent in it. They were called the Qadariyyah—meaning those who speak about the decree. Both sects have narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said: "The Qadariyyah are the Magians of this Ummah." Its application to them is clear, for they affirm a creator for actions who is the human being, and a creator for other things who is Allah, the Glorified. This is the doctrine of the dualists (الثنوية), who are the Magians, believing in two gods: a creator of good and a creator of evil.

There are other narrations from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and from the Imams of his Household (peace be upon them) that explain the narration in the sense mentioned above, affirming that there is a decree and that Allah has a will in the actions of His servants, as affirmed by the Qur'an.

The Mu'tazilites, who are the deniers of the decree, have interpreted the narration to mean that the "Qadariyyah" are those who affirm the decree, and that they are like the Magians in attributing all good and evil to a creator other than the human being. Some discussion on this has preceded, and its full treatment will come, Allah willing.

From what we have mentioned, it becomes clear that combining the statement "there is no decree" with the statement "the will and power are not for the human being nor unto him" is a combination of two contradictory things. For the denial of the decree necessitates the statement of the human being's independence in will and capability, while the affirmation of the decree necessitates the denial of his independence in will and power.

Accordingly, what occurred in the two manuscripts regarding the combination of their saying "there is no decree" and their saying "the will and power are not for them nor unto them" is nothing but a corruption by some scribes; the meaning became confused for him as he remembered the phrase "no decree" and altered the rest.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by Ahmad, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani in al-Kabir, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Shu'ab, from 'Uqbah ibn 'Amir, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: إذا رأيت الله يعطي العبد في الدنيا ـ وهو مقيم على معاصيه ما يحب ـ فإنما هو استدراج — "When you see Allah giving the servant in this world what he loves while he is persisting in his acts of disobedience, it is only a gradual enticement (استدراج)." Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited: فَلَمَّا نَسُوا ما ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ فَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ أَبْوابَ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "So when they forgot what they had been reminded of, We opened for them the gates of all things..." the verse and the one following it.

In the same source, narrated by Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh from 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit: That the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: إن الله تبارك وتعالى إذا أراد بقوم بقاء أو نماء ـ رزقهم القصد والعفاف ـ وإذا أراد بقوم اقتطاعا ـ فتح لهم أو فتح عليهم باب خيانة ـ حتى إذا فرحوا بما أوتوا أخذناهم بغتة ـ فإذا هم مبلسون فقطع دابر القوم ـ الذين ظلموا والحمد لله رب العالمين — "Indeed, when Allah, Blessed and Exalted, intends for a people survival or growth, He grants them moderation and chastity. And when He intends for a people to be cut off, He opens for them—or opens upon them—the gate of betrayal, until when they rejoice in what they were given, We seize them suddenly, and then they are in despair. Thus the roots of the people who were wrongdoers were cut off; and all praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds."

In the same source, narrated by Ibn al-Mundhir from Ja'far, who said: "Allah revealed to Dawud: 'Fear Me in every state, and be most fearful when blessings are showered upon you, lest I strike you down (لا أصرعك) then and never look upon you again.'"

I say: His saying "lest I strike you down" (لا أصرعك) is a prohibition conveying a warning, and His saying "then and never look..." is in the subjunctive as a consequence of the prohibition.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُ اللهِ بَغْتَةً أَوْ جَهْرَةً "Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah’s punishment come to you suddenly or openly...’" the verse. He said: "It was revealed when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) migrated to Medina and his companions were afflicted by hardship, ailments, and disease. They complained of that to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so Allah sent down: قُلْ 'Say' to them, O Muhammad, أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُ اللهِ بَغْتَةً أَوْ جَهْرَةً هَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الظَّالِمُونَ — meaning: nothing will afflict you except hardship and harm in this world; as for the painful punishment in which there is destruction, it only afflicts the wrongdoing people."

I say: This narration, despite its weakness, contradicts the widely transmitted reports that Surah al-An'am was revealed in Mecca all at once. Furthermore, the content of the verse does not fit the story, and the forced interpretation given to it is far from the order of the Qur'an.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْذِرْ بِهِ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ "And warn by its means those who fear..." the verse. He said: Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "Warn by the Qur'an those who fear reaching their Lord; make them desire what is with Him, for the Qur'an is an intercessor whose intercession is accepted for them."

I say: The apparent meaning of the hadith is that the pronoun in His saying "besides Him" (من دونه) refers to the Qur'an. This is a correct meaning, even if it is not common in the Qur'an for it to be called a "guardian" (ولي), though it is called an "Imam" (إمام).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, narrated by Ahmad, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu'aym in al-Hilyah from 'Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, who said: "The elite of Quraysh passed by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while Suhayb, 'Ammar, Bilal, Khabbab, and similar weak Muslims were with him. They said: 'O Muhammad, are you satisfied with these from among your people? Is it these whom Allah has favored from among us? Are we to be followers of these? Drive them away from you; perhaps if you drive them away, we might follow you.' So the Qur'an was revealed concerning them: وَأَنْذِرْ بِهِ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ أَنْ يُحْشَرُوا إِلى رَبِّهِمْ etc."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Majma' from al-Tha'labi with his chain from 'Abdullah ibn Mas'ud in a summarized form.

In the same source, narrated by Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir from 'Ikrimah, who said: "'Utbah ibn Rabi'ah, Shaybah ibn Rabi'ah, Qarazah ibn 'Abd 'Amr ibn Nawfal, al-Harith ibn 'Amir ibn Nawfal, and Mut'im ibn 'Adi ibn Khiyar ibn Nawfal—among the nobles of the disbelievers from Banu 'Abd Manaf—walked to Abu Talib and said: 'If only your nephew would drive away these slaves from us—for they are our slaves and servants—it would be greater for him in our hearts, we would be more obedient to him, and closer to following and believing him.'

Abu Talib mentioned that to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and 'Umar said: 'If only you did so, O Messenger of Allah, until we see what they intend by their words and what becomes of their matter.' So Allah sent down: وَأَنْذِرْ بِهِ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ أَنْ يُحْشَرُوا إِلى رَبِّهِمْ until His saying أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِالشَّاكِرِينَ.

He said: They were Bilal, 'Ammar ibn Yasir, Salim the client of Abu Hudhayfah, Subh the client of Usayd, and from the allies (الحلفاء) Ibn Mas'ud, al-Miqdad ibn 'Amr, Waqid ibn 'Abdullah al-Hanzali, 'Amr ibn 'Abd 'Amr Dhu al-Shimalayn, Marthad ibn Abi Marthad, and their likes.

And there was revealed concerning the leaders of disbelief from Quraysh, the clients, and the allies: وَكَذلِكَ فَتَنَّا بَعْضَهُمْ بِبَعْضٍ لِيَقُولُوا the verse. When it was revealed, 'Umar came and apologized for his statement, so Allah sent down: وَإِذا جاءَكَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِنا the verse."

In the same source, narrated by Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Majah, Abu Ya'la, Abu Nu'aym in al-Hilyah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il from Khabbab, who said: "Al-Aqra' ibn Habis al-Tamimi and 'Uyaynah ibn Hisn al-Fazari came and found the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sitting with Bilal, Suhayb, 'Ammar, and Khabbab among a group of weak believers. When they saw them around him, they despised them. They came to him and spoke to him privately, saying: 'We would like you to make for us a special gathering by which the Arabs may know our virtue. For the delegations of the Arabs will come to you, and we are ashamed for the Arabs to see us sitting with these slaves. So when we come to you, make them leave us; then when we are finished, you may sit with them if you wish.' He said: 'Yes.' They said: 'Then write for us a document to that effect.' He called for a parchment and called 'Ali to write, while we were sitting in a corner.

Suddenly Gabriel descended with this verse: وَلا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ until His saying فَقُلْ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ كَتَبَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) threw the parchment from his hand, then called us and we came to him while he was saying: 'Peace be to you; your Lord has prescribed mercy upon Himself.' We used to sit with him, and when he wanted to stand, he would stand and leave us. Then Allah sent down: وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ 'And content yourself with those who call upon their Lord morning and evening, seeking His countenance...' the verse. He said: So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would sit with us afterward, and when the time came for him to stand, we would stand and leave him so that he could stand."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Majma' from Salman and Khabbab. Regarding the meaning of these three preceding narrations, there are other reports. Referring back to what was mentioned at the beginning of the Surah regarding the abundance of reports that Surah al-An'am was revealed all at once, and then reflecting on the context of the verses, leaves no doubt that these narrations are of the type we call "application" (تطبيق). This means they found the contents of some verses applicable to certain stories that occurred during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so they considered the story a "reason for revelation" (سبب النزول). This is not in the sense that the verse was revealed alone and specifically for the occurrence of that event and to remove the doubt arising from it, but rather in the sense that the verse removes the doubt arising from that event just as it removes doubts arising from other similar events. This is evidenced by what you see in these three narrations regarding the reason for the revelation of His saying: وَلا تَطْرُدِ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ the verse. For the purpose in them is one, yet the stories are different while being similar. It is as if they came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and suggested that he drive away the weak from him time after time, and each time he had a number of weak believers with him, and the content of the verse reflects these suggestions or some of them.

In this manner, they used to mention the reasons for the revelation of verses in the sense of stories and events that occurred during his time (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) which had some relevance to the contents of the noble verses, without the verse necessarily having a specific view toward that particular story or event. Furthermore, the prevalence of "narration by meaning" (النقل بالمعنى) in the hadiths and the extreme latitude in the method of transmission gave the illusion that the verses were revealed specifically for those particular events as exclusive causes. Therefore, one cannot rely on such narrations regarding the reasons for revelation—especially in a Surah like this which was revealed all at once—beyond the fact that they reveal a type of connection between the verses and the events that took place during his time (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This is especially true considering the prevalence of fabrication and insertion in these narrations, the weakness found in them, and the leniency of the ancients in accepting and transmitting them.

It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Zubayr ibn Bakkar in Akhbar al-Madinah from 'Umar ibn 'Abdullah ibn al-Muhaj: That the verse was revealed regarding the suggestion of some people that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) drive away the weak from the People of the Bench (أصحاب الصفة) from himself, in a similar story. This is weakened by what was previously mentioned—that the Surah was revealed all at once in Mecca before the Migration.

In Tafsir al-'Ayyashi, from Abu 'Amr al-Zubayri, from Abu 'Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: "May Allah have mercy on a servant who repents to Allah before death, for repentance is a purifier from the filth of sin and a savior from the misery of destruction. Allah made it obligatory upon Himself for His righteous servants, saying: كَتَبَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ أَنَّهُ مَنْ عَمِلَ مِنْكُمْ سُوءاً بِجَهالَةٍ ثُمَّ تابَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ وَأَصْلَحَ فَأَنَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ, and وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ يَجِدِ اللهَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً."

In Tafsir al-Burhan, it is narrated from Ibn 'Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا جاءَكَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِنا the verse: "It was revealed concerning 'Ali, Hamzah, and Zayd."

I say: In several narrations, it is stated that the previous verses were revealed concerning the enemies of the Household (peace be upon them). It is apparent that all of these are of the type of "extension and application" (الجري والتطبيق) or taking the inner meaning (باطن المعنى). For the revelation of the Surah in Mecca all at once precludes making such narrations "reasons for revelation." For this reason, we have refrained from citing them. And Allah knows best.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنِّى نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُل لَّآ أَتَّبِعُ أَهْوَآءَكُمْ ۙ قَدْ ضَلَلْتُ إِذًا وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (٥٦)
Say, ‘I have been forbidden to worship those whom you invoke besides Allah.’ Say, ‘I do not follow your desires, for then I will have gone astray, and I will not be among the [rightly] guided.’ (56)
قُلْ إِنِّى عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَكَذَّبْتُم بِهِۦ ۚ مَا عِندِى مَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۖ يَقُصُّ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰصِلِينَ (٥٧)
Say, ‘Indeed I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord and you have denied it. What you seek to hasten is not up to me. Judgement belongs only to Allah; He expounds the truth and He is the best of judges.’ (57)
قُل لَّوْ أَنَّ عِندِى مَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِۦ لَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٨)
Say, ‘If what you seek to hasten were with me, the matter would surely have been decided between you and me, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers.’ (58)
۞ وَعِندَهُۥ مَفَاتِحُ ٱلْغَيْبِ لَا يَعْلَمُهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ ۚ وَمَا تَسْقُطُ مِن وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُهَا وَلَا حَبَّةٍ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا رَطْبٍ وَلَا يَابِسٍ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٥٩)
With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him. He knows whatever there is in land and sea. No leaf falls without His knowing it, nor is there a grain in the darkness of the earth, nor anything fresh or withered but it is in a manifest Book. (59)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَتَوَفَّىٰكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا جَرَحْتُم بِٱلنَّهَارِ ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ لِيُقْضَىٰٓ أَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٦٠)
It is He who takes your souls by night, and He knows what you do by day, then He reanimates you therein so that a specified term may be completed. Then to Him will be your return, whereat He will inform you concerning what you used to do. (60)
وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنَا وَهُمْ لَا يُفَرِّطُونَ (٦١)
He is the All-dominant over His servants, and He sends guards to [protect] you. When death approaches anyone of you, Our messengers take him away and they do not neglect [their duty]. (61)
ثُمَّ رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ أَلَا لَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ وَهُوَ أَسْرَعُ ٱلْحَـٰسِبِينَ (٦٢)
Then they are returned to Allah, their real master. Look! All judgement belongs to Him, and He is the swiftest of reckoners. (62)
قُلْ مَن يُنَجِّيكُم مِّن ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ تَدْعُونَهُۥ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً لَّئِنْ أَنجَىٰنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِۦ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (٦٣)
Say, ‘Who delivers you from the darkness of land and sea, [when] You invoke Him suppliantly and secretly: ‘‘If He delivers us from this, we will surely be among the grateful’’?’ (63)
قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُنَجِّيكُم مِّنْهَا وَمِن كُلِّ كَرْبٍ ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ (٦٤)
Say, ‘It is Allah who delivers you from them and from every agony, [but] then you ascribe partners [to Him].’ (64)
قُلْ هُوَ ٱلْقَادِرُ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ أَوْ مِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُم بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَفْقَهُونَ (٦٥)
Say, ‘He is able to send upon you a punishment from above you or from under your feet, or confound you as [hostile] factions, and make you taste one another’s violence.’ Look, how We paraphrase the signs variously so that they may understand! (65)
وَكَذَّبَ بِهِۦ قَوْمُكَ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ قُل لَّسْتُ عَلَيْكُم بِوَكِيلٍ (٦٦)
Your people have denied it, though it is the truth. Say, ‘It is not my business to watch over you.’ (66)
لِّكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُّسْتَقَرٌّ ۚ وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٧)
For every prophecy there is a [preordained] setting, and soon you will know. (67)
وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَخُوضُوا۟ فِى حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِۦ ۚ وَإِمَّا يُنسِيَنَّكَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَلَا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٦٨)
When you see those who gossip impiously about Our signs, avoid them until they engage in some other discourse; but if Satan makes you forget, then, after remembering, do not sit with the wrongdoing lot. (68)
وَمَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ وَلَـٰكِن ذِكْرَىٰ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (٦٩)
Those who are Godwary are in no way accountable for them, but this is merely for admonition’s sake, so that they may beware. (69)
وَذَرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لَعِبًا وَلَهْوًا وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَذَكِّرْ بِهِۦٓ أَن تُبْسَلَ نَفْسٌۢ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ لَيْسَ لَهَا مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِىٌّ وَلَا شَفِيعٌ وَإِن تَعْدِلْ كُلَّ عَدْلٍ لَّا يُؤْخَذْ مِنْهَآ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُبْسِلُوا۟ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ ۖ لَهُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ وَعَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ (٧٠)
Leave alone those who take their religion for play and diversion and whom the life of this world has deceived, and admonish with it, lest any soul should perish because of what it has earned: It shall not have any guardian besides Allah, nor any intercessor; and though it should offer every kind of ransom, it shall not be accepted from it. They are the ones who perish because of what they have earned; they shall have boiling water for drink and a painful punishment because of what they used to defy. (70)
قُلْ أَنَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُنَا وَلَا يَضُرُّنَا وَنُرَدُّ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَابِنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ كَٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَهْوَتْهُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ حَيْرَانَ لَهُۥٓ أَصْحَـٰبٌ يَدْعُونَهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَى ٱئْتِنَا ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ هُوَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۖ وَأُمِرْنَا لِنُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧١)
Say, ‘Shall we invoke besides Allah that which can neither benefit us nor harm us, and turn back on our heels after Allah has guided us, like someone seduced by the devils and bewildered on the earth, who has companions that invite him to guidance, [saying,] ‘‘Come to us!’’?’ Say, ‘Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is [true] guidance. And we have been commanded to submit to the Lord of all the worlds, (71)
وَأَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٧٢)
and that ‘‘Maintain the prayer and be wary of Him, and it is He toward whom you will be gathered.’’ ’ (72)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُن فَيَكُونُ ۚ قَوْلُهُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ وَلَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ ۚ عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (٧٣)
It is He who created the heavens and the earth with reason, and the day He says [to something], ‘Be!’ it is. His word is the truth, and to Him belongs all sovereignty on the day when the Trumpet will be blown. Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, He is the All-wise, the All-aware. (73)

(Commentary)

The verses are a continuation of the arguments against the polytheists (المشركين) regarding monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the related knowledge concerning prophethood (النبوة) and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and the final judgment. They possess a continuous and consistent context.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنِّي نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ (Say, ‘I have been forbidden to worship those whom you invoke besides Allah.’) until the end of the verse. He was commanded to inform them of the prohibition (النهي) issued to him against worshipping their partners (شركائهم); this is a prohibition against their worship expressed through a form of metonymy (الكناية).

Then He pointed to the basis of the prohibition against it by His saying: قُلْ لا أَتَّبِعُ أَهْواءَكُمْ (Say, ‘I do not follow your desires.’), which is that their worship is a following of whim (الهوى), and that has been forbidden. Then He pointed by His saying: قَدْ ضَلَلْتُ إِذاً وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُهْتَدِينَ (‘for then I would have gone astray, and I would not be among the guided.’) to the reason for refusing to follow whim, which is straying (الضلال) and exiting the community of the guided (المهتدين) — those who are characterized by the quality of accepting the guidance (هداية) of Allah, the Glorified, and are known by that. Thus, following whim contradicts the stability of the quality of guidance (الاهتداء) in the human soul and prevents the light of monotheism (التوحيد) from radiating upon the heart with a firm radiance that yields benefit.

From all this, a complete and reasoned explanation for the prohibition or the cessation of worshipping their idols is summarized: that in worshipping them there is a following of whim, and in following whim there is straying and an exit from the ranks of those guided by divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنِّي عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَكَذَّبْتُمْ بِهِ (Say, ‘I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord and you have denied it.’) until the end of the verse. The manifest proof (البينة) is the clear evidence (الدلالة الواضحة) derived from bayan (البيان), which is clarity. The root meaning of this material is the isolation of one thing from another and its separation from it such that they do not connect or mix; from it come terms like al-bayn (the gap), al-bawn (the distance), al-baynunah (separation), and others. The manifest proof (البينة) is so named because truth is distinguished (يبين) by it from falsehood, becoming clear and easy to grasp without toil or burden.

What is intended by the referent of the pronoun in His saying: وَكَذَّبْتُمْ بِهِ (and you have denied it) is the Quran. The apparent context suggests that the denial (التكذيب) pertains to the manifest proof (البينة) upon which he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stands, as is evident from the continuity of the meaning. This is supported by His subsequent saying: ما عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ (‘What you seek to hasten is not in my power.’) etc. For the sum of the speech, when this conclusion is joined to it, is: that which Allah has supported my message with of manifest proofs—namely the Quran—you deny; and that which you propose to me and seek to hasten of signs (الآيات) is not within my choice nor is its matter delegated to me. Thus, there is no common ground between us, for I have been given what you do not want, and you want what I have not been given.

From here it becomes clear that the genitive pronoun in His saying: وَكَذَّبْتُمْ بِهِ (and you have denied it) refers to the manifest proof (البينة) because what is intended by it is the Quran. Furthermore, His saying: ما عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ (What you seek to hasten is not with me) is intended to negate authority (التسلط) over what they seek to hasten through metonymy. For generally, regarding what a person is capable of—especially in the realm of giving and spending—that which he gives or spends is present with him or stored by him and under his authority, and then he spends from it what he spends. Thus, by His saying: ما عِنْدِي (is not with me), the negation of authority and power (القدرة) was intended by way of negating the necessitated (الملزوم) through the negation of the necessary consequence (اللازم).

His saying: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ (Judgment belongs only to Allah) etc., is an explanation of the reason for the negation, which is why it was brought with negation and exception (الاستثناء) to signify restriction (الحصر). This indicates, by the occurrence of the negation upon the genus, that nothing of the nature of judgment (الحكم) belongs to any other than Him, the Exalted, at all, and that it belongs to Allah, the Glorified, alone.

(A Discourse on the Meaning of Judgment and that it Belongs to Allah Alone)

The root of judgment (الحكم) indicates a type of precision (الإتقان) by which parts are harmonized and its flaws and gaps are closed, so that it does not fragment into parts nor dissipate into portions such that its effect weakens and its force breaks. To this returns the collective meaning among its various derivatives, such as perfecting (الإحكام), arbitration (التحكيم), wisdom (الحكمة), governance (الحكومة), and others.

Man has become aware of a type of realization of this meaning in master-servant duties and the rights circulating among people. For when masters and leaders command something, it is as if they are binding the obligation (التكليف) upon the subordinates and tethering them to it with a bond (عقداً) that accepts no dissolution and a tethering that leaves them no room for release. Similarly, the owner of a commodity or the possessor of a right in a matter has between him and his commodity or the matter a type of cohesion and connection that prevents another from intervening between him and his commodity by disposal, or between him and the object of his right, thereby restraining his hand. If someone disputes with the owner of a commodity regarding its ownership—such as claiming it for himself—or disputes with the possessor of a right, seeking to nullify his right, he has weakened this precision (الإحكام) and enfeebled this solidity. Then, if the judge (الحكم) or the magistrate to whom the case was raised affirms the ownership or the right for one of the disputants, he has created there a judgment (حكماً)—that is, a precision after laxity, and a strength of consolidation after weakness and frailty. His saying: "The ownership of the commodity belongs to so-and-so" or "The right in such-and-such belongs to so-and-so" is a judgment (حكم) by which the trouble of dispute and quarreling is removed, and no one other than the owner or the right-holder intervenes between the property or the right and its possessor. In short, the commander in his command and the judge in his judgment are as if they are creating a relation in the subject of the command and judgment, consolidating it thereby and removing weakness and laxity; this is what is called judgment (الحكم).

This is the path by which people became aware of the meaning of judgment in conventional, social matters (الأمور الوضعية الاعتبارية). Then they saw that its meaning is applicable to ontological, real matters (الأمور التكوينية الحقيقية) when attributed to Allah, the Glorified, in terms of His decree (قضائه) and measure (قدره). Thus, the fact that a seed, for example, grows in the soil, then extends into a stalk and branches, leaves and bears fruit; and the fact that a zygote transforms into a body possessing life and sensation—all of that is a judgment (حكم) from Allah, the Glorified, and a decree (قضاء). This is what we grasp of the meaning of judgment: the affirmation of a thing for a thing or the affirmation of a thing in the presence of a thing.

The theory of monotheism (التوحيد) upon which the Noble Quran builds the structure of its knowledge, since it affirms the reality of causality (التأثير) in existence (الوجود) for Allah, the Glorified, alone without any partner—even if the attribution differs according to the difference of things and does not proceed in a single manner, as you see that He, the Exalted, attributes creation (الخلق) to Himself and then attributes it in various instances to different things with different relations; and likewise knowledge (العلم), power (القدرة), life (الحياة), will (المشية), provision (الرزق), and beauty (الحسن), and so on—in short, since causality belongs to Him, the Exalted, then judgment (الحكم), which is a type of causality and origination (الجعل), belongs to Him, the Exalted, whether in ontological realities (الحقائق التكوينية) or in conventional, social laws (الشرائع الوضعية الاعتبارية). The speech of the Exalted has supported this meaning, such as His saying: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ (Judgment belongs only to Allah) (6:57, 12:67), and His saying: أَلا لَهُ الْحُكْمُ (Look! Judgment belongs to Him) (6:62), and His saying: لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُ الْحُكْمُ (All praise belongs to Him in the world and the Hereafter, and judgment belongs to Him) (28:70), and His saying: وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ (Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment) (13:41). If anyone other than Him, the Exalted, possessed judgment, he would be able to reverse His judgment and oppose His will. And His saying: فَالْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ الْعَلِيِّ الْكَبِيرِ (So judgment belongs to Allah, the All-exalted, the All-great) (40:12), among other verses. These are specific or general verses indicating the exclusivity of ontological judgment (الحكم التكويني) to Him, the Exalted.

The exclusivity of legislative judgment (الحكم التشريعي) specifically to Him, the Exalted, is indicated by His saying: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ (Judgment belongs only to Allah; He has commanded you to worship none except Him. That is the upright religion) (12:40). Thus, judgment belongs to Allah, the Glorified; no one else shares it with Him according to the apparent meaning of the aforementioned verses. However, He, the Exalted, sometimes attributes judgment—especially the legislative kind—in His speech to others, such as His saying: يَحْكُمُ بِهِ ذَوا عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ (as judged by two just men from among you) (5:95), and His saying to David (peace be upon him): إِنَّا جَعَلْناكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِالْحَقِّ (O David! Indeed We have made you a vicegerent on the earth. So judge between the people with falsehood) (38:26), and His saying to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): أَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ (and that you should judge between them by what Allah has sent down) (5:49), and His saying: فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ (so judge between them by what Allah has sent down) (5:48), and His saying: يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ (by which the prophets... would judge) (5:44), among other verses. Combining these with the first category yields the conclusion that true judgment (الحكم الحق) belongs to Allah, the Glorified, essentially (بالأصالة) and primarily (أولاً); no one else is independent (يستقل) in it. It exists for others by His permission (بإذنه) and secondarily (ثانياً). Therefore, He, the Exalted, considered Himself the Best of Judges (أحكم الحاكمين) and the Best of them, as that is a necessary consequence of essentiality, independence, and primacy. He said: أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِأَحْكَمِ الْحاكِمِينَ (Is not Allah the fairest of all judges?) (95:8) and said: وَهُوة خَيْرُ الْحاكِمِينَ (and He is the best of judges) (7:87).

The verses containing the attribution of judgment to others by His permission and the like—as you see—are specific to conventional, social judgment (الحكم الوضعي الاعتباري). As for ontological judgment (الحكم التكويني), there is nothing in them—as far as I recall—that indicates its attribution to others, even though the meanings of the general attributes (الصفات) and acts (الأفعال) attributed to Him, the Exalted, do not preclude attribution to others in some manner by His permission and the like, such as knowledge, power, life, creation, provision, giving life, will, and others in many verses that need not be cited.

Perhaps this is out of respect for His side, the Exalted, because the attribute suggests a type of independence (استقلال) that there is no justification for attributing to these intermediary causes (الأسباب المتوسطة), just as ontological decree (القضاء) and command (الأمر) are likewise. Similar to this are the terms the Originator (البديع), the Maker (البارئ), the Creator (الفاطر), and other terms that follow the same course in suggesting meanings that imply a type of exclusivity. Use of them for other than His instance, the Exalted, was withheld out of respect for the sanctuary of Lordship (الربوبية).

Let us return to our interpretation of the verse. His saying, the Exalted: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ (Judgment belongs only to Allah)—what is intended by judgment here is ontological decree (القضاء التكويني). The sentence is an explanation for the negation in His saying: ما عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ (What you seek to hasten is not with me). The meaning—as provided by the context—is that judgment belongs to Allah alone, and it is not for me to judge between myself and you; and that is what you seek to hasten by your proposal of a sign (الآية) to me.

Accordingly, His saying: ما عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ is used as a metonymy (الكناية), as if by their proposal to bring another sign besides the Quran, they were proposing to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he judge between himself and them. Perhaps this is the secret in the repetition of the relative pronoun (الموصول) and the relative clause (الصلة) in the following verse where He, the Exalted, says: قُلْ لَوْ أَنَّ عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ (Say, ‘If what you seek to hasten were in my power...’). The apparent requirement of the context would have been to say: "If that were with me." This is because by His saying: ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ (what you seek to hasten) in the first verse, the necessary consequence of the sign was intended—which is the judgment between him and them according to the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية)—and in the second verse, the sign itself was intended. It is also possible that the matter of metonymy is the reverse of that, such that what is intended by "what you seek to hasten" is the judgment explicitly in the first verse, and the sign by metonymy in the second verse.

His saying: يَقُصُّ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْفاصِلِينَ (He relates the truth, and He is the best of those who decide). Asim, Nafi', and Ibn Kathir from the Seven [readers] read it with qaf and the undotted sad (يَقُصُّ) from al-qass, which is to cut something and separate it from another; from this is His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَتْ لِأُخْتِهِ قُصِّيهِ (And she said to his sister, ‘Follow him up.’) (28:11). The rest read it with qaf and the dotted dad (يَقْضِي) from al-qada' (judgment). The ya has been omitted from the script as in His saying, the Exalted: فَما تُغْنِ النُّذُرُ (but the warnings do not avail) (54:5). Each of the two readings has a basis, and they return to the same meaning, for relating the truth (قص الحق) and separating it from falsehood is a necessary consequence of judgment (القضاء) and ruling by the truth, although His saying: وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْفاصِلِينَ (and He is the best of those who decide) is more suited to al-qass in the sense of separation (الفصل).

As for taking His saying يَقُصُّ الْحَقَ from al-qass in the sense of informing about something or in the sense of following a trace, as some commentators have suggested, it is not suitable for the context:

As for the first, it is because Allah, the Glorified, although He relates (قص) in His speech many stories of the prophets and their nations, the context here is devoid of that, so there is no reason to mention this attribute for Him and describe Him, the Exalted, by it.

As for the second, it is because the sum of its meaning would be that His sunnah, the Exalted, is to follow the truth and trace its path in the management of His kingdom and the organization of the affairs of His creation. While Allah, the Glorified, does not judge except by the truth and does not decree except by the truth, the etiquette of the Wise Quran precludes attributing following (الاتباع) and tracing (الاقتفاء) to Him, the Exalted. He, the Exalted, has said among what He said: الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ (The truth is from your Lord) (3:60) and did not say: "The truth is with your Lord," because of the trace of seeking support and the implication of weakness in the expression of "with-ness" (المعية).

(A Discourse on the Reality of His Act and His Judgment, the Exalted)

His act and His judgment, the Exalted, are the truth itself, not merely corresponding to the truth or agreeing with it. To explain this: a thing is only "truth" (حقاً) if it is established in external reality (الخارج), occurring in the concrete world (الأعيان) without being invented by imagination or fashioned by the mind—like the human being who is one of the external existents, the earth upon which he lives, and the plants and animals by which he is nourished. A report (الخبر) is only "truth" if it corresponds to the established reality in itself, independent of our perception. A judgment (الحكم) and a decree (القضاء) are only "truth" if they agree with the sunnah (السنة) prevailing in the cosmos. Thus, if a commander orders something or a judge decrees something, the judgment of the former and the decree of the latter are only "absolute truth" if they agree with the absolute interest (المصلحة المطلقة) derived from the sunnah prevailing in the cosmos; and it is a "relative truth" if it agrees with the relative interest derived from the sunnah of the cosmos in relation to some of its parts without regard for the general universal system.

So, if a commander orders us to adhere to justice or avoid injustice, that is considered "truth" only because the system of the cosmos guides things toward their felicity (سعادتها) and their good. It has decreed for man that he live socially, and it has decreed for every society composed of parts that its parts harmonize, that they do not crowd one another, and that one side does not corrupt another, so that it may attain what has been apportioned for it of the felicity of existence (سعادة الوجود), and that this be distributed among its social parts. Thus, the absolute interest of this species is its felicity in life, and the command to justice and the prohibition of injustice correspond to it; so each of them is a true judgment (حكم حق). The command to injustice and the prohibition of justice do not correspond to it, so they are of falsehood (الباطل). Monotheism (التوحيد) is truth because it guides to the felicity of man in his real life, and polytheism (الشرك) is falsehood because it drags man toward a destructive wretchedness and eternal punishment.

Likewise, the judgment between two disputants is only truth if it agrees with the prescribed judgment (الحكم المشروع) in which the absolute human interest is observed, or the interest of a specific people or a specific nation. The real interest (المصلحة الحقيقية)—as you have known—is derived from the sunnah prevailing in the cosmos, whether absolutely or relatively.

It has thus become clear that the truth (الحق), whatever it may be, is only derived from the external cosmos, the system spread over it, and the sunnah prevailing within it. There is no doubt that the cosmos and existence (الوجود), with its system, sunnahs, and laws, is His act, the Glorified; from Him it begins, by Him it subsists, and to Him it ends. Thus, the truth, whatever it may be, and the interest, however it is conceived, follow His act and trace its path; they are established by reliance upon Him, not that He, the Exalted, follows the truth in His act and traces its path. He, the Exalted, is truth by His essence, and everything besides Him is truth through Him.

We, the community of humans, since we seek through our voluntary acts to complete the deficiencies of our existence and fulfill the needs of our lives, and since our acts sometimes correspond to our sought-after felicity and sometimes contradict it, we are compelled in that to observe the side of interest (المصلحة) which we acknowledge to be an interest—that is, it contains the rectification of our state and the felicity of our fortune. This led to the acknowledgment of prevailing laws and general judgments, and the consideration of social codes (شرائع) and sunnahs that must be observed and followed due to their fulfillment of human interest and their agreement with the sought-after felicity.

This led to the acknowledgment that interests (المصالح) and harms (المفاسد) have a real establishment (ثبوتاً واقعياً) and a domain of realization distinct from the two worlds: the mind (الذهن) and the external (الخارج)—isolated from the two abodes: knowledge (العلم) and the concrete (العين). They exert their effect in the external cosmos through agreement and contradiction. If our acts or our judgments correspond to the real interests established in the "thing itself" (نفس الأمر), the interest appears in them and they end in felicity. If they contradict them and correspond to the real, actual harms, they lead us to every injury and evil. This type of establishment is a real establishment not subject to disappearance or change. Thus, real interests and harms, as well as the attributes accompanying them that call for action or omission—such as beauty (الحسن) and ugliness (القبح)—and likewise the judgments emanating from them—such as the necessity (وجوب) of action or omission, for example—all of that has a real establishment that refuses annihilation and nullity, and is impossible to change or alter. They are governing (حاكمة) within us, prompting us toward such acts or diverting us, and the intellect (العقل) perceives these matters of the "thing itself" just as it perceives other cosmic matters.

Then, when they found that divine judgments and codes do not differ from human judgments and laws made in societies in terms of the meaning of judgment (الحكم), and likewise His acts, the Exalted, do not differ from our acts in terms of the meaning of act (الفعل), they judged that divine judgments and the acts attributed to Allah, the Glorified, are like our acts in their correspondence to real interests and their characterization by the quality of beauty (الحسن). Thus, real interests have an effect on His acts, the Exalted, and a governance over His judgments, especially since He, the Exalted, is Knower of the realities of things and Seer of the interests of His servants.

All of this is from an excess of opinion. You have known from what preceded that these are conventional, social judgments and sciences (علوم اعتبارية) and not real ones; we were compelled to consider and establish them by natural needs and the necessity of social life. There is no report of them in the external world outside the domain of society, and they have no value except that they are matters established in the domain of convention and consideration (الوضع والاعتبار) by which man distinguishes what benefits him of actions from what harms him, what rectifies his state from what corrupts it, and what makes him happy from what makes him wretched.

Sectarian bias (العصبية المذهبية) drove the two groups investigating religious knowledge in the early days of Islam to a strange opposition of excess and neglect in this regard. One group—the Mufawwidah (the Delegationists)—affirmed interests and harms of the "thing itself" and real beauty and ugliness that are established with an eternal, everlasting establishment, unchanging and unaltering, and which govern Allah, the Glorified, by way of necessity and prohibition, affecting His acts ontologically and legislatively through restriction and permission. Thus, they removed Him, the Exalted, from His authority and nullified the absolute nature of His kingdom.

The other group—the Mujbirah (the Necessitarians)—negated all of that and insisted that beauty in a thing is only the attachment of the command to it, and ugliness is the attachment of the prohibition to it; and that there is no purpose or goal in creation or legislation, and that man possesses nothing of his act nor has power over it before the act. Just as the first group went to the view that the act is created by man and that Allah, the Glorified, possesses nothing of man's act nor does His power attach to it.

Both views—as you see—are excess and neglect. It is neither this nor that; rather, the reality of the matter is that these and their likes are conventional, social matters (أمور اعتبارية وضعية) that have a real basis. That basis is that man—and similarly other social animals, each according to its measure—in his vital path, by which he intends nothing but the preservation of life and the attainment of felicity, is deficient and needy. He removes the aspects of his deficiency and need through his social actions emanating from consciousness and will. This compelled him to describe his actions and the matters to which his actions relate in the path of reaching the goal of his felicity and avoiding his wretchedness with the descriptions of external matters—such as beauty and ugliness, necessity and prohibition, permissibility, ownership, right, and others. He applies within them the laws of causes and effects, and consequently establishes general and specific laws, believing for them a type of establishment that he believes for real matters, so that the affair of his social life may be completed for him.

Thus, you see us believing that justice is beautiful (حسن) just as the rose is beautiful and lovely, and that injustice is ugly and hideous just as the stinking carcass is so; and that wealth belongs to us just as our limbs belong to us, and that such-and-all action is necessary (واجب) just as effects are necessary for their complete causes. On this basis, you see that these views differ among peoples if the goals of their societies differ. You see these people deeming beautiful what others deem ugly, and you find one group nullifying judgments that another considers, and you find a nation denying what another nation knows or being pleased by what another finds repulsive. Sometimes a sunnah is adopted, then taken, then abandoned in a single nation according to the stages of the social path and its contact with the requirements of life. This is in the goals that differ in societies. As for the general goals in which no two people differ—such as the basis of society, justice, injustice, and the like—their descriptions of beauty, ugliness, necessity, prohibition, and others do not differ at all, nor is there disagreement in them; this is regarding what pertains to us.

Allah, the Glorified, when He cast His religion into the mold of general social sunnahs, He considered in His explanation of real knowledge—cast in the mold of social sunnahs—what we consider in the path of our lives. He wanted us to think about what pertains to His knowledge and to receive the realities He conveys to us just as we think about and receive the sunnahs of life that we have. Thus, He considered Himself a worshipped Lord (رباً معبوداً) and considered us worshipped servants (عباداً مربوبين). He mentioned to us that He has a religion composed of original beliefs and practical laws that entail reward and punishment, and that in following it is the rectification of our state, the goodness of our end, and the felicity of our fortune, in the manner of the path we follow in our social views.

So there are original beliefs that we must believe in and adhere to, and there are practical duties and divine laws in worship, transactions, and politics that we must act upon and observe, just as the matter is in all human societies.

This is what permits us to investigate religious knowledge—whether creedal or practical—just as we investigate social knowledge—whether creedal or practical—and to rely in religious knowledge upon rational views and practical premises in the exact same way we rely upon them in social knowledge. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, does not choose for His servants duties and obligations except those in which there is the interest that rectifies their affair in their world and their Hereafter; He does not command except the beautiful and lovely, and He does not forbid except the ugly and hideous in which there is the corruption of religion or world; He does not do except what the intellect prefers, and He does not omit except what ought to be omitted.

However, He, the Exalted, reminded us along with that of two matters:

First: that the matter in itself is greater than that, and even greater. For all of that is knowledge derived from the materials of social views, and in reality, they do not exceed the stage of society, nor do they ascend to the world of heaven, as He said: إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ ، وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ (Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is with Us in the Mother Book, [and it is] surely high and wise) (43:3-4). And He said in a parable He struck: أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِها فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَداً رابِياً وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِثْلُهُ كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْباطِلَ (He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity, and the flood carries a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire for making ornaments or housewares [there is] a similar scum. That is how Allah compares the truth and the falsehood...) (13:17). And he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "We, the community of prophets, have been commanded to speak to people according to the capacity of their intellects," among other things mentioned in the Book and the Sunnah.

The meaning of this investigation is not the negation of beauty and interest, for example, from His acts, the Exalted, in the sense of affirming their opposites—such that it would entail the affirmation of ugliness and harm or the fall of His acts from rational consideration like the acts of children; Exalted is He above that. Just as the negation of sight in the sense of a physical organ from the intellect does not necessitate the affirmation of blindness for it or its fall from the rank of perception; rather, it is a sanctification from deficiency.

Second: that the aspects of beauty and the merits of interests, although His acts, the Exalted, and the codes of His judgments are reasoned by them, and the duties of servitude are explained by them—just as our rational judgments and actions are reasoned—there is a difference between the two doors. It is that in our case, they govern the will and affect choice. We, as rational beings, if we find an act possessing the quality of beauty joined to an interest not crowded by another, it prompts us to commit the action; and if we find a judgment of this description, we do not hesitate to legislate it, and we judge by it and apply it in our society, for example.

These facets and reasons—I mean the aspects of beauty and interest—are nothing but meanings we have taken from the sunnah of creation (التكوين) and external existence (الوجود الخارجي) which is separate from our minds and independent of us. We intended, in choosing beautiful actions possessing interest, not to stumble in our path and that our actions correspond to the sunnah of creation and fall into the path of reality. Thus, these aspects and interests are meanings abstracted from the external world of concrete things and are derivative of it; and our actions are derivative of these aspects, governed by them, and affected by them. The discourse regarding our established judgments is similar to the discourse regarding our actions.

As for His act, the Exalted, it is the external cosmos itself and the concrete existence from which we were abstracting the aspects of beauty and interest, and they were derivative of it since they were abstracted from it. How then can His act, the Exalted, be considered derivative of them, governed by them, and affected by them? Likewise, His legislative judgments, the Exalted, entail reality (تستتبع الواقع) rather than following reality (تتبع الواقع); so understand that.

It has thus become clear: that the aspects of beauty and interest and their likes, while they exist in His acts, the Exalted, and His judgments, and in our acts and judgments as rational beings, they differ in that regarding our actions and judgments they are governing and affecting—or if you wish, say they are motives and final causes (علل غائية)—and regarding His acts and judgments, the Exalted, they are necessary and inseparable—or if you wish, say: consistent benefits (فوائد مطردة).
