We, as rational beings, do what we do and judge what we judge because we intend by it the attainment of good and felicity and the possession of what we do not yet possess. He, the Exalted, does what He does and judges what he judges because He is Allah; and there follows from His act what follows from our act of beauty and interest. Our acts are questioned, reasoned by their goals and interests; His acts are not questioned, nor reasoned by a goal He does not possess, but rather are disclosed by their necessary consequences and necessary attributes. "He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned"; so understand that.

This is what His speech, exalted is His Name, guides to, such as His saying, the Exalted: لا يُسْئَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْئَلُونَ (He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned) (21:23), and His saying: لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُ الْحُكْمُ (All praise belongs to Him in the world and the Hereafter, and judgment belongs to Him) (28:70), and His saying: وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ (and Allah does whatever He wishes) (14:27), and His saying: وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ (Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment) (13:41).

If His act, the Exalted, were like our rational acts, His judgment would have a reverser unless it were supported by a beautifying interest; and it would not be for Him to do what He wishes, but rather what the accompanying interest indicates. And His saying: قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ (Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not command indecency.’) (7:28), and His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ (O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life) (8:24), among other verses that reason the judgments by aspects of beauty and interest.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَوْ أَنَّ عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ لَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ (Say, ‘If what you seek to hasten were in my power, the matter would have been decided between you and me.’) until the end of the verse. That is: if I were capable of the sign you propose to me—and the case is such that if it were sent down to a messenger, it would not be inseparable from the decisive judgment between him and his nation—the matter would have been decided between me and you. Thereby, one of the two disputing, differing parties would be saved and the other punished and destroyed. No one would be punished thereby nor destroyed except you, because you are wrongdoers (ظالمون). Divine punishment only takes the wrongdoers for their wrongdoing, and He, the Glorified, is too sanctified for the matter to be confused for Him or for Him not to distinguish the wrongdoers from others, such that He would punish me instead of you.

In His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِالظَّالِمِينَ (and Allah knows best the wrongdoers) there is a type of metonymy and reasoning; that is: you are the ones to be punished because you are wrongdoers, and divine punishment does not bypass the wrongdoers to others. In the sentence is an allusion to what preceded of His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُ اللهِ بَغْتَةً أَوْ جَهْرَةً هَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الظَّالِمُونَ (Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah’s punishment were to come on you suddenly or openly, would any be destroyed except the wrongdoing lot?’) (6:47).

His saying, the Exalted: وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ (With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him.) until the end of the verse. They mentioned regarding the connection of the verse to what preceded it that the previous verse, when it concluded with His saying: وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِالظَّالِمِينَ (and Allah knows best the wrongdoers), Allah, the Glorified, increased in His explanation, mentioning that the treasuries of the Unseen (خزائن الغيب) or the keys to those treasuries are with Him, the Glorified; no one knows them except Him, and He knows every subtle and grand thing.

This explanation does not clarify the meaning of the restriction (الحصر) indicated by His saying: لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ (no one knows them except Him). It is better to explain the connection by what the sum of the two previous verses contains—namely His saying: قُلْ إِنِّي عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي until His saying وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِالظَّالِمِينَ. For the whole indicates that what they were proposing of the sign and what it entails of the decisive judgment and decree between him and them is only with Allah; there is no path to it for anyone else. He is the Knower of that, the Judge of it, and He does not err in His decisive judgment and the punishment of the wrongdoers because He knows them best. Thus, He is Knower of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — no one shares it with Him, and He is Knower of everything that is grand or subtle; He does not stray nor forget. Then He increased that in explanation by His saying: وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ (With Him are the treasures of the Unseen), whereby He explained His exclusivity, the Exalted, to the knowledge of the Unseen and the encompassment of His knowledge of everything. Then He completed the explanation with the three verses that follow it.

By this, the verses proceed in the same manner as their counterparts in similar instances, such as His saying, the Exalted, in the story of Hud and his people: قالُوا أَجِئْتَنا لِتَأْفِكَنا عَنْ آلِهَتِنا فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ. قالَ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَأُبَلِّغُكُمْ ما أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ (They said, ‘Have you come to turn us away from our gods? Then bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful.’ He said, ‘The knowledge is only with Allah, and I communicate to you what I have been sent with...’) (46:22-23).

Then we say: المفاتح (mafatih) is the plural of maftah with the fatha on the mim, which is the treasury (الخزينة). It is sometimes considered possible that it is the plural of miftah with the kasra on the mim, which is the key (المفتاح). This is supported by the anomalous reading: وعنده مفاتيح الغيب (With Him are the keys of the Unseen). The result of the two meanings is one, for he who possesses the keys to the treasuries is knowledgeable of what is in them and capable of disposing of them as he wishes, usually like one who possesses the treasuries themselves. However, the rest of His speech, the Exalted, in what resembles this instance supports the first meaning, for He, the Exalted, repeated in His speech the mention of His treasuries and the treasuries of His mercy—and that is in seven places—and did not mention keys for them in any of His speech. He, the Exalted, said: أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزائِنُ رَبِّكَ (Or do they possess the treasuries of your Lord?) (52:37), and said: لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ (I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah) (6:50), and said: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ (There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us) (15:21), and said: وَلِلَّهِ خَزائِنُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (To Allah belong the treasuries of the heavens and the earth) (63:7), and said: أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزائِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ (Do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord?) (38:9). Thus, it is more likely that what is intended by the mafatih of the Unseen is its treasuries.

In any case, His saying: وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ is intended to explain the restriction of the knowledge of the Unseen to Him, the Exalted—either because the treasuries of the Unseen are known only to Allah, or because the keys of the Unseen are known to none other than Him, the Exalted, so there is no path for anyone else to those treasuries since he has no knowledge of their keys by which he might reach their opening and disposal.

The beginning of the verse, although it informs of the restriction of the knowledge of the Unseen to Him, the Exalted, its conclusion is not specific to the knowledge of the Unseen; rather, it informs of the encompassment of His knowledge, the Exalted, of everything, whether it be Unseen (غيباً) or manifest (شهادة). For every "moist and dry" is not specific to what is Unseen, and that is evident. Thus, the verse in its entirety explains the encompassment of His knowledge, the Exalted, of every Unseen and manifest thing, except that its beginning is specific to the explanation of His knowledge of the Unseen, and its conclusion informs of His knowledge of everything, more general than the Unseen and the manifest.

From another perspective, the beginning of the verse addresses the Unseen things that are located in the treasuries of the Unseen under the veils of hiddenness and the locks of ambiguity. Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned in His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ (There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure) (15:21) that the things in the treasuries of the Unseen with Him are matters that the witnessed limits (الحدود المشهودة) in things do not encompass, nor do the familiar measures (الأقدار المعهودة) restrict them. There is no doubt that they only became hidden Unseen things because of their quality of exiting the rule of limit and measure; for we do not encompass with knowledge except that which is limited and measured. As for the things in the treasuries of the Unseen, they are—before descending to the station of the manifest (الشهود) and dropping to the landing of limit and measure, and in short, before they exist with their existence measured for them—unlimited and unmeasured, while being established with a type of establishment with Him, the Exalted, according to what the verse speaks of.

Thus, the matters occurring in this witnessed cosmos, imprisoned in the prison of time, are—before their occurrence and origination—existent with Allah, established in His treasuries with a type of ambiguous, unmeasured establishment, even if we cannot encompass the modality of their establishment. So, among what is located in the mafatih of the Unseen and its treasuries are things before their origination and their settlement in their station measured for them among the stations of time. Perhaps there are other things stored and hidden that do not resemble the witnessed, familiar temporal matters we have. Let us call this type of Unseen—which does not emerge into the arena of the manifest—the Absolute Unseen (الغيب المطلق).

As for things after they are clothed in the garment of realization and existence and descend to their station with limit and measure, that which is within their limits and measures returns in reality to what is in the treasuries of the Unseen and returns to the Absolute Unseen. As for them, with what they possess of limit and measure, they are those whose nature it is that our witnessing (شهودنا) falls upon them and our knowledge attaches to them. When we know them, they become of the manifest (الشهادة), and when we are ignorant of them, they become Unseen (غيباً). It is appropriate that we call them, when they become unknown to us, the Relative Unseen (الغيب النسبي), because this description that occurs to them then is a relative description that differs by relations and additions. Just as what is in the house, for example, is of the manifest in relation to those within it, and of the nature of the Unseen in relation to those outside it. Likewise, the lights and colors perceived by the sense of sight are of the manifest in relation to sight, and of the Unseen in relation to the sense of hearing. The sounds attained by hearing are manifest in relation to it and Unseen in relation to sight. The percepts of both are of the manifest in relation to the human being who possesses them in his body, and of the Unseen in relation to other humans.

Those which He, the Exalted, enumerated in the verse by His saying: وَيَعْلَمُ ما فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ وَما تَسْقُطُ مِنْ وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُها وَلا حَبَّةٍ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْأَرْضِ وَلا رَطْبٍ وَلا يابِسٍ (and He knows whatever there is in the land and in the sea, and not a leaf falls but He knows it, nor is there a grain in the darkness of the earth, nor anything fresh or withered...) are of this Relative Unseen. For they are all limited, measured matters that do not refuse, according to their nature, that our knowledge attach to them or that they be witnessed by us; so they are of the Relative Unseen.

The verse has indicated that these matters are in a Manifest Book (كتاب مبين). So what is it of them that is in a Manifest Book? Is it these things from the perspective of their manifestation and hiddenness together, or is it from the perspective of their hiddenness only? In other words: is the Manifest Book this cosmos containing the concrete entities of these things, such that nothing of them is hidden from it even if some are hidden from others? Or is it a matter beyond this cosmos in which these things are written with a type of writing, stored in it with a type of storage, hidden from the witnessing of the witnesses among the people of the world, such that what is in the Book is of the Absolute Unseen?

In another phrasing: are the things occurring in the cosmos, enumerated in a general way in the verse, occurring by themselves in the Manifest Book just as lines themselves occur in the books we have? Or do they occur by their meanings in it, just as external objectives occur by their meanings with a type of occurrence in the pages and letters we write, and then the external corresponds to the correspondence of knowledge to the concrete?

However, His saying, the Exalted: ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَبْرَأَها (No affliction visits the earth or your souls but it is in a Book before We bring it about) (57:22) indicates that the relation of this Book to external events is the relation of the book in which the program of action is written to the external action. Close to it is His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَعْزُبُ عَنْ رَبِّكَ مِنْ مِثْقالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي السَّماءِ وَلا أَصْغَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ وَلا أَكْبَرَ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ (Not an atom’s weight escapes your Lord in the earth or in the sky, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book) (10:61), and His saying: لا يَعْزُبُ عَنْهُ مِثْقالُ ذَرَّةٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا أَصْغَرُ مِنْ ذلِكَ وَلا أَكْبَرُ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ (Not an atom’s weight escapes Him in the heavens or in the earth, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book) (34:3), and His saying: قالَ فَما بالُ الْقُرُونِ الْأُولى ، قالَ عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي فِي كِتابٍ لا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلا يَنْسى (He said, ‘What about the former generations?’ He said, ‘Their knowledge is with my Lord in a Book. My Lord neither errs nor forgets.’) (20:51-52), among other verses.

Thus, the Manifest Book, whatever it may be, is something other than these external things with a type of otherness; it precedes them and then remains after their annihilation and expiration, like the written programs for actions that contain the specifications of the actions before their occurrence and then preserve the mentioned specifications after the occurrence.

Furthermore, these existents and events that are in our world are changing and transforming under the laws of general motion, while the verses indicate the impermissibility of change and corruption in what this Book contains, such as His saying, the Exalted: يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ (Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book) (13:39), and His saying: فِي لَوْحٍ مَحْفُوظٍ (in a Preserved Tablet) (85:22), and His saying: وَعِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ (and with Us is a book that preserves [everything]) (50:4). The verses—as you see—indicate that this Book, while it contains all the specifications of events and the particularities of changing, transforming individuals, does not transform in itself, nor does any change or corruption penetrate it.

From here it appears that this Book is, in one way, other than the mafatih of the Unseen and the treasuries of things that are with Allah, the Glorified. For Allah, the Exalted, described these mafatih and treasuries as being unmeasured and unlimited, and that measure only attaches to things upon their descent from the treasuries of the Unseen to this world which is the level of the manifest (الشهادة). He described this Book as containing the subtle limits of things and the limits of events. Thus, the Manifest Book is, from this perspective, other than the treasuries of the Unseen that are with Allah, the Glorified; rather, it is something fashioned by Allah, the Glorified, that records other things and preserves them after their descent from the treasuries and before their reaching the station of realization, and after realization and expiration.

This is evidenced by the fact that Allah, the Glorified, only mentioned this Book in His speech to explain the encompassment of His knowledge of the concrete entities of things and the events occurring in the world, whether they are hidden from us or witnessed by us. As for the Absolute Unseen, to which there is no path for anyone other than Him, the Exalted, to be informed of it, He only described it as being in His treasuries and the mafatih that are with Him; no one knows them except Him. Indeed, some verses perhaps suggest or indicate the permissibility of others being informed of the Book but not the treasuries, such as His saying, the Exalted: فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ ، لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ (in a guarded Book, no one touches it except the pure) (56:78-79).

So there is nothing of what Allah, the Glorified, created but that it has in the treasuries of the Unseen an origin from which it draws support; and there is nothing of what Allah created but that the Manifest Book enumerates it before its existence, during it, and after it. However, the Book is a degree lower than the treasuries. From here it becomes clear to the clever, contemplative person that the Manifest Book—while being a pure book—is not of the nature of physical tablets and papers. For a physical page, however it is conceived and however it is measured, cannot possibly contain the writing of its own history for as long as it exists, let alone the history of others, let alone everything for all eternity.

It has been shown by what has passed of the investigation, first: that what is intended by the mafatih of the Unseen are the divine treasuries (الخزائن الإلهية) that contain things before they are emptied into the mold of measures (الأقدار); they contain the Unseen of everything to the extent indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ (15:21).

Second: that what is intended by the Manifest Book is a matter whose relation to all things is the relation of the book containing the program of action to the action itself. In it is a type of determination and measure for things, yet it exists before the things, with them, and after them. It is that which contains His knowledge, the Exalted, of things—a knowledge to which there is no path for straying or forgetfulness. Therefore, it is sometimes surmised that what is intended by it is the rank of the reality of things and their external realization which has no path for change; for a thing is not prevented from the occurrence of change upon it except after occurrence, and it is that which is said: "A thing does not change from what it occurred upon."

In short, this Book enumerates all that has occurred in the world of fashioning and origination (عالم الصنع والإيجاد) of what was, what will be, and what is, without anything escaping it. Yet, along with that, it only contains things from the perspective of their being measured and limited. Beyond that are tablets and books that accept change and transformation, and admit effacement and confirmation, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ (13:39). For effacement and confirmation—especially when contrasted with the Mother of the Book—only occur in the Book.

At that point, the connection of the verse—namely His saying: وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ until the end of the verse—to the verses before it becomes clear. For the sum of the two previous verses is that what you propose to me of signs deciding between me and you is not in my power, nor does the true judgment return to me; rather, it is with my Lord in His knowledge and power. If that were up to me, it would have been decided between me and you, and the punishment would have taken you—which takes none but the wrongdoers—because Allah knows that you are the wrongdoers, and He is the Knower who is ignorant of nothing. As for the fact that there is no path to grasping and having authority over what He wills and decrees of a deciding sign, it is because the mafatih of the Unseen are with Him; no one knows them except Him. As for the fact that He knows the wrongdoers best and does not mistake them for others, it is because He knows what is in the land and the sea, and He knows every subtle and grand thing, and all is in a Manifest Book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ refers to the Absolute Unseen to which there is no path for anyone other than Him, the Exalted. His saying: لا يَعْلَمُها etc., is a circumstantial clause (حال), and it indicates that the mafatih of the Unseen are of the nature of knowledge, except that this knowledge is not of the same nature as the knowledge we are familiar with. For what comes to our minds from the meaning of knowledge is the form taken from things after their existence and their being measured by their measures. The mafatih of the Unseen—as has been shown—is knowledge of things while they are non-existent and not measured by their cosmic measures; that is, an infinite knowledge without being affected by the known.

His saying: وَيَعْلَمُ ما فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ (and He knows whatever there is in the land and in the sea) is a generalization of His knowledge to what the knowledge of others can attach to—of that which some of it may be present to some and some of it may be hidden from some. He mentioned what is in the land first because it is more familiar to the addressees among people.

His saying: وَما تَسْقُطُ مِنْ وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُها (and not a leaf falls but He knows it) was specifically mentioned because it is among what man finds difficult to attain knowledge of. For the extreme multiplicity found in the leaves of trees makes man unable to distinguish some from others, such that he might monitor each of them in the states that occur to it, and become aware of their decrease by the falling ones among them when they fall.

His saying: وَلا حَبَّةٍ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْأَرْضِ وَلا رَطْبٍ وَلا يابِسٍ — "nor a grain in the darkness of the earth, nor anything fresh nor any withered" (6:59), etc., are coordinated with His saying: مِنْ وَرَقَةٍ — "of a leaf" (6:59), according to the apparent context. The "darkness of the earth" (ظلمات الأرض) refers to its dark interior where grains settle; from them, some grow and others decay. Thus, the meaning is: no grain falls into the dark depths of the earth, nor does anything fresh or withered fall—whatever they may be—except that He knows it. Accordingly, His saying: إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ — "but it is in a manifest Book" (6:59) is a substitute (بدل) for His saying: إِلَّا يَعْلَمُها — "but He knows it" (6:59), taking its place. Its implied meaning is: except that it is a reality recorded in a manifest Book.

Describing the Book as "manifest" (المبين), if it means "that which makes evident" (المظهر), is because it discloses to its reader everything according to its true reality, without any ambiguity of change or alteration, or the veil of obscurity affecting any of its attributes. If "manifest" (المبين) means "that which is evident" (الظاهر), it amounts to the same thing, because the Book is, in reality, the "written" (المكتوب), and the "written" is that which is being recounted. Since the reality is evident with no veil or obscurity over it, the Book is likewise.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ ما جَرَحْتُمْ بِالنَّهارِ — "It is He who takes your souls by night, and He knows what you do by day" (6:60). Taking the soul (التوفي) means taking a thing in its entirety. Allah, the Glorified, uses it in His speech to mean the taking of the living spirit, as in the state of death, such as in His saying in the following verse: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا — "until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him" (6:61).

He has considered putting one to sleep as "taking the soul" (توفيا), just as He considered causing death as "taking the soul," based on His saying: اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها وَالَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنامِها — "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those who have not died, in their sleep" (39:42). This is because both share the cessation of the soul's (النفس) control over the body. Similarly, "resurrection" (البعث) in the sense of awakening after sleep shares with "resurrection" in the sense of reviving after death the return of the soul to its control over the body after its cessation. Restricting the "taking of the soul" to the night and "resurrection" to the day follows the general norm that people sleep at night and wake up during the day.

In His saying, the Exalted, يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ — "He takes your souls," there is an indication that the spirit (الروح) is the complete reality of the human being, which one expresses as "I." It is not as we might imagine—that the spirit is merely one of two parts of the human being and not his totality, or that it is an accidental form (هيئة) or attribute (صفة) occurring to him. Even clearer in its indication is His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ ، قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ — "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we be in a new creation?’ Indeed, they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls, then you will be brought back to your Lord’" (32:10-11). The disbelievers' deeming it improbable is based on the idea that the reality of the human being is the body, which vanishes and decays through the dissolution of its composition upon death, thus becoming "lost in the earth." The answer is based on the fact that his reality is the spirit (الروح) or soul (النفس); since the angel of death takes it and seizes it, nothing of him is lost.

His saying: وَيَعْلَمُ ما جَرَحْتُمْ بِالنَّهارِ — "and He knows what you do by day." Wounding (الجرح) refers to an action performed by a limb (الجارحة), and what is meant here is "acquisition" (الكسب); that is, He knows what you have acquired during the day. It is more appropriate that the "and" (الواو) be circumstantial (حالية), making the clause a circumstantial qualifier for the subject of "takes your souls." His saying: ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ — "then He raises you up in it" (6:60) then connects to His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ — "It is He who takes your souls," without the intervention of an extraneous meaning. This is because the two verses are in the position of explaining the occurrence of divine governance (التدبير الإلهي) over the human being in his worldly life, at death, and after it, until he returns to his Lord. The fundamental core of the sentences listed to explain this meaning is His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ ما جَرَحْتُمْ بِالنَّهارِ ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ — "It is He who takes your souls by night, and He knows what you do by day, then He raises you up in it"—meaning in the day—لِيُقْضى أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا وَهُمْ لا يُفَرِّطُونَ. ثُمَّ رُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِ — "so that a specified term may be completed. He sends guardians over you until, when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him, and they are not remiss. Then they are returned to Allah, their true Master" (6:60-62). This is the primary objective, and everything else is intended secondarily. The meaning is: He is the one who takes your souls by night while He knows what you have acquired in the day, then He raises you in the day, etc.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ لِيُقْضى أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى — "then He raises you up in it so that a specified term may be completed" (6:60), etc. Awakening and alerting are called "resurrection" (بعثا) in parallel to calling sleep "taking the soul" (توفيا). The purpose of this resurrection is made the completion of the "specified term" (الأجل المسمى), which is the time known to Allah that the worldly life of a human being does not exceed, as He said: فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ — "so when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it" (7:34).

The completion of the specified term was made the ultimate goal because He, the Exalted, is the swiftest of reckoners. Were it not for a prior decree, He would have seized them for their evil deeds and the consequences of their sins, as He said: وَما تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ — "They did not divide except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves; and were it not for a prior word from your Lord [deferring them] until a specified term, the matter would have been decided between them" (42:14). The prior decree is that which is contained in His saying, the Exalted, in the story of the descent of Adam (peace be upon him): وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَنْفُسِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ — "There will be for you on the earth an abode and enjoyment for a while" (7:24).

The meaning, then, is that Allah takes your souls by night while He knows the evil deeds and other things you have acquired in the day, but He does not retain your spirits to prolong death upon them; rather, He raises you in the day after the taking of the soul so that your specified terms may be completed. Then to Him is your return through the descent of death and the gathering (الحشر), and He will inform you of what you used to do.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ — "He is the All-Dominant over His servants" (6:61). The discussion regarding this has already preceded in the commentary (بيان) on verse 17 of this Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً — "and He sends guardians over you" (6:61), etc. The absolute mention of sending guardians without restriction—neither in the sending nor in the guardians—and then making it bounded by the arrival of death, is not without indication that these sent guardians have the function of protecting the human being from every calamity that heads toward him, every misfortune that seeks him out, and every blight that aims for him. For the realm (النشأة) we are in is a realm of interaction (التفاعل) and conflict (التزاحم); there is nothing in it except that it is afflicted by the conflict of something else from all directions, because every part of this natural world is in pursuit of perfection (الاستكمال) and increasing its share of existence (الوجود). Nothing increases in one thing except that it decreases proportionately in another; thus, things are always in a state of contention and overcoming. Among its parts is the human being, whose existential composition is the most subtle and precise of compositions within it, as far as we know. Therefore, his rivals in existence are more numerous and his enemies in life are more dangerous. So Allah sent to him guardians from among the angels who protect him from the occurrences of accidents and the assaults of calamities and misfortunes. They continue to protect him from destruction until, when his term comes, they leave him to the calamity, and it destroys him, as mentioned in the narrations.

As for what He mentioned in His saying: إِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحافِظِينَ. كِراماً كاتِبِينَ. يَعْلَمُونَ ما تَفْعَلُونَ — "Indeed, there are over you guardians, noble writers, who know what you do" (82:10-12), He intends by it the guardians over deeds. However, some have taken those verses as an explanation for this verse. While the verse does not entirely refuse this meaning, His saying: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ — "until, when death comes to one of you" (6:61) until the end of the verse, as previously mentioned, supports the first meaning.

His saying: تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا وَهُمْ لا يُفَرِّطُونَ — "Our messengers take him, and they are not remiss" (6:61). It appears that what is meant by "being remiss" (التفريط) is negligence or leniency in executing the divine command of taking the soul. For Allah, the Glorified, described His angels as doing what they are commanded, and mentioned that every nation is pledged to its term; when their term comes, they shall not defer it by an hour nor advance it. Thus, the angels charged with the matter of taking the soul do not fall short of the necessary, defined limit disclosed to them—that so-and-so dies at such-and-such an hour under such-and-such conditions. They are not lenient in taking the soul of the one they were commanded to take, not even by an atom's weight; thus, they are not remiss.

Are these messengers the same messengers mentioned first, such that the guardians are those entrusted with taking the soul? The verse is silent on that, except for a weak suggestion of unity. However, these messengers commanded to take the soul, whoever they may be, are among the assistants of the Angel of Death, due to His saying, the Exalted: قُل يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ — "Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls’" (32:11).

The attribution of taking the soul to these messengers, then to the Angel of Death in the verse just cited, then to Allah, the Glorified, in His saying: اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ — "Allah takes the souls" (39:42), is a matter of variation in the levels of attribution (النسب). For every matter ultimately returns to Allah, the Glorified, and He is the absolute Owner and Controller. The Angel of Death employs assistants to accomplish what he does in seizing spirits, and they are the causes (الأسباب), means (الوسائل), and instruments (الأدوات) of the act—like the line drawn by the pen, behind which is the hand, and behind them both is the human writer.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ رُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِ — "Then they are returned to Allah, their true Master" (6:62). This is an allusion to their return to Allah, the Glorified, through the resurrection after death. Describing Him, the Exalted, as their "true Master" (مولاهم الحق) is to indicate the cause of all His aforementioned actions—putting to sleep, awakening, governing, causing death, and resurrecting. In this is an analysis of the meaning of "Master" (المولى) and then an affirmation of the right of mastership (المولوية) for Him, the Exalted. The Master is the one who owns the essence (الرقبة), and thus it is His right to dispose of it however He wills. Since He, the Exalted, possesses the reality of ownership (حقيقة الملك), and He is the one who acts through creation (الإيجاد), governance (التدبير), and returning (الإرجاع), He is the true Master for whom the meaning of mastership is established with a permanence that never vanishes in any way.

"The Truth" (الحق) is among the Beautiful Names of Allah because of His establishment, the Exalted, in His essence and attributes with a permanence that accepts no cessation and is impossible to change or transfer. The pronoun in رُدُّوا — "they are returned" refers to the individuals alluded to in the preceding speech: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ — "until, when death comes to one of you" (6:61). For the decree of death encompasses every individual, and through it, their individuals are gathered into the group itself. From here it becomes clear that His saying: ثُمَّ رُدُّوا — "Then they are returned" is not a case of transition (الالتفات) from the previous second-person address to the third-person.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلا لَهُ الْحُكْمُ — "Look! All judgment belongs to Him" (6:62), etc. When He, the Exalted, explained His exclusive possession of the keys of the Unseen, His knowledge of the manifest Book in which everything is recorded, and His governance of the affairs of His creation from the moment they existed until they return to Him, it became clear that judgment belongs to Him and not to anyone else. This is what He mentioned previously in His saying: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ — "Judgment belongs only to Allah" (6:57). He announced the result of His explanation, saying: أَلا لَهُ الْحُكْمُ — "Look! All judgment belongs to Him," to alert them to what they had neglected.

Similarly, His saying: وَهُوَ أَسْرَعُ الْحاسِبِينَ — "and He is the swiftest of reckoners" (6:62) is another result of the preceding explanation. For it has become clear through it that He, the Exalted, does not delay the reckoning of people's deeds beyond the time suitable for it; rather, what is delayed is only delayed to reach the term that was appointed for it.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَنْ يُنَجِّيكُمْ مِنْ ظُلُماتِ الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ تَدْعُونَهُ — "Say, ‘Who delivers you from the darkness of land and sea, [when] you call Him’" (6:63) until the end of the verse. It seems that what is meant by deliverance from the "darkness of land and sea" is salvation from the hardships that afflict a human being during travels, whether traversing the land or sailing the sea—such as severe cold, rain, snow, highwaymen, storms, and the like. These are most difficult for a human being in the darkness of night, or clouds, or wind that stirs the dust of the earth, increasing the person's agitation, confusion, and loss of any way to repel it. For this reason, deliverance was linked to "darkness." The original meaning was an inquiry into who delivers the human being from the hardships he is afflicted with in his travels on land and sea. The hardships were attributed to the land and sea by way of "circumstantiality" (الظرفية), then attributed to the "darkness of land and sea" because darkness has a complete effect in intensifying these misfortunes. Then the "hardships" were omitted, and "darkness" was put in its place, and deliverance was linked to it; thus it was said: "delivers you from the darkness of land and sea."

Darkness was specifically mentioned—even though the one who delivers from every disliked thing and grief is Allah, the Glorified, as He mentions in the following verse—because travels on land and sea are known to the human being for their toil, hardship, and unpleasantness.

Supplication (التضرع) is the manifestation of "humility" (الضراعة), which is lowliness and submissiveness, as mentioned by al-Raghib. Therefore, it was contrasted with secretly (الخفية), which is hiddenness and concealment. Thus, "supplication" and "secretly" in prayer are its public and private forms. When a misfortune descends upon a human being, he begins by praying for salvation through secrecy and intimate discourse (المناجاة). Then, when it intensifies and some signs of despair and disconnection from causes appear, he does not care about those around him who might see his lowliness and misery, so he prays with "supplication" and loud calling. Thus, the mention of "supplication" and "secretly" is an indication that He, the Exalted, is the Deliverer from the misfortunes of land and sea, both the severe and the light.

In His saying: لَئِنْ أَنْجانا مِنْ هذِهِ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ — "‘If He delivers us from this, we will surely be among the grateful’" (6:63), there is an indication that the human being, in this state for which he prays for relief, adds to his prayer a covenant he offers to his Lord and a promise he makes to Him: that if Allah relieves him, he will surely be among the grateful and turn away from his previous disbelief.

The origin of this promise is taken from the custom prevalent among human individuals with one another. For when one of us is exhausted by all paths and calamity surrounds him—whether a crushing misfortune, poverty, or an enemy—and he seeks help to relieve his distress from one of the powerful who is capable of relieving it in his view, he promises him something to please him and strengthen his resolve and chivalry. This might be through beautiful praise, wealth, obedience, or loyalty. All of this is because the social acts that circulate among us are all transactions based on two parties, in which the human being gives something and takes something, because need surrounds the human being; he cannot perform an act or produce an effect except for a benefit returning to himself. The same applies to the rest of the parts of the universe.

However, Allah, the Glorified, is too noble for need to touch His presence or for any deficiency to occur to Him. He does not perform an act except that its benefit returns to others among His creation. The aspect of monotheism (التوحيد) in the human being's response to Him with the promise of gratitude and obedience in his primordial prayer (الدعاء الفطري) is that when a disaster descends upon him, and causes are cut off from him, and the means of escape vanish from his sight, he finds that Allah, the Glorified, is the sole cause capable of relieving his grief, and that He is the one who has governed his affair since He created him and governs the affair of every cause. He then finds himself a wrongdoer, remiss toward Allah, the Glorified, not deserving the relief of grief and the removal of need from His side, because of the evil deeds his hands have earned and the burden of sin his soul has carried. At that moment, he promises his Lord gratitude and obedience to validate his worthiness for the answering of his prayer and the removal of his harm.

Therefore, we find that when he is delivered from the disaster that befell him, he goes his way, forgetting the covenant he made with his Lord and his promise of gratitude, as He, the Exalted, said at the end of the following verse: ثُمَّ أَنْتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ — "then you ascribe partners [to Him]" (6:64).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ اللهُ يُنَجِّيكُمْ مِنْها وَمِنْ كُلِّ كَرْبٍ ثُمَّ أَنْتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ — "Say, ‘Allah delivers you from it and from every distress, then you ascribe partners [to Him]’" (6:64). Al-Raghib said in his Mufradat: "Distress (الكرب) is severe grief. He, the Exalted, said: وَنَجَّيْناهُ وَأَهْلَهُ مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ — ‘And We delivered him and his family from the great distress’ (37:76). A kurbah is like a ghummah (grief). The origin of that is from karb al-ard (plowing the earth) with a sukun on the ra', which is turning it over by digging; thus, grief stirs the soul as that stirs the earth. It was said in a proverb: ‘The plowing (al-kirab) is upon the oxen,’ and that is not related to their saying: ‘The dogs (al-kilab) are upon the oxen’ at all. It is possible that al-karb is from the sun karabat when it draws near to setting. Their saying: ‘A karban vessel’ means near to being full, like qurban. Or it is from al-karab (with two fathahs), which is a thick knot in the rope of a bucket; grief is sometimes described as a knot upon the heart. It is said: ‘I knotted (akrabtu) the bucket.’" End quote.

In this verse, "every distress" has been added to the "darkness of land and sea" to encompass everything. For no human being, in the span of his life, is free from some distress and grief. Thus, the petition and prayer are general among them, whether they announce it or keep it secret.

The summary of what is intended by the verse is: In the hardships that descend upon you in the darkness of land and sea and elsewhere, when you are cut off from apparent causes and stratagems fail you, you witness—by returning to your human primordial nature (الفطرة)—that Allah, the Glorified, is your Lord, there is no lord besides Him. You become certain that your worship of others is injustice and sin. The evidence for this is that you call upon Him then with supplication and in secret, and you promise Him that you will be grateful to Him thereafter and not disbelieve in Him if He delivers you. But after the deliverance, you break your covenant that you pledged to Him and continue in your previous disbelief. Thus, in the two verses, there is an argument against the polytheists and a rebuke for them for breaking the oath and failing the promise.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ الْقادِرُ عَلى أَنْ يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذاباً مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ — "Say, ‘He is able to send upon you a punishment from above you’" (6:65) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in the Mufradat: "The origin of resurrection/sending (البعث) is stirring a thing and directing it. It is said: ‘I sent it (ba‘athtuhu) and it was sent (fanba‘atha).’ The sending differs according to what it is attached to. ‘I sent the camel’ means I stirred it and made it move. His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَالْمَوْتى يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ — ‘and the dead Allah will raise them’ (6:36) means He will bring them out and move them toward the Resurrection." Until he said: "Sending is of two types: human, like sending a camel or sending a person for a need; and divine, which is of two types: one is the creation of essences, genera, and species from nothing, which is exclusive to the Creator, the Exalted, and no one else is capable of it; and the second is reviving the dead, which He has granted to some of His friends like Jesus (peace be upon him) and his likes." End quote.

In short, the word contains something of the meaning of "standing up" and "arousing." With this consideration, it is used for "directing" and "sending," because directing toward a need and sending toward a people usually occur after stillness and dormancy. Accordingly, the "sending of punishment" is not without an indication that it is a punishment whose nature is to be directed toward them and to befall them; it is only prevented from this exigency by a barrier such as faith and obedience. There is a completion to this discussion that we will provide you.

He said in al-Majma‘: "I confused (لبست) the matter for them when I did not make it clear and mixed some of it with others. I wore (لبست) the garment. Confusion (اللبس) is the mixing of a matter and the mixing of speech. I engaged (لابست) in the matter when I mixed with it. Sects (الشيع) are groups; every group is a shi‘ah on its own. The shi‘ah of so-and-so are his followers. Shiism (التشيع) is following by way of religiosity and loyalty." End quote.

Accordingly, what is meant by His saying: أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعاً — "or confuse you into sects" (6:65) is that He strikes some with others and mixes them while they are different sects and groups.

His saying: قُلْ هُوَ الْقادِرُ عَلى أَنْ يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذاباً مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ أَوْ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ — "Say, ‘He is able to send upon you a punishment from above you or from beneath your feet’" (6:65). Its apparent meaning is the affirmation of power for Allah, the Glorified, to send punishment upon them from above or from below. Power over a thing does not necessitate its performance. This—meaning the affirmation of power over the act, which is the punishment—is sufficient for frightening and warning. However, the context suggests that what is intended is not the mere affirmation of power; rather, they have a desert for such punishment. In the punishment, there is an exigency to be sent upon them if they do not unite upon faith in Allah and His signs, as previously inferred from the meaning of "sending." This is supported by His saying afterward: لِكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ — "For every news there is a settled time, and soon you will know" (6:67), for it is an explicit threat.

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, explicitly threatens this nation with punishment in instances similar to this one in His speech, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ — "For every nation there is a messenger; so when their messenger comes, it is decided between them with justice" (10:47) until He said: وَيَسْتَنْبِئُونَكَ أَحَقٌّ هُوَ قُلْ إِي وَرَبِّي إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ — "They ask you to inform them, ‘Is it true?’ Say, ‘Yes! By my Lord, it is indeed true, and you cannot frustrate [Him]’" (10:53). And His saying: إِنَّ هذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ ، وَتَقَطَّعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ — "Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so worship Me. But they fragmented their affair among themselves" (21:92-93) until the end of the verses. And His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً — "So set your face to the religion as a Hanif" (30:30) until He said: وَلا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ ، مِنَ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكانُوا شِيَعاً — "and do not be among the polytheists—of those who split up their religion and became sects" (30:31-32) until the end of the verses.

It has been said: What is meant by the punishment from above them is the Cry (الصيحة), stones, the flood, and the wind, as was done to ‘Ad, Thamud, the people of Shu‘ayb, and the people of Lot. By that which is from beneath their feet is the swallowing by the earth (الخسف), as was done to Korah. It has also been said: What is meant by what is from above them is the punishment coming from their superiors or their tyrannical sultans, and by what is from beneath their feet is what comes to them from their inferiors or their evil slaves. It has also been said: What is meant by what is from above and what is from below are the lethal firearms that humanity has recently invented—airplanes and balloons that drop incendiary and destructive bombs and others, and underwater vessels that sink ships and steamers. For the warning occurred in His speech, the Exalted, and He knows best what would happen in His kingdom.

The truth is that the wording is such that it accepts application to all the mentioned meanings. Events have occurred after the revelation that the wording applies to. The original source of these events, which paved the way for them, is the disagreement of word and the division that the nation began with, and which they confronted the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) with regarding what he was calling them to—of agreement upon the word of truth: "And that this is My path, straight, so follow it, and do not follow the [other] ways, for they will separate you."

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعاً وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُمْ بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ — "or confuse you into sects and make you taste one another’s violence" (6:65). Its apparent meaning is that it refers to the partisan factions that arose after the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny). This led to the occurrence of diverse schools of thought (مذاهب) that were clothed in the garment of fanaticism and pre-Islamic zeal (الحمية الجاهلية), and resulted in wars and slaughters where each group deemed every sanctity of the other permissible and expelled them, according to their claim, from the sanctity of religion and the core of Islam.

Accordingly, His saying: أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعاً وَيُذِيقَ — "or confuse you into sects and make [you] taste," etc., is one punishment, not two. Even if it is possible in one way to count each of the casting of division in word and the making of some taste the violence of others as an independent punishment in its own right, the division among the nation has another evil effect, which is the occurrence of weakness, the exhaustion of strength, and the fragmentation of power. However, what is taken in the verse and counted as a punishment—meaning His saying: وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُمْ — "and make you taste," etc.—is then, in relation to the mere casting of disagreement, in the position of the "restricted" (المقيد) in relation to the "absolute" (المطلق). It is not good to contrast the absolute with the restricted except with an additional consideration in the speech. Furthermore, the coordination with the "and" of gathering (واو الجمع) supports what we have mentioned.

In short, the meaning of the verse is: Say, O Messenger of Allah, addressing them and warning them of the consequence of their refusal to gather under the banner of monotheism and listen to the call of truth: Indeed, for this state of yours, there is an evil consequence within the power of Allah, the Glorified, to seize you with. It is that He sends upon you a punishment from which there is no escape and no refuge to seek—which is the punishment from above you or from beneath your feet—or that He strikes some of you with others, so you become different sects and groups, contending and warring, and He makes some of you taste the violence of others. Then He completed the explanation by saying, addressing His Prophet: انْظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ الْآياتِ لَعَلهم يَفْقَهُونَ — "Look how We dispense the signs so that they may understand" (6:65). The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذَّبَ بِهِ قَوْمُكَ وَهُوَ الْحَقُّ قُلْ لَسْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِوَكِيلٍ — "Your people have denied it, though it is the truth. Say, ‘I am not a trustee over you’" (6:66). The people of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) are Quraysh, or Mudar, or the Arabs in general. What is understood from the gist of some of His speech, the Exalted, in other places is that what is meant by his people (peace be upon him and his progeny) are the Arabs, such as His saying: وَلَوْ نَزَّلْناهُ عَلى بَعْضِ الْأَعْجَمِينَ ، فَقَرَأَهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ مُؤْمِنِينَ ، كَذلِكَ سَلَكْناهُ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ ، لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ حَتَّى يَرَوُا الْعَذابَ الْأَلِيمَ ، فَيَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ — "Had We sent it down to some of the non-Arabs, and he had recited it to them, they would not have believed in it. Thus do We let it pass through the hearts of the guilty: they do not believe in it until they sight the painful punishment, so that it comes upon them suddenly while they are unaware" (26:198-202). And His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسانِ قَوْمِهِ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ — "We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people, so that he might make [Our messages] clear to them" (14:4).

In any case, His saying: وَكَذَّبَ بِهِ قَوْمُكَ وَهُوَ الْحَقُّ — "Your people have denied it, though it is the truth" is in the position of a preamble to realize the news (النبأ) contained in the previous warning. It is as if it were said: O nation, gather in the monotheism of your Lord and agree in following the word of truth; otherwise, no believer can secure you from a punishment that comes to you from above or from below, or from disagreement and partisanship that results in the sword and the whip of some of you upon others. Then the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) was addressed and told: Indeed, your people have denied that, so let them prepare for a wretched punishment or a severe violence they will taste.

From here, several points become clear: First, the pronoun in His saying: وَكَذَّبَ بِهِ — "denied it" refers to the punishment, as al-Alusi attributed to the majority of commentators. It has also been said that it refers to the "dispensing of the signs" or to the Quran, which is remote. It is not remote that it refers to the "news" (النبأ) considering what the previous verse contains.

Second, this report—meaning His saying: وَكَذَّبَ بِهِ قَوْمُكَ وَهُوَ الْحَقُّ —according to what the context provides, is in the sense of mentioning the first report that paves the way for a promised news. It is as if it were said: It is obligatory for your nation to gather upon faith in Allah and His signs, and to be on guard and cautious lest disbelief in Allah and His signs seep into them and disagreement creep among them, so that the punishment of Allah, the Glorified, does not descend upon them. Then it was said: Indeed, your people—from among your entire nation and those who were your contemporaries or came after you from the people of the world—hastened to break what they were obligated to consolidate and denied the news. Thus, the matter was breached, and they will soon know. For those who denied the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), or the Quran, or this punishment, were not the Bedouins specifically—who are his people (peace be upon him and his progeny)—but rather the Jews and nations other than them denied him in his time and after him. Their denial and disagreement were all an established factor in what they were threatened with of punishment. Thus, specifying the denial of his people for mention in this state yields what we have mentioned.

The analytical study of the psychology of the Islamic society supports what we have inferred from the verse. For what the Islamic nation is afflicted with today—of decline in their psychology, weakness in their strength, and fragmentation in their word—ultimately leads, according to analysis, to what arose from the disagreements and quarrels in the first period after the passing of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny). Then it ascends to the events of the beginning of the Hijrah and before the Hijrah—of what the Prophet encountered from his people, and what they confronted him with of denial and deeming his opinion foolish.

These people, even if they gathered around the banner of the Islamic call and sought shade under its shadow after the word of truth appeared and its torch shone, the good religious society was not purified from the filth of hypocrisy (النفاق). Numerous verses of the Holy Quran have spoken of that, and the people of hypocrisy were not insignificant in number. It is impossible for the structure of society to be safe from their evil effect on the psychology of its parts. Their fuel did not remain without igniting, then it increased in ignition and has not ceased. Everything returns to what it began from, and "all the game is in the belly of the wild ass."

Third, His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَسْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِوَكِيلٍ — "Say, ‘I am not a trustee over you’" is phrased as a metonymy (كناية); that is, turn away from them and say: Your affair is not delegated to me, nor is it my responsibility such that I should prevent you from this denial as a piece of advice to you. Rather, what is mine according to my station is to warn you of a severe punishment that is lying in wait for you.

From here it also becomes clear that His saying: لِكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ — "For every news there is a settled time, and soon you will know" (6:67) is part of the commanded speech and the completion of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his progeny) speech to his people. This is supported by the address in His saying: وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ — "and soon you will know," for the people are in the position of being addressed in relation to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), not in relation to Him, the Exalted.

His saying: لِكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ is an explicit threat and an announcement of inevitable occurrence. It has become clear from what preceded the reason for the correctness of addressing the polytheists with what the Islamic nation will be afflicted with—of the fragmentation of the word and the descent of hardship. For the roots lead back to them, and people are but one nation; the last of them is seized for what the first of them earned, and what appears in the last of them returns to the first of them—whether they know it or not, whether they see it in themselves or are blind to it. He, the Exalted, said: بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ يَلْعَبُونَ ، فَارْتَقِبْ يَوْمَ تَأْتِي السَّماءُ بِدُخانٍ مُبِينٍ يَغْشَى النَّاسَ هذا عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ رَبَّنَا اكْشِفْ عَنَّا الْعَذابَ إِنَّا مُؤْمِنُونَ أَنَّى لَهُمُ الذِّكْرى وَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مُبِينٌ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّوْا عَنْهُ وَقالُوا مُعَلَّمٌ مَجْنُونٌ إِنَّا كاشِفُوا الْعَذابِ قَلِيلاً إِنَّكُمْ عائِدُونَ — "Rather they are in doubt, playing. So watch out for the day when the sky brings a manifest smoke, enveloping the people: ‘This is a painful punishment.’ ‘Our Lord! Remove the punishment from us; indeed we are believers.’ How will they take admonition, when there has already come to them a manifest apostle, then they turned away from him and said, ‘[He is] tutored, a madman’? Indeed We will withdraw the punishment a little, [but] you will indeed revert [to your old ways]" (44:9-15).

Ponder the verses: how the last of them were seized for what the first of them committed. These are among the verses of Surah Yunus, al-Anbiya, and al-Rum previously cited. In the Noble Quran, there are many verses announcing what will meet the nation of the dire consequence and the burden of the evil deed, then the reaching of divine care. It is among the worst shortcomings for our researchers to neglect the study of these noble verses, despite their abundance, importance, and intense relevance to the state of the nation and the success of its fortune in this world and the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِي آياتِنا فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ — "When you see those who gossip regarding Our signs, turn away from them until they gossip about other talk" (6:68). Al-Raghib mentioned in the Mufradat that gossiping/wading (الخوض) is entering into water and passing through it. It is used metaphorically for matters, and most of what appeared in the Quran appeared in what is censured to enter into. End quote. It is entering into false speech and becoming deeply involved in it, such as mentioning the true signs and mocking them and prolonging that.

What is meant by "turning away" (الإعراض) is not participating with them in what they are gossiping about, such as standing up from them and leaving their presence, or what is similar to that by which non-participation is realized. Restricting the prohibition with His saying: حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ — "until they gossip about other talk" is to indicate that what is prohibited is not absolutely sitting with them and staying with them, even if it were for a true purpose. Rather, what is prohibited is sitting with them as long as they are occupied with gossiping regarding the signs of Allah, the Glorified.

From here it becomes clear that in the speech there is a type of "conciseness by omission" (إيجاز الحذف). For the implied speech is: "When you see those who gossip regarding Our signs gossiping in them, then turn away from them," etc. The clause similar to the relative clause was omitted because it was dispensed with by it. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: When you see the people of gossiping and mockery regarding the signs of Allah continuing in their gossiping and mockery of the divine signs, then turn away from them and do not enter their circles until they gossip about other talk. When they enter into other talk, there is no barrier preventing you from sitting with them. Although the speech occurred in the context of arguing against the polytheists, the criterion (الملاك) alluded to generalizes it to include others as it includes them. At the end of the verse, His saying occurred: فَلا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ الذِّكْرى مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "do not sit with the wrongdoing people after [you] remember" (6:68). Thus, gossiping regarding the signs of Allah is injustice (ظلم), and the verse only prohibited participating with the wrongdoers in their injustice. It has appeared in another instance of His speech, the Exalted: إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً مِثْلُهُمْ — "you will then be like them" (4.140).

It has become clear that the verse does not command turning away from those who gossip regarding the signs of Allah, the Exalted, absolutely; rather, it only commands turning away from them if they are gossiping regarding the signs of Allah, as long as they are occupied with it.

The pronoun in His saying: غَيْرِهِ — "other [than it]" refers to the "talk" (الحديث) that is being gossiped about regarding the signs of Allah, considering that it is "gossiping" (خوض), and gossiping has been prohibited in the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِمَّا يُنْسِيَنَّكَ الشَّيْطانُ فَلا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ الذِّكْرى مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "And should Satan cause you to forget, do not sit with the wrongdoing people after [you] remember" (6:68). The إِمَّا in His saying إِمَّا يُنْسِيَنَّكَ is extra, yielding a type of emphasis or reduction, and the nun is for emphasis. The original is "and if he causes you to forget" (وإن ينسك). The speech is in the position of emphasizing and intensifying the prohibition; that is, even if you were heedless of Our prohibition due to what Satan caused you to forget, then you remembered, do not be negligent in standing up from them, and do not linger without standing up from them. For those who are God-conscious (يتقون) have no participation whatsoever with those who gossip, who play with the signs of Allah and mock them.

The address in the verse is to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), but the intended are others from the nation. The discussion regarding the infallibility (عصمة) of the prophets (peace be upon them) has already preceded, which negates the occurrence of this type of forgetfulness—which is the forgetting of a divine ruling and violating it in practice such that his act could be used as an argument against others and held against himself—from them (peace be upon them).

This is supported by the coordination of the speech in the following verse to the God-conscious (المتقين) of the nation, where He says: وَما عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شيءٍ — "Those who are God-conscious are not accountable for them in any way" (6:69) until the end of the verse.

Even clearer in its indication is His saying, the Exalted, in Surah al-Nisa: وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ أَنْ إِذا سَمِعْتُمْ آياتِ اللهِ يُكْفَرُ بِها وَيُسْتَهْزَأُ بِها فَلا تَقْعُدُوا مَعَهُمْ حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً مِثْلُهُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ جامِعُ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَالْكافِرِينَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ جَمِيعاً — "He has already sent down to you in the Book that when you hear Allah’s signs being disbelieved and mocked, do not sit with them until they gossip about other talk. You will then be like them. Indeed Allah will gather the hypocrites and the faithless in hell all together" (4.140). For what is intended in the verse—which is Medinan—by the ruling that was "sent down in the Book" is what is in this verse of Surah al-An‘am—which is Meccan—and there is no other verse. It mentions that the previously revealed ruling was directed to the believers. Its necessity is that the address in His saying: وَإِذا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِي آياتِنا — "When you see those who gossip regarding Our signs," etc., is directed to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) while the intended are others, according to their saying: "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor."

His saying, the Exalted: وَما عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "Those who are God-conscious are not accountable for them in any way" (6:69) until the end of the verse. He intends that the sin these gossipers earn is not carried except by themselves and does not exceed them to others, unless they become like them and participate with them in the act or are pleased with their act. No one is held accountable for an act except its doer. "But [this is] an admonition, so that they may be God-conscious" (6:69). For if a person is present in their gathering, even if it is possible for him not to follow them in what they gossip about and not to be pleased in his heart with their act, and it is possible that his presence with them is not considered an assistance to them in their injustice or a support for them in their speech—yet witnessing the violation and seeing the disobedience makes the matter of disobedience easy for the soul and makes the sin small in the eye of the witness. If its matter becomes easy, the person is on the verge of falling into it. For the soul has a desire (هوى) in every disobedience. It is obligatory for the God-conscious person, by virtue of the godwariness (التقوى) and piety (الورع) he possesses regarding the prohibitions of Allah, to avoid mixing with the people of desecration and audacity against Allah, just as it is obligatory for those afflicted with that—those gossiping regarding the signs of Allah—lest audacity against Allah and His signs become easy for him, bringing him close to disobedience and thus on the brink of destruction. "Whoever grazes around a sanctuary is on the verge of falling into it."

From this explanation, several points become clear: First, the negation of participation in the reckoning with the gossipers for the God-conscious specifically—even though the non-doer does not participate with the doer in the reward of his act—is only to intimate that whoever participates with them in their gathering and sits with them is not secure from participating with them in the reward of their act and the seizure for what they are seized for. The speech is in the position of our saying: "And those other than the gossipers are not accountable for them in any way if they are God-conscious regarding gossiping with them, but We only prohibit them from sitting with them so that they may continue in their godwariness from gossiping, or so that godwariness and piety regarding the prohibitions of Allah, the Glorified, may be complete for them."

Second, what is meant by godwariness (التقوى) in His saying: وَما عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ — "Those who are God-conscious" is general godwariness (التقوى)—which is the avoidance and guarding against absolutely everything that Allah does not approve of. In His saying: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ — "so that they may be God-conscious," it is godwariness from the specific disobedience of gossiping regarding the signs of Allah. Or what is meant by the first godwariness is the origin of godwariness, and by the second its completion. Or the first is godwariness in summary, and the second is its detail through actual application to every instance, including the instance of gossiping regarding the signs of Allah. There is another meaning here: that what is meant by the first is the godwariness of the believers, and by the second godwariness the godwariness of the gossipers; the implied speech being: "But remind the gossipers as an admonition so that they may be God-conscious regarding gossiping."

Third, His saying: ذكرى — "an admonition" is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb, the implied being: "But We remind them of that as an admonition," or "Remind them as an admonition." Or it is the predicate (خبر) for an omitted subject (مبتدأ), the implied being: "But this matter is an admonition." Or it is a subject for an omitted predicate, the implied being: "But upon you is their admonition." The middle option is the most immediate to the mind.

His saying, the Exalted: وَذَرِ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا دِينَهُمْ لَعِباً وَلَهْواً — "Leave alone those who take their religion for a play and a sport" (6:70) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: "Bals (البسل) is the joining of a thing and its prevention. Because it contains the meaning of joining, it was used metaphorically for the frowning of the face; thus it is said: he is basil and mubtasil of face. Because it contains the meaning of prevention, it was said of the prohibited and the pledged basl. His saying, the Exalted: وَذَكِّرْ بِهِ أَنْ تُبْسَلَ نَفْسٌ بِما كَسَبَتْ — ‘and remind with it, lest a soul should be delivered [to perdition] because of what it has earned’ (6:70); that is, it is deprived of reward. The difference between haram (prohibited) and basl is that haram is general for what was prevented by ruling and force, while basl is what is prevented by force. He, the Mighty and Majestic, said: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ أُبْسِلُوا بِما كَسَبُوا — ‘They are the ones who are delivered [to perdition] because of what they have earned’ (6:70); that is, they were deprived of reward." End quote.

He said in al-Majma‘: "It is said: ‘I delivered him (absaltuhu) for his crime,’ meaning I surrendered him. The one who surrenders himself (المستبسل) is the one who submits, knowing that he is not capable of escape." Until he said: "Al-Akhfash said: tubsal means it is recompensed. It was said: tubsal means it is pledged. The meanings are close." End quote.

The meaning is: "And leave those who took their religion as play and sport." He considered their practicing of religion according to what the desire of their souls calls them to as play and sport with their religion. In this is the assumption of a true religion for them, which is that to which their primordial nature (الفطرة) called them; it was obligatory for them to take it with seriousness and guard it against mixing and distortion. But they took it as play and sport, turning it however they wished from one state to another, and transforming it according to what the desire of their souls commanded them from one form to another.

Then He coordinated with their taking the religion as play and sport His saying: وَغَرَّتْهُمُ الْحياةُ الدُّنْيا — "and the life of the world has deceived them" (6:70), because of the necessary connection between them. For indulgence in the enjoyment of the pleasures of material life and seriousness in acquiring them necessitates turning away from seriousness in the true religion and jesting and playing with it.

Then He said: وَذَكِّرْ بِهِ — "and remind with it"—meaning with the Quran—lest a soul should be "delivered" (تبسل); that is, prevented because of the evil deeds it earned, or surrendered with what it earned to seizure and punishment. That is a soul that has no guardian or intercessor besides Allah. Even if it offers every ransom and every compensation, it will not be taken from it, because today is the day of recompense for deeds, not the day of selling and buying. "They are the ones who are delivered [to perdition]" and prevented from the reward of Allah or surrendered to His punishment. "They shall have a drink of boiling water and a painful punishment because of what they used to disbelieve" (6:70).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَنَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ ما لا يَنْفَعُنا وَلا يَضُرُّنا — "Say, ‘Shall we invoke besides Allah that which neither profits us nor harms us’" (6:71). This is an argument against the polytheists in the form of a rhetorical question. He only mentioned among the attributes of their partners that they neither profit nor harm because the taking of gods—as previously mentioned—was based on one of two foundations: hope and fear. Since the partners neither profit nor harm, there is no motive to invoke them, worship them, or draw near to them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنُرَدُّ عَلى أَعْقابِنا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدانَا اللهُ — "and shall we be turned back on our heels after Allah has guided us" (6:71) until His saying: ائْتِنا — "‘Come to us!’" (6:71). Seduction (الاستهواء) is the seeking of desire and falling. Being "turned back on the heels" is a metonymy for misguidance (الضلال) and the abandonment of guidance (الهدى). For the necessity of true guidance is falling into the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) and beginning to walk in it. Thus, the turning back on the heels is the abandonment of walking in the path and returning to what was left behind of the journey, which is misguidance. Therefore, He said: "and shall we be turned back on our heels after Allah has guided us," restricting the turning back to being after the divine guidance.

Among the strange deductions is the argument of some using this verse—meaning His saying: وَنُرَدُّ عَلى أَعْقابِنا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدانَا اللهُ—and verses that follow its course, such as the saying of Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) as Allah, the Exalted, recounted in his story: قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يا شُعَيْبُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَكَ مِنْ قَرْيَتِنا أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا قالَ أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كارِهِينَ ، قَدِ افْتَرَيْنا عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً إِنْ عُدْنا فِي مِلَّتِكُمْ بَعْدَ إِذْ نَجَّانَا اللهُ مِنْها وَما يَكُونُ لَنا أَنْ نَعُودَ فِيها إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ رَبُّنا — "The elite of his people who were arrogant said, ‘O Shu‘ayb, we will surely expel you and those who have faith with you from our town, or else you shall revert to our creed.’ He said, ‘What! Even if we should be unwilling? We would be fabricating a lie against Allah should we revert to your creed after Allah has delivered us from it. It does not behoove us to revert to it unless Allah, our Lord, should wish’" (7:88-89).

They argued from these that the prophets (peace be upon them) were upon disbelief before their mission and their being clothed in the garment of prophethood, because of the indication in the wording of "being turned back on the heels after Allah has guided" and "returning to the creed of polytheism after Allah delivered them from it" of their being adherents to it and falling into it before the deliverance. This is a false argument. For that was their speaking with the tongue of the religious society whose individuals were upon polytheism until Allah guided them by means of His prophets. We do not mean that the predominance of individuals who were upon polytheism in the beginning of their era made it permissible to attribute their previous disbelief to everyone, such that it would be a predominance of their polytheism over the faith of their prophet; for His speech, the Truth, does not admit that. Rather, we mean that the religious society encompassing the prophet and his nation—it is true of it that its individuals only escaped polytheism after the guidance of Allah, the Glorified, and they have nothing besides Him except misguidance. As for the nation, they were upon polytheism in a time before the time of their being guided by the religion. As for their prophets, their guidance is only by Allah, the Glorified; they have nothing from themselves—were it not for the divine guidance—except misguidance. For other than Him, the Exalted, does not possess for himself harm or profit. Thus, it is true in their right that it is not for them to be turned back on their heels after Allah has guided them or to return to polytheism after Allah has delivered them from it.

In short, the word is true of them in the manner of reality, even if some of their society—which is the prophet among them—was not a disbeliever before his prophethood. For faith and guidance are, in any case, for them from Allah, the Glorified, after the state they have from themselves; and their state from themselves is misguidance, as you have known.
