Furthermore, you have already known in the preceding diverse discussion regarding the infallibility of the prophets that the Holy Quran is explicit regarding the purity of their presence from the smallest of minor sins, so how much more so from the major ones, and from the greatest of major sins, which is polytheism (الشرك) against Allah, the Great.

His saying: كَالَّذِي اسْتَهْوَتْهُ الشَّياطِينُ فِي الْأَرْضِ حَيْرانَ — "like someone whom the devils have beguiled into wandering bewildered on the earth" (6:71), etc. This is a parable by which the state of the bewildered human being is exemplified—the one who was not given insight into his affair and a firm resolve for his happiness. He left the best and most upright path to his destination, which companions of his had traversed before him, being guided by it. He remained bewildered between devils calling him to ruin and destruction, and companions of his who are guided, who have settled in their stations or are on the verge of arriving, calling him to guidance: "Come to us!" He does not know what to do, being between a place of descent and a level ground.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى اللهِ هُوَ الْهُدى — "Say, ‘Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is the [true] guidance’" (6:71) until the end of the verse. That is, if the matter revolves between the call of Allah, the Glorified—which is that which accords with the primordial nature (الفطرة) and which the primordial nature calls the guidance of Allah—and the call of the devils—in which there is desire (الهوى) and the taking of religion as play and sport—then the guidance of Allah is the true guidance, and not anything else.

As for the fact that what accords with the call of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — is the guidance of Allah (هدى الله), there is no doubt concerning it. This is because the right of guidance (حق الهداية) is that which is articulated by the very act of fashioning and origination (الصنع والإيجاد), which belongs to none but Allah. We do not seek anything of religion or creed except to pursue correspondence with reality, and reality belongs to Allah; thus, His guidance does not bypass it. As for the fact that the guidance of Allah is the true guidance (الهدى الحقيقي) which must be followed to the exclusion of the satanic call, this is also evident. This is because Allah, the Glorified, is the One to Whom all our affairs return, regarding our beginning and our end, and all that we require in this world or the Hereafter.

His saying: وَأُمِرْنَا لِنُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ (and we have been commanded to submit to the Lord of all the worlds). It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: "The Arabs say: 'I commanded you so that you may do' (أمرتك لتفعل), 'I commanded you that you do' (أمرتك أن تفعل), and 'I commanded you with doing' (أمرتك بأن تفعل). Whoever says 'I commanded you with doing,' the particle ba (ب) denotes attachment (الإلصاق), meaning the command fell upon this action. Whoever says 'I commanded you that you do,' the preposition is omitted. Whoever says 'I commanded you so that you may do,' the meaning is 'I commanded you for the sake of the action.'" Al-Zajjaj said: "The underlying structure is: 'We were commanded so that we may submit' (أمرنا كي نسلم)."

The sentence—meaning His saying: وَأُمِرْنَا لِنُسْلِمَ etc.—is an explanatory apposition (عطف تفسير) for His saying: إِنَّ هُدَى اللَّهِ هُوَ الْهُدَى (Indeed, the guidance of Allah is the [true] guidance). Thus, the command to submit (الإسلام) is the instantiation (مصداق) of Allah's guidance. The meaning is: Allah commanded us so that we may submit to Him. The agent of the verb was left implicit to serve as a preamble for placing His saying: لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ (to the Lord of all the worlds) in the position of the pronoun, thereby indicating the cause (علة) of the command. Thus, the meaning is: We were commanded from the realm of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — to submit to Allah because He is the Lord of all the worlds; there is no other lord for all of them, nor other lords for parts of them, as the pagans (الوثنية) claim.

The apparent sense of the verse is that what is intended by submission (الإسلام) is the surrendering of all affairs to Him, the Exalted, not merely the utterance of the two testimonies (الشهادتين). This is evident in His saying: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ (Indeed, with Allah religion is submission [Islam]) (3:19), as previously discussed in the commentary (تفسير) of that verse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَاتَّقُوهُ (and [we were commanded] to maintain the prayer and be God-conscious of Him). This is a stylistic variation (تفنن) in the discourse by taking the "command" (الأمر) in the sense of "saying" (القول) and proceeding according to this consideration. It is as if it were said: "And it was said to us: Submit to the Lord of all the worlds, and maintain the prayer, and be God-conscious of Him."

He has summarized the details of religious acts a second time in His saying: وَاتَّقُوهُ (and be God-conscious of Him), except that He explicitly mentioned the prayer (الصلاة) by name among them to magnify its status and emphasize its importance. The concern of the Noble Quran for the matter of prayer is evident and beyond doubt.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (and it is He to whom you will be mustered). Therefore, it is mandatory to submit to Him and be God-conscious (يتقى) of Him, because the return is to Him, and the reckoning (الحساب) and recompense (الجزاء) are in His hand.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ (It is He who created the heavens and the earth with the truth) until the end of the verse. These are several names and attributes of Him, the Glorified, mentioned to clarify and provide the rationale (تعليل) for the preceding discourse. For He, the Exalted, mentioned that guidance is His guidance, then explained it in a way through submission to Him, prayer, and godwariness (التقوى) — which constitutes the entirety of religion. Then He explained the reason why His guidance is the guidance that one is not permitted to deviate from, which is that the mustering (حشر) of all is toward Him.

Then He explained it most fully by His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ etc. These are names and attributes (نعوت) of Him, the Exalted; if even one were absent, the explanation would not be complete.

His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ etc., intends that the entirety of creation (الخلقة) is His act, and He brought it about with truth (بالحق), not in vain (بالباطل). When an act is not in vain, there is no escape from the necessity of an ultimate purpose (الغاية) for it. Thus, for creation there is a purpose, which is the return to Him, the Exalted. This is one of the two arguments He mentioned in His saying, exalted is the Speaker: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَاطِلًا ذَلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who are faithless) until the end of the two verses (38:27). Thus, the creation of the heavens and the earth as a true creation leads to the fact that the creation shall be mustered unto Him.

His saying: يَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ (on the day He says, ‘Be!’ and it is). The context indicates that what is addressed by the word is the Day of Mustering (يوم الحشر), even if every created existent is brought about in this manner, as He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ (All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is) (36:82). "Day" (يوم) is an adverbial qualifier (ظرف) related to the "saying" (القول). The meaning is: The day He says to the Day of Resurrection: "Be," and it is. It has been said that the addressed object is "the thing" (الشيء), and the underlying structure is: "The day He says to a thing: Be, and it is." However, what we have mentioned is more in accord with the context.

His saying: قَوْلُهُ الْحَقُّ (His word is the truth) is a rationale (تعليل) for the sentence preceding it; the evidence for this is the separation (فصل) of the sentence. The "truth" (الحق) is that which is established (الثابت) by the reality of the meaning of being established, which is external existence (الوجود الخارجي) and concrete being (الكون العيني). Since His word is His act and His bringing into existence (إيجاده), as indicated by His saying: وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ, then His saying, the Exalted, is the truth itself; there is no repelling it, nor any changer of His words. He, the Exalted, said: وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ (and I speak the truth) (38:84).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَهُ الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ (and to Him belongs the kingdom on the day the Trumpet is blown). He intends by it the Day of Resurrection. He, the Exalted, said: يَوْمَ هُمْ بَارِزُونَ لَا يَخْفَى عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ (The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah. ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’) (40:16). The intent behind affirming the kingdom (الملك) for Him, the Exalted, on the day the Trumpet is blown—despite the fact that the kingdom belongs to Him eternally—is the manifestation of that fact through the severing of causes (الأسباب) and the cutting off of ties and lineages. Some fragments of this discussion have preceded, and a full investigation of it and the meaning of the Trumpet (الصورة) will come in its appropriate place, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

His saying: عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ (Knower of the Unseen and the manifest). Its meaning has preceded. It is a name through whose meaning the reckoning and recompense are sustained. Likewise are the two names: the All-Wise (الحكيم) and the All-Aware (الخبير). Thus, He, the Exalted, by His knowledge of the Unseen and the manifest, knows the outward (ظاهر) and inward (باطن) of things; no outward thing is hidden from Him due to its manifestation, nor any inward thing due to its hiddenness. By His wisdom (حكمة), He perfects the governance of creation and distinguishes the necessary recompense as is appropriate, so He does not wrong nor act haphazardly. By His awareness (خبرة), no subtle thing escapes Him due to its subtlety, nor any grand thing due to its grandeur.

These names and attributes clarify most fully that all shall be mustered unto Him, and that His guidance is the [true] guidance, and that the religion of the primordial nature (دين الفطرة) which He commanded is the true religion. For He, the Exalted, created the world for a desired purpose He intended from it, which is the return to Him. Since He intends it, He will say to it "Be," and it is, because His word is truth and cannot be repelled. On that day, it will become manifest that the kingdom belongs to Him; no other thing has authority (سلطنة) over anything else. At that point, those who obeyed Him will be distinguished from those who disobeyed Him by His distinction, for He knows every unseen and manifest thing through wisdom and awareness.

From what has preceded, it has become clear: First, that His saying: بِالْحَقِّ (with the truth) intends that the creation of the heavens and the earth is a true creation, meaning that "truth" is its description. The meaning of His act and word being "truth" has recently preceded. As for what has been said—that the meaning is "He created the heavens and the earth by the true word"—it is far-fetched.

Second, that the apparent sense of His saying: وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ, by the indication of the context, is an explanation of the matter of the Day of Resurrection, even if the matter in the creation of all things follows this method.

Third, that the specific mention of the blowing of the Trumpet (نفخ الصور) among the descriptions of the Resurrection in His saying: وَلَهُ الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ is to point to the meaning of the general summoning (الإحضار العام), which is appropriate for the explanation of His saying at the end of the previous verse: وَهُوَ الَّذِي إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ. For mustering (الحشر) is the bringing forth of people and their being moved together with a kind of agitation. The Trumpet is only blown for the gathering of the members of an army for a matter of concern to them. Therefore, the Trumpet is blown—meaning the second blast—on the Day of Resurrection so that they may be present at the arena of mustering for the final judgment. He, the Exalted, said: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَإِذَا هُمْ مِنَ الْأَجْدَاثِ إِلَى رَبِّهِمْ يَنْسِلُونَ (And the Trumpet will be blown, and behold, they will emerge from the graves, hastening toward their Lord) until He said: إِنْ كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ . فَالْيَوْمَ لَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئًا وَلَا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (It will be but a single cry, and behold, they will all be brought before Us. Today no soul shall be wronged in the least, nor shall you be requited except for what you used to do) (36:51-54).

The word "day" (اليوم) in the two places does not carry the same meaning. In the first instance, it intends an absolute temporal container (ظرف) like the container for the Day of Resurrection through a kind of rhetorical consideration, as when we say: "The day Allah created motion" or "When Allah created the days and nights," whereas the "day" is a derivative of motion and branches from it, and "time" (الحين) is the day and the night. The "day" intended in the second instance is the Day of Resurrection itself.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَقُصُّ الْحَقَّ (He relates the truth), al-Daraqutni in al-Ifrad and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b that he said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله taught a man to recite: يَقُصُّ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْفَاصِلِينَ (He relates the truth, and He is the best of those who decide)."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَعِنْدَهُ مَفَاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لَا يَعْلَمُهَا إِلَّا هُوَ (With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him), Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Hashish ibn Asram in al-Istiqamah, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Umar that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "مفاتيح الغيب خمس لا يعلمها إلا الله: لا يعلم ما في غد إلا الله، ولا يعلم متى تغيض الأرحام إلا الله، ولا يعلم متى يأتي المطر أحد إلا الله، ولا تدري نفس بأي أرض تموت إلا الله، ولا يعلم أحد متى تقوم الساعة إلا الله تبارك وتعالى" — "The keys of the Unseen are five; none knows them except Allah: none knows what is in tomorrow except Allah; none knows when the wombs will fall short except Allah; no one knows when the rain will come except Allah; no soul knows in what land it will die except Allah; and no one knows when the Hour will be established except Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He."

I say: This narration, assuming its authenticity, should not be considered contradictory to what preceded regarding the generality of the verse, because a number does not imply exclusivity (لا مفهوم له). What is mentioned in the narration of the "keys" is unified by the knowledge of events before their occurrence, and the Unseen (الغيب) has other instances besides these five, by the evidence of the verse itself.

In the same source, al-Khatib narrated in his Tarikh with a weak chain from Ibn Umar that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "ما من زرع على وجه الأرض ولا ثمار على أشجار ـ إلا عليها مكتوب ـ بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم هذا رزق فلان ابن فلان ، وذلك قوله تعالى : وَما تَسْقُطُ مِنْ وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُها ـ وَلا حَبَّةٍ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْأَرْضِ وَلا رَطْبٍ وَلا يابِسٍ ـ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ" — "There is no crop on the face of the earth, nor any fruit on trees, except that it is written upon it: 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful; this is the provision of so-and-so, son of so-and-so.' And that is His saying, the Exalted: 'Not a leaf falls but He knows it, nor a grain in the darkness of the earth, nor anything fresh or withered but it is in a manifest Book.'"

I say: Despite the weakness of its chain, the content of the narration does not correspond to the verse in that exact manner.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Abu al-Rabi‘ al-Shami, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah: وَمَا تَسْقُطُ مِنْ وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُهَا until His saying إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مُبِينٍ. He said: 'الورقة السقط، والحبة الولد، وظلمات الأرض الأرحام ـ والرطب ما يحيى، واليابس ما يغيض، وكل ذلك في كتاب مبين' — 'The leaf is the miscarriage, the grain is the child, the darkness of the earth is the wombs, the fresh is that which lives, and the withered is that which is lost; and all of that is in a manifest Book.'"

I say: It was also narrated by al-Kulayni and al-Saduq from Abu al-Rabi‘ from him, and by al-Qummi without a chain (مرسلاً). The narration does not correspond to the literal sense (ظاهر) of the verse. Similar to it is another narration narrated by al-Ayyashi from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id from Abu al-Hasan عليه‌السلام.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ الْقَادِرُ عَلَى أَنْ يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ (Say, ‘He is able to send upon you a punishment from above you’). He said: "The tyrannical rulers." أَوْ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ (or from under your feet). "The evil slaves and those in whom there is no good." He said: "And this is narrated from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام."

He said: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا (or to confound you as [hostile] factions). It was said: "He means striking some of you with others—through the enmity and partisanship (العصبية) He casts among you." And this is narrated from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام. He said: Regarding His saying: وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُمْ بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ (and make you taste one another’s violence). It was said: "It is bad neighborliness." And this is narrated from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ. He said: "The unjust ruler." أَوْ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ. He said: "The lowly and those in whom there is no good." أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا. He said: "Partisanship." وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُمْ بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ. He said: "Bad neighborliness."

Al-Qummi said: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: هُوَ الْقَادِرُ عَلَى أَنْ يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ. He said: "It is the smoke and the cry (الصيحة)." أَوْ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ. He said: "It is the swallowing by the earth (الخسف)." أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا. "It is disagreement in religion and some of you defaming others." وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُمْ بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ. "It is some of you killing others. All of this is among the people of the Qiblah." Allah says: انْظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ الْآيَاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَفْقَهُونَ (Look how We dispense the signs in various ways so that they may understand).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd al-Razzaq, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Nu‘aym ibn Hammad in al-Fitan, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah who said: "When this verse was revealed: قُلْ هُوَ الْقَادِرُ عَلَى أَنْ يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'أعوذ بوجهك' — 'I seek refuge in Your Face.' أَوْ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ. He said: 'أعوذ بوجهك' — 'I seek refuge in Your Face.' أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُمْ بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ. He said: 'هذا أهون أو أيسر' — 'This is lighter or easier.'"

I say: Something close to this was also narrated by Ibn Mardawayh from Jabir.

In the same source, Ahmad and al-Tirmidhi (who graded it fair), Nu‘aym ibn Hammad in al-Fitan, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Sa‘d ibn Abi Waqqas from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding this verse: قُلْ هُوَ الْقَادِرُ عَلَى أَنْ يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ ـ أَوْ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "أما إنها كائنة ولم يأت تأويلها بعد" — "Indeed, it shall happen, but its interpretation has not yet come."

I say: There are many narrations narrated through Sunni paths and other narrations through Shi‘i paths from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام stating that what Allah promised in the verse of punishment descending from above them and from under their feet—meaning the cry and the swallowing by the earth—will occur to this nation. As for their being confounded as factions and tasting one another's violence, its occurrence is already a settled matter.

Al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur and Ibn Kathir in his Tafsir have narrated many reports indicating that when the verse قُلْ هُوَ الْقَادِرُ عَلَى أَنْ يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ until its end was revealed, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sought refuge with his Lord and supplicated Him not to punish his nation with the types of punishment He had promised them. His Lord answered him regarding some and did not answer him regarding others, which was that He would not confound them as factions nor make them taste one another's violence.

These narrations—despite their abundance—even if they include both strong and weak ones in terms of their chains, are all weakened by their contradiction to the literal sense of the verse. For the saying of the Exalted in the following two verses: وَكَذَّبَ بِهِ قَوْمُكَ وَهُوَ الْحَقُّ قُلْ لَسْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِوَكِيلٍ ، لِكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (Your people have impugned it, though it is the truth. Say, ‘I am not a trustee over you.’ For every prophecy there is a settled term, and soon you will know) (6:66-67) is an explicit threat of occurrence. These verses—which are from Surah al-An‘am—were revealed all at once, and Allah commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to convey that to his nation. If there were any change in the divine will (بداء) regarding the removal of the affliction, it would have been mandatory for us to find it in His speech, which falsehood cannot approach from before it or from behind it. There is no trace of that; rather, the matter is to the contrary, as preceded in the previous commentary that several verses of the Noble Quran support these verses in their content, such as those in Surah Yunus, al-Rum, and others.

Furthermore, they contradict many other narrations from the paths of both groups indicating that this will occur and descend upon the nation in the future.

Moreover, these narrations—despite their abundance and the agreement of many of them that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله only supplicated with these matters following the revelation of this verse: قُلْ هُوَ الْقَادِرُ عَلَى أَنْ يَبْعَثَ—do not agree on the number of matters; in some, they were three, and in others, four. Nor do they agree on the number of those answered; in some, it was one, and in others, two. Nor do they agree on the matters themselves; in some, they were stoning from the sky, drowning from the earth, that He not confound them as factions, and that He not make them taste one another's violence. In some, they were drowning, famine (السنة), and making their violence among themselves. In some, they were general famine, that He not give an enemy from other than them mastery over them, and that He not make them taste one another's violence. In some, the matters were that He not gather his nation upon misguidance, that He not make an enemy from other than them prevail over them, that He not destroy them with famines, and that He not confound them as factions and make them taste one another's violence. In some, they were that He not make an enemy from other than them prevail over them, that He not destroy them by drowning, and that He not make their violence among themselves. In some, they were that He not destroy them with what He destroyed those before them, that He not make an enemy from other than them prevail over them, and that He not confound them as factions and make them taste one another's violence. In some, they were the punishment from above them and from under their feet, that He confound them as factions, and that He make them taste one another's violence.

Furthermore, in many of them, his supplication صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was in Harrah Bani Mu‘awiyah, a village of the Ansar in the highlands (العالية), which necessitates it being after the Migration (الهجرة), while Surah al-An‘am is among the surahs revealed in Mecca before the Migration all at once. There are other discrepancies in the narrations that become apparent to whoever reviews them.

If one must take something from the narrations, the proper way is to choose what was narrated from Abd al-Razzaq, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh from Shaddad ibn Aws, tracing it back to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: "إن الله زوي لي الأرض ـ حتى رأيت مشارقها ومغاربها ، وإن ملك أمتي سيبلغ ما زوي لي منها ، وإني أعطيت الكنزين : الأحمر والأبيض ، وإني سألت ربي أن لا يهلك قومي بسنة عامة ـ وأن لا يلبسهم شيعا ـ ولا يذيق بعضهم بأس بعض ، فقال : يا محمد إني إذا قضيت قضاء فإنه لا يرد ، وإني أعطيتك لأمتك أن لا أهلكهم بسنة عامة ـ ولا أسلط عليهم عدوا من سواهم فيهلكوهم ـ حتى يكون بعضهم يهلك بعضا ـ وبعضهم يقتل بعضا ، وبعضهم يسبي بعضا ـ" — "Indeed, Allah folded the earth for me until I saw its easts and its wests. The kingdom of my nation shall reach what was folded for me of it. I was given the two treasures: the red and the white. I asked my Lord that He not destroy my people with a general famine, that He not confound them as factions, and that He not make them taste one another's violence. He said: 'O Muhammad, when I decree a decree, it is not repelled. I have granted you for your nation that I will not destroy them with a general famine, nor will I give mastery over them to an enemy from other than them who would destroy them, until some of them destroy others, some of them kill others, and some of them take others captive.'"

Then the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "إني أخاف على أمتي الأئمة المضلين ـ فإذا وضع السيف في أمتي لم يرفع عنهم إلى يوم القيامة" — "I fear for my nation the misguiding leaders. When the sword is placed among my nation, it shall not be lifted from them until the Day of Resurrection."

This narration and what is in its content are free from most of the previous objections. It does not state that the supplication was immediately following the revelation of the verse. It should, nevertheless, be interpreted to mean the removal of general destruction and the general famine that annihilates the nation; otherwise, the famines, exemplary punishments, and massive slaughters that the nation encountered in the Mongol and Crusader wars, in Andalusia, and elsewhere are things that cannot be denied. It should also be interpreted to mean that the supplication and request were in the early days of the Mission (البعثة) before the revelation of the Surah; otherwise, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is more knowledgeable of the station of his Lord and of too great a status to receive these verses by revelation and then refer back to his Lord to change what He had decreed and commanded him to convey and warn his nation with.

After all is said and done, the Noble Quran indicates by its verses the core of the matter: that this religion is standing until the Day of Resurrection, that the nation will not be annihilated entirely, and that this nation will be afflicted with the likes of what the previous nations were afflicted with, step by step (حذو النعل بالنعل) without any difference or deviation. The widely transmitted (المستفيضة) narrations narrated from the Prophet and the Imams from his Ahl al-Bayt صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, which are certain in their issuance and indication, speak of that.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Nahhas narrated in his al-Nasikh from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: قُلْ لَسْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِوَكِيلٍ (Say, ‘I am not a trustee over you’). He said: "This verse was abrogated by the Verse of the Sword: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ (kill the polytheists wherever you find them)."

I say: You have known from the preceding explanation that His saying: قُلْ لَسْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِوَكِيلٍ is phrased as a preamble to the threat contained in His saying: لِكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ. This meaning does not accept abrogation (نسخ).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِي آيَاتِنَا (When you see those who gossip about Our signs) until the end of the verse, with his chain to Abd al-A‘la ibn A‘yan, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'من كان يؤمن بالله واليوم الآخر ـ فلا يجلس في مجلس يسب فيه إمام أو يغتاب فيه مسلم ـ فإن الله يقول في كتابه : إِذا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِي آياتِنا ـ فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ ـ وَإِمَّا يُنْسِيَنَّكَ الشَّيْطانُ ـ فَلا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ الذِّكْرى مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ' — 'Whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day should not sit in a gathering where an Imam is insulted or a Muslim is backbitten, for Allah says in His Book: "When you see those who gossip about Our signs, avoid them until they engage in some other discourse. And if Satan makes you forget, then, after remembering, do not sit with the wrongdoing people."'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Abu Nu‘aym in al-Hilyah narrated from Abu Ja‘far that he said: "Do not sit with the people of disputations (أهل الخصومات), for they are those who gossip about the signs of Allah."

In the same source, Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Muhammad ibn Ali that he said: "The people of whims (أصحاب الأهواء) are among those who gossip about the signs of Allah."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Rib‘i ibn Abdillah from someone who mentioned it from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah: وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِي آيَاتِنَا. He said: "Speaking about Allah and arguing about the Quran. فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ. He said: 'Among them are the storytellers (القصاص).'"

I say: The narrations—as you see—generalize the verse, which is an application of the underlying principle (الملاك).

In al-Majma‘, Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام said: "When فَلَا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ الذِّكْرَى مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ (do not sit with the wrongdoing people) was revealed, the Muslims said: 'What shall we do? If every time the polytheists mock the Quran we stand and leave them, we will not enter the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) nor circumambulate the Sacred House (البيت الحرام).' So Allah, the Exalted, revealed: وَمَا عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ (Those who are God-conscious are not accountable for them in any way), commanding them to remind them as much as they could."

I say: The narration—as you see—is based on taking His saying: ذِكْرَى (reminding) as a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) and returning the two pronouns in His saying: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (so that they may become God-conscious) to the polytheists. The underlying structure is: "But remind them with a reminding so that they may become God-conscious." According to the narration, the Surah remains as having been revealed all at once.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Ibn Jurayj who said: "The polytheists used to sit with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, liking to hear from him. When they heard, they mocked. So وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِي آيَاتِنَا فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ was revealed. He said: 'So whenever they mocked, he stood up. They became cautious and said: "Do not mock, for he will stand up."' That is His saying: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (so that they may be cautious) of gossiping so that you stand up. And there was revealed: وَمَا عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ (Those who are God-conscious are not accountable for them in any way) if you sit with them, but do not sit. Then that was abrogated by His saying in Medina: وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتَابِ أَنْ إِذَا سَمِعْتُمْ until His saying إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا مِثْلُهُمْ (then you will be just like them). It abrogated His saying: وَمَا عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ."

I say: If the verse of Surah al-Nisa’: وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ—which is identical in meaning to His saying: وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ—were an abrogator for His saying: وَمَا عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ, then the latter—meaning His saying: وَمَا عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ—would be an abrogator for His saying: وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ, which is obvious, and the revelation of the Surah all at once rejects this.

Furthermore, the meaning he mentioned does not necessitate a contradiction between the three verses leading to abrogation, such that the second would be an abrogator for the first and abrogated by the third, as is evident.

Similar to this narration is what was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Nahhas in his al-Nasikh from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ. He said: "This is Meccan, abrogated in Medina by His saying: وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتَابِ أَنْ إِذَا سَمِعْتُمْ آيَاتِ اللَّهِ يُكْفَرُ بِهَا (He has already sent down to you in the Book that when you hear Allah’s signs being impugned...)."

In Tafsir al-Burhan, regarding His saying, the Exalted: قَوْلُهُ الْحَقُّ وَلَهُ الْمُلْكُ until the end of the verse, from Ibn Babawayh with his chain to Tha‘labah ibn Maymun from some of our companions from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ. He said: "عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ ما لم يكن وَالشَّهَادَةِ ما قد كان" — "'Knower of the Unseen' is what has not been, and 'the manifest' is what has already been."

I say: In it is the mention of the most well-known instances of the Unseen and the manifest to us. It has preceded in the previous commentary just now and elsewhere that the Unseen has other instances.

Ayahs

۞ وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ ءَازَرَ أَتَتَّخِذُ أَصْنَامًا ءَالِهَةً ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكَ وَقَوْمَكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٧٤)
When Abraham said to Azar, his father, ‘Do you take idols for gods? Indeed I see you and your people in manifest error.’ (74)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُوقِنِينَ (٧٥)
Thus did We show Abraham the dominions of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude. (75)
فَلَمَّا جَنَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلَّيْلُ رَءَا كَوْكَبًا ۖ قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَبِّى ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَ قَالَ لَآ أُحِبُّ ٱلْـَٔافِلِينَ (٧٦)
When night darkened over him, he saw a star and said, ‘This is my Lord!’ But when it set, he said, ‘I do not like those who set.’ (76)
فَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلْقَمَرَ بَازِغًا قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَبِّى ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَ قَالَ لَئِن لَّمْ يَهْدِنِى رَبِّى لَأَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٧٧)
Then, when he saw the moon rising, he said, ‘This is my Lord!’ But when it set, he said, ‘Had my Lord not guided me, I would surely have been among the astray lot.’ (77)
فَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلشَّمْسَ بَازِغَةً قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَبِّى هَـٰذَآ أَكْبَرُ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَفَلَتْ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تُشْرِكُونَ (٧٨)
Then, when he saw the sun rising, he said, ‘This is my Lord! This is bigger!’ But when it set, he said, ‘O my people, indeed I disown what you take as [His] partners.’ (78)
إِنِّى وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِىَ لِلَّذِى فَطَرَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٧٩)
‘Indeed I have turned my face toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth, as a Hanif, and I am not one of the polytheists.’ (79)
وَحَآجَّهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ ۚ قَالَ أَتُحَـٰٓجُّوٓنِّى فِى ٱللَّهِ وَقَدْ هَدَىٰنِ ۚ وَلَآ أَخَافُ مَا تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ رَبِّى شَيْـًٔا ۗ وَسِعَ رَبِّى كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا ۗ أَفَلَا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٨٠)
His people argued with him. He said, ‘Do you argue with me concerning Allah, while He has guided me for certain? I do not fear what you ascribe to Him as [His] partners, excepting anything that my Lord may wish. My Lord embraces all things in [His] knowledge. Will you not then take admonition? (80)
وَكَيْفَ أَخَافُ مَآ أَشْرَكْتُمْ وَلَا تَخَافُونَ أَنَّكُمْ أَشْرَكْتُم بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا ۚ فَأَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ أَحَقُّ بِٱلْأَمْنِ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٨١)
How could I fear what you ascribe [to Him] as [His] partners, when you do not fear ascribing to Allah partners for which He has not sent down any authority to you? So [tell me,] which of the two sides has a greater right to safety, if you know? (81)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنَهُم بِظُلْمٍ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلْأَمْنُ وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٨٢)
Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided.’ (82)
وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ ۚ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ مَّن نَّشَآءُ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ (٨٣)
This was Our argument that We gave to Abraham against his people. We raise in rank whomever We wish. Indeed your Lord is all-wise, all-knowing. (83)

(Commentary)

These are ten verses in which Allah, the Glorified, mentions the proof (الحجة) He granted to the great Prophet Abraham (عليه‌السلام) against the polytheists, by guiding him to His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and His transcendence (التنزيه). He then mentions His guidance of His prophets by purifying their innermost souls (سرهم) from polytheism (الشرك). Among them, He named Noah (عليه‌السلام), who preceded Abraham (عليه‌السلام), and sixteen prophets from the progeny of Noah (عليه‌السلام).

In reality, the verses are an exposition of a perfect instance of upholding the primordial religion (دين الفطرة) and rising to spread the creed of monotheism (التوحيد) and transcendence (التنزيه) from the polytheism (الشرك) of idolatry (الوثنية). This is what Abraham (عليه‌السلام) rose for, arguing against idolatry (الوثنية) at a time when the world was outwardly consumed by it, and people had forgotten the way of monotheism (التوحيد) established by Noah (عليه‌السلام) and his followers among the prophets of his progeny. Thus, the verses—containing the instruction of proof and guidance toward the primordial religion (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — serve as an insight into the preceding proofs that Allah, the Glorified, taught His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in this Surah through His saying: "Say such-and-such" and "Say such-and-such." Indeed, the word "Say" (قل) is repeated forty times in this noble Surah, more than twenty of which occur before these verses. It is as if it were said: "And mention, among what you say to your people and argue with them regarding the evidences of monotheism (التوحيد) and the negation of any partner (الشريك), Our instruction to you of what Abraham said to his father and his people, from the proof (الحجة) We gave him against his people through what We showed him of the kingdom (ملكوت) of the heavens and the earth." He was arguing with them based on a divine outpouring (إفاضة إلهية) of knowledge and wisdom, and a showing from Him, the Exalted, of His kingdom (ملكوت) based on certainty (اليقين), not from a manufactured thought that does not exceed the limits of imagination and conceptualization, nor is it free from the affectation and arbitrariness that the pure primordial nature (الفطرة) does not echo.

The tone of Abraham's (عليه‌السلام) speech, as recounted by Allah, the Glorified, in these verses—if we reflect upon them with minds free from the details mentioned in the narrations and traditions despite their flagrant discrepancies, and untainted by the disputes that occurred among the exegetes who mixed the interpretation of the verses with the contents of narrations, historical accounts, the Torah, and other Isra'iliyyat (إسرائيليات) — narratives originating from Judeo-Christian traditions — that follow them—in short, the tone of his speech (عليه‌السلام) in what is recounted of him in these verses clearly suggests that it is speech originating from a pure mind, not filled with the embellishments of diverse thoughts and illusions. His pure primordial nature (الفطرة) poured it into the mold of words through its primary rationalization, thinking, and subtle perceptions and feelings.

One who stands in a position of fairness while reflecting on these verses will not doubt that his recounted speech with his people most resembles the speech of a primitive human being—hypothetically speaking—who lived in a subterranean tunnel or a mountain cave, associating only with those who provided his necessary food and clothing. He had never witnessed the sky with its brilliant stars and planets, nor the rising moon and sun. He had not dwelled in a human society with its numerous individuals, vast lands, differing thoughts, scattered goals and needs, and various religions and sects. Then, by chance, he entered one of the great societies and witnessed wondrous things he had no prior knowledge of: celestial bodies, earthly regions, and groups of people devoted to their preoccupations, toiling toward their needs and goals, with nothing diverting them—between the moving and the still, the worker and the one worked for, the servant and the served, the commander and the commanded, the leader and the led, some intent on earning and laboring, and some ascetic and devout, worshipping the Deity.

He was bewildered by the wonder of what he saw and absorbed by the strangeness of what he witnessed. He began to ask those he felt comfortable with about the state of one thing after another that attracted his soul, fell under his gaze, and increased his amazement—similar to what we see in the state of a child when he looks at the vast expanse of the sky with its luminous lamps, brilliant luminaries, and the scattered constellations of stars in a tranquil state. We see him asking his mother: "What are these that I witness and am filled with love and wonder for? Who hung them there? Who lit them? Who made them?"

However, what we do not doubt is that this human being only begins his questioning about the realities of the things he witnesses and wonders at with that which is close to what he knew in his state of wildness and isolation from society, and he only asks about the purposes and ends (الغايات) that the senses do not perceive.

This is because a human being only seeks to know the state of unknown things (المجهولات) through the primary materials of knowledge (مواد العلم الأولية) he possesses. He does not transition from the unknown except to that which corresponds to some of the information (المعلومات) he has. This is a manifest and perceptible matter from the state of those with simple minds, such as children and desert dwellers, when they encounter things they have no prior knowledge of; they begin by seeking to know the state of what they become familiar with after the initial familiarity, asking about its reality, its causes (أسباب), and its ends (غايات).

The hypothetical human being—who is approximately the primitive, primordial human (الإنسان الفطري الأولي) —since he was only occupied with the simplest means of living, his mind was not occupied with what occupies the mind of the civilized, urban human who is surrounded by these countless, natural preoccupations that leave him no leisure, even for a moment. Therefore, the hypothetical human was in a state of intellectual leisure and mental emptiness, while the numerous celestial and earthly cosmic events surrounded him without him knowing their natural causes (الأسباب الطبيعية). Thus, his mind was more prepared to transition to their cause which is higher than natural causes. This is what the civilized human becomes aware of after finishing the enumeration of natural causes for cosmic events—above these causes—if he finds leisure. Therefore, what occurs first to the mind of this hypothetical human is the transition to this Higher Cause (السبب الأعلى) if he witnesses civilized people occupied with it, practicing devotion and worship (العبادة) toward it.

Among the evidences for what we have mentioned is that we find the engagement in religious rituals and the investigation of divinity (اللاهوت) in Asia to be more prevalent and highly valued than in Europe, and in villages and small towns, it has a firmer place than in great cities. This proportion holds in great cities and the masses as well; for as the scope of society expands, vital needs increase, and human preoccupations multiply and accumulate, leaving no leisure for the human to let his soul rest in its spiritualities (معنوياتها) and turn toward the investigation of its origin (المبدأ) and its return (المعاد).

In short, if we review the story of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) contained in these verses and its counterparts in Surah Maryam, al-Anbiya, al-Saffat, and others, we find his state (عليه‌السلام) in his arguments with his father and his people to be most similar to the state of the hypothetical simple human. We find him asking about the idols (الأصنام) and debating the people regarding their state, and speaking about the matter of the star, the moon, and the sun—the questioning of one who has no prior knowledge of what people, especially his polytheistic people, do with idols. He says to his father and his people: مَا هَٰذِهِ التَّمَاثِيلُ الَّتِي أَنتُمْ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ — "What are these images to which you keep cleaving?" (21:52). And he says to his father and his people: مَا تَعْبُدُونَ * قَالُوا نَعْبُدُ أَصْنَامًا فَنَظَلُّ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ * قَالَ هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ إِذْ تَدْعُونَ * أَوْ يَنفَعُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَضُرُّونَ * قَالُوا بَلْ وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا كَذَٰلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ — "‘What do you worship?’ They said, ‘We worship idols, and are constant in our devotion to them.’ He said, ‘Do they hear you when you call [them]? Or do they benefit you, or harm [you]?’ They said, ‘No, but we found our fathers acting likewise.’" (26:70-74).

This is the speech of one who has not seen an idol nor witnessed a polytheist worshipping an idol, yet he (عليه‌السلام) was in the cradle of idolatry (الوثنية), which was Babylon of the Chaldeans, and he lived among them for a period of time. Was such an expression from him (عليه‌السلام): "What are these images" (ما هذه التماثيل) intended to belittle the idols and intimate that he does not place them in the position people place them, nor does he acknowledge for them the sanctity and merit they acknowledge—as if he does not know them? This would be like Pharaoh's saying to Moses (عليه‌السلام): وَمَا رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ — "And what is the Lord of all the worlds?" (26:23), and the saying of the disbelievers of Mecca to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) as recounted by Allah the Exalted: وَإِذَا رَآكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَٰذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ وَهُم بِذِكْرِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ هُمْ كَافِرُونَ — "When the faithless see you, they only take you in derision: ‘Is this the one who makes mention of your gods?!’ And they are faithless at the mention of the All-beneficent." (21:36).

However, this is rendered unlikely by the fact that Abraham (عليه‌السلام) used nothing but the most beautiful etiquette (الأدب) in addressing his father Azar, even when his father expelled him and threatened him with stoning, Abraham said to him: سَلَامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ كَانَ بِي حَفِيًّا — "‘Peace be to you!’ he said. ‘I will plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed He is gracious to me.’" (19:47).

It is unlikely that the first words he would confront him with would include a belittling of the status of his gods, which were sacred to him, in a tone of distortion and insult, thereby provoking his zealotry and polytheistic tendency. Allah, the Glorified, has forbidden in this religion (الملة) —which is the religion of Abraham, the upright (حنيفاً) —the reviling of the gods of the polytheists lest it provoke them to confront the Muslims with the like. The Exalted said: وَلَا تَسُبُّوا الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ فَيَسُبُّوا اللَّهَ عَدْوًا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ — "Do not abuse those whom they invoke besides Allah, lest they should abuse Allah out of hostility, without any knowledge." (6:108).

Furthermore, after finishing his argument with his father Azar and his people regarding the idols, he (عليه‌السلام) occupied himself with their lords, which are the star, the moon, and the sun. He said when he saw a star: "This is my lord" (هذا ربي). Then he said when he saw the moon rising: "This is my lord" (هذا ربي). Then he said when he saw the sun rising: "This is my lord; this is bigger" (هذا ربي هذا أكبر). These expressions are also the expressions of one who, it seems, had not seen a star, a moon, or a sun. The clearest of expressions indicating this meaning is his saying (عليه‌السلام) regarding the sun: "This is my lord; this is bigger" (هذا ربي هذا أكبر). For this is the speech of one who does not know what the sun is, nor what the moon and the star are, except that he finds people submitting to them, worshipping them, and offering sacrifices to them, as history recounts regarding the people of Babylon. This is like when you see the silhouette of a person and you do not know if it is a man or a woman; you ask and say: "Who is this?" (من هذا؟) referring to the person (الشخص) because you know nothing more than that it is the person of a human being. It is then said: "The wife of so-and-so" or "He is so-and-so." And if you see a silhouette and do not know if it is a human, an animal, or an inanimate object, you say: "What is this?" (ما هذا؟) referring to the silhouette or the thing pointed to, since you have no knowledge of its state except that it is a physical thing (شيء جسماني), whatever it may be. It is then said to you: "This is Zayd" or "This is the wife of so-and-so" or "It is such-and-such a landmark." In all of this, while you are ignorant of the matter, you observe the status of rational beings (أولي العقل) and others, and masculinity and femininity, to the extent of your knowledge. As for the knowledgeable respondent who knows the reality of the situation, he must observe the reality.

Thus, the outward meaning of his saying (عليه‌السلام): "This is my lord" (هذا ربي) and his saying: "This is my lord; this is bigger" (هذا ربي هذا أكبر) is that he did not know anything about the state of the sun except that it was a rising thing larger than the moon and the star, which people intended for worship (العبادة) and ritual (النسك). Pointing to such a known thing is undoubtedly done with the word "this" (هذا) [in the masculine]. As for it being a sun—that is, a celestial body or a luminous disc that manages the earthly world with its light and delineates night and day by its movement according to outward perception—or it being a moon or a star that rises every night from the eastern horizon and sets in the opposite west, he did not know that, as suggested by this speech. Had he known that, he would have said regarding the sun: "This [feminine] is my lord; this [feminine] is bigger" (هذه ربي هذه أكبر) or said: "Indeed it [feminine] is my lord; indeed it [feminine] is bigger" (إنها ربي إنها أكبر), just as he observed this point later when he argued with King Nimrod, having known it by then: فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْتِي بِالشَّمْسِ مِنَ الْمَشْرِقِ فَأْتِ بِهَا مِنَ الْمَغْرِبِ — "‘Allah brings the sun from the east; now bring it from the west.’" (2:258). He did not say: "Bring it [masculine] from the west" (فأت به من المغرب).

Similarly, as he said to his father and his people as recounted by Allah: مَا تَعْبُدُونَ * قَالُوا نَعْبُدُ أَصْنَامًا فَنَظَلُّ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ * قَالَ هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ إِذْ تَدْعُونَ أَوْ يَنفَعُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَضُرُّونَ * قَالُوا بَلْ وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا كَذَٰلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ — "‘What do you worship?’ They said, ‘We worship idols, and are constant in our devotion to them.’ He said, ‘Do they hear you when you call [them]? Or do they benefit you, or harm [you]?’ They said, ‘No, but we found our fathers acting likewise.’" (26:70-74). He began by asking about their object of worship with the word "what" (ما) [for non-rational things] since he had no knowledge at that time of its state except that it was a "thing." Then, when they mentioned the idols—and they did not believe them to possess any perception or will—they said: "constant to them" (فَنَظَلُّ لَهَا) using the feminine [plural for non-rational objects]. Then, when he heard of their divinity from them—and it is necessary for a deity to be characterized by benefit, harm, and hearing the call of the one who calls—he expressed them using the terms for rational beings (أولي العقل). Then, when they mentioned to him in the story of breaking the idols: لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ يَنطِقُونَ — "‘You certainly know that these do not speak.’" (21:65) in response to his saying: فَاسْأَلُوهُمْ إِن كَانُوا يَنطِقُونَ — "‘Ask them, if they can speak.’" (21:63), he stripped them of the status of rational beings and said: أَفَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُكُمْ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَضُرُّكُمْ * أُفٍّ لَّكُمْ وَلِمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ — "‘Do you then worship besides Allah that which cannot benefit you in any way, nor harm you? Fie on you and what you worship besides Allah! Do you not exercise your reason?’" (21:66-67).

We cannot arbitrarily claim that he (عليه‌السلام) intended by his saying: "This is my lord; this is bigger" (هذا ربي هذا أكبر) the celestial body (الجرم) or the thing pointed to (المشار إليه), or that the state of his language in which he spoke—Syriac—was observed, which does not observe femininity like most non-Arabic languages; for that is an imposition (تحكم). Furthermore, he (عليه‌السلام) said to the king specifically regarding the sun itself: فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْتِي بِالشَّمْسِ مِنَ الْمَشْرِقِ فَأْتِ بِهَا مِنَ الْمَغْرِبِ — "‘Allah brings the sun from the east; now bring it from the west.’" (2:258). The Quran did not recount what he uttered with the description found in his language, so why would this instance (هذا ربي هذا أكبر) be singled out for this specific recounting?

A similar question arises regarding his asking his people about the state of the idols: مَا هَٰذِهِ التَّمَاثِيلُ الَّتِي أَنتُمْ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ — "What are these images to which you keep cleaving?" (21:52), as well as his saying in his supplication: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَن نَّعْبُدَ الْأَصْنَامَ * رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ النَّاسِ — "‘and keep me and my children away from worshipping idols. My Lord! Indeed they have misled many of the people.’" (14:35-36).

Likewise, we cannot say that he (عليه‌السلام), in mentioning the pointing to the sun, protected the Deity from the blemish of femininity out of veneration, or that the speech is a case of the subject following the predicate which is masculine—namely his saying: "my lord" (ربي) and his saying: "bigger" (أكبر). All of this is an imposition (تحكم) for which there is no evidence, and the detailed investigation of this will follow.

In conclusion, what Allah the Exalted recounts in these verses and their counterparts of the saying of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) to his father and his people regarding His monotheism (التوحيد) and the negation of any partner (الشريك) for Him is speech whose context indicates that he (عليه‌السلام) had lived before that in isolation from the atmosphere in which his father and his people lived. He did not know what those living in society knew of the details of the states of the parts of the universe and the social norms (السنن الاجتماعية) circulating among people in society. At that time, in the early period of his maturity and discernment, he left his isolation and joined his father. He found idols with him and asked him about their state. When his father informed him of that, he argued with him regarding their divinity and established the proof (الحجة) against him. Then he argued with his people regarding the idols and silenced them. Then he turned to their worship of the lords of the idols—the star, the moon, and the sun—and he went along with them in the assumption of their lordship (ربوبيتها), one after another. He continued to observe their state, and whenever one of them set, he rejected it, invalidated its lordship, and assumed the lordship of another among those they worshipped, until he had gone through all of them in his day and night, as is apparent from the verses. Then he returned to pure monotheism (التوحيد الخالص) by his saying: إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ حَنِيفًا وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — "‘Indeed I have turned my face toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth, as a hanif, and I am not one of the polytheists.’" (6:79). It seems this was completed for him in approximately two days and a night between them, as we shall explain, if Allah wills.

He (عليه‌السلام) possessed insight (بصيرة) that the world has a Creator, the Originator (فاطر) of the heavens and the earth, who is Allah alone, with no partner in that. He was only investigating whether people—including Abraham himself—have a lord (رب) other than Allah who is one of His creatures, such as the sun, the moon, or others, who lords over them, manages their affairs, and partners with Allah in His command, or whether they have no lord other than Allah, the Glorified, alone, with no partner.

In all these stages that he traversed, Allah, the Glorified, was supporting and directing him by showing him the kingdom (ملكوت) of the heavens and the earth and turning his noble soul toward the direction from which things are attributed to Allah in terms of creation (خلقاً) and management (تدبيراً). Thus, whenever he saw a thing, he saw its attribution to Allah, its creation, and its management by His command before he saw its own selfhood and the effects of its selfhood, as is apparent from the context of His saying: وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth..." (6:75), and His saying at the end of the verses: وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنَا آتَيْنَاهَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَاتٍ مَّن نَّشَاءُ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ — "This was Our proof that We gave to Abraham against his people. We raise in rank whomever We wish. Indeed your Lord is all-wise, all-knowing." (6:83), and His saying: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ رُشْدَهُ مِن قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا بِهِ عَالِمِينَ — "Certainly We had given Abraham his rectitude before, and We knew him [well]." (21:51).

And the saying of Abraham to his father as recounted by Allah the Exalted: يَا أَبَتِ إِنِّي قَدْ جَاءَنِي مِنَ الْعِلْمِ مَا لَمْ يَأْتِكَ فَاتَّبِعْنِي أَهْدِكَ صِرَاطًا سَوِيًّا — "‘O father! Indeed there has come to me a knowledge that has not come to you. So follow me, I will guide you to a right path.’" (19:43), among other verses.

Then he argued with King Nimrod regarding his claim of lordship (الربوبية), as was the habit of many tyrants of the past, and from similar instances, idolatry (الوثنية) arose. His people had many gods for which they had idols they worshipped, and among them were those who worshipped the lords of the idols, such as the sun, the moon, and the star mentioned by the Noble Quran, which was perhaps Venus.

This is a summary of what is benefited from the noble verses, and we shall investigate their contents in detail to the best of our ability, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ آزَرَ — "When Abraham said to Azar, his father..." (6:74). In the seven recitations, "Azar" (آزَرَ) is with the fatha [vowel], thus being an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) or a substitute (بدل) for "his father." In some recitations, "Azar" (آزَرُ) is with the damma [vowel], and its outward meaning is that it is a vocative (منادى) raised by the call, the estimation being: "O Azar, do you take idols as gods?" Among the recitations is "A-azran tattakhidhu" (أأزراً تتخذ), beginning with the interrogative hamza, followed by "azran" (أزراً) in the accusative as a verbal noun (مصدر) of azara ya'ziru meaning "he strengthened." The meaning would be: "When Abraham said to his father: 'Do you take idols for strength and support?'"

The exegetes have differed regarding the first famous recitation and the second anomalous one as to whether "Azar" is a proper name (اسم علم) for his father or a title (لقب) intended for praise or blame, meaning "the supporter," "the lame," "the crooked," or otherwise. The origin of this is what has been mentioned in several narrations that his father's name was "Taruh" (تارح) with the unpointed or pointed ha, and this is supported by what history has recorded of his father's name, and what occurs in the existing Torah that he (عليه‌السلام) was the son of Terah.

They also differed as to whether the "father" (الأب) refers to the biological father (الوالد), the uncle, the maternal grandfather, or the obeyed elder. The origin of this also lies in the discrepancy of narrations. Some of them contain that he was his biological father and that Abraham (عليه‌السلام) will intercede (يشفع) for him on the Day of Resurrection, but he will not be granted intercession; rather, Allah will transform him into a stinking hyena, and Abraham will disassociate from him. Others indicate that he was not his biological father, and that his biological father was a monotheist (موحد), not a polytheist. There are also narrations indicating that the ancestors of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were all monotheists, not polytheists, among other narrations. They have differed regarding the rest of what is recounted of Abraham's story with a wondrous discrepancy, to the point that some narrations include counterparts to what the Old Testament attributes to him, from which divine friendship (الخلة الإلهية), prophethood (النبوة), and messengership (الرسالة) are transcendent.

They have prolonged this type of investigation until it led to distant ends where the features of exegetical research—which seeks to make the noble verses speak of their objectives—disappear from the researcher's view. Whoever wishes to become acquainted with that should refer to the detailed commentaries and books of traditional exegesis (التفسير بالمأثور).

That which reflection upon the verses dealing with his stories (عليه‌السلام) guides to is that when he (عليه‌السلام) first associated with his people, he began with the matter of a man whom the Quran mentions was his father Azar. He insisted that he reject the idols and follow him in the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), so that he might guide him, until his father expelled him and ordered him to leave him. The Exalted said: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ إِنَّهُ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا * إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ يَا أَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لَا يَسْمَعُ وَلَا يُبْصِرُ وَلَا يُغْنِي عَنكَ شَيْئًا * يَا أَبَتِ إِنِّي قَدْ جَاءَنِي مِنَ الْعِلْمِ مَا لَمْ يَأْتِكَ فَاتَّبِعْنِي أَهْدِكَ صِرَاطًا سَوِيًّا — "And mention in the Book Abraham. Indeed he was a truthful one, a prophet. When he said to his father, ‘O father! Why do you worship that which neither hears nor sees, and is of no avail to you in any way? O father! Indeed there has come to me a knowledge that has not come to you. So follow me, I will guide you to a right path.’" —until He said— قَالَ أَرَاغِبٌ أَنتَ عَنْ آلِهَتِي يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ لَأَرْجُمَنَّكَ وَاهْجُرْنِي مَلِيًّا — "He said, ‘O Abraham! Are you averse to my gods? If you do not desist, I will surely stone you. Now leave me for a long time.’" (19:41-46). Abraham then bid him peace and promised to plead for forgiveness for him, perhaps out of hope for his faith and to entice him toward felicity and guidance. The Exalted said: قَالَ سَلَامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ كَانَ بِي حَفِيًّا * وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَمَا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَأَدْعُو رَبِّي عَسَىٰ أَلَّا أَكُونَ بِدُعَاءِ رَبِّي شَقِيًّا — "‘Peace be to you!’ he said. ‘I will plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed He is gracious to me. I will distance myself from you and what you invoke besides Allah, and I will supplicate my Lord. Hopefully, I will not be disappointed in supplicating my Lord.’" (19:47-48). The second verse is the best context (قرينة) indicating that he (عليه‌السلام) only promised to plead for forgiveness for him in this world, not to intercede for him on the Day of Resurrection while he remained a disbeliever, or on the condition that he did not know of his disbelief.

Then Allah, the Glorified, recounted his (عليه‌السلام) fulfillment of this promise and his pleading for forgiveness for his father in His saying: رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ * وَاجْعَل لِّي لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ * وَاجْعَلْنِي مِن وَرَثَةِ جَنَّةِ النَّعِيمِ * وَاغْفِرْ لِأَبِي إِنَّهُ كَانَ مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ * وَلَا تُخْزِنِي يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ * يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ * إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ — "‘My Lord! Grant me wisdom, and join me with the righteous, and appoint for me a worthy mention among the later generations, and make me one of the heirs to the paradise of bliss, and forgive my father, for he was one of those who are astray. Do not disgrace me on the day they will be resurrected, the day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart.’" (26:83-89). His saying: "for he was one of those who are astray" (إنه كان من الضالين) indicates that he (عليه‌السلام) only made this supplication for his father after his death or after parting from him and leaving him, due to the use of "was" (كان). The conclusion of his speech recounted in the verses indicates that it was a form of supplication he performed to fulfill the obligation of what he had promised and committed to; for he (عليه‌السلام) says: "forgive this stray one on the Day of Resurrection," then he describes the Day of Resurrection as a day when nothing avails except a sound heart.

Allah, the Glorified, disclosed this reality by His saying—which is in the form of an excuse—: مَا كَانَ لِلنَّبِيِّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَن يَسْتَغْفِرُوا لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَلَوْ كَانُوا أُولِي قُرْبَىٰ مِن بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَابُ الْجَحِيمِ * وَمَا كَانَ اسْتِغْفَارُ إِبْرَاهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَن مَّوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَهَا إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِّلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ إِنَّ إِبْرَاهِيمَ لَأَوَّاهٌ حَلِيمٌ — "The Prophet and the faithful may not plead for the forgiveness of the polytheists, even if they should be [their] relatives, after it has become clear to them that they are the inmates of hell. Abraham’s pleading forgiveness for his father was only to fulfill a promise he had made him. So when it became manifest to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he disavowed him. Indeed Abraham was most tender-hearted and forbearing." (9:113-114). The verse, by its context, testifies that this supplication only originated from him (عليه‌السلام) in this world, as did the disavowal of him, not that he would supplicate for him and then disavow him on the Day of Resurrection. For the context is that of a general prohibitive legal obligation (تكليف تحريمي عام), from which Abraham's supplication was excepted, and it was explained that it was in reality a fulfillment of what he had promised. There is no meaning in excepting what will occur, for example, on the Day of Resurrection from a legal ruling prescribed in this world, and then mentioning the disavowal on the Day of Resurrection.

In short, He, the Glorified, explains Abraham's (عليه‌السلام) supplication for his father and then his disavowal of him, all of which occurred in the early period of Abraham's life and before he migrated to the Holy Land, as evidenced by his asking for wisdom, joining the righteous, and righteous children, as benefited from His saying in the previous verses: "My Lord! Grant me wisdom, and join me with the righteous..." and His saying—which includes the disavowal of his father and his people and the exception of pleading for forgiveness as well—: قَدْ كَانَتْ لَكُمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ فِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ إِذْ قَالُوا لِقَوْمِهِمْ إِنَّا بُرَآؤُا مِنكُمْ وَمِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ كَفَرْنَا بِكُمْ وَبَدَا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ الْعَدَاوَةُ وَالْبَغْضَاءُ أَبَدًا حَتَّىٰ تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَحْدَهُ إِلَّا قَوْلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ لَأَسْتَغْفِرَنَّ لَكَ وَمَا أَمْلِكُ لَكَ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِن شَيْءٍ — "There is certainly a good exemplar for you in Abraham and those who were with him, when they said to their own people, ‘Indeed we are quit of you and of what you worship besides Allah. We deny you, and there has arisen between us and you enmity and hatred for ever, until you believe in Allah alone,’—except for Abraham’s saying to his father, ‘I will surely plead forgiveness for you, though I cannot avail you anything against Allah.’" (60:4).

Then Allah mentions his (عليه‌السلام) resolve to migrate to the Holy Land and his asking for righteous children by His saying: فَأَرَادُوا بِهِ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَاهُمُ الْأَسْفَلِينَ * وَقَالَ إِنِّي ذَاهِبٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ * رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "They desired to devise a stratagem against him, but We made them the lowliest. He said, ‘Indeed I am going to my Lord; He will guide me. My Lord! Give me [a child] from among the righteous.’" (37:98-100).

Then He mentions his going to the Holy Land and his being granted righteous children by His saying: وَأَرَادُوا بِهِ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَاهُمُ الْأَخْسَرِينَ * وَنَجَّيْنَاهُ وَلُوطًا إِلَى الْأَرْضِ الَّتِي بَارَكْنَا فِيهَا لِلْعَالَمِينَ * وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نَافِلَةً وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا صَالِحِينَ — "They desired to devise a stratagem against him, but We made them the greatest losers. We delivered him and Lot to the land which We have blessed for all the nations. And We gave him Isaac, and Jacob as well, as a bonus, and each of them We made righteous." (21:70-72). And His saying: فَلَمَّا اعْتَزَلَهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا نَبِيًّا — "So when he had left them and what they worshipped besides Allah, We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and each We made a prophet." (19:49).

Then He mentions the last of his supplications in Mecca, which occurred at the end of his (عليه‌السلام) life after he had migrated to the Holy Land, children were born to him, he settled Ishmael in Mecca, the town flourished, and the Kaaba was built. This is the last of his speech recounted in the Noble Quran: وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَٰذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِنًا وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَن نَّعْبُدَ الْأَصْنَامَ —until He said— رَّبَّنَا إِنِّي أَسْكَنتُ مِن ذُرِّيَّتِي بِوَادٍ غَيْرِ ذِي زَرْعٍ عِندَ بَيْتِكَ الْمُحَرَّمِ رَبَّنَا لِيُقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ —until He said— الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِسْحَاقَ إِنَّ رَبِّي لَسَمِيعُ الدُّعَاءِ —until He said— رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسَابُ — "When Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Make this city a sanctuary, and keep me and my children away from worshipping idols... Our Lord! I have settled some of my descendants in a barren valley near Your sacred House, our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer... All praise belongs to Allah, who, despite [my] old age, gave me Ishmael and Isaac. Indeed my Lord hears all supplications... Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and all the faithful, on the day when the reckoning is held.’" (14:35-41).

The verse, by its context and the surrounding clues (القرائن), is the best witness that the biological father (الوالد) for whom he supplicated therein is other than the one whom He, the Glorified, mentions by His saying: "to his father Azar" (لأبيه آزر). For the verses, as you see, explicitly state that Abraham (عليه‌السلام) pleaded for forgiveness for him in fulfillment of his promise, then disavowed him when it became manifest to him that he was an enemy of Allah. There is no meaning in his repeating the supplication for one he had disavowed and sought refuge with his Lord from being touched by him. Thus, his father Azar is other than his biological father (والده الصلبي) for whom he supplicated along with his mother in his final supplication.

Among the subtle indications in this supplication—namely his final supplication—is what is in his saying: "and for my parents" (ولوالدي), where he expressed it using al-walid (الوالد), and al-walid is only applied to the biological father who begets and is begotten by the human. This is in contrast to his other supplication: "and forgive my father (أبي), for he was one of those who are astray," and the other verses containing the mention of his father Azar, for they express him using al-ab (الأب). Al-ab is sometimes applied to the grandfather, the uncle, and others. The Noble Quran has contained this very usage in the saying of the Exalted: أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَاءَ إِذْ حَضَرَ يَعْقُوبَ الْمَوْتُ إِذْ قَالَ لِبَنِيهِ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن بَعْدِي قَالُوا نَعْبُدُ إِلَٰهَكَ وَإِلَٰهَ آبَائِكَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِسْحَاقَ إِلَهًا وَاحِدًا وَنَحْنُ لَهُ مُسْلِمُونَ — "Were you witnesses when death approached Jacob, when he said to his sons, ‘What will you worship after me?’ They said, ‘We will worship your God and the God of your fathers, Abraham, Ishmael, and Isaac, the One God, and to Him do we submit.’" (2:133). Abraham was Jacob's grandfather and Ishmael was his uncle, yet each of them was called "father" (الأب). And the saying of the Exalted recounting the speech of Joseph (عليه‌السلام): وَاتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ آبَائِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ — "I follow the creed of my fathers, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob." (12:38). Isaac was Joseph's grandfather and Abraham (عليه‌السلام) was his father's grandfather, yet each of them was called "father" (الأب).

Thus, it is established that Azar, whom the verse mentions, was not Abraham's father in reality, but was characterized by some descriptions and titles that justify applying the term "father" to him, and for Abraham (عليه‌السلام) to address him as "O father" (يا أبت). Language permits the application of "father" to the grandfather, the uncle, the mother's husband after the father, anyone who takes charge of a person's affairs, and every obeyed elder. This expansion is not unique to the Arabic language; rather, other languages share it and its likes, such as the expansion in applying "mother," "uncle," "brother," "sister," "head," "eye," "mouth," "hand," "arm," "finger," and so on, which the taste for subtlety and artistry in communication and understanding guides to.

It has thus become clear, firstly, that there is no reason to engage in the aforementioned investigations of narrations, history, and linguistics regarding his father and the word "Azar"—whether it is a proper name, a title of praise or blame, or the name of an idol. There is no need for any of that to obtain the intent of the verse.

Furthermore, most of what they have cited in this regard is an imposition (تحكم) for which there is no evidence, along with its corruption of the outward meaning of the verse and its disruption of the context due to the strange constructions they mentioned for the sentence: "Azar, do you take idols as gods?" involving advancement, delay, omission, and estimation.

Secondly, his real biological father was other than Azar, but the Quran did not explicitly state his name. It has occurred in narrations, and it is supported by what is found in the Torah, that his name was "Terah."

Among the strange illusions is what some researchers have mentioned: that the Noble Quran often neglects, in what it mentions of the history of prophets and nations and what it recounts of the stories of the past, important matters that are among the essentials of stories, such as the date of occurrence, its location, and the natural, social, and political conditions that influenced the formation of events and were involved in the composition of occurrences. Among these is the case under discussion; for one of the constitutive factors for knowing the reality of this story is knowing the name of Abraham's father, his lineage, and the date of his upbringing, uprising, call, and migration.

This is only because the Quran followed in its stories the excellent path guided to by the art of true storytelling: that the storyteller chooses in his story every possible path leading to his goal and purpose in a beautiful manner, and represents the objective with a complete and profound representation, without exaggerating in distinguishing the sound of what he recounts from the unsound, nor enumerating all that is among the essentials of the story, such as the date of occurrence, its place, and its other necessary attributes. Thus, it is permissible for the Noble Quran, in the way of attaining its goal—which is guidance toward human felicity—to take stories circulating among people or among the People of the Book in the era of the call, even if their authenticity is not verified or all their essential aspects are not clarified in what they possess of the story, even if it were a fictional story (قصة تخييلية). This has been said regarding the story of Moses and his servant, and the story of the leaders who left their homes while they were thousands in fear of death, and others. For the art of storytelling does not prevent any of that once the storyteller has determined that the story is the most eloquent means and the easiest way to attain his goal.

This is an error. For what he mentioned regarding the art of storytelling is true, but it is not applicable to the case of the Noble Quran. The Quran is not a book of history, nor a journal of fictional stories; rather, it is a Mighty Book (كتاب عزيز) — a scripture of unparalleled authority and protection — falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it. It has explicitly stated that it is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, and that He says nothing but the truth, and that after the truth there is nothing but error, and that He does not seek help for the truth through falsehood, nor does He derive guidance from error. It is a book that guides to the truth and to a straight path (صراط مستقيم), and what is in it is a proof (حجة) for those who take it and against those who leave it, in numerous verses that need not be cited here. How then could it be permissible for a researcher investigating the objectives of the Quran to allow its inclusion of a false opinion, a lying false story, a myth, or a fiction?

I do not mean that the requirement of faith in Allah and His Messenger and what His Messenger brought is to negate from the Quran that it contains falsehood, lies, or myths—though that is the case. Nor that it is obligatory for every person of sound mind, correct thought, and upright affair to submit his soul to the Quran by believing it and negating every error and slip from it in the means of knowledge he used to reach his objectives, and in those objectives themselves—though that is also the case.
