He said: 'We seek refuge in Allah, O Abu Basir, from being among those who taint their faith with wrongdoing.' Then he said: 'Those are the Kharijites (الخوارج) and their companions.'"

In the same source: From Ya'qub ibn Shu'ayb from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying: وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ. He said: "Misguidance (الضلال) and what is above it."

I say: It is as if what is meant by "misguidance" in the narration is polytheism, which is the root of all wrongdoing above it and the sins and injustices that exceed it. Or what is meant by misguidance is the minimum by which wrongdoing is realized through sins, and by "what is above it" is polytheism, which is the severe level of misguidance, for every sin is misguidance.

The narrations—as you see—vary in the interpretation of "wrongdoing" (الظلم) in the verse. Sometimes they interpret it as polytheism, sometimes as doubt, and sometimes as what the Kharijites follow. In some, it is said that the guardianship (ولاية) of their enemies is part of it. All of this serves as evidence for what we previously established: that wrongdoing in the verse is absolute (مطلق), and in its absoluteness, it possesses levels according to the degrees of understanding.

(A Discourse on the Story of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) and His Personality)

This includes various researches: some Quranic, others scientific, historical, and otherwise.

1 - The Story of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) in the Quran: Abraham (عليه‌السلام), from his childhood until the beginning of his discernment, lived in isolation from the society of his people. He then went out to them and joined his father, finding him and his people worshipping idols. He did not accept that from him or them. His primordial nature (الفطرة) was pure and untainted, supported by Allah the Glorified with true witnessing (الشهود الحق) and the showing of the kingdom (ملكوت) of everything—in short, with true speech and righteous action.

He began to argue with his father regarding his worship of idols, calling him to reject them, to unify Allah the Glorified, and to follow him so that he might guide him to the straight path (مستقيم الصراط) and distance him from the guardianship of Satan (ولاية الشيطان). He continued to argue with him and persist until his father rebuked him and drove him away, threatening to stone him if he did not cease mentioning his gods with ill and turning away from them.

Abraham (عليه‌السلام) acted with kindness toward him, out of compassion and affection—for he possessed a noble character and pleasing speech—so he bid him peace and promised to seek forgiveness for him and to withdraw from him and his people and what they worshipped besides Allah. (19:41-48).

On another front, he was arguing with the people regarding the matter of the idols (21:51-56, 26:69-77, 37:83-87). He also argued with other groups among them who worshipped the sun, the moon, and the star regarding them, until he established the truth against them. The news of his deviation from the idols and gods spread (6:74-82). One day, the people went out to a collective worship outside the city. He feigned illness and did not go out with them, staying behind. He entered the house of idols and turned upon their gods, striking them with his right hand, reducing them to fragments except for a large one of them, that they might perhaps return to it. When they returned and saw what had happened to their gods and searched for who had committed it, they said: "We heard a youth mentioning them, who is called Abraham."

They brought him to their assembly, bringing him before the eyes of the people that they might bear witness. They interrogated him, saying: "Was it you who did this to our gods, O Abraham?" He said: "Rather, this big one of them did it; so ask them, if they can speak." He had left the largest of the idols and had not broken it, placing the axe on its shoulder or near it, so that the situation would testify that it was the one that broke the other idols.

He (عليه‌السلام) only said that while knowing they would not believe him, and they knew it was an inanimate object (جماد) incapable of that. However, he said what he said so that he might follow it with his saying: "So ask them, if they can speak," until they would confess with explicit speech that they were inanimate objects without life or consciousness. Therefore, when they heard his words, they returned to themselves and said: "Indeed, you are the wrongdoers." Then they were confounded: "You certainly know that these do not speak." He said: "Do you then worship besides Allah that which neither harms you nor benefits you? Fie on you and on what you worship besides Allah! Do you not exercise your intellect? Do you worship what you yourselves carve, while Allah created you and what you make?"

They said: "Burn him and help your gods!" So they built a structure for him and kindled a blazing fire within it. The people participated collectively in his affair and threw him into the blaze, but Allah made it coolness and peace for him and frustrated their plot (21:57-70, 37:88-98). During these events, he was brought before the king, whom the people worshipped and took as a lord. He argued with Abraham regarding his Lord. Abraham said: "My Lord is He who gives life and causes death." The king used a sophistry (غالطه) and said: "I give life and cause death," such as by killing a prisoner or releasing him. Abraham then argued with him with the most explicit point that would cut off his sophistry, saying: "Indeed Allah brings the sun from the east, so bring it from the west." Thus was the disbeliever confounded (2:258).

Then, when Allah saved him from the fire, he began to call to the Hanif religion, the religion of monotheism (التوحيد). A small group believed in him, among whom Allah named Lot, and among them was his wife with whom he migrated. He had married her before leaving the land for the Holy Land.

Then he (عليه‌السلام) and the believers with him disassociated themselves from their people. He disassociated himself from Azar, whom he used to call father but who was not his true parent. He migrated with his wife and Lot to the Holy Land to call to Allah the Glorified without any opponent from his coarse and unjust people (60:4, 21:71). There, Allah the Glorified gave him the glad tidings of Ishmael, and of Isaac, and after Isaac, Jacob. He had grown old and reached an advanced age. Ishmael was born to him, then Isaac was born to him, and Allah the Glorified blessed him, his two sons, and their children.

Then, by a command from his Lord, he went to the land of Mecca, which was a valley without cultivation. He settled his son Ishmael there while he was a child and returned to the Holy Land. Ishmael grew up there, and a group of Arabs residing there gathered around him, and thus the city of Mecca was built.

He (عليه‌السلام) would occasionally visit Ishmael in the land of Mecca before the building of Mecca and the House, and after that (2:126, 14:35-41). Then he built the Kaaba, the Sacred House, with the participation of Ishmael. It was the first house established for the people by Allah, blessed and a guidance for all the worlds; in it are clear signs, the station of Abraham, and whoever enters it is safe (2:127-129, 3:96-97). He announced the Hajj among the people and legislated the rites of Hajj (22:26-30).

Then Allah commanded him to sacrifice his son Ishmael (عليه‌السلام). He went out with him for the rite. When the boy reached the age of running with him, he said: "O my son! I see in a dream that I am sacrificing you." He said: "O my father! Do what you are commanded. You will find me, if Allah wills, to be patient." When they had both submitted and he had laid him down on his forehead, he was called: "O Abraham! You have indeed fulfilled the vision." And Allah the Glorified ransomed him with a great sacrifice (37:101-107).

The last of what the noble Quran recounts of his stories (عليه‌السلام) are his supplications during some of the days of his presence in Mecca, recorded in Surah Ibrahim (14:35-41). The last mentioned therein is his saying (عليه‌السلام): رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوالِدَيَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسابُ — "Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and the faithful, on the day when the reckoning is held." (14:41).

Abraham's Status with Allah the Glorified and His Station of Servitude: Allah the Exalted praised Abraham (عليه‌السلام) in His speech with the most beautiful praise and lauded his trial for His sake with the most eloquent commendation. He repeated his mention by name in over sixty places in His Book and mentioned many of His gifts and blessings upon him. Here are some of them: Allah gave him his rectitude (رشده) from before (21:51). He chose him in this world, and in the Hereafter he is among the righteous. When his Lord said to him, "Submit (أسلم)," he said, "I submit to the Lord of all the worlds" (2:130-131). He is the one who turned his face toward his Lord as a Hanif and was not of the polytheists (6:79). He is the one whose heart found tranquility (اطمأن قلبه) in Allah and who attained certainty (أيقن) in Him through what Allah showed him of the kingdom of the heavens and the earth (2:260, 6:75).

Allah took him as a friend (خليلا) (4:125) and placed His mercy and blessings upon him and the people of his house. He described him as one who fulfilled (التوفية) (53:37). He praised him as being clement (حليم), compassionate (أواه), and penitent (منيب) (10:73-75). He praised him as being a nation (أمة), obedient (قانتا) to Allah, a Hanif, and not of the polytheists, grateful for His blessings; He chose him and guided him to a straight path, gave him good in this world, and in the Hereafter he is among the righteous (16:120-122).

He was a truthful man (صديقا) and a prophet (19:41). Allah counted him among His faithful servants and among the virtuous (المحسنين) and bid him peace (37:83-111). He is among those whom He described as possessing strength (الأيدي) and insight (الأبصار) and whom He distinguished with a pure quality: the remembrance of the Abode (38:45-46).

Allah made him an Imam (إماما) for the people (2:124). He made him one of the five "Possessors of Resolve" (أولي العزم) who were given the Book and the Law (33:7, 42:13, 87:18-19). Allah gave him knowledge (العلم), wisdom (الحكمة), the Book, sovereignty (الملك), and guidance, and made it a lasting word among his posterity (4:54, 6:74-90, 43:28). He placed prophethood and the Book in his progeny (57:26) and granted him a mention of truth (لسان صدق) among the later generations (26:84, 19:50). These are the summaries of the divine offices and stations of servitude (مقامات العبودية) that Allah the Glorified granted him. The noble Quran did not detail the attributes and miracles of any of the honored prophets and messengers as it detailed his attributes and miracles (عليه‌السلام).

One should refer to the commentary of each of his mentioned stations in the specific places we have explained previously or will explain, Allah the Exalted willing; for engaging in that here would take us away from the purpose for which these researches were established.

Allah the Glorified has preserved his noble life and religious personality by naming this upright religion "Islam," just as he (عليه‌السلام) named it and attributed it to him. The Exalted said: مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْراهِيمَ هُوَ سَمَّاكُمُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ — "the faith of your father, Abraham. He named you 'Muslims' beforehand" (22:78). And He said: قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدانِي رَبِّي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ دِيناً قِيَماً مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً وَما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — "Say, 'Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path, the upright religion, the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists.'" (6:161).

He made the Kaaba, the Sacred House which he built, the direction of prayer (قبلة) for all the worlds, and legislated the rites of Hajj, which are in reality acts representing the story of his settling his son and the mother of his child, the sacrifice of his son Ishmael, his striving toward his Lord, his turning to Him, and his bearing of harm and trial for His sake, as previously indicated in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ جَعَلْنَا الْبَيْتَ مَثابَةً لِلنَّاسِ (And when We made the House a place of return for the people) the verse (2:125) in the first volume of the book.

3 - His Blessed Impact on Human Society: Among his abundant favors is that the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) traces back to him wherever it is and with whomever it is. For the religion described as monotheism today is the religion of the Jews, which traces back to the Speaker (الكليم) Moses ibn Imran (عليه‌السلام), whose lineage traces back to Israel, Jacob ibn Isaac ibn Abraham (عليه‌السلام). And the religion of Christianity traces back to the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary (عليه‌السلام), who is from the progeny of Abraham (عليه‌السلام). And the religion of Islam, whose proclaimer is Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), whose lineage traces back to Ishmael the Sacrificed, son of Abraham the Friend (عليه‌السلام). Thus, the religion of monotheism in the world is his good and blessed impact. Observed in Islam from his laws are the prayer, zakat, Hajj, the permissibility of the meat of livestock, disassociation from the enemies of Allah, the greeting of peace, and the ten purifications of the pure Hanifiyyah: five in the head and five in the body. As for those in the head: trimming the mustache, letting the beard grow, cutting the hair, using the tooth-stick (السواك), and using the toothpick (الخلال). As for those in the body: shaving body hair, circumcision, clipping the nails, the major ritual bath (الغسل) from ritual impurity, and purification with water.

Exhaustive research supports that the righteous traditions (السنن الصالحة) of belief and action in human society, whatever they may be, are among the good impacts of prophethood, as repeatedly indicated in previous discussions. Thus, Abraham (عليه‌السلام) has beautiful favors upon all of humanity today, whether they know it or not.

4 - What the Current Torah Recounts Concerning Abraham: The Torah says: Terah (Abraham's father) lived seventy years and begot Abram, Nahor, and Haran. These are the generations of Terah: Terah begot Abram, Nahor, and Haran. Haran begot Lot. Haran died before his father in the land of his birth, in "Ur" of the Chaldeans. Abram and Nahor took wives for themselves—the name of Abram's wife was "Sarai," and the name of Nahor's wife was Milkah, daughter of Haran, the father of Milkah and Iscah. Sarai was barren and had no child. Terah took Abram his son, and Lot son of Haran, his grandson, and Sarai his daughter-in-law, the wife of Abram his son. They went out together from Ur of the Chaldeans to go to the land of Canaan. They came to Haran and stayed there. The days of Terah were two hundred and five years, and Terah died in Haran.

The Torah says: The Lord said to Abram: "Go from your land, from your kindred, and from your father's house to the land that I will show you. I will make you a great nation, I will bless you and make your name great, and you shall be a blessing. I will bless those who bless you, and him who curses you I will curse, and in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed." So Abram went as the Lord had told him, and Lot went with him. Abram was seventy-five years old when he departed from Haran. Abram took Sarai his wife and Lot his brother's son, and all their possessions that they had gathered, and the souls they had acquired in Haran, and they set out to go to the land of Canaan. They came to the land of Canaan.

Abram passed through the land to the place of "Shechem," to the "Oak of Moreh." The Canaanites were then in the land. The Lord appeared to Abram and said: "To your offspring I will give this land." So he built there an altar to the Lord who had appeared to him. Then he moved from there to the mountain east of "Bethel" and pitched his tent, with "Bethel" on the west and "Ai" on the east. He built there an altar to the Lord and called upon the name of the Lord. Then Abram journeyed on, still going toward the South.

There was a famine in the land, so Abram went down to Egypt to sojourn there, for the famine was severe in the land. When he was about to enter Egypt, he said to Sarai his wife: "I know that you are a woman beautiful in appearance. When the Egyptians see you, they will say, 'This is his wife,' and they will kill me but let you live. Say you are my sister, that it may go well with me because of you, and that my soul may live for your sake." When Abram entered Egypt, the Egyptians saw that the woman was very beautiful. Pharaoh's officials saw her and praised her to Pharaoh, and the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house. He treated Abram well for her sake, and he acquired sheep, oxen, male donkeys, male and female servants, female donkeys, and camels.

The Lord struck Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai, Abram's wife. Pharaoh called Abram and said: "What is this you have done to me? Why did you not tell me she was your wife? Why did you say, 'She is my sister,' so that I took her to be my wife? Now then, here is your wife; take her and go." He gave men orders concerning him, and they sent him on his way with his wife and all that he had.

Then the Torah mentioned: that Abram went up from Egypt with Sarai and Lot, and with them were sheep, servants, and great wealth. They came to "Bethel," the place where his tent had been pitched between "Bethel" and "Ai." After a time, he and Lot separated because the land could not support them both. Abram dwelt in Canaan—the Canaanites and Perizzites were dwelling there—and Lot settled in the land of Sodom.

Then it mentioned: that in those days, war broke out in the land of Sodom between "Amraphel" king of Shinar with three other kings, and Bera king of Sodom with four allied kings. The king of Sodom and those with him were soundly defeated and fled from the land after many were killed. Their wealth was plundered and their women and children were taken captive. Among those captured was Lot and all his family, and his wealth was plundered.

The Torah says: One who had escaped came and told Abram the Hebrew, who was living by the "Oaks of Mamre" the Amorite, brother of "Eshcol" and "Aner," who were allies of Abram. When Abram heard that his kinsman had been taken captive, he led forth his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen, and went in pursuit as far as "Dan." He divided his forces against them by night, he and his servants, and defeated them and pursued them to "Hobah," north of Damascus. He brought back all the goods, and also brought back his kinsman Lot with his goods, and the women and the people.

The king of Sodom went out to meet him after his return from the defeat of "Chedorlaomer" and the kings who were with him, at the Valley of Shaveh (that is, the King's Valley). And Melchizedek king of "Salem" brought out bread and wine; he was priest of God Most High. And he blessed him and said: "Blessed be Abram by God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth; and blessed be God Most High, who has delivered your enemies into your hand!" And Abram gave him a tenth of everything.

The King of Sodom said to Abram, "Give me the persons, and take the property for yourself." But Abram said to the King of Sodom, "I have raised my hand to the Lord, God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth, that I will take nothing, from a thread to a sandal strap, nor anything that is yours, lest you should say, 'I have made Abram rich.' I will take nothing but what the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men who went with me: Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre; let them take their portion."

The account continues until it says: Now Sarai had borne him no children. She had an Egyptian maidservant whose name was Hagar. So Sarai said to Abram, "See now, the Lord has restrained me from bearing children. Please, go in to my maidservant; perhaps I shall obtain children by her." And Abram heeded the voice of Sarai. Then Sarai, Abram’s wife, took Hagar her Egyptian maidservant, after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan, and gave her to her husband Abram to be his wife. So he went in to Hagar, and she conceived.

Then it mentions: When Hagar saw that she had conceived, she despised Sarai and acted arrogantly toward her. Sarai complained about this to Abram,

(1) A name of one of the kings contemporary to him (peace be upon him).

so Abram delegated her matter to her, and Hagar fled from her. An angel met her and commanded her to return to her mistress, informing her that she would bear a male child and should call his name Ishmael, because the Lord had heard of her affliction. He also told her that he would be a wild man, his hand against every man and every man’s hand against him. Hagar bore a son to Abram, and Abram named him Ishmael. Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to Abram.

The Torah says: When Abram was ninety-nine years old, the Lord appeared to Abram and said to him, "I am Almighty God; walk before Me and be blameless. And I will make My covenant between Me and you, and will multiply you exceedingly." Then Abram fell on his face, and God talked with him, saying: "As for Me, behold, My covenant is with you, and you shall be a father of a multitude of nations. No longer shall your name be called Abram, but your name shall be Abraham; for I have made you a father of a multitude of nations. I will make you exceedingly fruitful; and I will make nations of you, and kings shall come from you. And I will establish My covenant between Me and you and your descendants after you in their generations, for an everlasting covenant, to be God to you and your descendants after you. Also I give to you and your descendants after you the land in which you are a stranger, all the land of Canaan, as an everlasting possession; and I will be their God."

Then it mentions: The Lord made this a covenant between Him and Abraham and his descendants—that he and everyone with him should be circumcised, and they should circumcise their children on the eighth day after birth. So Abraham was circumcised when he was ninety-nine years old, and his son Ishmael was circumcised when he was thirteen years old, along with all the males among his sons and servants.

The Torah says: And God said to Abraham, "As for Sarai your wife, you shall not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall be her name. And I will bless her and also give you a son by her; then I will bless her, and she shall be a mother of nations; kings of peoples shall be from her." Then Abraham fell on his face and laughed, and said in his heart, "Shall a child be born to a man who is one hundred years old? And shall Sarah, who is ninety years old, bear a child?"

And Abraham said to God, "Oh, that Ishmael might live before You!" Then God said: "No, Sarah your wife shall bear you a son, and you shall call his name Isaac; I will establish My covenant with him for an everlasting covenant, and with his descendants after him. And as for Ishmael, I have heard you. Behold, I have blessed him, and will make him fruitful, and will multiply him exceedingly. He shall beget twelve princes, and I will make him a great nation. But My covenant I will establish with Isaac, whom Sarah shall bear to you at this set time next year." Then He finished talking with him, and God went up from Abraham.

Then it mentions the story of the Lord’s descent with the two angels to destroy the people of Sodom, the people of Lot. They came to Abraham, and he hosted them. They ate of the food he prepared for them from a calf he slaughtered, and the butter and milk which he

presented to them. Then they gave him and Sarah the glad tidings of Isaac. They mentioned the matter of the people of Lot, and Abraham argued with them regarding their destruction, but they convinced him. After that, the destruction of the people of Lot occurred.

Then it mentions that Abraham moved to the land of "Gerar" and stayed there as a stranger. He represented to its king, "Abimelech," that Sarah was his sister, so the king took her from him. Then the Lord rebuked him in a dream. He summoned Abraham and rebuked him for saying, "She is my sister." Abraham excused himself, saying he only said it for fear of being killed, and admitted that she was indeed his sister through his father but not his mother, and he had married her. So he returned Sarah to him and gave them great wealth (similar to what was narrated regarding Pharaoh).

The Torah says: And the Lord visited Sarah as He had said, and the Lord did for Sarah as He had spoken. For Sarah conceived and bore Abraham a son in his old age, at the set time of which God had spoken to him. And Abraham called the name of his son who was born to him—whom Sarah bore to him—Isaac. Then Abraham circumcised his son Isaac when he was eight days old, as God had commanded him. Now Abraham was one hundred years old when his son Isaac was born to him. And Sarah said, "God has made me laugh; all who hear will laugh with me." She also said, "Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children? For I have borne him a son in his old age." So the child grew and was weaned. And Abraham made a great feast on the same day that Isaac was weaned.

And Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Abraham, scoffing. Therefore she said to Abraham, "Cast out this bondwoman and her son; for the son of this bondwoman shall not be heir with my son, Isaac." And the matter was very displeasing in Abraham’s sight because of his son. But God said to Abraham, "Do not let it be displeasing in your sight because of the lad or because of your bondwoman. Whatever Sarah has said to you, listen to her voice; for in Isaac your seed shall be called. Yet I will also make a nation of the son of the bondwoman, because he is your seed."

So Abraham rose early in the morning, and took bread and a skin of water; and putting it on her shoulder, he gave it and the boy to Hagar, and sent her away. Then she departed and wandered in the Wilderness of Beersheba. And when the water in the skin was used up, she placed the boy under one of the shrubs. Then she went and sat down opposite him at a distance of about a bowshot; for she said to herself, "Let me not see the death of the boy." So she sat opposite him, and lifted her voice and wept. And God heard the voice of the lad. Then the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven, and said to her, "What ails you, Hagar? Fear not, for God has heard the voice of the lad where he is. Arise, lift up the lad and hold him with your hand, for I will make him a great nation." Then God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water. And she went and filled the skin with water, and gave the lad a drink. So God was with the lad; and he grew and dwelt in the

wilderness, and became an archer. He dwelt in the Wilderness of Paran; and his mother took a wife for him from the land of Egypt (1).

The Torah says: Now it came to pass after these things that God tested Abraham, and said to him, "Abraham!" And he said, "Here I am." Then He said, "Take now your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love, and go to the land of Moriah, and offer him there as a burnt offering on one of the mountains of which I shall tell you." So Abraham rose early in the morning and saddled his donkey, and took two of his young men with him, and Isaac his son; and he split the wood for the burnt offering, and arose and went to the place of which God had told him. Then on the third day Abraham lifted his eyes and saw the place afar off. And Abraham said to his young men, "Stay here with the donkey; the lad and I will go yonder and worship, and we will come back to you." So Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son; and he took the fire in his hand, and a knife, and the two of them went together. But Isaac spoke to Abraham his father and said, "My father!" And he said, "Here I am, my son." Then he said, "Look, the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?" And Abraham said, "My son, God will provide for Himself the lamb for a burnt offering." So the two of them went together.

Then they came to the place of which God had told him. And Abraham built an altar there and placed the wood in order; and he bound Isaac his son and laid him on the altar, upon the wood. And Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slay his son. But the Angel of the Lord called to him from heaven and said, "Abraham, Abraham!" So he said, "Here I am." And He said, "Do not lay your hand on the lad, or do anything to him; for now I know that you fear God, since you have not withheld your son, your only son, from Me." Then Abraham lifted his eyes and looked, and there behind him was a ram caught in a thicket by its horns. So Abraham went and took the ram, and offered it up for a burnt offering instead of his son. And Abraham called the name of the place "The-Lord-Will-Provide"; as it is said to this day, "In the Mount of the Lord it shall be provided." Then the Angel of the Lord called to Abraham a second time from heaven, and said: "By Myself I have sworn, says the Lord: because you have done this thing, and have not withheld your son, your only son—blessing I will bless you, and multiplying I will multiply your descendants as the stars of the heaven and as the sand which is on the seashore; and your descendants shall possess the gate of their enemies. In your seed all the nations of the earth shall be blessed, because you have obeyed My voice." So Abraham returned to his young men, and they rose and went together to Beersheba; and Abraham dwelt at Beersheba.

(1) In the narrations of the Muslims, the reality is that he married from the Jurhum, who are among the clans of the Yemeni Arabs.

Then it mentions his marrying Isaac to someone from his kin in Chaldea, then the death of Sarah—at the age of one hundred and twenty-seven—in Hebron. Then Abraham’s marriage after her to Keturah, who bore him several sons. Finally, the death of Abraham at the age of one hundred and seventy-five, and his sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of "Machpelah," which is the Sanctuary of al-Khalil today.

This is a summary of the stories of Abraham (peace be upon him) and the history of his life as presented in the Torah (Genesis, Chapters 11 to 25). It is for the critical researcher to compare what is mentioned there with what the Noble Quran narrates, and then form his own opinion.

5 — What the narrated story contains, despite the contradictions between its sentences and the inconsistencies between its parts, confirms what the Quran claims: that this holy book has been tampered with by the hands of distortion (التحريف).

Among the major obscurities in it is that it neglected to mention his struggles in his early days, his arguments with his people, and the trials and persecutions he endured from them—which are the brilliant and shining precursors of his history (peace be upon him).

Among these is its neglect to mention his building of the Noble Kaaba (الكعبة المشرفة), his establishing it as a secure sanctuary, and his legislating the Pilgrimage (الحج). No religious researcher or social critic can doubt that this Ancient House (البيت العتيق), which still stands on its foundations after four thousand years, is among the greatest divine signs (الآيات الإلهية) that remind the people of the world of Allah, the Glorified, and His signs, and preserves the word of truth for long ages. It is the first house of Allah established for mankind, blessed and a guidance for all the worlds.

The neglect of its mention is due to nothing but an Israelite bias from the scribes and authors of the Torah, which prompted them to overlook the mention of the Kaaba while meticulously counting the altars he built: an altar in the land of Shechem, another east of Bethel, and another on the Mount of the Lord.

Furthermore, the description they gave of the noble Prophet Ishmael—that he was a wild lad, his hand against people and people’s hands against him, and that he had no honor except being expelled from his father's presence to grow up as an archer! They wish to extinguish the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah will perfect His light.

Among these: are the things attributed to him that do not befit the station of prophethood (النبوة) nor the spirit of godwariness (التقوى) and chivalry (الفتوة).

For instance, its saying: that Melchizedek, king of "Salem," brought out bread and wine to him, and he was a priest of God Most High and blessed him. (1)

Another example is its saying: that Abraham once told the chiefs of Pharaoh of Egypt that Sarah was his sister, and instructed Sarah to confirm this, saying to her: "Say you are my sister, that it may be well with me for your sake, and that my soul may live because of you." And he represented another time to Abimelech, king of Gerar, that she was his sister. Thus, Pharaoh took her as a wife once, and Abimelech another time. Then the Torah mentions Abraham’s interpretation of his saying "She is my sister"—once that she was his sister in religion, and another that she was the daughter of his father but not his mother, and so she became his wife.

The least that can be said of this discourse is that it portrays Abraham (far be it from the station of the Friend of Allah) as exposing his wife Sarah to the likes of Pharaoh and Abimelech, exploiting her so they might take her as a wife while she was already married, so that he might receive great gifts and draw from them the wealth they possessed!

Moreover, the text of the Torah is explicit that Sarah was at that time—especially when Abimelech took her—an old woman who had reached seventy or more. Custom dictates that a woman at the age of the elderly loses the freshness of her youth and the radiance of her beauty, and kings and luxurious tyrants are not inclined toward anyone but young women of exquisite beauty and fresh charm.

Perhaps something resembling this meaning is found in some narrations. In the two Sahihs of al-Bukhari and Muslim, narrated from Abu Hurayrah, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "The Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) never lied except three lies:

(1) They might justify this by saying that Melchizedek, this priest of the Lord, is "Amraphel," king of Shinar mentioned at the beginning of the story, who is "Hammurabi," the king and lawgiver who is one of the first dynasty of the kings of Babylon. There is great disagreement regarding the date of his reign; most of what has been said does not align with the time of Abraham's life, which is (2000) BCE. It is mentioned in the book The Arabs Before Islam that he ruled Babylon in the years 2287–2232 BCE. In The Code of Hammurabi, quoting the book The Oldest Laws in the World by Professor F. Edwards, the years of his reign were 2205–2167 BCE. In the Dictionary of Famous People of the East and West, it says he took the sultanate of Babylon in 1728–1686 BCE, and in the Bible Dictionary, it says he took it in 1975–1920 BCE.

What most clearly contradicts this event is that the steles discovered in the ruins of Babylon containing the Code of Hammurabi include the mention of several Babylonian gods, indicating that Hammurabi was among the polytheists, which makes it untenable for him to be a priest of the Lord.

two for the sake of Allah: his saying, 'I am indeed sick' and his saying, 'Rather it was this biggest one of them who did it'; and one regarding Sarah—for he came to the land of a tyrant while accompanied by Sarah, who was the most beautiful of people. He said to her: 'If this tyrant learns that you are my wife, he will overpower me to take you. If he asks you, tell him that you are my sister, for you are my sister in Islam, as I know of no Muslim on earth other than you and me.' When he entered his land, some of the tyrant's people saw her and went to him, saying: 'A woman has come to your land who should belong to no one but you.' So he sent for her and she was brought. Abraham stood up to pray. When she entered upon him, he could not restrain himself and stretched his hand toward her, but his hand was seized with a violent seizure. He said to her: 'Pray to Allah to release my hand and I will not harm you.' She did so, but he returned [to his intent] and was seized even more severely than the first two times (1). He said: 'Pray to Allah to release my hand, and you have Allah's promise that I will not harm you.' She did so, and his hand was released. He summoned the one who had brought her and said to him: 'You have brought me a devil, not a human being.' So he expelled her from his land and gave her Hagar."

He said: "She came walking, and when Abraham (peace be upon him) saw her, he finished [his prayer] and said to her: 'What news?' She said: 'Good; Allah restrained the hand of the profligate and provided a servant.'" Abu Hurayrah said: "That is your mother, O children of the water of heaven."

In Sahih al-Bukhari, through many chains from Anas and Abu Hurayrah; in Sahih Muslim, from Abu Hurayrah and Hudhayfah; in Musnad Ahmad, from Anas and Ibn Abbas; and narrated by al-Hakim from Ibn Mas'ud, al-Tabarani from Ubadah ibn al-Samit, Ibn Abi Shaybah from Salman, al-Tirmidhi from Abu Hurayrah, and Abu Awanah from Hudhayfah from Abu Bakr, is the hadith of the intercession (الشفاعة) of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) on the Day of Resurrection. It is a long hadith which mentions: that the people at the gathering place will go to the prophets one by one, asking them for intercession before Allah. Every time they go to a prophet and ask him for intercession, he refers them to the one after him and excuses himself by mentioning some of his lapses—until they reach the Seal of the Prophets, Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny), who grants their request. In the hadith: they come to Abraham (peace be upon him) asking him to intercede for them before Allah, and he says to them: "I am not the one for you; I told three lies: his saying, 'I am indeed sick', his saying, 'Rather it was this biggest one of them who did it', and his saying to his wife, 'Tell him I am your brother.'"

Sound reasoning (الاعتبار الصحيح) does not agree with the content of these two hadiths, as mentioned by some researchers. For if the intent was that the three statements described as lies were not actual lies but rather a type of allusion (التوريات) and rhetorical double-entendre (المعاريض)—as might be suggested by some wordings of the hadith, such as what appeared in

(1) Thus in the original source from which it was quoted; it seems there is an omission.

some of its chains from the saying of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny): "Abraham did not lie except three lies, all of them for the sake of Allah," and likewise his saying (peace be upon him and his progeny): "None of them was a lie except that he was defending (ماحل) the religion of Allah"—then why does Abraham, in the hadith of the Resurrection, count them as sins for himself that prevent him from undertaking the matter of intercession, and why does he excuse himself because of them? For in this case, they would have been among his trials for the sake of Allah and his good deeds in religion—if it were permissible for a prophet among the prophets to lie for the benefit of religion. However, you have already learned in the previous discussions on prophethood in the second volume of this book that this is absolutely impermissible for the prophets (peace be upon them), as it would necessitate the loss of trust in their reports and discourses fundamentally.

Furthermore, if this type of reporting were permissible to be counted as a lie and a hindrance to intercession before Allah, the Glorified, then his saying (peace be upon him) when he saw a star, the moon, and the sun: "This is my Lord" and "This is my Lord," would be more worthy of being counted as a lie preventing intercession, which signifies proximity to Allah.

Moreover, his saying (peace be upon him), as Allah narrated by His saying: "Then he cast a glance at the stars, and said, ‘I am indeed sick’" [Qarai: 37:88-89], does not appear by any contextual clues to be a lie inconsistent with reality. Perhaps he (peace be upon him) was sick with a type of illness that did not prevent him from what he intended of breaking the idols.

Likewise, his saying (peace be upon him) to the people when they asked him about the matter of the broken idols: "They said, ‘Was it you who did this to our gods, O Abraham?’" [Qarai: 21:62], to which he replied—while they knew their idols were inanimate objects with no consciousness or will: "He said, ‘Rather it was this biggest one of them who did it’" [Qarai: 21:63], then followed it with his saying: "‘Ask them, if they can speak’" [Qarai: 21:63]. There is no way to count this as a lie, for it is a statement placed for the purpose of rebuking, intended to compel the opponent to admit the falsehood of his doctrine. Therefore, the people found no choice but to admit this, saying: "‘You certainly know that these do not speak.’ He said, ‘Do you then worship besides Allah that which cannot benefit you in anything, nor harm you? Fie on you and on what you worship besides Allah!’" [Qarai: 21:65-67].

If the intent were that the three statements were actual lies, that would be a blatant contradiction of the Book of Allah. We leave this to the understanding of the critical researcher; let him refer to what was mentioned in Chapter 2 regarding the status of Abraham (peace be upon him) before Allah and his station of servitude (العبودية), for which Allah praised him with the most beautiful praise and commended his station most eloquently.

I wonder how the soul of a critical researcher could accept or allow that a verse like His saying, the Exalted: "And mention in the Book Abraham. Indeed, he was a truthful man, a prophet" [Qarai: 19:41], could apply to a liar who resorts to lying whenever paths become narrow for him? Or how could Allah praise with those noble commendations a man who does not observe Allah, the Glorified, in truth or sincerity? (Far be the sanctuary of the Friend of Allah from such a thing).

As for the reports narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), they confirm the Torah in the essence of the story, but they exalt Abraham (peace be upon him) above the lies and other things attributed to him that do not suit his holy sanctuary. Among the most comprehensive accounts containing the story of the Friend (peace be upon him) is what is in al-Kafi, from Ali, from his father, and several of our companions from Sahl, all from Ibn Mahbub, from Ibrahim ibn Zayd al-Karkhi, who said: I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: "Abraham (peace be upon him) was born in Kutha (1) and his father was from its people. The mother of Abraham, the mother of Lot (peace be upon him), Sarah, and Waraqah—and in one version, Raqabah—were sisters, the daughters of Lahij. Lahij was a warning prophet but not a messenger.

Abraham (peace be upon him) in his youth was upon the primordial nature (الفطرة)—the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being—until Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, guided him to His religion and chose him. He married Sarah, the daughter of Lahij, who was his maternal cousin. Sarah possessed much livestock, vast land, and was in a good state. She had given Abraham ownership of all she possessed, so he managed it and improved it, and the livestock and crops increased until there was no man in the land of Kutha in a better state than him.

When Abraham (peace be upon him) broke the idols of Nimrod, Nimrod ordered him to be bound. He built an enclosure (حيرا) (2) for him, gathered wood in it, and ignited the fire. Then he cast Abraham (peace be upon him) into the fire to burn him. They then withdrew from it until the fire died down. When they looked into the enclosure, they found Abraham (peace be upon him) safe, released from his bonds. Nimrod was informed of his news, so he ordered them to exile Abraham (peace be upon him) from his land and to prevent him from leaving with his livestock and wealth. Abraham (peace be upon him) argued with them at that point, saying: 'If you take my livestock and wealth, then it is my right upon you that you return to me what has passed of my life in your land.' They took the dispute to Nimrod's judge, who ruled against Abraham (peace be upon him) that he must surrender to them all he had acquired in their land, and ruled against Nimrod's companions that they must return to Abraham (peace be upon him) what had passed of his life in their land. Nimrod was informed of this

(1) It was a village in the outskirts of Kufa; al-Jazari recorded it as Kutha. (2) Al-Hayr is a shortened form of al-Ha'ir, which means a walled enclosure.

and ordered them to let him go, along with his livestock and wealth, and to expel him. He said: 'If he remains in your land, he will corrupt your religion and harm your gods.' So they expelled Abraham and Lot (peace be upon them) with him from their land to Syria.

Abraham set out, and with him were Lot, who did not leave him, and Sarah. He said to them: 'I am going to my Lord; He will guide me' [Qarai: 37:99], meaning to Jerusalem. Abraham traveled with his livestock and wealth. He made a chest and placed Sarah in it, locking it out of protective jealousy (غيرة) for her. He proceeded until he left the dominion of Nimrod and traveled into the dominion of a man from the Copts called 'Azarah.' He passed by a tax collector who intercepted him to take a tithe of what he had. When he reached the collector with the chest, the collector said to Abraham (peace be upon him): 'Open this chest so we may tithe what is in it.' Abraham (peace be upon him) said to him: 'Assume whatever you wish is in it of gold or silver, so that we may give its tithe, but do not open it.' He said: The collector refused anything but opening it. He said: Abraham (peace be upon him) was forced (غصب) to open it. When Sarah appeared to him—and she was described as having great beauty and grace—the collector said to him: 'What is this woman to you?' Abraham (peace be upon him) said: 'She is my wife (حرمتي) and my maternal cousin.' The collector said to him: 'What prompted you to hide her in this chest?' Abraham (peace be upon him) said: 'Protective jealousy for her, lest anyone see her.' The collector said to him: 'I will not let you leave until I inform the king of her state and yours.'

He said: So he sent a messenger to the king and informed him. The king sent a messenger from his side to bring the chest. They came to take it, but Abraham (peace be upon him) said to them: 'I shall not part with the chest until my soul parts from my body.' They informed the king of this, so the king sent word: 'Carry him and the chest with him.' So they carried Abraham (peace be upon him), the chest, and all that was with him until he was brought before the king. The king said to him: 'Open the chest.' Abraham (peace be upon him) said to him: 'O king, in it is my wife and my maternal cousin, and I am willing to ransom its opening with all that I have.'

He said: The king forced Abraham to open it. When he saw Sarah, his self-restraint could not overcome his folly, and he stretched his hand toward her. Abraham (peace be upon him) turned his face away from her and from him out of protective jealousy and said: 'O Allah, withhold his hand from my wife and my maternal cousin.' His hand did not reach her nor did it return to him. The king said to him: 'Is it your God who did this to me?' He said: 'Yes, my God is jealous and hates the forbidden (الحرام); He is the one who came between you and the forbidden act you intended.' The king said to him: 'Then pray to your God to return my hand to me; if He answers you, I will not approach her.' Abraham (peace be upon him) said: 'My God, return his hand to him so he may refrain from my wife.' He said: So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, returned his hand to him. The king turned his gaze toward her again and moved his hand toward her once more. Abraham turned his face away from him out of protective jealousy and said: 'O Allah, withhold his hand from her.' He said: So his hand withered and did not reach her.

The king said to Abraham (peace be upon him): 'Indeed your God is jealous and you are jealous. Pray to your God to return my hand to me, for if He does, I will not repeat it.' Abraham (peace be upon him) said: 'I ask Him that on the condition that if you repeat it, you will not ask me to pray for you.' The king said to him: 'Yes.' Abraham (peace be upon him) said: 'O Allah, if he is truthful, return his hand to him.' So his hand returned to him.

When the king saw that protective jealousy and saw the sign (الآية) in his hand, he magnified Abraham (peace be upon him), stood in awe of him, honored him, and feared him. He said to him: 'You are safe from me approaching her or anything you have. Go wherever you wish, but I have a request of you.' Abraham (peace be upon him) said: 'What is it?' He said: 'I would like you to permit me to give her a beautiful and intelligent Coptic girl I have to be a servant for her.' He said: Abraham (peace be upon him) permitted it, so he called for her and gifted her to Sarah—and she was Hagar, the mother of Ishmael (peace be upon him).

Abraham (peace be upon him) traveled with all he had, and the king went out with him, walking behind Abraham (peace be upon him) out of magnification for Abraham and awe of him. Then Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, revealed to Abraham (peace be upon him): 'Stop and do not walk in front of the domineering tyrant while he walks behind you. Rather, place him in front of you and walk behind him, magnifying him and respecting him, for he is a ruler, and there must be a leadership in the land, whether righteous or profligate.' So Abraham (peace be upon him) stopped and said to the king: 'Proceed, for my God has just revealed to me that I should magnify you and respect you, and that I should place you in front of me and walk behind you out of honor for you.' The king said to him: 'Was this revealed to you?' Abraham (peace be upon him) said: 'Yes.' The king said to him: 'I bear witness that your God is Kind, Clement, and Generous, and you make me desire your religion.'

He said: The king bid him farewell, and Abraham (peace be upon him) traveled until he settled in the upper parts of Syria, leaving Lot (peace be upon him) in the lower parts of Syria. Then, when the child was delayed for Abraham (peace be upon him), he said to Sarah: 'If you wish, you could sell Hagar to me; perhaps Allah will grant us a child from her who will be a successor for us.' So Abraham (peace be upon him) bought Hagar from Sarah, and he went in to her, and she bore Ishmael (peace be upon him)."

Among these [distortions] is what the Torah mentioned in the story of the sacrifice: that the one to be sacrificed was Isaac rather than Ishmael (peace be upon them), despite the fact that the story of his being settled in the land of Tihama, his building of the Noble Kaaba, and the legislation of the acts of Pilgrimage—which reenact the trial and hardship he and his mother underwent for the sake of Allah, including the circumambulation (الطواف), the striving (السعي), and the sacrifice (التضحية)—all of this supports that the one to be sacrificed was Ishmael rather than Isaac (peace be upon them).

It occurs in the Gospel of Barnabas that the Messiah blamed the Jews and rebuked them for their claim that the one to be sacrificed was Isaac rather than Ishmael. He said in Chapter 44: "Then God spoke to Abraham, saying: 'Take your son, your firstborn Ishmael, and go up the mountain to offer him as a sacrifice.' How then could Isaac be the firstborn, when Ishmael was seven years old when he was born?" (Chapter 44, verses 11-12).

As for the Quran, its verses are almost explicit that the one to be sacrificed was Ishmael (peace be upon him). The Exalted said, after mentioning the story of breaking the idols and his being cast into the fire and it being made cool and safe: "They desired to outwit him, but We made them the lowermost. He said, ‘I am going to my Lord; He will guide me. My Lord! Give me [a son], one of the righteous.’ So We gave him the good news of a forbearing lad. When he was old enough to assist him, he said, ‘My son! I see in a dream that I am sacrificing you. See what you think.’ He said, ‘O my father! Do whatever you are commanded to do. If Allah wishes, you will find me to be patient.’ So when they had both surrendered [to Allah’s will] and he had laid him down on his forehead, We called out to him, ‘O Abraham! You have indeed fulfilled the vision! Thus do We reward the virtuous! This was indeed a manifest trial.’ Then We ransomed him with a great sacrifice, and We left for him a good name among posterity. ‘Peace be to Abraham!’ Thus do We reward the virtuous. Indeed he was one of Our faithful servants. And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet, one of the righteous. And We blessed him and Isaac, and among the descendants of the two there are the virtuous and those who are manifest wrongdoers to themselves." [Qarai: 37:98-113].

One who contemplates these noble verses finds no choice but to admit that the one to be sacrificed is the one whom Allah, the Glorified, mentioned the glad tidings of in His saying: "So We gave him the good news of a forbearing lad," and that the other glad tidings mentioned at the end in His saying: "And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet, one of the righteous," is different from the first glad tidings. The one announced in the second—who is Isaac (peace be upon him)—is other than the one announced in the first, which was followed by the mention of the story of his sacrifice.

As for the narrations, those reported through Shiah chains from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) mention that the one to be sacrificed was Ishmael (peace be upon him). Those reported through Sunni chains are varied: one group mentions Ishmael and another mentions Isaac (peace be upon him), but you have seen that the first group is the one that accords with the Book.

Al-Tabari said in his Tarikh: "The predecessors among the scholars of the community of our Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) differed regarding which of his two sons Abraham was commanded to sacrifice. Some said: it was Isaac son of Abraham, and others said: it was Ishmael son of Abraham. Both views have been narrated from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny); had one of them been authentic, we would not have bypassed it for another. However, the evidence from the Quran for the correctness of the narration reported from him (peace be upon him and his progeny) that he said: 'It is Isaac,' is clearer and more manifest than for the correctness of the other."

Until he said: "As for the indication from the Quran which we said points to Isaac being more correct, it is the saying of the Exalted reporting the prayer of His Friend Abraham when he left his people, migrating to his Lord toward Syria with his wife Sarah: 'I am going to my Lord; He will guide me. My Lord! Give me [a son], one of the righteous.' This was before he knew Hagar and before she became the mother of Ishmael. Then our Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, followed that with the report of answering his prayer and giving him the good news of a forbearing lad, then the vision of Abraham that he was sacrificing that lad when he reached the age of assisting him.

We do not know in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, any glad tidings to Abraham of a male child except for Isaac, and that is His saying: 'And his wife was standing by and she laughed; so We gave her the good news of Isaac, and of Jacob after Isaac' and His saying: 'Then he felt a fear of them. They said, "Do not be afraid," and they gave him the good news of a learned boy. Then his wife came forward with a cry and slapped her face and said, "A barren old woman!"'

Furthermore, this is the case in every place where the glad tidings to Abraham of a lad are mentioned; it only mentions Allah's glad tidings to him through his wife Sarah. Therefore, it is necessary that in His saying: 'So We gave him the good news of a forbearing lad,' it should be similar to what is in the other surahs of the Quran regarding the glad tidings to him through his wife Sarah.

As for the reasoning of those who argued that Allah did not command the sacrifice of Isaac because the glad tidings from Allah had come before his birth regarding his birth and the birth of Jacob from him after him, it is a reasoning that does not necessitate the correctness of what they said. This is because Allah only commanded Abraham to sacrifice Isaac after Isaac reached the age of assisting, and it is possible that Jacob was born to him before his father was commanded to sacrifice him.

Likewise, there is no weight to the reasoning of those who argued from the horns of the ram that he saw hanging in the Kaaba, for it is not impossible that it was carried from Syria to the Kaaba and hung there." End of his words.

I wonder how it was hidden from him that when Abraham (peace be upon him) asked his Lord for a child upon his migration to Syria—while Sarah was with him and there was no news of Hagar at that time—he asked for it by saying: "My Lord! Give me [a son], one of the righteous." He asked his Lord for a child, and did not ask that He grant it to him from Sarah, such that the glad tidings mentioned after it must be interpreted as the glad tidings of Isaac. He only said: "My Lord! Give me," and did not say: "My Lord! Give me from Sarah."

As for what he mentioned—that what is known from other places in the Book of Allah is the glad tidings of Isaac, so we must interpret the glad tidings in this place as him as well—besides what will come of the discussion regarding the other instances he pointed to, it is in itself an analogy (قياس) for which there is no evidence; rather, the evidence is to the contrary. For Allah, the Glorified, in these verses, when He mentioned the glad tidings of a forbearing lad and then mentioned the story of the sacrifice, He initiated a second mention of the glad tidings of Isaac. One who contemplates this context will not doubt that the one announced the second time is other than the one announced the first time. Abraham (peace be upon him) was given the glad tidings of another son before Isaac, and that is none other than Ishmael. All narrators, transmitters, and historians agree that Ishmael was born to Abraham before Isaac (peace be upon them both).

Among these [distortions] is the manifest contradiction in what the Torah mentions regarding the matter of Ishmael. It explicitly states that Ishmael was born to Abraham (peace be upon him) nearly fourteen years before Isaac was born to him, and that Abraham (peace be upon him) expelled him and his mother Hagar after the birth of Isaac when he mocked Sarah. Then it narrates the story of their settling in the valley, the depletion of the water Hagar carried, Ishmael's thirst, and then the angel showing her the water. One who looks and contemplates the story will not doubt that Ishmael was at that time a nursing infant—so you must refer to it and reflect upon it. This is what accords with what is reported in our narrations.

6 — The Noble Quran pays the utmost attention to the story of Abraham (peace be upon him) regarding himself and regarding his two noble sons, Ishmael and Isaac, and their descendants together. This is unlike what is encountered in the Torah, for it limits the reports about him to what relates to Isaac and the people of Israel, and does not pay attention to Ishmael except with some things that belittle his matter and disparage his status. Despite this, the little it reports is not free from contradiction: at one time it mentions Allah's address to Abraham (peace be upon him) that "your remaining seed is from Isaac," and at another time His address that "Allah has blessed your seed from the offspring of Ishmael and will make him a great nation." At one time it describes him as a wild man, his hand against people and people's hands against him, having grown up as an archer expelled from his father's house, and at another time it mentions that Allah is with him.

By reflecting on what has preceded of his story (peace be upon him) in the Quran, the answer to two objections raised against the Mighty Book becomes clear.

The First Objection: What was mentioned by some Orientalists (1) that the Quran in its Meccan surahs does not

(1) Quoted by al-Najjar in Stories of the Prophets from the Orientalist Hurgronje and the Orientalist Wensinck in the Encyclopedia of Islam.

address the matter of Abraham and Ishmael (peace be upon them) except as it addresses the matter of other messengers—that they were upon the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), warning the people and calling them to Allah, the Glorified—without mentioning his building of the Kaaba, his connection to Ishmael, and their being callers of the Arabs to the religion of primordial nature (دين الفطرة) and the Hanifi creed (الملة الحنيفية). However, the Medinan surahs, such as al-Baqarah, al-Hajj, and others, mention Abraham and Ishmael connected by the bond of fatherhood and sonship, as the two fathers of the Arabs who legislated for them the religion of Islam and built the Kaaba, the Sacred House.

The secret of this difference—according to them—is that Muhammad had relied on the Jews in Mecca, but they soon adopted a hostile stance toward him, so he had no choice but to seek another supporter. There, a guided intelligence led him to a new role for the father of the Arabs, Abraham. By this, he was able to break free from the Judaism of his time to connect his rope to the Judaism of Abraham, counting him as the father of the Arabs, the builder of their religion, Islam, and the builder of their holy house in Mecca, since this city occupied most of his thinking. End of the summary.

The objector has disparaged himself with this slander attributed to the Mighty Book, which has a global fame that cannot be hidden from Easterner or Westerner. Every contemplating researcher observes that the Noble Quran did not flatter a polytheist, a Jew, a Christian, or anyone else in a Meccan or Medinan surah, nor did the tone of its speech differ in pointing out the errors of the Jews or others according to whether the surahs were Meccan or Medinan.

However, since the Quranic verses were revealed gradually (نجوما) according to the occurrence of events related to the religious call, and the trial with the matter of the Jews was after the Migration (الهجرة), the addressing of their affairs and the clarification of the severity regarding them was necessarily in the verses revealed within the Medinan surahs—just like the details of the legislated rulings that were revealed therein according to the pressing need arising from events.

As for what they claimed regarding the exclusivity of the discourse on the connection of Ishmael to Abraham (peace be upon him), the building of the Kaaba, and the establishment of the Hanifi religion to the Medinan surahs, it is refuted by the saying of the Exalted in Surah Ibrahim, which is Meccan, in what He narrated of the prayer of Abraham (peace be upon him): "When Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Make this city a sanctuary, and save me and my children from worshipping idols... Our Lord! I have settled some of my descendants in a barren valley near Your Sacred House, our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer. So make the hearts of some people fond of them, and provide them with fruits, so that they may give thanks... All praise belongs to Allah, who, despite [my] old age, gave me Ishmael and Isaac. Indeed my Lord hears all supplications.’" [Qarai: 14:35-39]. Its like has already passed in the verses quoted from Surah al-Saffat just now, informing of the story of the one to be sacrificed.

As for what they mentioned regarding the Judaism of Abraham (peace be upon him), the Quran refutes it by the saying of the Exalted: "O People of the Book! Why do you argue concerning Abraham, when the Torah and the Evangel were not sent down until after him? Do you not exercise your reason?... Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was a Hanif, a Muslim, and he was not one of the polytheists." [Qarai: 3:65-67].

The Second Objection: That the Sabians (الصابئين)—who are the star-worshippers whose gods the Quran mentions Abraham (peace be upon him) addressing in His saying: "When night darkened over him, he saw a star and said, ‘This is my Lord’" [Qarai: 6:76] until the end of the verses—were only in the city of Harran, to which Abraham (peace be upon him) migrated from Babylon or from "Ur." This necessitates that his argument with the star-worshippers occurred some time after his argument with the idol-worshippers, his breaking of the idols, and his entering the fire. This does not align with what is apparent from the verses: that the story of the argument with the idol-worshippers and the star-worshippers all occurred within two days at the beginning of his confrontation with his father and his people, as previously explained.

I say: This is in reality an objection to the interpretation (التفسير) that was previously presented in the explanation of the verses, not to the Book itself.

Nevertheless, it contains an oversight regarding what history establishes and what sound reasoning provides. As for reasoning: a kingdom that adopts in some of its great cities one of the widespread, known religions—like Sabianism, which was then among the known religions in the world—is not free from its prevalence in the rest of its cities and the presence of a group of its adherents scattered throughout its regions.

As for history: it mentions its prevalence just like the prevalence of idolatry in Babylon, and the existence of many temples therein built in the names of the stars with idols for them erected inside. It is mentioned in the history of the land of Babylon and its surroundings that the building of the temple of the sun-god and the moon-god occurred around the year 3200 BCE. In the stele of the Code of Hammurabi, the sun-god and the moon-god are mentioned, which is close to the time of the Friend Abraham (peace be upon him).

It has already been mentioned in what we quoted from the book The Remaining Signs by Abu Rayhan al-Biruni (1): that Budasaf appeared after one year of the reign of Tahmuras in the land of India, brought the Persian script, and called to the

(1) In a historical discussion under verse 62 of Surah al-Baqarah.

creed of the Sabians, and many people followed him. The Pishdadian kings and some of the Kayanids who inhabited Balkh used to magnify the two luminaries (the sun and moon), the stars, and the universal elements until the time of the appearance of Zoroaster after thirty years of the reign of Vishtaspa.

He continued the discourse until he said: "They attribute governance to the celestial sphere and its bodies, and they speak of their life, speech, hearing, and sight. They magnify the lights. Among their remains is the dome above the mihrab at the enclosure of the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus; it was their place of prayer. The Greeks and Romans were upon their religion. Then it came into the hands of the Jews, who made it their synagogue. Then the Christians overcame them and turned it into a church until Islam and its people came and took it as a mosque.

They had temples and idols in the names of the sun with known shapes, as mentioned by Abu Ma'shar al-Balkhi in his book on houses of worship, such as the temple of Baalbek which belonged to the idol of the sun, and Harran which is attributed to the moon, its construction being in its image like a mantle (طيلسان). Near it is a village called Salamsin, its ancient name being 'Idol of Sin,' meaning the idol of the moon; and another village called Tar' 'Uz, meaning the Gate of Venus.

They mention that the Kaaba and its idols belonged to them, and its worshippers were among them; that al-Lat was in the name of Saturn, and al-Uzza in the name of Venus." End of his words.

Al-Mas'udi mentioned that the doctrine of the Sabians was a type of transformation and evolution within the religion of idolatry, and that Sabianism might transform into idolatry due to the proximity of their origins. Idolatry might involve worshipping the idols of the sun, moon, Venus, and other stars to seek proximity through them to their gods, and then to the God of gods.

He said in The Meadows of Gold: "Many of the people of India, China, and other groups believed that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is a body, and that the angels are bodies with specific measures, and that Allah, the Exalted, and His angels are veiled by the heaven. This prompted them to take statues and idols in the image of the Creator, the Mighty and Majestic, and some in the image of the angels with different statures and shapes. Some were in the image of humans and others in different forms, which they worshipped, offered sacrifices to, and made vows for, because of their resemblance—in their view—to the Creator, the Exalted, and their proximity to Him.

They remained upon this for a period of time and a number of ages until some of their sages alerted them that the celestial spheres and stars are the closest visible bodies to Allah, the Exalted, and that they are living and speaking, and that the angels move between them and Allah, and that everything that happens in this world is only according to what the stars bring about by the command of Allah. So they magnified them and offered sacrifices to them so they might benefit them, and they remained upon that for an age."

When they saw that the stars were hidden during the day, and at certain times of the night due to atmospheric veils, some of the sages among them commanded that idols and statues be fashioned according to their forms and shapes. Thus, they made idols and statues corresponding to the number of the great and famous stars. Each group among them would venerate a specific star, offering it a type of sacrifice (القربان) different from the others, believing that if they venerated the fashioned idols, the celestial bodies of the seven planets would move in their favor according to their desires. They built a separate house and temple (هيكل) for each idol and named those temples after those stars.

Some people have even suggested that the Sacred House (البيت الحرام) is the House of Saturn (زحل). According to them, the reason this House has endured through the passage of ages, remaining venerated across all eras, is that it is the House of Saturn, and that Saturn took charge of it. This is because Saturn’s nature is endurance and stability; thus, whatever belongs to it neither vanishes nor perishes, nor does its veneration cease. They mentioned other matters which we have refrained from citing due to the repulsiveness of their description.

As time passed, they worshipped the idols on the basis that they would bring them closer to Allah, and they abandoned the direct worship of the stars. They remained in this state until Budasaf (يوذاسف) appeared in the land of India; he was an Indian. Budasaf traveled from India to Sindh, then to the lands of Sijistan and Zabulistan—which were the lands of Firuz ibn Kabk—then entered Sindh again, and finally reached Kerman.

He claimed prophethood and asserted that he was a messenger of Allah and a medium (واسطة) between Allah and His creation. He arrived in the land of Persia during the early reign of Tahmuras, the King of Persia; it is also said this occurred during the reign of Jam. He was the first to manifest the doctrines of the Sabians (الصابئة) according to what we have previously presented in this book.

Budasaf had commanded the people toward asceticism (الزهد) in this world and to occupy themselves with the higher realms, since the origin of souls (النفوس) is from there and to those realms the return (الصدر) from this world occurs. Budasaf renewed the worship of idols and prostration to them among the people to serve as reminders of their likenesses, and he made their worship acceptable to their intellects through various types of ruses and deceptions.

Those expert in the affairs of this world and the history of its kings mentioned that King Jam was the first to venerate fire and call people to its veneration. He said that it resembles the light of the sun and the stars, for in his view, light (النور) was superior to darkness (الظلمة), and he established ranks for light. Subsequently, people disputed after him, and each group venerated what they deemed worthy of veneration among things, seeking proximity to Allah thereby.

Al-Mas'udi then mentioned the houses they held in veneration, which were seven: the Kaaba, the Sacred House (البيت الحرام), in the name of Saturn; a house on Mount Mars in Isfahan; the House of Mandusan in the land of India; the House of Nawbahar in the city of Balkh in the name of the Moon; the House of Ghamdan in the city of Sana'a in Yemen in the name of Venus; the House of Kawusan in the city of Fergana in the name of the Sun; and a house in the upper regions of China in the name of the First Cause (العلة الأولى).

The Greeks, the ancient Romans, and the Slavs also had venerated houses, some of which were built in the name of the stars, such as the house in Tunis belonging to the Romans, which was in the name of Venus.

Al-Mas'udi further mentioned that the Sabians of Harran (١) had temples (هياكل) named after the intellectual substances (الجواهر العقلية) and the stars. Among these were the Temple of the First Cause (العلة الأولى) and the Temple of the Intellect (العقل). He said: "Among the temples of the Sabians are the Temple of the Chain, the Temple of the Form, and the Temple of the Soul; these are circular in shape. The Temple of Saturn is hexagonal, the Temple of Jupiter is triangular, the Temple of Mars is an oblong rectangle, the Temple of the Sun is square, the Temple of Mercury is triangular, the Temple of Venus is a triangle inside an oblong rectangle, and the Temple of the Moon is octagonal. The Sabians have symbols and secrets regarding what we have mentioned which they keep hidden." End quote. A similar account is found in al-Milal wa al-Nihal by al-Shahrastani.

It has become clear from what we have quoted: First, that just as paganism (الوثنية) involved worshipping idols of the deities and the lords of the species (أرباب الأنواع), it also involved worshipping the idols of the stars, the sun, and the moon, and they possessed temples in their names. It is possible that the disputation (حجاج) of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) regarding the star, the moon, and the sun was with the pagans who worshipped them and sought proximity through them, rather than the Sabians. It is also possible that it was with some of the Sabians in the city of Babylon, the town of Ur, or Kutharya, according to some of the narrations cited previously.

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of what the Noble Quran relates is that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) argued with his father and his people and endured their harm for the sake of Allah until he withdrew from them and left them, migrating from their land to the Holy Land, without first traveling from their land to Harran and then from Harran to the Holy Land. That which history books have recorded regarding his migration to Harran first and then to the Holy Land has no source other than the Torah or unreliable reports influenced by Israelite narrations (نفثة إسرائيلية), as is evident to anyone who contemplates the history of al-Tabari and others.

Moreover, some have mentioned that the Harran mentioned in the Torah was a town near Babylon between the Euphrates and Khabur, and it is not the Harran located near Damascus that exists today. (١) Yes, al-Mas'udi mentioned that what remained of their—the Sabians'—venerated temples at that time—which was the year 332 AH—was a house of theirs in the city of Harran at the Gate of Raqqa known as "Maghlitiya," which they consider to be the temple of Azar, the father of Ibrahim the Friend (peace be upon him). These people have much to say regarding Azar and his son Ibrahim. End quote. However, there is no proof in their claims for anything.

Secondly: Just as paganism (الوثنية) sometimes worshipped the sun, the moon, and the stars, the Sabians likewise built houses and temples for the worship of things other than the stars, the moon, and the sun—such as the First Cause (العلة الأولى), the Intellect (العقل), and the Soul (النفس)—and they sought proximity to them just as the pagans did. Herodotus mentioned in his history, while describing the Temple of Babylon, that it consisted of eight towers built one upon another, and that the last and highest tower contained a spacious dome in which there was nothing but a great throne, in front of which was a golden table. There were no statues or idols in the dome, and no one spent the night there except a woman whom the people claimed Allah had chosen for service and appointed for attendance. End quote. (٢) Perhaps this was for the First Cause (العلة الأولى), which is transcendent (المنزهة) above forms and shapes, even if they sometimes depicted it with images they imagined, as mentioned by al-Mas'udi. It has been established that their philosophers used to transcend Allah the Exalted above corporeal forms (الهيئات الجسمانية), material shapes, and positions, describing Him with attributes befitting Him. However, they used to fear the common folk and would not manifest what they believed regarding Him, the Glorified—either because the commoners' understanding was not prepared to receive that, or for political purposes and objectives that necessitated concealing the truth.

(١) The Harranians are referred to as Sabians in an absolute sense due to the fame of Harran for this religion. (١) Mentioned in the Dictionary of the Bible under "Harran." (٢) The History of Herodotus the Greek, authored around 500 BC.

Ayahs

وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ كُلًّا هَدَيْنَا ۚ وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِۦ دَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُوسُفَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٨٤)
And We gave him Isaac and Jacob and guided each of them. And Noah We had guided before, and from his offspring, David and Solomon, Job, Joseph, Moses and Aaron—thus do We reward the virtuous— (84)
وَزَكَرِيَّا وَيَحْيَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَإِلْيَاسَ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٨٥)
and Zechariah, John, Jesus and Ilyas—each of them among the righteous— (85)
وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَٱلْيَسَعَ وَيُونُسَ وَلُوطًا ۚ وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٦)
and Ishmael, Elisha, Jonah and Lot—each We graced over all the nations— (86)
وَمِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ وَإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ ۖ وَٱجْتَبَيْنَـٰهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٨٧)
and from among their fathers, their descendants and brethren—We chose them and guided them to a straight path. (87)
ذَٰلِكَ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ يَهْدِى بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٨٨)
That is Allah’s guidance: with it, He guides whomever He wishes of His servants. But were they to ascribe any partners [to Allah], what they used to do would not avail them. (88)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ ۚ فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيْسُوا۟ بِهَا بِكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٩)
