Through this, the system of human livelihood is organized and the affair of his life is set straight. Therefore, He concluded it with His saying: ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ — "That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing." For He is the All-mighty (العزيز) whom no overcomer can overpower to spoil any of His governance, and the All-knowing (العليم) who is not ignorant of any of the interests of His kingdom, such that He might organize it in a way that might spoil itself or not endure by its nature.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ النُّجُومَ لِتَهْتَدُوا بِها — "It is He who made the stars for you, so that you may be guided by them" (6:97) to the end of the verse. The meaning is clear, and what is meant by "detailing the signs" (تفصيل الآيات) is either detailing them according to the creative making (الجعل التكويني) or detailing them according to the verbal exposition.

There is no contradiction between intending the interests of the human being in his life and livelihood in this station—from what appears to the outward senses of the movements of these great celestial bodies and the gravitating heavenly spheres—and each of these bodies being intended by a separate Divine will and created by a volition (مشية) that pertains to its own essence and specifies its individual person. For the aspects are different, and the realization of some of these aspects does not prevent the realization of others; connection and attachment (الارتباط والاتصال) govern all parts of the world.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌ — "It is He who produced you from a single soul, then there is a residing place and a depository" (6:98) to the end of the verse. It has been recited as "residing place" (مُسْتَقَرٌّ) with a fathah on the qaf and with a kasrah. According to the first recitation, it is a noun of place meaning the location of stability; thus, "depository" (مُسْتَوْدَعٌ) would also be a noun of place meaning the location of depositing, which is the place where a trust is put. The mention of the "residing place" and the "depository" occurred in His saying, the Exalted: وَما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ رِزْقُها وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّها وَمُسْتَوْدَعَها كُلٌّ فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ — "There is no animal on the earth, but that its sustenance is upon Allah, and He knows its residing place and its depository. Everything is in a manifest Book" (11:6). In the speech, there is omission and brevity; the implied meaning is: "Among you is he who is in a residing place, and among you is he who is in a depository." According to the second recitation, which is more weightier, "residing" (مُسْتَقِرٌّ) is an active participle, and "depository" (مُسْتَوْدَعٌ) must be a passive participle; the implied meaning is: "Among you is one who is settled, and among you is one who is deposited and has not yet settled."

It is apparent that what is meant by His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ — "It is He who produced you from a single soul" — is the termination of the human progeny, despite its multiplicity and spread, at Adam, whom the Noble Quran considers the origin of the existing human race. What is meant by the "residing place" (المستقر) is that portion of human individuals who have undergone birth and thus settled on the earth, which is the residing place for this species, as the Exalted said: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ — "and for you there is a residing place on the earth" (2:36). What is meant by the "depository" (المستودع) is whoever is deposited in the loins and wombs and has not yet been born, but will be born after a time. This is what is appropriate for the station of explaining the sign by the production of all individuals of the species from a single individual. It is also possible to take "residing place" and "depository" as mim-infinitives (مصدر ميمي).

He expressed this with the word "production" (الإنشاء) rather than "creation" (الخلق) and the like, which is apparent in instantaneous occurrence and what is like it, rather than gradual process. This meaning is also supported by what was previously mentioned in His saying: وَما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ رِزْقُها وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّها وَمُسْتَوْدَعَها — as is not hidden—meaning He knows what has settled of them on the earth by the actuality of formation, and what is on the way to formation from that which has not yet actually formed nor settled on the earth.

The meaning is: He is the one who brought you into existence, O assembly of humans, from a single soul, and populated the earth with you until a time; so it remains occupied by you as long as you do not become extinct. Thus, some of you are always settled in it, and some of you are deposited in the loins and wombs, or in the loins only, on the way to settling in it.

The commentators have offered other meanings for the verse, such as the saying of some: that what is meant by producing them from a single soul is creating them from one type of soul, which is the human soul; or that what is meant is the production from one type of psychological and physical composition, which is the human reality composed of a human soul and body.

And like the saying of some: that the "residing place" refers to the wombs and the "depository" to the loins; and the saying of others: that the "residing place" is the earth and the "depository" is the grave; and the saying of others: that the "residing place" is the womb and the "depository" is the earth or the grave; and the saying of others: that the "residing place" is the spirit and the "depository" is the body—among other sayings of theirs which are of little benefit to address.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً — "It is He who sends down water from the sky" (6:99) to the end of the verse. "The sky" (السماء) is the direction of height; everything that is above you and shades you is a sky. What is meant by His saying: فأخرجنا به نبات كل شيء — "then We bring forth with it the growth of all things" — according to what has been said, is: We brought forth by the water We sent down from the sky the vegetation and growth that is in every growing thing that has the power of vegetation, from latency to manifestation; meaning, We caused to grow by it every vegetative thing such as herbs, trees, humans, and other animals.

"Greenery" (الخضر) is that which is green, and it is as if it were a contraction of "the greening" (الخاضر). "Clustered grain" (تراكب الحب) is the setting of some of it above others, as in an ear of corn. "Spathe" (الطلع) is the first thing that appears of the fruit of the date-palm. "Clusters" (القنوان) is the plural of "cluster" (قنو), which is the bunch (العذق) of dates, like a cluster of grapes. "Low-hanging" (الدانية) means near. "Similar and dissimilar" (المشتبه وغير المتشابه) means resembling and not resembling in type, shape, and other qualities. "The ripening of the fruit" (ينع الثمر) is its maturity.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned matters from what He created so that he who has sight and insight may look into them, and be guided by looking into them to His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. These are earthly matters like the splitting of the grain and the pit and such; and celestial matters like the night, the morning, the sun, the moon, and the stars; and a matter pertaining to the human being himself, which is the production of his species from a single soul, then a residing place and a depository; and matters composed of all these, like the sending down of rain from the sky and the preparation of food from plants, grains, and fruits, and the growing of that which has the power of growth, such as plants, animals, and humans, from that.

He counted the stars as a sign specifically for a people who "know" (يعلمون), and the production of human souls as a sign specifically for a people who "understand" (يفقهون), and the governance of the system of vegetation as a sign for a people who "believe" (يؤمنون). The appropriateness is apparent: for looking into the matter of the system is a simple matter that does not require extra burden; rather, ordinary understanding attains it provided it is enlightened by the fairness of faith and not stained by the filth of obstinacy and stubbornness. As for looking into the stars and celestial positions, it is something that does not go beyond the scholars of this field who know the stars, their positions, and other celestial states to some extent, and general common understanding does not attain it except with burden. As for the sign of the souls, gaining knowledge of them and the secrets of creation they possess requires, in addition to theoretical research, internal vigilance, deep immersion, and profound stability, which is "understanding" (الفقه).

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ الْجِنَّ وَخَلَقَهُمْ — "They make the jinn partners of Allah, though He created them" (6:100) to the end of the verse. "The jinn" (الجن) is either the object of "they make" (جعلوا) and its other object is "partners" (شركاء), or it is an appositive (بدل) for "partners." His saying: وَخَلَقَهُمْ — "though He created them" — is as if it were a circumstantial clause (حال), even if some grammarians forbid it, though their argument is not clear. In any case, the word is in the station of refuting them; the meaning is: they made the jinn partners to Him while He created them, and the created cannot possibly share with its Creator in His station.

What is meant by "the jinn" are the devils, as is attributed to the Magians who say: Ahriman and Yazdan. Similar to it is what the Yazidis hold, who say in the divinity of Iblis (Malak Taus—Shah Pariyan). Or it refers to the well-known jinn, based on what is attributed to the Quraysh that they used to say: Allah had intermarried with the jinn, and the angels were born between them. This is more appropriate to the context of His saying: وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ الْجِنَّ وَخَلَقَهُمْ وَخَرَقُوا لَهُ بَنِينَ وَبَناتٍ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ — "They make the jinn partners of Allah, though He created them, and they fabricate sons and daughters for Him without any knowledge" (6:100). Based on this, the sons and daughters are all from the angels; they "fabricated" (خرقوا) them, meaning they invented them and attributed them to Him as a fabrication against Him, the Glorified, and Exalted is He above what they associate.

If what was meant was more general than the angels, it would not be far-fetched that what is meant by them is what exists in other non-Islamic nations. Brahmanism and Buddhism say something similar to what the Christians said regarding the sonship of the Messiah, as previously mentioned in the third volume of this book. Other ancient polytheists used to affirm for Allah, the Glorified, sons and daughters from the gods, according to what discovered artifacts indicate; and the Arab polytheists used to say: the angels are the daughters of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: بَدِيعُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "The Originator of the heavens and the earth" (6:101) to the end of the verse. This is a response to their saying regarding sons and daughters. Its summary is that there is no way for the reality of a child to be realized except by taking a female companion, and He, the Exalted, has no companion; so how could He have a child?

Also, He, the Exalted, is the Creator of every thing and its Originator. A child is a part of a thing that it nurtures through a type of fertilization, and the part of a thing and its like cannot possibly be created by it. All of this is gathered by the fact that He, the Exalted, is the Originator (بديع) of the heavens and the earth, whom nothing of its parts resembles in any way. So how could He have a companion to marry, or sons and daughters who resemble Him in species? This is a matter informed by Allah, for whom there is no way to ignorance; for He is All-knowing of every thing. What was previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying: ما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُ اللهُ — "It is not for a human that Allah should give him..." (3:79) in the third volume of this book contains what is useful in this station.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "That is Allah, your Lord, there is no god except Him, the creator of all things" (6:102) to the end of the two verses. The first sentence, namely His saying: ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ — "That is Allah, your Lord" — is a conclusion drawn from the exposition provided in the previous verses. The meaning is: if the matter is as mentioned, then Allah whom We have described is your Lord and none else. His saying: لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "there is no god except Him" — is like an explicit declaration of the implicit monotheism contained in the previous sentence. Moreover, it serves the meaning of a justification (تعليل); i.e., He is the Lord and there is no Lord besides Him because He is Allah, besides whom there is no god. And how could another be a Lord when he is not a god?

His saying: خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "the creator of all things" — is a justification for His saying: لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ; i.e., divinity is restricted to Him because He is the Creator of every thing without exception. Thus, there is no creator of any thing besides Him such that he might share with Him in divinity; and every thing is created by Him, submitting to Him in servitude (العبودية), so nothing equals Him in it.

His saying: فَاعْبُدُوهُ — "so worship Him" — follows as a result of His saying: ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ; i.e., if Allah, the Glorified, is your Lord and none else, then worship Him. His saying: وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ — "and He is watchful over all things" — means He is the one standing over every thing, governing its affair, and organizing the system of its existence and life. If it is so, then it is obligatory that He be feared, and that no partner be taken for Him without knowledge. The sentence is like an emphasis for His saying: فَاعْبُدُوهُ; i.e., do not refrain from His worship because He is a guardian over you, not heedless of the system of your deeds.

As for His saying: لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصارُ — "The sights do not perceive Him" (6:103) — it is to repel the misconception suggested by His saying: وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ according to what the simple understandings of the polytheists receive—and the address is to them. That is, if He becomes a guardian over them, He would be a corporeal thing like other corporeal things that undertake corporeal acts. So He repelled this by stating that He, the Exalted, is not perceived by sights due to His transcendence above corporeality and its concomitants. His saying: وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصارَ — "and He perceives the sights" — repels what comes to the minds of these polytheists who are accustomed to material thinking and have clung to the senses and the sensible. That is, if He is raised above the attachment of sights to Him, He would exit the realm of the senses and the sensible, and the type of existential connection that is the basis of consciousness and knowledge would be nullified; He would be severed from His creatures, so He would not know anything just as nothing knows Him, and He would not see anything just as nothing sees Him. So He, the Exalted, answered this by His saying: وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصارَ. Then He justified this claim by His saying: وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ — "and He is the All-attentive, the All-aware." The "All-attentive" (اللطيف) is the subtle one who penetrates into a thing, and the "All-aware" (الخبير) is the one who possesses expertise. Thus, if He, the Exalted, encompasses every thing in the true sense of encompassing, He is a witness over every thing; neither the outward of any thing nor its inward is missing from Him. Since He is, along with that, possessed of knowledge and expertise, He is knowledgeable of the outward and inward of things without one thing distracting Him from another, or one thing being veiled from Him by another. Thus, He, the Exalted, perceives the sight and the seen together, while the sight only perceives the seen.

He attributed His perception to the "sights" (الأبصار) themselves rather than the "possessors of sights," because the perception existing in Him, the Exalted, is not of the kind of our sensory perceptions such that it would attach to the outward of things from their accidents—like sight, for example, which attaches to lights and colors, and through which proximity, distance, greatness, smallness, movement, and rest are perceived in a certain way. Rather, the accidents and their subjects, in their outward and inward, are present before Him, disclosed to Him, not veiled from Him nor absent. Thus, He, the Exalted, finds the sights in their realities and what they possess, while they do not attain Him.

In these two verses, there is such loftiness of exposition, ease of path, and brevity of speech that it bewilders the intellect; yet they guide the one who ponders them to secrets behind which are veils.

(A Discussion on the Universality of Creation and its Extension over Every Thing)

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "That is Allah, your Lord, there is no god except Him, the creator of all things" — its literal meaning is the universality of creation for every thing and the extension of His bringing into existence, the Exalted, over everything that has a share of existence and realization. This phrase, namely His saying: خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "the creator of all things" — has been repeated by Him, the Exalted, in His speech without there being anything that could serve to specify it in any way. The Exalted said: قُلِ اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "Say, 'Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the One, the All-paramount'" (13:16). And the Exalted said: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ — "Allah is the creator of all things, and He is watchful over all things" (39:62). And the Exalted said: ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "That is Allah, your Lord, the creator of all things, there is no god except Him" (40:62).

Wondrous disputes have broken out among researchers from the people of various religions regarding this issue, followed by different sayings even from the theologians and philosophers of the Christians and Jews, let alone the theologians and philosophers of Islam. It does not concern us to hasten to cite their sayings and opinions and to debate them; rather, our research is Quranic and exegetical, and we have no business with anything other than what is obtained as a summary of the view of the Noble Quran by pondering over the various aspects of its noble verses.

We find that the Noble Quran accepts what we accept: that external subjects and things occurring in the realm of existence—such as the sky and its planets and stars; the earth and its mountains, valleys, plains, seas, lands, elements, and minerals; the clouds, thunder, lightning, thunderbolts, rain, and hail; the herbs, trees, animals, and humans—possess effects and properties which are their acts, and these are attributed to them as the attribution of an act to its agent and an effect to its cause.

We find it confirming that the human being, like all other existing species, has acts that are attributed to him and subsist through him, such as eating, drinking, walking, and sitting; and like health, illness, growth, understanding, consciousness, joy, and happiness, without differentiating between him and other species in any of that. Thus, it informs of his deeds, commands him, and forbids him; were it not that he possessed an act, none of that would return to a realized meaning. Thus, the Quran weighs an individual human being with the same measure we humans weigh in our society: we believe he has acts and effects attributed to him, for some of which we hold him accountable—those that return in some way to his choice (اختياره), such as eating, drinking, and walking—and we excuse him in what does not return to his choice from his effects subsisting through him, such as health, illness, youth, old age, and so on.

Thus, the Quran organizes the existing system just as it is organized before our senses and supported by our intellects through the experiences that accompany them: that the parts of this system, despite the difference in their identities and types, are active through their acts, influencing and being influenced by others and from others. Through this, the parts of the existing system—each part of which has a complete connection with every other part—are joined together. This is the general law of causality (قانون العلية العام) in things: that everything for which it is possible in itself to exist and not to exist, only exists through another. Thus, the effect (المعلول) is impossible of existence in the absence of its cause (علته). The Noble Quran has endorsed the validity of this law and its universality; were it not valid or were it to fail in some instances, no deduction could be made through it at all. The Quran has deduced through it the existence of the Maker, His oneness, His power, His knowledge, and the rest of His attributes.

Just as the effect among things is impossible of existence in the absence of its cause, it must also necessarily exist with the existence of its cause, in fulfillment of the existential link (الرابطة الوجودية) between them. Allah, the Glorified, has enacted this in His speech in many instances where He deduced from the path of His supreme attributes the establishment of their effects and consequences, such as His saying: وَهُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "and He is the One, the All-paramount" — and His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انتِقامٍ — "Indeed Allah is All-mighty, Master of Retribution" — and أَنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ — "that Allah is All-mighty, All-wise" — and إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "Indeed Allah is All-forgiving, All-merciful" — and so on. He also deduced many events and matters from the establishment of other things whose establishment follows them, such as His saying: فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ — "But they would not believe in what they had denied before" (10:74) — and other things mentioned regarding the affair of the believers, the disbelievers, and the hypocrites. If it were possible for an effect to fail its producer when the necessary conditions are met and the contrary obstacles are removed, none of these arguments and proofs would be valid at all.

Thus, the Quran accepts the sovereignty of the general law of causality in existence: that every thing among existing things and their accidents, and every event among occurring events, has a cause or a set of causes by which its existence becomes necessary and without which its existence becomes impossible. This is something beyond doubt upon initial reflection.

Furthermore, we find that Allah, the Glorified, in His speech, generalizes His creation to everything to which the name of a part of the cosmos applies. The Exalted said: قُلِ اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "Say, 'Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the One, the All-paramount'" (13:16) — among other verses cited just now. This extends His causality and agency, the Exalted, to every thing, alongside the flow of cosmic causality and effectuality among them all, as previously explained.

The Exalted said: الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — until He said — وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً — "He to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth... and He created every thing and ordained it in a [precise] ordaining" (25:2). And He said: الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (20:50). And He said: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى ، وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى — "He who created and fashioned, and who ordained and guided" (87:2-3) — among other verses.

In these verses, there is another type of exposition in which things are taken as attributed to creation (الخلقة), and their acts and types of effects, movements, and rests are taken as attributed to Divine ordaining (التقدير) and guidance (الهداية). Thus, to His ordaining, the Exalted, terminate the specificities of the acts of things and their effects—such as the human being stepping and walking in his spatial transition, the fish swimming, and the bird flying with its wings. The Exalted said: فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلى بَطْنِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلى رِجْلَيْنِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَمْشِي عَلى أَرْبَعٍ يَخْلُقُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ — "Some of them crawl on their bellies, some of them walk on two feet, and some of them walk on four. Allah creates whatever He wishes" (24:45). The verses in this meaning are many. Thus, the specificities of the acts of things, their limits, and their measures terminate in Him, the Exalted. Likewise, the goals (الغايات) that things aim for—despite their differences, dispersion, and variety—are only determined for them and sought by them through the Divine guidance that accompanies them from the first of their existence to its end; and that terminates in the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing.

Thus, things in their substances (جواهرها) and essences (ذواتها) rely on Divine creation; and the limits of their existence, their transformations, their goals, and their objectives in the course of their existence and life—all of that terminates in the ordaining that terminates in the specificities of Divine creation. There are many other verses stating that the parts of the cosmos are connected to one another, harmonious with one another, unified in existence, governed by a single system for which there is no governor but Allah, the Glorified; this is what is sometimes called the "proof of the connection of governance" (برهان اتصال التدبير).

This is what reflection on His speech, the Exalted, produces. However, there are other aspects that the reflecting researcher should not overlook, and they are three:

First: That among things are those whose ugliness and repulsiveness are beyond doubt, such as types of injustice and immorality, from whose attribution to the realm of sanctity and majesty the intellect recoils. The Noble Quran also sanctifies Him, the Exalted, from every injustice and evil in many verses, such as His saying: وَما رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ — "and your Lord is not at all unjust to [His] servants" (41:46) — and His saying: قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ — "Say, 'Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency'" (7:28) — and so on. This [seemingly] contradicts the universality of creation for every thing; thus, it is obligatory to specify the verse with this intellectual and legal specifier.

This results in the human acts being created by the human being, while what lies beyond him of things—their essences and effects—are created by Allah, the Glorified.

Furthermore, human acts being created by Him, the Exalted, would nullify them being from human choice, and thereby nullify the system of command and prohibition, obedience and disobedience, reward and punishment, the sending of messengers, the revelation of books, and the legislation of laws. This is what a group of researchers has mentioned.

These people, in their research, have failed to differentiate between real matters (الأمور الحقيقية) that attain existence and realization in reality, and conventional matters (الأمور الاعتبارية) and positional aspects that have no establishment in reality, but which the human being was forced to conceptualize or affirm due to the need of life and the pursuit of the happiness of existence through society and civilization. Thus, they mixed between the existential and non-existential aspects of things. A portion of this research has already passed in the discussion on predestination and free will (الجبر والتفويض) in the first volume of this book.

What is appropriate for this station of the discourse is that the literal meaning of His saying: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Allah is the creator of all things" — generalizes creation to every thing. Then His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ — "who perfected everything He created" (32:7) — establishes beauty (الحسن) for everything that Allah has created. It is obtained from the two verses that everything to which the name of a "thing" applies—other than Allah—is created, and that every creature is characterized by beauty. Thus, creation and beauty are inseparable in existence; every thing, from the aspect that it is created by Allah—i.e., in its entire external reality—is beautiful. If the accident of evil and ugliness befalls it, it is from the aspect of relations, additions, and other matters other than the aspect of its reality and real existence by which it is attributed to Allah, the Glorified, and to its agent to whom it is presented.

Furthermore, we find in His speech, the Exalted, many instances where He mentions evil (السيئة), injustice (الظلم), sin (الذنب), and others as a matter of acceptance. Let us conclude by joining this to what preceded: that these are non-real meanings and titles that do not attach to a thing from the aspect of its attribution to Allah, the Glorified, and His creation of it. Rather, they attach to the subject through which the effect and act subsist from the aspect of a convention, relation, or addition. For every disobedience and injustice has with it, of its own nature, that which is not a disobedience; they only differ from the aspect of one of them containing a violation of a legislative or intellectual command, or containing a corruption in society or a violation of a goal, unlike the other. An example of this is adultery and marriage: they are two similar acts that do not differ in their reality and their specific existence, for example; they only differ by agreement with or violation of the Divine law, or the social custom, or the interest of society. Those are conventional matters and relational aspects. Creation and bringing into existence only pertain to the aspect of creation (التكوين) and the external world. As for the relational aspects and conventional titles that attach to things according to their correspondence to social interests and corruptions—which are followed by praise and blame, or reward and punishment, according to what the practical intellect and social consciousness specify and judge—they are matters that do not exceed the stage of society and do not enter the realm of creation at all, except for their effects, which are the types of reward and punishment, for example.

Thus, the specific act, such as injustice by its title which is "injustice," is ugly in the sphere of society and a disobedience that entails blame and punishment among those in society. As for according to creation, it is nothing but an effect or a set of effects of the kind of movements occurring to the human being; and the external causes—especially the first Divine causality—only produce this aspect, which is the aspect of creation. As for its ugly title and what attaches to it, it is the product of the view of legislation or the rational [convention]; there is no news of it from the view of creation. Just as Zayd the "President" is, by his title which is "presidency," a social subject for us having effects resulting from it in society, such as respect, precedence, influence of word, and administration of affairs; but from the aspect of creation and reality, he is nothing but an individual from among human individuals, no different from the subordinate individual at all, and there is no news in this view of presidency and the effects resulting from it. Likewise are the rich and the poor, the master and the servant, the mighty and the lowly, the noble and the base, and such examples that cannot be counted.

In summary, creation—while it encompasses every thing—only pertains to the subjects and acts occurring in the sphere of society, titled with their various titles, by the aspect of their creation and their external reality. As for what lies beyond that of the aspects of ugliness and beauty, disobedience and obedience, and the rest of the social descriptions and titles occurring to acts and subjects, creation and bringing into existence do not pertain to them. They have no establishment except in the sphere of legislation or social judgment and the arena of convention and status.

Once it is clear that the sphere of the realization of command and prohibition, the emergence of beauty and ugliness, obedience and disobedience, and the attachment of reward and punishment and their link to the act—as well as the rest of the social matters and titles such as mastership, servitude, presidency, subordination, might, lowliness, and the like—is other than the sphere of creation and the arena of external reality to which creation and bringing into existence pertain, it becomes clear that the universality of creation for every thing does not entail any of the corruptions they mentioned, such as the nullification of the system of command and prohibition, reward and punishment, and other things previously mentioned.

And how could it be permissible for one who ponders His speech, the Exalted, to rule with such dualism (الثنوية) when His speech is filled with the fact that He is the Creator of every thing, and that He is Allah, the One, the All-paramount; and that His decree, ordaining, creative guidance, Lordship, and governance encompass every thing, nothing escaping Him; and that His kingdom, authority, encompassment, and Chair (الكرسي) encompass every thing; and that to Him belongs what is in the heavens and the earth, what is manifest and what is hidden? How could any of these Divine teachings—informing of His monotheism in His Lordship—stand with the existence of countless creatures of another besides Him amidst His creatures?

Second: That the Noble Quran, in attributing the creation of every thing to Him, the Exalted, and restricting the active cause to Him, would necessitate the nullification of the link of causality and effectuality among things. Thus, there would be no influencer in existence but Allah; it would only be His custom, the Exalted, that has run to create what we call an "effect" following what we call a "cause," without there being between them a link that necessitates the existence of the effect from them following the cause. Thus, the fire that is followed by heat—its relation to heat and cold would be equal; and heat—its relation to fire and ice would be equal; except that the custom of Allah has run to create heat following fire and cold after ice, without there being any necessity or requirement there at all.

This view nullifies the general law of causality and effectuality upon which the intellectual judgment is based. By its nullification, the door to proving the Maker is closed; and with that, it would not even reach the turn of a Divine Book to argue through it for the nullification of the link of causality and effectuality among things. How could the Noble Quran possibly nullify an explicit intellectual judgment and dismiss the intellect from its judgment? Its truthfulness and authority are only established by the intellectual judgment and the intuitive decree; this would be the nullification of the conclusion for its own proof, which only results in the nullification of the conclusion for itself.

These people only fell into what they fell into because of their mixing between vertical (طولية) and horizontal (عرضية) causes. It is only impossible for two causes to converge upon one thing if they are in one horizontal plane, not if one of them is in the vertical line of the other. An example of this is that the complete cause for the existence of fire, just as it necessitates the existence of fire, likewise necessitates the existence of heat; and there do not converge in the heat, along with that, two necessities, nor do two independent complete causes act upon it; rather, it is a cause that is an effect of a cause.

In another, more precise approach: the source of the error is the failure to distinguish between the agent in the sense of "that from which" (ما منه) and the agent in the sense of "that by which" (ما به). The exhaustive discussion of this issue belongs elsewhere.

Third: Which is close in derivation to the second, is that when they found that He, the Exalted, attributes the creation of every thing to Himself, while He, the Exalted, simultaneously accepts the existence of the link of causality and effectuality among things themselves, they thought that whatever among things has an apparent, known cause, that cause is for it instead of Him, the Exalted; otherwise, it would necessitate the convergence of two independent causes upon one effect. Thus, there remains for His influence, the Exalted, nothing but the origination (حدوث) of things and the beginning of their existence. Therefore, you see them seeking to prove the Maker from the aspect of the origination of things, such as the origination of the human being after he was not, the origination of the earth after it was not, and the origination of the world after it was not.

They add to this the existence of matters or the occurrence of events whose causes are unknown to the human being, such as the spirit, and life in humans, animals, and plants—for the human being has not yet discovered the causes of their existence. The simple among them add to this things like clouds, snow, rain, comets, earthquakes, famine, high prices, general diseases, and so on, whose natural causes do not appear to common understandings. Then, whenever the natural cause of any of them appears to them, they flee from it to another, changing one position for another or surrendering to the opponent.

This, according to the scholarly language, is that the contingent existence (الوجود الممكن) only needs the Necessary (الواجب) in its origination, not in its persistence (بقائه). This is what a vast multitude of the people of theology (أهل الكلام) insist upon, until some of them explicitly stated: that if non-existence were possible for the Necessary, His non-existence would not harm the existence of the world—Exalted and Sanctified is Allah. This—in our estimation—is an Israelite opinion that has seeped into the minds of a number of Muslim researchers. Among its branches is their saying of the impossibility of "change in Divine decree" (البداء) and "abrogation" (النسخ). Yet the opinion remains prevalent among people nonetheless.

In any case, it is among the worst of illusions, and the Quranic argumentation opposes it. For Allah, the Glorified, deduces the existence of the Maker and His unity through the witnessed signs in the world, which is the system running in every type of creation, and what runs upon it in the course of its existence and the span of its life of change, transformation, action, and affection, and the benefits it draws from that and delivers to others—such as the sun, the moon, the stars, their rising and setting, and the benefits people derive from them; and the seasonal transformations occurring to the earth, the seas, the rivers, and the ships that run in them; and the clouds and rain; and what the human being benefits from animals and plants; and what runs upon him of natural states and cosmic changes from the stage of the sperm, the embryo, childhood, youth, old age, decrepitude, and so on.

All of these are from the aspects returning to things from the aspect of their persistence; and their subjects are the causes of their accidents and effects. Every set of them at one moment is the cause for the set obtained after that moment; the events of today are the causes of the events of tomorrow, just as they are the effects of the events of yesterday.

If matters, from the aspect of their persistence, were independent of Allah, the Glorified, and were independent through the events that surround them and the effects and acts that occur to them, none of these brilliant arguments and conquering proofs would be valid. That is because the Quran's argumentation with these manifest signs is from two aspects:

One of them: From the aspect of the Agent, as indicated by such sayings of the Exalted: أَفِي اللهِ شَكٌّ فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Is there any doubt about Allah, the originator of the heavens and the earth?" (14:10). For it is necessary that none of these existents originated its own essence nor brought itself into existence, nor did another thing like it bring it into existence, for it would resemble it in the need for a bringer-into-existence to bring it into existence. If the matter did not terminate in an existent existing by its essence, which does not accept the occurrence of non-existence upon it, none of these things would exist in the external world. Thus, they exist by the bringing into existence of Allah, who is in Himself the Truth (حق) who accepts neither nullification nor change in any way from what He is upon.

Furthermore, when they exist, they are not independent of Him. The bringing into existence of a thing by another is not like the heating of a heater, for example, where the heat is poured by separation from the heater into the heated, so the heated becomes a possessor of the description whether the heater remains after that or vanishes. For if the emanation of existence were upon this manner, the emanated existence would become independent by itself, necessary by its essence, not accepting non-existence due to contradiction. This is what the simple, primordial understanding expresses by saying that if things possessed their own existence and were independent in any way from their Lord, they would not accept destruction and corruption; for it is impossible for a thing to demand the nullification of itself or its misery.

This is what is benefited from such sayings as: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ — "Everything is to perish except His Face" (28:88) — and His saying: وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتاً وَلا حَياةً وَلا نُشُوراً — "and they do not control any harm or benefit for themselves, nor do they control death or life or resurrection" (25:3). This is also indicated by the many verses showing that Allah, the Glorified, is the Owner of every thing, there is no owner besides Him, and that every thing is owned by Him, having no state but being owned.

Thus, just as things receive the emanation of existence from Him, the Exalted, in the beginning of their being and origination, they likewise receive that emanation in the state of their persistence and the extension of their being and life. Thus, the thing continues to exist as long as existence is emanated upon it; and if the emanation is cut off from it, its trace is erased from the tablet of existence. The Exalted said: كُلًّا نُمِدُّ هؤُلاءِ وَهَؤُلاءِ مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً — "To these and to those We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not forbidden" (17:20) — among many other verses.

The second of them: From the aspect of the goals (الغايات), as indicated by the verses describing the system running in the cosmos—its parts being harmonious, its sides being in agreement—the course of one of them ensuring the delivery of the other to its perfection. What occurs at one end of the ordered chain is directed toward the happiness of what is at the other end of it. In this, the human being, for example, benefits from the system running in animals and plants; and the plant, for example, from the system running in the earth and the atmosphere surrounding it; and the earthly things seek support from the celestial things, and the celestial from the earthly. Thus, the whole becomes possessed of one connected system that drives every type of species to what it finds happiness in within its being and what it succeeds with in its existence. The sound primordial nature and the living consciousness refuse but to judge that all of that is from the ordaining of an All-mighty, All-knowing, and the governance of an All-wise, All-aware.

This ordaining and governance are nothing but from the originating of their essences, the bringing into existence of their identities, and the fashioning of their beings by casting each of them into a mold that ordains for it its acts and restricts it to what was intended from it in its station and what it shall result in at the destinations prepared along the extension of its course. It is in all these stages upon the mounts of means, between the driver of "ordaining" (القدر) and the leader of "decree" (القضاء).

The Exalted said: أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ — "Look! To Him belong the creation and the command" (7:54); and He said: أَلَا لَهُ الْحُكْمُ — "Look! All judgement belongs to Him" (6:62); and He said: وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا — "Everyone has a direction to which he turns" (2:148); and He said: وَاللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ لَا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ — "Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgement" (13:41); and He said: أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قَائِمٌ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ — "Is He who stands over every soul [to observe] what it earns..." (13:33).

How can one who contemplates such verses possibly divert their clear meanings and explicit contents to the notion that Allah, the Glorified, created the essences of things with their specific properties and individualities, then withdrew from them? As if He had no choice but to withdraw and merely observe, while things began to interact and organize themselves through the spirit of causality (العلية) and effectuality (المعلولية) inherent within them, becoming independent in action and reception, while their Creator watches them from His seclusion, waiting for the day when everything perishes so He may renew a new creation for them, rewarding those who listened to His call in their first life and punishing the arrogant and disdainful. According to this view, He has endured their opposition throughout time, though perhaps He occasionally becomes angry at what He witnesses from them, thus opposing them in their course and preventing the effect of some of their schemes by way of counteraction and obstruction.

This would mean that He, the Exalted, emerges from His state of seclusion only when the independent cosmic causes and effects lead to something contrary to His pleasure, or fail to lead to what accords with His will. In such cases, He would intervene in the cosmic causes by bringing about the events He desires. Yet, He could not intervene in anything except by nullifying the law of causality (قانون العلية) currently operating in that instance; for if He were to bring about what He willed through the path of causes and means, the effect—according to their claim—would belong to the cosmic causes rather than to Him, the Exalted. This is the secret behind the insistence of these people that miracles (المعجزات) and the breaking of habitual norms (خوارق العادات) and the like are realized through the Divine Will alone by violating the general law of causality. Thus, the matter must inevitably conclude with the violation of cosmic causality and the nullification of the law of cause and effect. Consequently, the very foundation of their premise—that things are in need of Him, the Exalted, for their origination (حدوثها) but independent of Him for their survival (بقائها)—is rendered void.

These people are forced to commit to one of two positions: either to say that the world, with its vastness and the system operating within it, is independent of Allah, the Glorified, not needing Him at all, and that He, the Exalted, has no influence over any of its parts or the transformations occurring within it, except for its initial need for Him at the moment of its first origination; and once He brought it into being, that need was lifted and the connection severed.

Or, they must say that Allah is the Creator of everything that falls under the name of "thing" (شيء) and the Bestower of existence upon it both at the moment of origination and during the state of survival, and that no essence or action is independent of Him, the Exalted, for even the blink of an eye.

You have already seen that Quranic inquiry refutes the first of these two positions, as the verses support one another in extending the creation and the unseen authority (السلطة الغيبية) over the outward and inward of things, their beginning and their end, their essences and their actions, both during their origination and their survival. Thus, the second position is the one determined to be correct, and precise rational inquiry (البحث العقلي) supports, in its conclusion, what is derived from the noble verses.

It has become clear from all that has preceded that what appears from His saying: اللَّهُ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Allah is the creator of all things" (39:62) remains upon its apparent generality without being restricted by any rational or legal specifier.

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ جَاءَكُمْ بَصَائِرُ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ فَمَنْ أَبْصَرَ فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ عَمِيَ فَعَلَيْهَا — "Certainly, insights have come to you from your Lord. So whoever sees, it is to his own benefit, and whoever is blind, it is to his own loss" (6:104). It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: "Insight (البصيرة) is the clear proof and evidence by which a thing is seen as it truly is, and basa’ir (بصائر) is its plural." End quote. It has also been said: Insight (البصيرة) is to the heart what sight (البصر) is to the eye. In any case, the root of the matter is perception through the sense of sight, which is considered the strongest of perceptions and a direct grasping of the witnessed object. "Seeing" (الإبصار) and "blindness" (العمى) in the verse refer to knowledge and ignorance, or faith and disbelief, by way of metaphorical extension.

It is as if He, the Exalted, is pointing by His saying: "Certainly, insights have come to you from your Lord" to the brilliant arguments He mentioned in the preceding verses regarding His oneness and the negation of any partner. The meaning is that these arguments are insights that have come to you from Allah's side through the revelation to me. The address is from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who then mentions to the addressees—the polytheists—that they have a choice regarding their own souls: if they wish, they may see through them, and if they wish, they may remain blind to them; however, the seeing is for their own benefit and the blindness is to their own detriment.

From here, it becomes clear that what is meant by "being a guardian over them" (الحفظ عليهم) is the return of the affairs of their souls and the management of their hearts to him. Thus, He only negates his being a guardian over them in an ontological sense (تكوينًا); rather, he is merely a sincere advisor to them. The verse is like a parenthetical statement between the preceding and succeeding verses. It is an address from Him, the Exalted, through the tongue of His Prophet, like a messenger who brings a message to a people and delivers it to them, and during the delivery, he speaks to them from himself with that which rouses them to listen and obey, and urges them toward submission by showing sincerity and negating any corrupt motives on his part.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ الْآيَاتِ وَلِيَقُولُوا دَرَسْتَ — "Thus do We vary the signs, and so that they may say, ‘You have studied [them]’" (6:105). It has been recited as darasta (دَرَسْتَ) in the second person, dā rasta (دَارَسْتَ), and darasat (دَرَسَتْ) in the feminine third person. It is said that "variation" (التصريف) is the circulation of a recurring meaning through successive contexts so that various aspects of benefit may be gathered therein. His saying: "You have studied" (دَرَسْتَ) is from al-dars, which is learning and teaching through recitation. The recitation dā rasta carries this meaning, though the increase in the structure of the word indicates an increase in meaning. As for the recitation darasat (دَرَسَتْ) in the feminine third person, it is from al-durūs, meaning the effacement of a trace—that is, these sayings have become worn out and obsolete, like their saying: "fables of the ancients."

The meaning is: In this manner, We vary the signs and transform them as an explanation for many ends, among which is that these wretched ones may complete their wretchedness by accusing you, O Muhammad, of having learned them from some of the People of the Book, or they may say: "These sayings have become obsolete, their time has passed, and there is no benefit in them today." And [another end is] that We may make it clear for a people who know, by purifying their hearts and expanding their breasts through it. This is like His saying: وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ مَا هُوَ شِفَاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَسَارًا — "We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the faithful, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss" (17:82).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, who said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: إن الله لا يوصف ، وكيف يوصف وقال في كتابه : « وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ » فلا يوصف بقدر إلا كان أعظم من ذلك — "Indeed, Allah cannot be described. And how could He be described when He said in His Book: 'They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him' (6:91)? Thus, He is not described by any measure except that He is greater than that."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding His saying: وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ — "They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him." He said: "They are the disbelievers who did not believe in Allah's power over them. Whoever believes that Allah is powerful over all things has regarded Allah with His due regard, and whoever does not believe that has not believed in Allah with His due regard. إِذْ قالُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلى بَشَرٍ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — 'when they said, "Allah has not sent down anything to any human."' This refers to the Children of Israel. The Jews said: 'O Muhammad, has Allah sent down a book to you?' He said: 'Yes.' They said: 'By Allah, Allah has not sent down any book from the heaven.' So Allah revealed: قُلْ — 'Say,' O Muhammad, مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِهِ مُوسى نُوراً وَهُدىً لِلنَّاسِ — 'Who sent down the Book that was brought by Moses as a light and guidance for the people' until His saying وَلا آباؤُكُمْ قُلِ اللهُ — 'nor your fathers? Say, "Allah [sent it down]."'"

I say: The meaning mentioned at the beginning of the narration was previously noted in the preceding commentary to be contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse. Rather, the apparent meaning is that those who said: "Allah has not sent down anything to any human" are the ones who did not regard Allah, the Glorified, with His due regard.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from al-Suddi regarding His saying: إِذْ قالُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلى بَشَرٍ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "when they said, 'Allah has not sent down anything to any human.'" He said: "Phinehas the Jew said: 'Allah has not sent down anything to Muhammad.'"

I say: The discrepancy between the narrator and the narrated event spoils the meaning, and the possibility of a paraphrase (النقل بالمعنى) given this flagrant discrepancy is unjustifiable.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, who said: "A man from the Jews called Malik ibn al-Sayf came and disputed with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The Prophet said to him: 'I adjure you by Him who sent down the Torah to Moses—do you find in the Torah that Allah hates the fat rabbi?' He was a fat rabbi, so he became angry and said: 'By Allah, Allah has not sent down anything to any human.' His companions said to him: 'Woe to you! Not even to Moses?' He said: 'Allah has not sent down anything to any human.' So Allah revealed: وَما قَدَرُوا اللهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ — 'They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him' the verse."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Buraydah that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "أم القرى مكة — 'The Mother of Cities is Mecca.'"

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from ‘Ali ibn Asbat, who said: I said to Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): "Why was the Prophet called al-Ummi?" He said: "نسب إلى مكة وذلك من قول الله : « لِتُنْذِرَ أُمَّ الْقُرى وَمَنْ حَوْلَها » وأم القرى مكة ، ومن حولها الطائف — 'He was attributed to Mecca, and that is from the saying of Allah: "so that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it" (6:92). The Mother of Cities is Mecca, and those around it is al-Ta’if.'"

I say: According to what is in the narration, His saying: "so that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it" becomes similar to His saying: وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ — "Warn your nearest relations" (26:214). The command to warn a specific group does not contradict the universality of the message to all people, as indicated by verses such as His saying: لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ — "that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach" (6:19), and His saying: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَالَمِينَ — "It is nothing but a reminder for all the nations" (6:90), and His saying: قُلْ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعًا — "Say, ‘O mankind! I am indeed the Apostle of Allah to you all’" (7:158).

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: قُلْ مَنْ أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ — "Say, 'Who sent down the Book...'" until His saying تَجْعَلُونَهُ قَراطِيسَ تُبْدُونَها وَتُخْفُونَ كَثِيراً — "you make it into parchments, displaying them while concealing much" (6:91). He said: "كانوا يكتمون ما شاءوا ويبدون ما شاءوا — 'They used to hide what they wished and show what they wished.'"

He said: In another narration from him (peace be upon him), he said: "كانوا يكتبونه في القراطيس ـ ثم يبدون ما شاءوا ويخفون ما شاءوا. قال : كل كتاب أنزل فهو عند أهل العلم — 'They used to write it on parchments, then show what they wished and hide what they wished.' He said: 'Every book that was sent down is with the People of Knowledge (أهل العلم).'"

I say: "The People of Knowledge" is an allusion to the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرَىٰ — "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates..." (6:93), al-Hakim narrated in al-Mustadrak from Shurahbil ibn Sa‘d, who said: "It was revealed concerning ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Sarh: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً أَوْ قالَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ وَلَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ شَيْءٌ — 'Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or says, "It has been revealed to me," while nothing has been revealed to him.' When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered Mecca, he fled to ‘Uthman, his milk-brother, who hid him until the people of Mecca felt secure, then he sought amnesty for him."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from ‘Ikrimah regarding His saying: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً أَوْ قالَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ وَلَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ شَيْءٌ — "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or says, 'It has been revealed to me,' while nothing has been revealed to him." He said: "It was revealed concerning Musaylimah regarding the rhymed prose and soothsaying he used to produce. وَمَنْ قالَ سَأُنْزِلُ مِثْلَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ — 'and him who says, "I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down."' He said: 'It was revealed concerning ‘Abdullah ibn Sa‘d ibn Abi Sarh. He used to write for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and when he would dictate عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ — "All-mighty, All-wise," he would write "All-forgiving, All-merciful," changing it. Then he would read it back to him as such-and-such, and he [the Prophet] would say: "Yes, it is the same." So he apostatized from Islam and joined the Quraysh.'"

I say: This meaning has been narrated through other chains as well, besides what has been mentioned.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, he said: My father narrated to us from Safwan, from Ibn Muskan, from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "إن عبد الله بن سعد بن أبي السرح ـ كان أخا لعثمان من الرضاعة قدم إلى المدينة وأسلم ، وكان له خط حسن ، وكان إذا نزل الوحي على رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ـ دعاه ليكتب ما نزل عليه فكان إذا قال له رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله : والله سميع بصير يكتب سميع عليم ، وإذا قال : « وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ » يكتب بصير ـ وكان يفرق بين التاء والياء ، وكان رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله يقول : هو واحد. فارتد كافرا ورجع مكة وقال لقريش : والله ما يدري محمد ما يقول ـ أنا أقول مثل ما يقول فلا ينكر علي ذلك ـ فأنا أنزل مثل ما أنزل الله ـ فأنزل الله على نبيه صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله في ذلك : « وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً ـ أَوْ قالَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ وَلَمْ يُوحَ إِلَيْهِ شَيْءٌ ـ وَمَنْ قالَ سَأُنْزِلُ مِثْلَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ ». فلما فتح رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله مكة أمر بقتله ـ فجاء به عثمان وقد أخذ بيده ورسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله في المسجد ـ فقال : يا رسول الله اعف عنه فسكت رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ـ ثم أعاد فقال : هو لك ، فلما مر قال رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله : ألم أقل : من رآه فليقتله؟ فقال رجل : كانت عيني إليك يا رسول الله ـ أن تشير إلي فأقتله ، فقال رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله : إن الأنبياء لا يقتلون بالإشارة ، فكان من الطلقاء — 'Indeed, ‘Abdullah ibn Sa‘d ibn Abi al-Sarh was the milk-brother of ‘Uthman. He came to Medina and embraced Islam. He had fine handwriting, and whenever revelation descended upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he would call him to write what was revealed. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would say to him: "And Allah is All-hearing, All-seeing," he would write "All-hearing, All-knowing." And when he said: "And Allah is All-aware of what you do," he would write "All-seeing." He would also distinguish between the tā’ and the yā’, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would say: "It is one." So he apostatized as a disbeliever and returned to Mecca, saying to the Quraysh: "By Allah, Muhammad does not know what he is saying. I say the like of what he says and he does not object to it; so I can send down the like of what Allah has sent down." Thus, Allah revealed to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding that: "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or says, 'It has been revealed to me,' while nothing has been revealed to him, and him who says, 'I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down.'" When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) conquered Mecca, he ordered him to be killed. ‘Uthman brought him, holding his hand, while the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in the mosque, and said: "O Messenger of Allah, pardon him." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained silent. Then he repeated it, and he said: "He is yours." When he passed by, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Did I not say: whoever sees him should kill him?" A man said: "My eyes were on you, O Messenger of Allah, waiting for you to signal me so I could kill him." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Indeed, Prophets do not kill by signaling." So he was among the freed ones (الطلقاء).'"

I say: This meaning has been narrated in al-Kafi, the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, and al-Majma‘ al-Bayan through other chains from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them).

Some commentators mentioned, after citing the story from the narrations of al-Suddi and ‘Ikrimah, that these two narrations are false, for there is nothing in the Meccan surahs containing "All-hearing, All-knowing," "All-knowing, All-wise," or "All-mighty, All-wise," except in Surah Luqman, which is narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas to have been revealed after Surah al-An‘am, and that the verse ending with His saying, the Exalted, "All-mighty, All-wise" and the two following it are Medinan, as stated in al-Itqan. He said: "What has been said regarding the possibility of this verse being revealed in Medina is unnecessary, and the narration is not authentic."

He said: "It is narrated that when ‘Abdullah ibn Sa‘d apostatized, he used to slander the Quran. Perhaps he said some of what was mentioned in the narrations about him as a lie and fabrication, for the surahs revealed during the time he was a scribe did not contain anything of what was narrated that he altered, as you have known. He returned to Islam before the conquest, and had he altered the Quran in a way that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) approved, causing him to doubt the revelation, he would not have returned to Islam." End quote.

You have already seen that the reliable narrations from the two Sadiqs (peace be upon them) are explicit that the story of Ibn Abi Sarh occurred in Medina after the Hijrah, not in Mecca. The reports narrated through the chains of the Sunnis are not explicit in their occurring in Mecca, if they do not actually appear to have occurred in Medina. As for what he relied upon from the narration of Ibn ‘Abbas regarding the order of the revelation of the Quranic surahs, it is no stronger in authority than what he dismissed.

As for what he mentioned regarding Ibn Abi Sarh's voluntary embrace of Islam before the conquest, you have seen the narration from both sides that he did not return to Islam until the day of the conquest, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had declared his blood legal on the day of the conquest until ‘Uthman interceded for him and he was pardoned.

However, it remains that according to the apparent meaning of the narrations, His saying, the Exalted: "and him who says, 'I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down'" does not clearly fit the saying of Ibn Abi Sarh as he recounts it: "So I send down the like of what Allah has sent down."

Furthermore, the fact that His saying, the Exalted: "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or says, 'It has been revealed to me,' while nothing has been revealed to him, and him who says, 'I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down'" was revealed in Medina does not harmonize with this apparent connection between it and what follows it until the end of the second verse. If it were revealed in Medina, it would be more likely that both verses were Medinan.

There is another narration expressing another occasion of revelation (شأن النزول), which is what ‘Abd ibn Humayd narrated from ‘Ikrimah, who said: "When وَالْمُرْسَلاتِ عُرْفاً فَالْعاصِفاتِ عَصْفاً — 'By the [angels] sent forth successively, by the tempestuous winds' (77:1-2) was revealed, al-Nadr, who was from the Banu ‘Abd al-Dar, said: 'And by the grinders grinding, and the kneaders kneading,' and many such sayings. So Allah revealed: 'Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or says, "It has been revealed to me," while nothing has been revealed to him' the verse."

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Salam, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذابَ الْهُونِ — "Today you shall be requited with a humiliating punishment" (6:93). He said: "العطش يوم القيامة — 'Thirst on the Day of Resurrection.'"

I say: It was also narrated from al-Fudayl from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), and in it he said: "Thirst."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Ibrahim, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "إن الله عز وجل لما أراد أن يخلق آدم ـ بعث جبرئيل في أول ساعة من يوم الجمعة ـ فقبض بيمينه قبضة ـ بلغت من السماء السابعة إلى السماء الدنيا ، وأخذ من كل سماء تربة وقبض قبضة أخرى ـ من الأرض السابعة العليا إلى الأرض السابعة القصوى ـ فأمر الله عز وجل كلمته ـ فأمسك القبضة الأولى بيمينه والقبضة الأخرى بشماله ـ ففلق الطين فلقتين فذرا من الأرض ذروا ـ ومن السماوات ذروا فقال للذي بيمينه : منك الرسل والأنبياء والأوصياء والصديقون ـ والمؤمنون والشهداء ومن أريد كرامته ، فوجب لهم ما لهم ما قال كما قال ، وقال للذي بشماله : منك الجبارون والمشركون والمنافقون والطواغيت ـ ومن أريد هوانه أو شقوته فوجب لهم ما قال كما قال. ثم إن الطينتين خلطتا جميعا وذلك قوله تعالى : « إِنَّ اللهَ فالِقُ الْحَبِّ وَالنَّوى » فالحب طينة المؤمنين التي ألقى الله عليها محبته ، والنوى طينة الكافرين الذين نأوا عن كل خير ، وإنما سمي النوى من أجل أنه نأى عن الحق وتباعد منه. وقال الله عز وجل : « يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَمُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتِ مِنَ الْحَيِ » فالحي المؤمن الذي يخرج من طينة الكافر ، والميت الذي يخرج من الحي هو الكافر ـ الذي يخرج من طينة المؤمن ـ فالحي المؤمن والميت الكافر وذلك قول الله عز وجل : « أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ » فكان موته اختلاط طينته مع طينة الكافر ـ وكان حياته حين فرق الله عز وجل بينهما بكلمته ـ كذلك يخرج الله عز وجل المؤمن في الميلاد ـ من الظلمة بعد دخوله فيها إلى النور ، ويخرج الكافر من النور إلى الظلمة ـ بعد دخوله إلى النور وذلك قول الله عز وجل : « لِيُنْذِرَ مَنْ كانَ حَيًّا وَيَحِقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ » — 'When Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, willed to create Adam, He sent Gabriel in the first hour of Friday. He took a handful with his right hand that reached from the seventh heaven to the lowest heaven, taking soil from every heaven. And he took another handful from the highest seventh earth to the furthest seventh earth. Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, commanded His Word, so He held the first handful in His right and the other handful in His left. He split the clay into two parts and scattered those from the earth and those from the heavens. He said to that which was in His right: "From you are the Messengers, the Prophets, the Successors, the Truthful, the Believers, the Martyrs, and those whose honor I intend." Thus, what was for them became mandatory as He said. And He said to that which was in His left: "From you are the tyrants, the polytheists, the hypocrites, the false deities, and those whose humiliation or wretchedness I intend." Thus, what was for them became mandatory as He said. Then the two clays were mixed together, and that is His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah is the splitter of the grain and the pit" (6:95). The grain (الحب) is the clay of the believers upon which Allah cast His love, and the pit (النوى) is the clay of the disbelievers who distanced themselves (نأوا) from all good. It was called nawā (pit) because it distanced itself (na’ā) from the truth and moved away from it. And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: "He brings forth the living from the dead and He is the bringer forth of the dead from the living" (6:95). The living is the believer who comes out from the clay of the disbeliever, and the dead who comes out from the living is the disbeliever who comes out from the clay of the believer. So the living is the believer and the dead is the disbeliever, and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "Is he who was dead and whom We gave life..." (6:122). His death was the mixing of his clay with the clay of the disbeliever, and his life was when Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, separated between them by His Word. Likewise, Allah brings forth the believer in birth from darkness into light after his entry into it, and He brings forth the disbeliever from light into darkness after his entry into light. That is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "so that he may warn him who is alive and that the word may come true against the faithless" (36:70).'"

I say: This narration is among the reports concerning the primordial clay (أخبار الطينة), and the discussion regarding them will come in its appropriate place, God willing. The interpretation of the grain and the pit with the meaning contained therein is of the nature of the inward (الباطن) rather than the outward (الظاهر), and this meaning has occurred in other narrations besides this one.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ سَكَناً — "and made the night for rest" (6:96), from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali ibn Bint Ilyas, who said: I heard Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) saying: "إن الله جعل الليل سكنا وجعل النساء سكنا ، ومن السنة التزويج بالليل وإطعام الطعام — 'Indeed, Allah made the night for rest and made women for rest. It is from the Sunnah to marry at night and to provide food.'"

In the same source, from ‘Ali ibn ‘Uqbah, from his father, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "تزوجوا بالليل فإن الله جعله سكنا ـ ولا تطلبوا الحوائج بالليل — 'Marry at night, for Allah has made it for rest, and do not seek your needs at night.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Yunus, from some of our companions, from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him), who said: "إن الله خلق النبيين على النبوة فلا يكونون إلا أنبياء ، وخلق المؤمنين على الإيمان فلا يكونون إلا مؤمنين ـ وأعار قوما إيمانا ـ فإن شاء تممه لهم وإن شاء سلبهم إياه. قال : وفيهم جرت « فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌ » وقال : إن فلانا كان مستودعا ـ فلما كذب علينا سلبه الله إيمانه — 'Indeed, Allah created the Prophets upon prophethood, so they are nothing but Prophets. And He created the believers upon faith, so they are nothing but believers. And He lent faith to a people; if He wills, He completes it for them, and if He wills, He strips them of it.' He said: 'Concerning them, the verse "a place of stay and a place of storage" (6:98) was applied.' And he said: 'So-and-so was a place of storage; when he lied against us, Allah stripped him of his faith.'"

I say: Regarding the interpretation of "the place of stay" (المستقر) and "the place of storage" (المستودع) as the two types of faith, there are many narrations from them (peace be upon them) in the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi and al-Qummi. This narration directs it as being of the nature of application and instantiation (الجري والانطباق).

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Sa‘d ibn Sa‘id Abu al-Asbagh, who said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌ — "a place of stay and a place of storage." He said: "مستقر في الرحم ومستودع في الصلب ، وقد يكون مستودع الإيمان ثم ينزع منه — 'A place of stay in the womb and a place of storage in the loin. And it may be a place of storage for faith, which is then stripped from him.'" The tradition continues.

In the same source, from Sudayr, who said: I heard Humran asking Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: بَدِيعُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "the Originator of the heavens and the earth" (6:101). Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said to him: "ابتدع الأشياء كلها بعلمه على غير مثال كان ، وابتدع السماوات والأرضين ـ ولم يكن قبلهن سماوات ولا أرضون ـ أما تسمع قوله : « وَكانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْماءِ »؟ — 'He originated all things by His knowledge without any prior model. And He originated the heavens and the earths when there were no heavens or earths before them. Have you not heard His saying: "and His Throne was upon the water" (11:7)?'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain to ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصارُ — "The sights do not perceive Him" (6:103). He said: "إحاطة الوهم ألا ترى إلى قوله : « قَدْ جاءَكُمْ بَصائِرُ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ » ليس يعني من البصر بعينه « وَمَنْ عَمِيَ فَعَلَيْها » ليس يعني عمى العيون إنما عنى إحاطة الوهم ـ كما يقال : فلان بصير بالشعر ، وفلان بصير بالفقه ، وفلان بصير بالدراهم ، وفلان بصير بالثياب ، الله أعظم من أن يرى بالعين — 'It means the encompassment of the imagination (إحاطة الوهم). Do you not see His saying: "Certainly, insights have come to you from your Lord"? He does not mean the physical eye itself. "And whoever is blind, it is to his own loss"—He does not mean the blindness of the eyes; He only meant the encompassment of the imagination. Just as it is said: "So-and-so is insightful (بصير) in poetry," "So-and-so is insightful in jurisprudence," "So-and-so is insightful in dirhams," and "So-and-so is insightful in garments." Allah is too great to be seen by the eye.'"

I say: This was narrated in al-Tawhid through another chain from him (peace be upon him), and with its chain to Abu Hashim al-Ja‘fari from al-Rida (peace be upon him).

In the same source, with its chain to Safwan ibn Yahya, who said: Abu Qurrah the traditionist asked me to introduce him to Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him). I sought his permission, and he granted it to me. He entered and asked him about the lawful, the prohibited, and the rulings, until his questioning reached monotheism (التوحيد). Abu Qurrah said: "We have narrated that Allah divided vision and speech between two Prophets: He assigned speech to Moses and vision to Muhammad." Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) said: "فمن المبلغ عن الله إلى الثقلين من الجن والإنس ، لا تدركه الأبصار ، ولا يحيطون به علما ، وليس كمثله شيء؟ أليس محمد صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله؟ قال : بلى. قال : كيف يجيء رجل إلى الخلق جميعا فيخبرهم ـ أنه جاء من عند الله ، وأنه يدعوهم إلى الله بأمر الله فيقول : لا تدركه الأبصار ، ولا يحيطون به علما ، وليس كمثله شيء ثم يقول : أنا رأيته بعيني وأحطت به علما ـ وهو على صورة البشر ، أما تستحون؟ ما قدرت الزنادقة أن ترميه بهذا ـ أن يكون يأتي من عند الله بشيء ـ ثم يأتي بخلافه من وجه آخر — 'Who then is the conveyer from Allah to the two weighty groups of jinn and men [the message]: "The sights do not perceive Him," "they do not encompass Him with knowledge," and "nothing is like Him"? Was it not Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'How can a man come to all of creation and inform them that he has come from Allah, and that he calls them to Allah by Allah's command, saying: "The sights do not perceive Him," "they do not encompass Him with knowledge," and "nothing is like Him," and then say: "I saw Him with my own eyes and encompassed Him with knowledge, and He is in the form of a human"? Are you not ashamed? Even the heretics (الزنادقة) could not accuse him of this—that he would bring something from Allah and then bring its opposite from another side.'"

Abu Qurrah said: "But He says: وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى — 'He certainly saw him yet another time' (53:13)." Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) said: "إن بعد هذه الآية ما يدل على ما رأى ـ حيث قال : « ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى » يقول : ما كذب فؤاد محمد ما رأته عيناه ـ ثم أخبر بما رأى فقال : « لَقَدْ رأى مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرى » فآيات الله غير الله وقد قال الله : « وَلا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْماً » فإذا رأته الأبصار فقد أحاط به العلم ووقعت المعرفة — 'Indeed, after this verse is that which indicates what he saw, where He said: "The heart did not lie about what it saw" (53:11). He says: The heart of Muhammad did not lie about what his eyes saw. Then He informed of what he saw, saying: "Certainly he saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord" (53:18). The signs of Allah are other than Allah. And Allah has said: "they do not encompass Him with knowledge." If the sights had seen Him, then knowledge would have encompassed Him and [limited] recognition would have occurred.'"

Abu Qurrah said: "Do you then reject the narrations?" al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: "إذا كانت الروايات مخالفة للقرآن كذبتها ، وما أجمع المسلمون عليه أنه لا يحاط به علما ، ولا تدركه الأبصار ، وليس كمثله شيء — 'If the narrations are contrary to the Quran, I reject them. And the Muslims have reached a consensus that He is not encompassed by knowledge, the sights do not perceive Him, and nothing is like Him.'"

I say: This meaning is reported in other traditions narrated from them (peace be upon them).
