There are other narrations that affirm "vision" (الرؤية) in another, more subtle sense befitting His holy presence, which we will cite, God willing, in the commentary on Surah al-A‘raf. The severe rejection of vision in this narration was because the famous affirmation of vision in their era was the affirmation of physical vision with the physical eye, which is negated by explicit reason and the text of the Book. In the Tafsir of al-Tabari, from ‘Ikrimah, from Ibn ‘Abbas, he said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw his Lord." A man said to him at that: "Has Allah not said: 'The sights do not perceive Him'?" ‘Ikrimah said to him: "Do you not see the sky?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Do you see all of it?"—among other such reports.

That which they affirm of vision is explicitly physical vision with the seeing organ, which has been negated by both reason and tradition. ‘Ikrimah failed to realize that if the meaning of "The sights do not perceive Him" were the negation of encompassing all sides of a thing, there would be no reason for this to be exclusive to Him, the Exalted. For no physical thing with surfaces of different directions—such as humans, animals, other earthly bodies, and celestial bodies—is touched by the sense of sight except for that which faces the ray circulating between the seer and the seen, as determined by the laws of optics (المناظر والمرايا).

When we see a human, for instance, we only see some of the many surfaces surrounding his body—from above, below, front, back, right, and left. It is impossible for the sight to fall upon all the various surfaces that surround him. Thus, if the meaning of "The sights do not perceive Him" were the negation of this type of visual perception—which is impossible for Him and for others—it would be a meaningless statement.

In al-Tawhid, with its chain to Isma‘il ibn al-Fadl, who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah Ja‘far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) about Allah, the Blessed and Exalted: "Will He be seen in the Hereafter?" He said: "سبحان الله وتعالى عن ذلك علوا كبيرا ـ يا بن الفضل إن الأبصار لا تدرك إلا ما له لون وكيفية ، والله خالق الألوان والكيفيات — 'Glory be to Allah and Exalted is He above that with a great sublimit. O son of al-Fadl, the sights do not perceive except that which has color and quality (كيفية), and Allah is the Creator of colors and qualities.'"

Ayahs

ٱتَّبِعْ مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٠٦)
Follow that which has been revealed to you from your Lord, there is no god except Him, and turn away from the polytheists. (106)
وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَآ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۗ وَمَا جَعَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِوَكِيلٍ (١٠٧)
Had Allah wished they would not have ascribed partners [to Him]. We have not made you a caretaker for them, nor is it your duty to watch over them. (107)
وَلَا تَسُبُّوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَسُبُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَدْوًۢا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ زَيَّنَّا لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ عَمَلَهُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِم مَّرْجِعُهُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٠٨)
Do not abuse those whom they invoke besides Allah, lest they should abuse Allah out of hostility, without any knowledge. That is how to every people We have made their conduct seem decorous. Then their return will be to their Lord and He will inform them concerning what they used to do. (108)
وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ لَئِن جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَةٌ لَّيُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهَا ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَا يُشْعِرُكُمْ أَنَّهَآ إِذَا جَآءَتْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٠٩)
They swear by Allah with solemn oaths that were a sign to come to them they would surely believe in it. Say, ‘These signs are only from Allah,’ and what will bring home to you that they will not believe even if they came? (109)
وَنُقَلِّبُ أَفْـِٔدَتَهُمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ كَمَا لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَنَذَرُهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (١١٠)
We transform their hearts and their visions as they did not believe in it the first time, and We leave them bewildered in their rebellion. (110)
۞ وَلَوْ أَنَّنَا نَزَّلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ وَكَلَّمَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَحَشَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ قُبُلًا مَّا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَجْهَلُونَ (١١١)
Even if We had sent down angels to them, and the dead had spoken to them, and We had gathered before them all things manifestly, they would [still] not believe unless Allah wished. But most of them are ignorant. (111)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا لِكُلِّ نَبِىٍّ عَدُوًّا شَيَـٰطِينَ ٱلْإِنسِ وَٱلْجِنِّ يُوحِى بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ زُخْرُفَ ٱلْقَوْلِ غُرُورًا ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّكَ مَا فَعَلُوهُ ۖ فَذَرْهُمْ وَمَا يَفْتَرُونَ (١١٢)
That is how for every prophet We appointed as enemy the devils from among humans and jinn, who inspire each other with flashy words, deceptively. Had your Lord wished, they would not have done it. So leave them with what they fabricate, (112)
وَلِتَصْغَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ أَفْـِٔدَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَلِيَرْضَوْهُ وَلِيَقْتَرِفُوا۟ مَا هُم مُّقْتَرِفُونَ (١١٣)
so that toward it may incline the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter, and so that they may be pleased with it and commit what they commit. (113)

(Commentary)

The connection of these verses to what preceded them is clear and beyond doubt, as the discourse continues to flow regarding monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts.

His saying, the Exalted: «Follow what has been revealed to you from your Lord, there is no god but Him, and keep away from the polytheists.» (6:106). This is a command to follow what has been revealed to him from his Lord regarding the matter of monotheism (التوحيد) and the foundations of the religious laws (شرائع الدين), without being deterred by the arrogance (استكبار) he witnesses from the polytheists (المشركين) in their refusal to submit to the word of truth and their turning away from the call of religion.

In His saying, "from your Lord" (مِنْ رَبِّكَ), which suggests a heightened degree of special distinction (الاختصاص), there is an allusion to the encompassment of special divine providence (العناية الخاصة الإلهية). However, since His saying "from your Lord" was followed by His saying, "and keep away from the polytheists," it might have mistakenly suggested that the meaning was: "Follow the revelation and worship your Lord, and turn away from them so they may worship their lords." This would not be free from an implicit endorsement of their way and their polytheism (شركهم). Therefore, He preceded the command "and keep away..." with His saying, "there is no god but Him" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ), to dispel this illusion, clarify the meaning of "and keep away...", and establish its proper context.

Thus, the meaning is: Follow what has been revealed to you from your Lord, who possesses ultimate providence over you and mercy encompassing you, as He has distinguished you with His revelation and supported you with the spirit of following. Turn away from these polytheists, not by leaving them to what they worship and remaining silent in approval of their polytheism—for that would be an endorsement of idolatry (الوثنية)—since the God is but One, and He is your Lord who reveals to you; there is no god but Him. Rather, turn away from them by not exhausting yourself in trying to compel them toward monotheism (التوحيد), nor bearing a hardship beyond your capacity. Your duty is only the delivery of the message (البلاغ), and you are not a guardian (حفيظ) over them, nor a trustee (وكيل). The true Guardian and Trustee is Allah, and He did not will monotheism for them; had He willed it, they would not have associated partners with Him. However, He left them to their misguidance (ضلالهم) because they turned away from the truth and disdained submission to it.

His saying, the Exalted: ولو شاء الله ما أشركوا وما جعلناك عليهم حفيظاً وما أنت عليهم بوكيل — "Had Allah wished, they would not have ascribed partners [to Him]. We have not made you a guardian over them, nor are you a trustee over them." (6:107). This is to comfort the heart of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله so that he does not feel distress over their polytheism (شركهم) nor grieve over the apparent failure of the effort in calling them. They cannot frustrate Allah in their polytheism; rather, the will (المشية) belongs to Allah. Had He willed, they would not have committed polytheism; instead, they would have embraced faith (الإيمان) out of choice and desire, just as those granted success (توفيق) for faith embraced it. This is because they acted arrogantly in the land, exalted themselves against Allah, and plotted against Him. By doing so, they destroyed themselves, so Allah turned their plot back upon them and deprived them of the success (التوفيق) for faith and guidance (الاهتداء).

Just as the established sunnah (السنة الجارية) in the realm of creation (التكوين) is the sunnah of causes (الأسباب) and the general law of causality (العلية والمعلولية), and the divine will (المشية الإلهية) only attaches to things and occurs in events according to this law—such that when the causes and conditions are complete and the obstacles to existence are removed, the divine will attaches to its realization—similarly, the sunnah in the system of legislation (التشريع) and guidance (الهداية) is the sunnah of causes. Whoever seeks mercy from Allah, He shows him mercy; and whoever turns away from His mercy, He deprives him. Guidance (الهداية) in the sense of "showing the way" (إراءة الطريق) encompasses everyone. Whoever exposes himself to this divine fragrance and does not block the path of its arrival to him through transgression (الفسق), disbelief (الكفر), and stubbornness (العناد), it encompasses him and revives him with the finest life. But whoever follows his whims, opposes the truth, exalts himself against Allah, and begins to plot against Allah and mock His signs, Allah deprives him of felicity (السعادة) and sends down wretchedness (الشقوة) upon him, leading him astray based on knowledge and sealing him with disbelief, so he shall never be saved.

If the divine will (المشية الإلهية) did not proceed according to this sunnah, the system of causes and the law of causality would be nullified, and a haphazard will (الإرادة الجزافية) would take its place, rendering interests, wisdoms, and purposes (الغايات) futile. The corruption of this system would lead to the corruption of the system of creation (التكوين), because legislation ultimately traces back to creation in a certain way, and the creeping of corruption into it leads to the corruption of its origin.

This is similar to how, if Allah the Glorified were to compel the polytheists into faith (الإيمان), the human species would be removed from the branching paths of faith and disbelief. The bestowed faculty of choice (الاختيار) would fall away, and faith would become a necessity of creation (الخلقة). Man would be settled upon the throne of perfection from the first moment of his existence, and everyone would be equal in proximity and nobility. The consequence of this would be the nullification of the system of the Call (الدعوة), the futility of spiritual training (التربية) and perfection (التكميل), and the removal of differences between degrees. This would lead to the nullification of the diversity of aptitudes (الاستعدادات), actions, states, and dispositions (الملكات). Consequently, the human system and the system of existence surrounding and acting upon it would be transformed into another system in which there is no trace of "man" or what characterizes him. Understand this well.

From here, it becomes clear that there is no need to interpret His saying, "Had Allah willed, they would not have committed polytheism," as referring to compulsory faith (الإيمان الاضطراري), or that the meaning is that if Allah willed to make them leave polytheism by force and compulsion, He would have forced them to do so. Rather, what was mentioned previously—that the meaning refers to the attachment of the divine will (المشية الإلهية) to their leaving polytheism by choice (اختياراً), just as it attached to that in the believers—is more consistent with the perfection of Power and more suitable for consoling the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and comforting his heart.

The meaning, then, is: Turn away from them, and let not distress or grief seize you on account of their polytheism, for Allah is capable of willing faith from them so that they would believe, just as He willed that from the believers and they believed. Furthermore, you are not responsible for their affair, neither in the realm of creation (تكونياً) nor otherwise; so let your soul be at peace.

It also appears from this that His saying, وما جعلناك عليهم حفيظاً وما أنت عليهم بوكيل — "We have not made you a guardian over them, nor are you a trustee over them," is likewise intended for consolation and comforting the soul. It is as if the "guardian" (الحفيظ) is the one who oversees the management of the affairs of their existence, such as life, growth, and sustenance (الرزق); while the "trustee" (الوكيل) is the one who oversees the management of actions to bring about benefits and repel harms directed toward the one on whose behalf he acts. The sum of the meaning of His saying, "We have not made you...", etc., is that neither the matter of their cosmic life (حياتهم الكونية) nor the matter of their religious life (حياتهم الدينية) is entrusted to you, such that their rejection of your call or their failure to respond to your request should grieve you.

It is sometimes said that the "guardian" (الحفيظ) is the one who repels harm from the one he guards, and the "trustee" (الوكيل) is the one who brings benefits to the one for whom he acts. This is not without some distance from the primary meaning, for "guardian" (الحفيظ), as it immediately comes to mind, pertains specifically to creation (التكوين), while "trustee" (الوكيل) encompasses creation and other realms. There is little utility in interpreting one of the sentences as referring to a creative aspect and the other to what encompasses it and more; rather, the proper way is to interpret the first as one aspect and the second as the other.

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تسبوا الذين يدعون من دون الله فيسبوا الله عدواً بغير علم — "Do not abuse those whom they invoke besides Allah, lest they should abuse Allah out of hostility, without any knowledge." (6:108). Abuse (السب) is well-known. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: Hostility (العدو) is transgression and the negation of harmony. Sometimes it is considered in the heart, and it is called enmity (العداوة) and antagonism (المعاداة); sometimes in walking, and it is called running (العدو); and sometimes in the violation of justice in dealings, and it is called transgression (العدوان) and hostility (العدو). He said: (lest they should abuse Allah out of hostility (عدواً) without knowledge). And sometimes it refers to the parts of a place, and it is called al-adwa' (العدواء); it is said "a place of adwa'" meaning its parts are not harmonious. (End of quote).

The verse mentions a religious etiquette (أدباً دينياً) by which the sanctity of the religious community's sacred symbols is preserved, and their domain is protected from being defiled by the stain of insult and disparagement through foul speech, abuse, cursing, mockery, and the like. For man is instinctively disposed to defend the dignity of what he holds sacred and to retaliate in transgression against whoever he perceives as transgressing against himself. Anger may drive him to use foul language and abuse against that which holds the highest station of might and dignity in his view. Thus, if the believers were to abuse the gods of the polytheists, the zeal of ignorance (عصبية الجاهلية) would drive them to oppose the believers by abusing that which possesses the dignity of divinity in their eyes, which is Allah—exalted is His Name. Thus, abusing their gods becomes a kind of cause for mentioning Him, the Exalted, in a manner that does not befit His sanctuary of holiness and majesty.

The generality of the reasoning understood from His saying, "Thus have We made to seem fair to every community their deeds," implies the generality of the prohibition for every evil word that leads to mentioning any of the religious sanctities in an evil manner, in whatever way it may lead.

His saying, the Exalted: كذلك زينا لكل أمة عملهم ثم إلى ربهم مرجعهم فينبئهم بما كانوا يعملون — "Thus have We made to seem fair to every community their deeds. Then to their Lord will be their return, and He will inform them of what they used to do." (6:108). Adornment (الزينة) is a beautiful and beloved matter that is joined to something in a way that attracts desire toward it and makes it beloved to the one seeking it. Thus, he moves toward the adornment and ends up at the thing adorned by it, like a garment adorned with a beautiful form that a person wears for decoration, by which his body is protected from heat and cold.

Allah the Glorified willed for man to live this worldly life with its various branches and sectors, and to continue his specific earthly life by exercising his active faculties. Thus, he perceives what benefits him and what harms him through his external senses, then processes them with his internal senses and faculties. He then nourishes himself by eating and drinking certain things, is moved toward procreation through specific acts, wears clothes, seeks shelter, attracts benefits, and repels harms, and so on.

In all these actions and what pertains to them, he experiences accompanying pleasures and vital goals (غايات حيوية) toward which he ends. The ultimate end he reaches is either the true felicitous life for which he was created, or the life he imagines to be the true felicitous life. In what he performs of deeds, he only intends the material pleasure associated with it—such as the pleasure of food, drink, procreation, and so on—or the intellectual pleasure, such as the pleasure of medicine, the pleasure of progress, intimacy, praise, pride, eternal remembrance, revenge, wealth, security, and other innumerable things.

These pleasures are matters with which these deeds and their attachments have been adorned (زينت). Allah the Glorified has subjected man through them; so he performs actions and pursues deeds for their sake. Through their realization, the divine goals and creative purposes (الأغراض التكوينية) are realized, such as the survival of the individual and the continuation of the progeny. Had it not been for the desired pleasure in eating, drinking, and procreation, man would not have burdened himself with these arduous movements that exhaust his body and are heavy upon his soul. Consequently, the system of life would have been disrupted, the individual would have perished, and the progeny would have been cut off, leading to the extinction of the species and the nullification of the wisdom of creation (حكمة التكوين) without any doubt.

Whatever of this adornment is natural and ingrained in the natures of things—such as the delicious tastes in various types of food and the pleasure of procreation—is attributed to creation (الخلقة) and ascribed to Allah the Glorified, occurring in the course of driving things toward their creative goals (غاياتها التكوينية). There is no driver for them toward these goals except Allah the Glorified; for He is the one who gave everything its creation and then guided it.

Whatever of it is an intellectual pleasure that rectifies man's life in his world and does not harm him in his hereafter is also ascribed to Allah the Glorified, because it arises from the sound primordial nature (الفطرة السليمة) upon which Allah has originated mankind; there is no change in Allah's creation. He, the Exalted, said: حبب إليكم الإيمان وزينه في قلوبكم — "He has made faith dear to you and adorned it in your hearts" (49:7).

Whatever of it is an intellectual pleasure that accords with whims (الهوى) and causes wretchedness in the hereafter and the world by nullifying servitude (العبودية) and corrupting the "good life" (الحياة الطيبة), it is a pleasure deviated from the path of the sound primordial nature (الفطرة). For the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — is the divine creation that Allah organized such that it treads toward felicity (السعادة). The rulings arising from it and the thoughts emanating from it do not oppose their origin which prompts them. If they oppose the primordial nature and do not ensure felicity, then they do not emanate from it; rather, they arise from a satanic impulse and a psychological slip. Thus, they are ascribed to Satan, like the illusory satanic pleasures found in various types of transgression (الفسوق) insofar as it is transgression. This is an adornment ascribed to Satan and not ascribed to Allah the Glorified except by permission (الإذن). He, the Exalted, said, narrating the words of Iblis: لأزينن لهم في الأرض ولأغوينهم أجمعين — "I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them all" (15:39). And He, the Exalted, said: فزين لهم الشيطان أعمالهم — "Satan made their deeds seem fair to them" (16:63).

As for the fact that they are not ascribed to Allah the Glorified without mediation, it is because He, the Exalted, is the one who organized the system of creation (التكوين), driving things within it toward their goals and guiding them toward their felicity. Then, He branched out from man's sound cosmic primordial nature certain beliefs and intellectual views upon which he builds his actions, which bring him felicity and protect him from wretchedness and the failure of effort. Far removed is His sanctuary—exalted is His Name—from then turning back to command indecency (الفحشاء), forbid the recognized good (المعروف), and prompt every ugly and heinous act, thereby commanding all people with both the beautiful and the ugly together, and forbidding all people from both the ugly and the beautiful together. This would disrupt the system of religious obligation (التكليف), legislation (التشريع), and subsequently reward and punishment. Then He would describe the religion characterized by this as being a "right religion" (دين قيم), the primordial nature of Allah upon which He originated mankind. The primordial nature (الفطرة) is innocent of such contradiction and its like, refusing and disdaining that there be ascribed to it what it considers foolishness and insanity.

If you say: What prevents the call to obedience and disobedience from being ascribed to Him, the Exalted, in the sense that souls adorned with godwariness (التقوى) and equipped with a righteous inner state are prompted by Allah toward obedience and righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة), while souls defiled by the filth of transgression and clothed in the malice of the interior are called by Allah the Glorified toward immorality and transgression according to the difference in their aptitudes (استعداداتها)? Thus, the one calling to good and evil, and the one prompting to obedience and disobedience, is Allah the Glorified.

I say: This is another perspective, different from the perspective we were discussing. This is the perspective on obedience and disobedience in terms of the mediation of intervening causes between them and Him, the Exalted. There is no doubt that psychological states, whether good or evil, have a role in the realization of the acts of obedience or disobedience that suit them. Given these states, obedience and disobedience are ascribed to them without mediation, and to Allah the Glorified by permission (الإذن); for Allah the Glorified is the one who permitted every cause to lead to its effect.

As for the perspective we are currently in, it is the perspective on the state of obedience and disobedience in terms of the legislation of rulings (تشريع الأحكام), and in terms of the souls being prompted toward them, regardless of other prompting and calling causes. Is it possible to say that Allah the Glorified calls to both faith and disbelief together, or prompts toward both obedience and disobedience together? He is the one who describes His religion as the "right religion" (الدين القيم) over human society, built upon the divine primordial nature (الفطرة الإلهية). These divine laws, and then the psychological motives that agree with them, are all primordial (فطرية). The psychological motives that agree with the whims of the soul and oppose the rulings of the Sharia are contrary to the primordial nature; the call toward them cannot be ascribed to one possessing a sound primordial nature. Thus, it is impossible for it to be ascribed to Him, the Exalted. He, the Exalted, said: وإذا فعلوا فاحشة قالوا وجدنا عليها آباءنا والله أمرنا بها قل إن الله لا يأمر بالفحشاء أتقولون على الله ما لا تعلمون ، قل أمر ربي بالقسط — "When they commit an indecency, they say, ‘We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us.’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecencies. Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know?’ Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice...’" (7:28-29).

As for their being ascribed to Him, the Exalted, by permission (الإذن), it is because the Sovereignty (الملك) is universal and the divine authority (السلطنة الإلهية) is absolute. Far be it that anyone should be able to act in any part of His kingdom except by His permission. Thus, what Satan adorns in the hearts of his allies of polytheism (الشرك), transgression (الفسق), and everything that leads in any way to the wrath of Allah the Glorified, is only by divine permission. Through this, the sunnah of trial and testing (الامتحان والاختبار) is completed, without which the system of legislation and the path of the Call and guidance would not be complete. He, the Exalted, said: ما من شفيع إلا من بعد إذنه — "There is no intercessor except by His leave" (10:3), and He said: وليمحص الله الذين آمنوا ويمحق الكافرين — "and so that Allah may purge those who believe and wipe out the faithless" (3:141).

Thus, it has become clear that the adornment of deeds has an ascription to Him, the Exalted, more general than being through permission or without mediation. Upon this proceeds His saying, the Exalted: كذلك زينا لكل أمة عملهم — "Thus have We made to seem fair to every community their deeds" (6:108). Even clearer in its application to what has preceded is His saying, the Exalted: إنا جعلنا ما على الأرض زينة لها لنبلوهم أيهم أحسن عملاً — "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct" (18:7).

The commentators (المفسرين), according to their differences regarding the ascription of actions to Him, the Exalted, have various views on the verse.

Among them: That what is meant is adornment through command and prohibition and the clarification of beauty and ugliness. The meaning would be: Just as We have adorned for you, O believers, your deeds, We adorned for every community before you their deeds of beautifully calling to Allah and refraining from abusing idols, and We forbade them from performing actions that would repel the disbelievers from accepting the truth. The critique of this is that it contradicts the apparent generality of the verse, and there is no evidence to restrict it to what they mentioned, as has become clear from what preceded.

Among them: That the meaning is: Likewise, We adorned for every community their deeds through the inclination of their natures (ميل الطباع) toward them, but We have made the truth known to them nonetheless, so that they may perform the truth and avoid falsehood. The critique of this is that just as it is not correct to ascribe the call to obedience and disobedience, or faith and disbelief, to Him, the Exalted, without mediation, it is likewise not correct to ascribe the inclination of natures toward both good and evil deeds on the same footing to Him, the Exalted. The distinction between the creative call (الدعوة التكوينية) and its like, and the legislative call (الدعوة التشريعية) toward ugliness and evils—ascribing the former to Him, the Exalted, but not the latter—is misplaced.

Among them: That what is meant is adornment by mentioning the reward. It would be like His saying: ولكن الله حبب إليكم الإيمان وزينه في قلوبكم وكره إليكم الكفر والفسوق والعصيان — "but Allah has made faith dear to you and adorned it in your hearts, and He has made faithlessness, transgression and disobedience detestable to you" (49:7). That is, He made faith dear to you by mentioning its reward and praising its doers for their action, and He made disbelief detestable by mentioning its punishment and censuring its doers. The critique of this is that it involves restricting "deeds" to good ones without a restrictor. Furthermore, it is a meaning far from the context and from the literal sense of the word "adornment" (التزيين). Additionally, adornment in this sense is not exclusive to the believers.

Among them: That what is meant is the adornment of all deeds, their good and their evil, initially and without mediation, and the call from Him, the Exalted, to both obedience and disobedience together, based on the view that man is compelled (مجبر) in the actions ascribed to him. The critique of this is that the literal sense of the verse is more consistent with choice (الاختيار) than with compulsion (الإجبار). For adornment is only joined to a thing so that man may desire it and it may be made beloved to him, thus becoming a preponderant factor (مرجحة) for his attachment to it and his leaving of others. If the ratio of his performing the act and leaving it were not equal, there would be no reason for its preponderance. Thus, adorning the act with what the agent desires is a kind of means employed to bring about its occurrence. In acts of obedience and good deeds, this corresponds to what is called in the language of the Sharia "guidance" (هداية) and "success" (توفيق); and in acts of disobedience and evil deeds, it corresponds to what is considered "misguidance" (إضلال) and "divine plotting" (مكر إلهي). There is no barrier to ascribing misguidance and plotting to Him, the Exalted, if they are by way of recompense (المجازاة), rather than being initial misguidance and plotting. The discussion of these meanings has preceded in various places in this book, as has the discussion of compulsion (الجبر) and its opposite, delegation (التفويض), and the "matter between the two matters" (الأمر بين الأمرين) in the first volume of the book.

His saying, the Exalted: ثم إلى ربهم مرجعهم فينبئهم بما كانوا يعملون — "Then to their Lord will be their return, and He will inform them of what they used to do," supports what preceded: that the ruling of adornment is general, encompassing all internal deeds such as faith and disbelief, and external deeds such as the good and evil actions of the limbs. For the literal sense of the verse is that man only intends these actions and performs them because of the adornment he desires in them, being heedless of the realities hidden beneath these adornments, over which the veil of heedlessness (حجاب الغفلة) has been drawn. Then, when they return to their Lord, He will inform them of the reality of what they used to do, and they will witness what they were diverted from. As for the allies of the All-Beneficent (أولياء الرحمن), they will find what they did not know of the "delight of the eyes" hidden for them; and as for the allies of Satan, there will appear to them from Allah what they had not expected. Thus, the manifestation of the realities of deeds on the Day of Resurrection is not exclusive to one of the two groups, whether for good deeds or evil ones.

His saying, the Exalted: وأقسموا بالله جهد أيمانهم — "They swear by Allah with their most solemn oaths..." until His saying "...with Allah." Al-Jahd (الجهد) with a fatha on the jim means capacity (الطاقة), and al-ayman (الأيمان) is the plural of yamin, meaning an oath. "The jahd of oaths" means the utmost their power can reach, which is their full capacity. The meaning is that they exaggerated in the oath and emphasized it as much as they could. The meaning of the signs being "with Allah" (عند الله) is their being in His possession and under His authority; no one attains them except by His permission.

The meaning, then, is: They swore by Allah and exaggerated in it that if His signs were to come to them, indicating the truthfulness of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in what he calls to, they would surely believe in that sign—this is a suggestion for a sign on their part, expressed indirectly. Say: The signs are only with Allah; He is the one who possesses them and encompasses them, and I have no part in their matter such that I could respond to you of my own accord.

His saying, the Exalted: وما يشعركم أنها إذا جاءت لا يؤمنون — "And what will make you realize that even if they should come, they will not believe?" (6:109). It has been recited as "they will not believe" (لا يؤمنون) with the ya' of the third person and the ta' of the second person. According to the first recitation, the address is to the believers by way of a kind of transition (الالتفات); and according to the second, it is to the polytheists, and the speech is a continuation of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله words, which is apparent.

It appears that "what" (ما) in His saying "And what will make you realize" is for interrogation. The meaning is: What is it that provides you with knowledge of the reality of the matter, which is that they will not believe if the signs come to them? The speech is in the sense of our saying: These people swear by Allah that if the signs come to you, they will surely believe in them; so perhaps you have believed and trusted their oath, but you have no knowledge that if the signs come to them, they will not believe in them because Allah has not willed their faith. Thus, the speech is of the nature of foretelling (الملاحم).

It is sometimes said that "that" (أن) in His saying "that even if they should come...", etc., is in the sense of "perhaps" (لعل). This is an anomalous (شاذ) meaning; the speech of Allah is not to be interpreted based on such a thing even if it were established linguistically.

His saying, the Exalted: ونقلب أفئدتهم وأبصارهم كما لم يؤمنوا به أول مرة — "We will turn their hearts and their sights, just as they did not believe in it the first time..." (6:110). The literal context suggests that the sentence is coordinated with His saying "they will not believe," and it serves as an explanation for their lack of faith. What is meant by "the first time" (أول مرة) is the first call before the descent of the signs, in contrast to what might be imagined as the second time, which is the call accompanied by the descent of the signs.

The meaning is that they would not believe even if the signs were sent down to them. This is because We turn their hearts so they do not understand with them as they ought to understand, and We turn their sights so they do not see with them what they ought to see. Thus, they would not believe in them just as they did not believe in the Quran the first time of the call, before the descent of these hypothesized signs; and We leave them to wander and hesitate in their rebellion (طغيانهم). This is what the literal context of the verse dictates.

The commentators have many strange views on this verse that are of no utility to mention or investigate. Whoever wishes to become acquainted with them should refer to the relevant sources.

His saying, the Exalted: ولو أننا نزلنا إليهم الملائكة وكلمهم الموتى — "Even if We had sent down the angels to them, and the dead had spoken to them..." (6:111) until the end of the verse. This is another clarification of His saying, "The signs are only with Allah," and that their saying, "If a sign comes to them, they will surely believe in it," is a false claim prompted by their ignorance of the station of their Lord. It is not within the power of the signs—which they imagine to be independent causes in producing faith in their hearts and enabling them to embrace it—to instill faith in their souls except by the will of Allah.

This context indicates that there is an omission and brevity in the speech. The meaning is: Even if We had responded to them in their request and brought them the wonders of signs—sending down the angels to them so they could witness them, reviving the dead for them so they could face them, speak to them, and inform them of the truth of what they are called to, and gathering and assembling everything before them group by group and category by category, or gathering everything before them face to face so they could testify for them by their state or their speech—they would not have believed, and none of that would have affected their response to faith, unless Allah willed their faith.

Faith is not achieved for them by any of the causes and reasons except by the will of Allah. For the cosmic system, in its vast breadth, although it proceeds according to the wisdom of causality and the general law of cause and effect, the causes and reasons are themselves needy and dependent upon their Lord, not independent in any of their affairs or requirements. No ruling appears for them except by the will of Allah, and no trace of them is revived except by His permission.

However, most of the polytheists—and perhaps they are the non-scholars among them—are ignorant of the station of their Lord and cling to causes as if they were independent in themselves, self-sufficient from their Lord. Thus, they imagine that if the cause of faith—which is the suggested sign—came to them, they would believe and follow the truth. The matter has become confused for them due to their ignorance, so they have taken these deficient causes, which are needy of the divine will, as independent, complete, and self-sufficient causes.

His saying, the Exalted: وكذلك جعلنا لكل نبي عدواً شياطين الإنس والجن — "That is how We have assigned to every prophet an enemy, the devils from among humans and jinn..." (6:112) until the end of the verse. Al-Shayatin (الشياطين) is the plural of Shaytan, which linguistically means the evil one; its usage became predominant for Iblis, whom the Quran describes, and his progeny. Al-Jinn (الجن) is from al-jann with a fatha, meaning concealment (الاستتار). In the terminology of the Quran, it refers to a type of being possessing consciousness and will, concealed from our senses according to their nature; they are other than the angels. The Quran mentions that Iblis the Devil is of their kind. Al-Wahy (الوحي) is hidden speech by gesture or the like. Al-Zukhruf (الزخرف) is embellished adornment or an embellished thing. Thus, "the embellishment of speech" (زخرف القول) is embellished, deceptive speech that resembles the truth but is not. "Deceptively" (غروراً) is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb of its kind, or an accusative of purpose (مفعول له).

The meaning is: Just as We have assigned for you, We have assigned for every prophet an enemy, who are the devils of humans and jinn, some of them suggesting to others—and it is as if what is meant is the suggestion (وحي) of the devils of the jinn through whispering and prompting to the devils of humans, and the suggestion of some devils of humans to others among them by whispering plots and enticements—with embellished words and deceptive speech, deceiving them thereby with a deception, or for the purpose of their deception and misguidance.

His saying: ولو شاء ربك ما فعلوه — "Had your Lord wished, they would not have done it," indicates that the ruling of the Will (المشية) is general, ongoing, and effective. Just as the signs do not affect their faith at all except by the will of Allah, similarly, the enmity of the devils toward the prophets and their suggestion of embellished speech deceptively—all of that is by the permission of Allah. Had Allah willed, they would not have done it and would not have suggested that, and thus they would not have been enemies to the prophets. In this sense, this verse connects to what preceded it due to their shared clarification of the dependence of matters upon the Will.

His saying: فذرهم وما يفترون — "so leave them with what they fabricate," is a derivation from the effectiveness of the Will. That is, if this enmity and corruption through whisperings are all by divine permission, and they cannot frustrate Allah in His effective and dominant Will, then let not what you witness of their disruption and corruption of the matter grieve you. Rather, leave them and what they fabricate against Allah of claiming partners and the like.

Thus, His saying: "Had your Lord wished, they would not have done it," until the end of the verse, is in the sense of His saying at the beginning of the verses: "and keep away from the polytheists... Had Allah wished, they would not have ascribed partners [to Him]."

The discussion regarding His saying, the Exalted: "That is how We have assigned to every prophet an enemy, the devils from among humans and jinn...", etc., where He apparently ascribed assigning them as enemies to the prophets—which involves causing evil and prompting toward polytheism and disobedience—to Allah the Glorified, who is transcendent above all evil and ugliness, is similar to the discussion regarding His ascribing the adornment of deeds to Allah the Glorified in His saying: "Thus have We made to seem fair to every community their deeds." The discussion on this has preceded. The same applies to the literal sense of what His saying in the following verse provides: ولتصغى إليه أفئدة الذين لا يؤمنون بالآخرة — "and so that the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter may incline toward it..." (6:113), where He made these injustices and sins divine goals for the true Call.

The commentators, regarding these two verses and according to their different schools of thought concerning the ascription of actions to Allah the Glorified, have views similar to their aforementioned views on the ascription of the adornment of deeds to Him.

You have already learned that what the literal sense of the noble verse provides is that everything to which the name "thing" applies is owned by Him, the Exalted, and ascribed to Him without exception. However, the verses that transcend His sanctuary of holiness from all evil and ugliness establish that all good things and beauties are dependent upon His will and ascribed to Him, either without mediation or with it. As for evils and sins, they are dependent upon others besides Him, the Exalted—such as Satan and the soul—without mediation. They are only ascribed to Him, the Exalted, by permission (الإذن); thus they are owned by Him, the Exalted, and occur by His permission, so that the matter of divine trial may be established, and through it, the matter of the divine Call through command, prohibition, reward, and punishment may be completed. Had it not been for this, the divine sunnah in directing man, like other species, toward his felicity in this cosmic world—in which there is no path to perfection and felicity except through gradual progression—would have been nullified and rendered futile.

His saying, the Exalted: ولتصغى إليه أفئدة الذين لا يؤمنون بالآخرة — "and so that the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter may incline toward it..." (6:113) until the end of the verse. Al-Iqtiraf (الاقتراف) is acquisition (الاكتساب). The singular pronoun refers to the "suggestion" (الوحي) mentioned in the previous verse. The lam in "so that they may incline" (لتصغى) is for the purpose (للغاية), and the sentence is coordinated with an omitted part. The estimation is: We did what We did and willed what We willed, and We did not prevent the suggestion of some of them to others of embellished speech deceptively, for hidden purposes, and so that the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter may incline and respond to it, and so that they may be pleased with it and acquire what they are acquiring, so that they may all attain thereby what they ask for by the tongue of their aptitude (استعدادهم) of the wretchedness of the Hereafter. For Allah the Glorified supports both the people of felicity and the people of wretchedness with what completes their journey to their destinations and provides them with what they suggest by the tongue of their aptitude. He, the Exalted, said: كلاً نمد هؤلاء وهؤلاء من عطاء ربك وما كان عطاء ربك محظوراً — "To these and those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined" (17:20).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Suddi who said: When death approached Abu Talib, the Quraysh said: "Let us go and enter upon this man, and command him to forbid his nephew from us. For we are ashamed to kill him after his death, lest the Arabs say: 'He used to protect him, but when he died, they killed him.'"

So Abu Sufyan, Abu Jahl, al-Nadr ibn al-Harith, Umayyah and Ubayy the sons of Khalaf, 'Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt, 'Amr ibn al-'As, and al-Aswad ibn al-Bakhtari set out. They sent a man from among them called al-Muttalib, and they said: "Seek permission for us to enter upon Abu Talib." He came to Abu Talib and said: "These are the elders of your people; they wish to enter upon you." He gave them permission, and they entered and said: "O Abu Talib, you are our elder and our master. Muhammad has harmed us and harmed our gods. We would like you to call him and forbid him from mentioning our gods, and we will leave him and his God." So he called him, and the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله came. Abu Talib said to him: "These are your people and your cousins." The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "What do they want?"

They said: "We want you to leave us and our gods, and we will leave you and your God." The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "What do you think? If I give you this, will you give me one word which, if you speak it, you will rule the Arabs by it, and the non-Arabs will submit to you in tribute?" Abu Jahl said: "By your father, we will give it to you and ten like it! What is it?" He said: "Say: There is no god but Allah." They refused and were repulsed.

Abu Talib said: "Say something else, for your people have become terrified of this." He said: "O uncle, I am not one to say anything else, even if they were to bring the sun and place it in my hand. Even if they brought the sun and placed it in my hand, I would not say anything else"—intending to make them despair. They became angry and said: "Either you cease from insulting our gods, or we will surely insult you and insult the one who commands you." Then Allah sent down: ولا تسبوا الذين يدعون من دون الله فيسبوا الله عدواً بغير علم — "Do not abuse those whom they invoke besides Allah, lest they should abuse Allah out of hostility, without any knowledge."

I say: The narration—as you see—its end does not harmonize with its beginning. For the requirement of its beginning is that they were asking him to cease from [opposing] their gods, meaning not to call the people to reject them and leave seeking proximity to them. When they despaired of his response, they threatened him with insulting his Lord if he insulted their gods. The requirement of the flow of the speech would be that they threaten him regarding the call to reject them, not that they ignore that and mention his insulting and threaten him for that. There is no indication in the verse to the beginning of the story, which is its core.

Furthermore, the dignity of prophethood and the "great character" (عظيم الخلق) that characterized his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله social interaction would prevent him from uttering abuse (الشتم), which is of the nature of vain speech (لغو القول). What has been narrated of his cursing some of the leaders of the Quraysh by saying: "O Allah, curse so-and-so and so-and-so," as well as what has appeared in His, the Exalted, speech of the nature of His saying: لعنهم الله بكفرهم — "Allah has cursed them for their faithlessness" (4:46), and His saying: فقتل كيف قدر — "Death to him! How he laid his plans!" (74:19), and His saying: قتل الإنسان — "Perish man!" (80:17), and His saying: أف لكم ولما تعبدون من دون الله — "Fie on you and on what you worship besides Allah!" (21:67), and their likes—these are of the nature of supplication (الدعاء), not abuse (الشتم), which is the mention of something by what is ugly and heinous for the purpose of imaginary insult. What has appeared of the nature of His, the Exalted, saying: مناع للخير معتد أثيم ، عتل بعد ذلك زنيم — "[who is] a hinderer of the good, a transgressor, a sinner, callous, and moreover, of doubtful birth" (68:12-13), is of the nature of clarifying the reality. Thus, it appears that the general masses of the believers in the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله were perhaps led by dispute and argument with the polytheists to mention their gods in an evil manner, as often happens among the general public in their arguments. So Allah forbade them from that, as the following hadith indicates.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: He said: My father narrated to me from Mas'adah ibn Sadaqah from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام who said: He was asked about the saying of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Indeed, polytheism (الشرك) is more hidden than the creeping of an ant on a black rock in a dark night." He said: "The believers used to abuse what the polytheists worshipped besides Allah, so the polytheists would abuse what the believers worshipped. Thus, Allah forbade the believers from abusing their gods, so that the disbelievers would not abuse the God of the believers, whereby the believers would have associated partners with Allah from where they did not know." Then He said: ولا تسبوا الذين يدعون من دون الله فيسبوا الله عدواً بغير علم.

In Tafsir al-'Ayyashi, from 'Amr al-Tayalisi from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام who said: I asked him about the saying of Allah: ولا تسبوا — the verse. He said: "O 'Amr, have you ever seen anyone abuse Allah?" He said: I said: "May Allah make me your ransom, how so?" He said: "Whoever abuses a friend (ولي) of Allah has abused Allah."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from Muhammad ibn Ka'b al-Qurazi who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله spoke to the Quraysh, and they said: "O Muhammad, you tell us that Moses had a staff with which he struck the rock, and that Jesus used to revive the dead, and that Thamud had a she-camel. So bring us some signs so that we may believe you." The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "What would you like me to bring you?" They said: "Turn al-Safa into gold for us." He said: "If I do so, will you believe me?" They said: "Yes, by Allah, if you do so, we will all surely follow you." So the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله stood up to supplicate, and Gabriel came and said to him: "If you wish, it shall become gold; but if they do not believe after that, We will surely punish them. And if you wish, leave them until the one who repents among them may repent." He said: "Rather, let the one who repents among them repent." Then Allah sent down: وأقسموا بالله جهد أيمانهم — until His saying يجهلون.

I say: The story mentioned as the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول) in the narration does not accord with the literal sense of the verses. It has already preceded that their literal sense is an informing that they will not believe in the signs that come to them, and that they will not depart from polytheism even if every possible sign came to them, unless Allah wills faith from them, and He has not willed that. If this is what is apparent from the verses, how can it accord with what is in the narration of Gabriel's saying: "If you wish, it shall become gold; but if they do not believe, they will be punished; and if you wish, leave them until the one who repents among them may repent," etc.?

Thus, it appears that the verses are in the sense of His saying: إن الذين كفروا سواء عليهم أأنذرتهم أم لم تنذرهم لا يؤمنون — "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not believe" (2:6). It is as if a group of the leaders of the polytheists suggested signs other than the Quran and swore by Allah with their most solemn oaths that if they came to them, they would surely believe in them. So Allah deemed them liars through these verses and informed that they would never believe because He, the Exalted, did not will that as a punishment (نكالا) upon them.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: ونقلب أفئدتهم وأبصارهم — the verse. In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام regarding the verse, he says: "We overturn their hearts so that the bottom of their hearts becomes the top, and We blind their sights so they do not see the guidance." And 'Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام said: "Indeed, that which you are overcome by in jihad is the jihad with your hands, then the jihad with your hearts. Whoever's heart does not recognize a good (معروفاً) and does not reject an evil (منكراً), his heart is overturned so that its bottom becomes its top, and it will never accept any good."

I say: What is meant by that is the turning of the soul in its perception and the inversion of its rulings due to following whims and turning away from the sound intellect (سليم العقل) which moderates the suggestions of the rebellious animalistic faculties.

In Tafsir al-'Ayyashi, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام: regarding the saying of Allah: ونقلب أفئدتهم وأبصارهم until the end of the verse. As for His saying: كما لم يؤمنوا به أول مرة, it was when the Covenant (الميثاق) was taken from them.

I say: The decisive discussion on the Covenant (الميثاق) will come in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وإذ أخذ ربك من بني آدم من ظهورهم ذريتهم — "When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants..." (7:172). However, it has preceded that the literal context suggests that what is meant by their not believing in it the first time is their not believing in the Quran at the beginning of the Call.

Ayahs

أَفَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْتَغِى حَكَمًا وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مُفَصَّلًا ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُۥ مُنَزَّلٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ (١١٤)
[Say,] ‘Shall I seek a judge other than Allah, while it is He who has sent down to you the Book, well-elaborated?’ Those whom We have given the Book know that it has been sent down from your Lord with the truth; so do not be one of the skeptics. (114)
وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقًا وَعَدْلًا ۚ لَّا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (١١٥)
The word of your Lord has been fulfilled in truth and justice. Nothing can change His words, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (115)
وَإِن تُطِعْ أَكْثَرَ مَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يُضِلُّوكَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ (١١٦)
If you obey most of those on the earth, they will lead you astray from the way of Allah. They follow nothing but conjectures and they do nothing but surmise. (116)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ مَن يَضِلُّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۖ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (١١٧)
Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way; and He knows best those who are guided. (117)
فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا ذُكِرَ ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ إِن كُنتُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦ مُؤْمِنِينَ (١١٨)
Eat from that over which Allah’s Name has been mentioned, if you are believers in His signs. (118)
وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا ذُكِرَ ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ وَقَدْ فَصَّلَ لَكُم مَّا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِلَّا مَا ٱضْطُرِرْتُمْ إِلَيْهِ ۗ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا لَّيُضِلُّونَ بِأَهْوَآئِهِم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (١١٩)
Why should you not eat that over which Allah’s Name has been mentioned, while He has already elaborated for you whatever He has forbidden you, excepting what you may be compelled to [eat in an emergency]? Indeed many mislead [others] by their fancies, without any knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best the transgressors. (119)
وَذَرُوا۟ ظَـٰهِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَبَاطِنَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْسِبُونَ ٱلْإِثْمَ سَيُجْزَوْنَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَقْتَرِفُونَ (١٢٠)
Renounce outward sins and the inward ones. Indeed those who commit sins shall be requited for what they used to commit. (120)
وَلَا تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا لَمْ يُذْكَرِ ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَفِسْقٌ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ لَيُوحُونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآئِهِمْ لِيُجَـٰدِلُوكُمْ ۖ وَإِنْ أَطَعْتُمُوهُمْ إِنَّكُمْ لَمُشْرِكُونَ (١٢١)
Do not eat [anything] of that over which Allah’s Name has not been mentioned, and that is indeed transgression. Indeed the satans inspire their friends to dispute with you; and if you obey them, you will indeed be polytheists. (121)

(Commentary)

The verses, in terms of their connection to what preceded them—as indicated by the branching particle fa (ف) in His saying: أَفَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَبْتَغِي حَكَماً (Shall I seek a judge other than Allah...) etc.—possess an internal cohesion among themselves. These eight verses are interconnected, with some parts relating back to others. They contain a rejection of taking any judge other than Allah, while He has detailed His rulings in His Book. They also contain a prohibition against following and obeying people, noting that obeying the majority of people leads to straying because they follow nothing but conjecture (الظن) and base their views on surmise (الخرص) and guesswork (التخمين). At the end of these verses, it is mentioned that the polytheists (المشركين), who are the allies of the devils (أولياء الشياطين), dispute with the believers regarding the matter of eating carrion (الميتة). Within them is the command to eat that over which the name of Allah has been mentioned and the prohibition of eating that over which the name of Allah has not been mentioned, and that this is what He has detailed in His Book and approved for His servants.

All of this supports what was narrated from Ibn Abbas: that the polytheists disputed with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believers regarding the matter of carrion, saying: "Do you eat from what you yourselves have killed, but do not eat from what Allah has killed?" Thus, these verses were revealed, and the purpose of these verses is to clarify the distinction and establish the ruling.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَبْتَغِي حَكَماً وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْكِتابَ مُفَصَّلاً — أَفَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَبْتَغِي حَكَماً وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْكِتابَ مُفَصَّلاً (Shall I seek a judge other than Allah, while it is He who has sent down to you the Book, well-elaborated?) It is stated in al-Majma‘: "The term al-hakam (الحكم) and al-hakim (الحاكم) share the same meaning, except that al-hakam is more laudatory because it signifies one who is worthy of being sought for judgment; such a person judges only by the truth, whereas a hakim might judge without truth." He also said: "The meaning of elaboration (التفصيل) is the clarification of meanings in a way that negates confusing ambiguity (التخليط المعمي) and also negates the overlapping (التداخل) that causes the explanation to fall short of the intended meaning." End quote.

In His saying: أَفَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَبْتَغِي حَكَماً (Shall I seek a judge other than Allah), there is a branching (تفريع) from the preceding mention of the 'insights' (البصائر) that came from Him, the Exalted. It was mentioned previously in the Quran that it is a Book He sent down, blessed and confirming what was before it of the Torah and the Gospel. The meaning is: Shall I seek a judge—whose judgment is to be followed—other than Allah from among the other deities you call upon or those who affiliate with them, while He is the One who sent down to you this Book, which is the Quran, well-elaborated (مفصلاً), with its various branches of knowledge distinguished from one another and its rulings not mixed with each other? No one deserves to be the judge except He who possesses this attribute. Thus, the verse is like His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ يَقْضِي بِالْحَقِّ وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ لا يَقْضُونَ بِشَيْءٍ إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ — Allah judges with justice, while those they invoke besides Him do not judge with anything. Indeed Allah is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (40:20).

And His saying: أَفَمَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى — Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who guides not unless he [himself] is guided? (10:35).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَعْلَمُونَ (And those to whom We have given the Book know...) to the end of the verse. This is a return to addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with that which reinforces his certainty and increases his steadfastness in the discourse he delivered to the polytheists—a discourse indicating that the Book revealed to him is sent down from his Lord with the truth (بالحق). Thus, there is a shift in address (التفات) in the speech, serving as a parenthetical statement to increase his firmness and the tranquility of his heart, and so that the polytheists may know that he possesses clear insight (بصيرة) regarding his affair.

His saying: بِالْحَقِ (with the truth) is connected to His saying: مُنَزَّلٌ مِنْ رَبِّكَ (sent down from your Lord). The revelation being "with the truth" means it is not through the descent of devils by way of seduction (التسويل) or through the path of soothsaying (الكهانة), as in His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ عَلى مَنْ تَنَزَّلُ الشَّياطِينُ ، تَنَزَّلُ عَلى كُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ — Should I inform you on whom the devils descend? They descend on every sinful liar. (26:221-222). Nor is it by the devils mixing some falsehood with the divine revelation (الوحي الإلهي). Allah has secured His Messenger from that with verses such as His saying: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً ، لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ — Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him, that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord. (72:26-28).

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقاً وَعَدْلاً لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ — The word of your Lord has been completed in truth and justice. Nothing can change His words, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (6:115). The "word" (الكلمة)—which is that which indicates a complete or incomplete meaning—is sometimes used in the Quran to refer to the truthful statement (القول الحق) uttered by Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, regarding a decree (القضاء) or a promise (الوعد), as in His saying: وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ — Had it not been for a prior word from your Lord, it would have been decided between them. (10:19). This refers to His saying to Adam upon the descent: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ — There will be for you on the earth an abode and a provision for a while. (2:36). And His saying, the Exalted: حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِمْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ — the word of your Lord has become due against them (10:96), referring to His saying to Iblis: لأَمْلأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together. (38:85). He explained it in another place by His saying: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ — and the word of your Lord has been fulfilled: ‘I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together.’ (11:119). Similarly, His saying, the Exalted: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ الْحُسْنى عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ بِما صَبَرُوا — And the best word of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of their patience. (7:137), referring to what He promised them—that He would save them from Pharaoh and make them inherit the land, as indicated by His saying: وَنُرِيدُ أَنْ نَمُنَّ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَنَجْعَلَهُمْ أَئِمَّةً وَنَجْعَلَهُمُ الْوارِثِينَ — And We desired to show favour to those who were abased in the land, and to make them imams, and to make them the heirs. (28:5).

Sometimes the "word" is used to refer to an external entity (العين الخارجي), such as a human being, for example, in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ — Allah gives you the good news of a Word from Him whose name is Messiah, Jesus son of Mary. (3:45). The subtle point (العناية) here is that he (peace be upon him) bypassed the custom of gradual development and was created by a divine, existential word (كلمة إلهية موجدة). The Exalted said: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى عِنْدَ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was. (3:59).

The apparent context of the verses in our current discussion suggests that the meaning of His saying: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقاً وَعَدْلاً (The word of your Lord has been completed in truth and justice) is the word of the Islamic call (الدعوة الإسلامية) and what it entails of the prophethood of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the revelation of the Quran—which is the guardian (المهيمن) over the previous heavenly books, containing the sum of divine knowledge and the universal principles of religious laws (كليات الشرائع الدينية). This is as He indicated in what He recounted of the prayer of Abraham (peace be upon him) during the building of the Kaaba: رَبَّنا وَابْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولاً مِنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ — ‘Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among them, who should recite to them Your signs, and teach them the Book and wisdom, and purify them.’ (2:129).

He also indicated its prior mention in the heavenly books in His saying: الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَالْإِنْجِيلِ — those who follow the Apostle, the untaught prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel. (7:157). This is also suggested by His saying in the previous verse: وَالَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ مُنَزَّلٌ مِنْ رَبِّكَ بِالْحَقِ (And those to whom We have given the Book know that it is sent down from your Lord with the truth) and His saying: الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَعْرِفُونَهُ كَما يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْناءَهُمْ — Those whom We have given the Book recognize him just as they recognize their sons. (2:146), among many other such verses.

Thus, the meaning of the "completion of the word"—and Allah knows best—is this word reaching, i.e., the manifestation of the Islamic call through the prophethood of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the revelation of the Book that is the guardian over all books, the level of established reality and its settlement in the abode of actualization. This occurred after it had traveled for a long time through the stages of gradual progression, prophet after prophet and law after law. Indeed, the noble verses indicate that the Islamic law (الشريعة الإسلامية) encompasses the sum of the laws that preceded it and adds to them what was not in them, as in His saying, the Exalted: شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى — He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus. (42:13).

From this, the meaning of the "completion of the word" becomes clear: it refers to the end of the gradual progression of religious laws from the stages of deficiency to the stage of perfection, and its concrete instance (مصداقه) is the Muhammadan religion. The Exalted said: وَاللهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْكافِرُونَ ، هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُشْرِكُونَ — and Allah will perfect His light though the faithless should be averse. It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse. (61:8-9).

The completion of this divine word "in truth" (صدقاً) means that the statement is verified by its actualization in the external world according to the attribute with which it was described. "In justice" (وعدلاً) means it is characterized by equitable distribution (التقسيط على سواء), such that none of its parts fail to align with the others, and it weighs things in the manner they ought to be weighed, without loss, bias, or injustice. Therefore, He clarified these two conditions—namely, "truth and justice"—by His saying: لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ (Nothing can change His words). For if the divine word does not accept alteration from any changer—whether the changer be Himself, the Exalted, such as by annulling what He decreed due to a change in will or by breaking His promise, or whether the changer be someone other than Him, the Exalted, such as by someone rendering Him incapable or overpowering Him against what He intends—then His word is truth (صدق), occurring exactly as He said, and justice (عدل), not deviating from the state it was in or the attribute with which it was described. Thus, the phrase: لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ (Nothing can change His words) serves as a justification (تعليل) for His saying: صِدْقاً وَعَدْلاً (in truth and justice).

Among the views of the commentators regarding the verse is that the "word" and "words" refer to the Quran. It is also said that the "word" refers to the Quran, while the "words" refer to what it contains other than the religious laws, for laws accept change through abrogation (النسخ), whereas Allah, the Glorified, says: لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ (Nothing can change His words). It is also said that the "word" refers to the religion (الدين), or the proof (الحجة). It is also said that "truth" refers to the reports (الأخبار) in the Quran, and "justice" refers to the rulings (الأحكام) therein.

And His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ (and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing), meaning the All-hearing who responds to what you call upon Him with the tongue of your need, the All-knowing of the reality of the need you possess. Or, the All-hearing of what occurs in His kingdom through the intermediary of the messenger-angels, and the All-knowing of that without any intermediary. Or, the All-hearing of your words, the All-knowing of your deeds.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ تُطِعْ أَكْثَرَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ يُضِلُّوكَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ — If you obey most of those on the earth, they will lead you astray from the way of Allah. (6:116) to the end of the verse. Al-kharṣ (الخرص) is lying and guesswork (التخمين). The second meaning is more appropriate to the context of the verse, for the phrase: وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ (and they do nothing but surmise) and the one before it: إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَ (They follow nothing but conjecture) serve as the justification (تعليل) for His saying: وَإِنْ تُطِعْ أَكْثَرَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ (If you obey most of those on the earth...). Following conjecture and speaking by surmise and guesswork are naturally causes for straying in matters where it is not permissible to rely on anything except knowledge (العلم) and certainty (اليقين), such as the divine sciences (المعارف) relating to Him, the Exalted, and the religious laws taken from Him.

Although a human being's journey and vital conduct in this world cannot be completed without relying on conjecture and seeking help from guesswork—to the extent that one who researches the conventional human sciences (علوم الإنسان الاعتبارية) and the causes and reasons that prompt him to formulate them and apply them to his vital affairs, actions, and states, will hardly find an instance where a human relies on pure knowledge and absolute certainty, except for some theoretical universals (الكليات النظرية) that he eventually reaches and is forced to acknowledge and rely upon.

However, all of that pertains to the particulars of life's affairs that accept approximation and guesswork. As for human felicity (السعادة الإنسانية), in which lies the success and prosperity of this species, and the wretchedness (الشقاء) associated with eternal perdition and permanent loss, and the insight required for both—which involves contemplating the world, its Maker, the purpose of its creation, the ultimate end of resurrection (البعث والنشور), and what relates to it of prophethood, the Book, and judgment—all of this is among that which does not accept reliance on conjecture and guesswork. Allah, the Glorified, does not accept from His servants in these matters anything but knowledge and certainty. The verses regarding this are very numerous, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَقْفُ ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ — Do not follow that of which you have no knowledge. (17:36).

Among the clearest in indication is this verse we are discussing, in which He clarifies that the majority of the people of the earth, due to their general reliance on conjecture and guesswork, may not be obeyed in what they call to and command regarding the way of Allah and the path of His servitude. This is because conjecture is not that by which the truth (الحق)—in which one finds rest regarding the matter of Lordship and servitude—is uncovered, due to its inherent ignorance of reality and lack of tranquility toward it. There is no servitude (عبودية) alongside ignorance of the Lord and what He desires from His servant.

This is what clear reason (العقل الصريح) dictates, and Allah, the Glorified, has endorsed it, as in His saying in the following verse regarding the justification for the prohibition of obedience: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ مَنْ يَضِلُّ عَنْ سَبِيله وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ (Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided), where He justified the ruling by Allah's knowledge rather than the judgment of reason. He, the Glorified, combined both paths in His saying: وَما لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَإِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً — They have no knowledge of it. They follow nothing but conjecture, and indeed conjecture is no substitute for the truth. (53:28)—this is an adoption of the judgment of reason—فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اهْتَدى — So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided. (53:29-30). In the end of the verse, there is a reliance on the knowledge and judgment of Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ مَنْ يَضِلُّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ (Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided). It has been mentioned that the word a‘lam (أعلم), when not completed with the particle min (من), sometimes conveys the meaning of preference (التفضيل) and is sometimes used as a simple attribute (الصفة) devoid of preference. The verse allows for both meanings. If the intended meaning is the reality of knowledge regarding those who stray and those who are guided, then that belongs to Allah, the Glorified, and no one shares it with Him such that He would be preferred over them. If the intended meaning is absolute knowledge—regardless of whether the one characterized by it is so by essence or by His gift—then the meaning of preference is necessary, for others besides Allah, the Glorified, possess knowledge of the straying and the guided to the extent of the knowledge Allah has bestowed upon them.

The transitivity of a‘lam with the particle ba (ب) in His saying: أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ (knows best those who are guided) indicates that His saying: مَنْ يَضِلُ (those who stray) is in the accusative case due to the removal of the preposition, the implied meaning being: "knows best of those who stray" (أعلم بمن يضل). This is supported by what we quoted just now from the verse of Surah al-Najm.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكُلُوا مِمَّا ذُكِرَ اسْمُ اللهِ عَلَيْهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ بِآياتِهِ مُؤْمِنِينَ — Eat, then, of that over which Allah’s name has been mentioned, if you are believers in His signs. (6:118). Once the preceding clarification—which is a proof that Allah, the Glorified, is worthier of being obeyed than anyone else—was established, He derived from it the obligation of adopting the ruling He legislated, which is what this verse indicates, and the obligation of rejecting what others permit according to their whims without knowledge, disputing with the believers through the inspiration of the devils. This is what is indicated by His saying: وَلا تَأْكُلُوا مِمَّا لَمْ يُذْكَرِ اسْمُ اللهِ عَلَيْهِ (And do not eat of that over which Allah’s name has not been mentioned...) to the end of the verse.

From here, it becomes clear that the primary focus (العناية الأصلية) is directed toward two sentences among the sentences arranged in the verses up to the completion of four verses, while the other sentences are intended subordinately to clarify what the objective depends upon in its various aspects. The core of the speech is: "Eat of that over which Allah's name has been mentioned and do not eat of that over which Allah's name has not been mentioned," meaning: distinguish between the slaughtered animal (المذكى) and the carrion (الميتة); eat from the former and do not eat from the latter, even if the polytheists dispute with you regarding this distinction.

Thus, His saying: فَكُلُوا مِمَّا ذُكِرَ اسْمُ اللهِ (Eat, then, of that over which Allah’s name [has been mentioned]) is a branching of the ruling from the previous clarification. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ بِآياتِهِ مُؤْمِنِينَ (if you are believers in His signs). What is meant by "that over which Allah's name has been mentioned" is the slaughtered animal (الذبيحة المذكاة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما لَكُمْ أَلَّا تَأْكُلُوا مِمَّا ذُكِرَ اسْمُ اللهِ عَلَيْهِ — Why should you not eat of that over which Allah’s name has been mentioned... (6:119) to the end of the verse. This is a detailed explanation of the summary branching in the previous verse. The meaning is: Allah has detailed for you what He has forbidden to you and excepted the case of necessity (الاضطرار), and among what He detailed for you is not that over which Allah's name has been mentioned, so there is no harm in eating it. Indeed, many lead others astray by their whims without knowledge. Indeed, your Lord knows best the transgressors (المعتدين) who overstep His bounds, and these are the polytheists who say: "There is no difference between what you have killed and what Allah has killed, so eat all of it or leave all of it."
