As for what was mentioned in the second verse—avoiding the orphan's wealth, giving full measure and weight with justice, justice in speech, and fulfilling the covenant of Allah—these are matters not at the same level of obviousness as those recited in the first verse. Rather, man, along with being equipped with reason, needs to "take admonition" (التذكر) — the act of recalling and reflecting upon truths — which is to return to the general benefits and harms known to the primordial reason, so that he may perceive the corruption within them that destroys the structure of his society and brings him and the rest of his species to destruction. For what good remains in a human society where the young and weak are not shown mercy, where measure and weight are cheated, where judgment and adjudication are not just, and where the word of truth is not heeded? For this subtle point, the verse ended with His saying: ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ.

The purpose for which the third verse was presented is the prohibition of division and difference in religion by following ways other than the way of Allah. Following those ways is such that religious godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being God-conscious and guarding oneself against sin — cannot be attained except by avoiding them.

This is because religious godwariness is only achieved by insight into the divine prohibitions and refraining from His forbidden matters through the exercise of reason and taking admonition; in other words, by adhering to the human primordial nature upon which the religion is built. The Exalted has said: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها — "by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its God-consciousness" (91:7-8). Allah has promised the God-conscious that if they are God-conscious, He will aid them with that which clarifies their path and distinguishes between truth and falsehood for them, saying: وَمَنْ يَتَّقِ اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ مَخْرَجاً — "and whoever is God-conscious, He will make for him a way out" (65:2), and saying: إِنْ تَتَّقُوا اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ فُرْقاناً — "If you are God-conscious, He will provide you with a criterion" (8:29).

Thus, one is on the path of godwariness as long as he is adherent to the way of exercising reason and taking admonition, proceeding according to the course of the primordial nature. If he deviates to the outside of this path—which is nothing but following whims, clinging to the earth, and being deceived by the glitter of the life of this world—whims and emotions pull him toward indulgence and devotion to opposing sound reason and abandoning religious godwariness, without concern for the ill-fated end that threatens him, like a drunkard who knows neither what he does nor what is being done to him.

Psychological whims (الأهواء النفسانية) are diverse; there is no standard to regulate them nor a system to govern them in which their followers can unite. Therefore, you hardly see two people of whims adhering to one path or accompanying each other to one goal. Allah the Glorified has counted for them in His speech various ways, such as His saying: وَلِتَسْتَبِينَ سَبِيلُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ — "and so that the way of the guilty may become clear" (6:55), His saying: وَلا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ — "and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption" (7:142), His saying: وَلا تَتَّبِعانِّ سَبِيلَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "and do not follow the way of those who do not know" (10:89), and His saying regarding the polytheists: إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَما تَهْوَى الْأَنْفُسُ وَلَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ مِنْ رَبِّهِمُ الْهُدى — "They follow nothing but conjectures and what their souls desire, while guidance has already come to them from their Lord" (53:23). If you trace the verses of guidance and misguidance, and following and obedience, you will find much regarding this meaning.

In short, religious godwariness is not achieved through division and difference, nor by entering any path that is laid out, nor by walking in any valley that appears to the traveler. Rather, it is achieved by adhering to the straight path in which there is no failure nor difference. That is what is hoped to lead to being clothed in the garment of godwariness. Therefore, Allah the Glorified followed His saying وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ with His saying: ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ.

It is said in Ruh al-Ma‘ani: "The first verse was ended with His saying, the Glorified: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ, and this one—meaning the second—with His saying: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ, because the people were persisting in polytheism, killing children, approaching adultery, and killing the inviolable soul without right, without loathing them or reasoning their foulness. So He forbade them so that they might reason their foulness, thus loathing them and abandoning them. As for preserving the wealth of orphans, giving full measure, justice in speech, and fulfilling the covenant, they used to do these things and take pride in being characterized by them. So Allah the Exalted commanded them with that so that they might take admonition should forgetfulness occur to them. This was said by al-Qutb al-Razi." End quote.

You are well aware that what he mentioned regarding their being characterized by preserving the wealth of orphans, giving full measure, and justice in speech does not agree with what history has recorded of the traits of the Arabs in the Age of Ignorance. Furthermore, the way he interpreted "taking admonition" (التذكر) is the meaning of "remembrance" (الذكر) rather than "taking admonition" in the usage of the Quran.

Then he said: "The Imam—meaning al-Razi—said in al-Tafsir al-Kabir: ‘The reason for ending each verse with what it was ended with is that the five obligations mentioned in the first verse are manifest and clear, so it was necessary to reason and understand them. The four obligations mentioned in this verse—meaning the second—are hidden and obscure matters that require much exertion (الاجتهاد) and reflection until one stands upon the point of moderation (الاعتدال), which is taking admonition.’" End quote.

What he mentioned of the reason is close in approach to what we have presented, except that the four matters mentioned in the second verse are things man attains with the slightest reflection; they are not of that hiddenness and obscurity he described. Therefore, he was forced to return "taking admonition" to standing upon the limit of moderation in them rather than their essence, thereby corrupting the meaning of the verse. For the requirement of the context is the return of the hope of taking admonition to the essence of what was enjoined therein. That which naturally needs to stand upon its limit of moderation are the first two matters, namely approaching the orphan's wealth and giving full measure and weight, and their matter was addressed by His saying: لا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها. So understand that.

Then he said regarding the third verse: "Abu Hayyan said: ‘Since the straight path is the gatherer of the obligations, and Allah the Glorified commanded its following and forbade following other paths, He ended that with godwariness, which is guarding against the Fire; for whoever follows His path attains eternal salvation and gains everlasting happiness.’" End quote.

This is based on making the command to follow the straight path in the verse that to which the primary intention is attached. It has already been mentioned that the requirement of the context is its being a preamble for the prohibition of division by following other ways, and a preparation for His saying: وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ تَماماً عَلَى الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ — "Then We gave Moses the Book, completing [the blessing] on him who was virtuous..." to the end of the verse. Since what He mentioned and enjoined of the universals of the laws are legislated obligations general to all the religion given to the prophets—and they are universal, concise matters—this made it appropriate to shift to clarifying that He, the Glorified, after legislating them for all in a concise manner, detailed them where their detailing was required: for Musa (peace be upon him) first in what He sent down to him of the Book, and for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) second in what He sent down to him of a blessed Book. So He said: ثُمَّ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ تَماماً عَلَى الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ etc.

The meaning of the verse is: "After We legislated the concise religious laws, We gave Musa the Book as a completion (تماماً) by which the deficiency of those who did good among them—in terms of the concise law—is completed, and as a detailing (تفصيلاً) by which every thing of the branches of these concise laws is detailed, of what the Children of Israel need, and as a guidance and mercy so that they might believe in the encounter with their Lord." This is what is provided by the context of the verse, which is connected to the context of the three preceding verses.

Thus, His saying ثُمَّ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ is a return to the previous context that was before His saying قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ etc., which is the speech of Allah to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the first-person plural form. It has been conveyed by the delay understood from the word ثُمَّ (Then) that this Book was only sent down to be a completion and a detailing for the conciseness that was in those general, universal laws.

The commentators have explained His saying ثُمَّ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ with strange views: Among them: that there is an omission in the speech, and the estimation is: "Then say, O Muhammad: We gave Musa the Book." Among them: that the estimation is: "Then I inform you that Musa was given the Book." Among them: that the estimation is: "Then I recite to you: We gave Musa the Book." Among them: that it is connected to His saying in the story of Ibrahim: وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ (And We gave him Isaac and Jacob), and the structure is: "And We gave him Isaac and Jacob, then We gave Musa the Book."

What drove them to these forced interpretations (التكلفات) is that the Torah was before the Quran, and the word ثُمَّ necessitates a delay (التراخي), which would imply the descent of the Torah after the Quran, while it was said before that: قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ. The previous explanation suffices you from the burden of these views.

His saying تَماماً عَلَى الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ (completing [the blessing] on him who was virtuous) clarifies that the sending down of the Book was so that the deficiency of those who did good among the Children of Israel in acting upon these universal, general laws might be completed. The Exalted said in the story of Musa after the descent of the Book: وَكَتَبْنا لَهُ فِي الْأَلْواحِ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ فَخُذْها بِقُوَّةٍ وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا بِأَحْسَنِها — "We wrote for him in the Tablets advice concerning all things and an elaboration of all things: ‘Hold on to them with power, and bid your people to hold on to the best of them.’" (7:145), and He said: وَادْخُلُوا الْبابَ سُجَّداً وَقُولُوا حِطَّةٌ نَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطاياكُمْ وَسَنَزِيدُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — "and enter the gate prostrating, and say, ‘Relief!’ We will forgive you your sins and We will soon enhance the virtuous." (2:58). Accordingly, the relative pronoun (الموصول) in His saying عَلَى الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ denotes the genus.

They have mentioned other views regarding the meaning of the sentence. It was said: "The meaning is: a completion upon the excellence of Musa with prophethood and honor." It was said: "The meaning is: a completion of the blessing upon those who did good among the believers." It was said: "The meaning is: a completion of the blessing upon the prophets who did good." It was said: "The meaning is: a completion of his honor in Paradise for his excellence in the world." It was said: "The meaning is: a completion upon that which Allah bestowed of excellence upon Musa of honor with prophethood and other things." It was said: "It is connected to the story of Ibrahim, and the meaning is: a completion of the blessing upon Ibrahim." The weakness of all these is apparent.

His saying وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ (and an elaboration of all things) means of what the Children of Israel need or what others after them benefit from, and a guidance by which they are guided and a mercy by which they are blessed. His saying لَعَلَّهُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ (so that they may believe in the encounter with their Lord) contains an indication that the Children of Israel were sluggish or refused to believe in the encounter with Allah and the Last Day. This is supported by the fact that the current Torah—which the Quran mentions is distorted—contains no mention of the resurrection on the Day of Judgment. Some of their historians have mentioned that the people of Israel did not believe in the Return (المعاد).

His saying, the Exalted: وَهذا كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ مُبارَكٌ — "This is a blessed Book that We have sent down..." to the end of the verse. That is: this is a blessed Book that shares with the Book of Musa in what We mentioned of the characteristic, so follow it, etc.

His saying, the Exalted: أَنْ تَقُولُوا إِنَّما أُنْزِلَ الْكِتابُ عَلى طائِفَتَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِنا — "lest you should say, ‘The Book was only sent down to two groups before us...’" etc. أَنْ تَقُولُوا means "for fear that you say" or "lest you say," and this is common in speech. It is connected to His saying in the previous verse: أَنْزَلْناهُ (We have sent it down).

His saying طائِفَتَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِنا (two groups before us) refers to the Jews and the Christians, upon whom the Torah and the Gospel were sent down. As for the books of the prophets sent down before them which the Quran mentions, such as the Book of Nuh and the Book of Ibrahim (peace be upon them), they did not contain the detailing of the laws, even if they included their foundation. As for the rest of what is attributed to the prophets (peace be upon them) of books, such as the Psalms (زبور) of Dawud (peace be upon him) and others, they did not contain laws nor did they have a covenant regarding them.

The meaning is: "We sent down the Quran for fear that you say: ‘The divine Book detailing its laws was only sent down to two groups before us, the Jews and the Christians, and we were unaware of their study and recitation, and there is no blame upon us despite the unawareness.’"

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ تَقُولُوا لَوْ أَنَّا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْكِتابُ لَكُنَّا أَهْدى مِنْهُمْ — "or lest you should say, ‘If the Book had been sent down to us, we would surely have been better guided than them.’" to the end of the verse. That is, better guided than those to whom the Book was sent down before us. His saying فَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ بَيِّنَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ (Now there has come to you a clear proof from your Lord) is a derivation (تفريع) from both His sayings أَنْ تَقُولُوا and أَوْ تَقُولُوا. He substituted "the Book" with "clear proof" (بينة) to indicate the manifestness of its argument and the clarity of its indication, such that no excuse remains for an apologizer nor any pretext for a pretender. الصدف (Turning away) means aversion, and the meaning of the verse is clear.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Abu Basir, who said: "I was sitting with Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him)—and he was leaning on his cushion—when he recited the decisive verses (الآيات المحكمات) which nothing has abrogated from al-An‘am. He said: ‘Seventy thousand angels escorted them: قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ ـ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً.’"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from ‘Ubadah ibn al-Samit, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: ‘Which of you will pledge allegiance to me upon these three verses?’ Then he recited: قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ to the end of the three verses."

Then he said: "So whoever fulfills them, his reward is with Allah. And whoever falls short in anything of them and Allah catches him in this world—that is his punishment. And whoever is delayed until the Hereafter, his matter is with Allah—if He wishes, He will punish him, and if He wishes, He will forgive him."

I say: The narration is not without issue, for among what is mentioned in the verses is polytheism against Allah, and the punishment of this world does not suffice for it, nor does it receive forgiveness in the Hereafter by the explicit text of the Quran. The Exalted said: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ — "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him" (4:48), and He said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَماتُوا وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ أُولئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ ، خالِدِينَ فِيها لا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابُ وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ — "Indeed those who disbelieve and die while they are disbelievers—it is they on whom shall be the curse of Allah, the angels and all mankind. They will remain in it [forever]; their punishment shall not be lightened, nor shall they be granted any respite." (2:161-162).

Furthermore, the apparent sense of the narration is that these rulings are among what is exclusive to this law, as suggested by what was narrated from some of the Companions and the Successors, such as what was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from a group from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "Whoever would be pleased to look at the bequest of Muhammad upon which is his seal—let him read these verses: قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ to His saying لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ." Similar to this is what was narrated from Mundhir al-Thawri from al-Rabi‘ ibn Khaytham.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from ‘Amr ibn Abi al-Miqdam from his father from ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him): الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ (indecencies, the outward among them and the inward). He said: "What is outward is marrying the father's wife, and what is inward is adultery."

I say: This is by way of mentioning some of the instances (المصاديق).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Nasa’i, al-Bazzar, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) drew a line with his hand and then said: ‘This is the way of Allah, straight.’ Then he drew lines to the right of that line and to its left—then he said: ‘And these are ways; there is not a way among them except that there is a devil upon it calling to it.’ Then he recited: وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ ـ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ."

In it, Ahmad, Ibn Majah, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah, who said: "We were sitting with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and he drew a line like this in front of him—and said: ‘This is the way of Allah.’ And he drew two lines to its right and two lines to its left—and said: ‘This is the way of Satan.’ Then he placed his hand on the middle line and recited: وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ the verse."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali informed us from his father from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id from Muhammad ibn Sinan from Abu Khalid al-Qammat from Abu Basir from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ ـ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ. He said: "We are the way (السبيل); so whoever refuses, then these are the ways, and he has indeed disbelieved."

I say: This is of the nature of application (الجري). What he (peace be upon him) mentioned is derived from His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except love of [my] relatives.’" (42:23), when joined with His saying: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, except that anyone who wishes should take the way to his Lord.’" (24:57).

Several narrations have been reported from both Shi‘i and Sunni chains that ‘Ali is the straight path, and reference to this has already been made in the commentary on Surah al-Fatihah in the first volume of the book.

Ayahs

هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ رَبُّكَ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ ۗ يَوْمَ يَأْتِى بَعْضُ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكَ لَا يَنفَعُ نَفْسًا إِيمَـٰنُهَا لَمْ تَكُنْ ءَامَنَتْ مِن قَبْلُ أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِىٓ إِيمَـٰنِهَا خَيْرًا ۗ قُلِ ٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُنتَظِرُونَ (١٥٨)
Do they not consider that the angels may come to them, or your Lord may come, or some of your Lord’s signs may come? The day when some of your Lord’s signs do come, faith shall not benefit any soul that had not believed beforehand and had not earned some goodness in its faith. Say, ‘Wait! We too are waiting!’ (158)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ شِيَعًا لَّسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِى شَىْءٍ ۚ إِنَّمَآ أَمْرُهُمْ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (١٥٩)
Indeed those who split up their religion and became sects, you will not have anything to do with them. Their matter rests only with Allah; then He will inform them concerning what they used to do. (159)
مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُۥ عَشْرُ أَمْثَالِهَا ۖ وَمَن جَآءَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ فَلَا يُجْزَىٰٓ إِلَّا مِثْلَهَا وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (١٦٠)
Whoever brings virtue shall receive [a reward] ten times its like; but whoever brings vice shall not be requited except with its like, and they will not be wronged. (160)

(Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them, and they contain a threat to those polytheists (المشركين) who disdained the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) and split into sects (شيعا), a clearance of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from those who divided their religion, a fair promise for whoever brings a righteous deed (الحسنة), and the fulfillment of the recompense.

His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ رَبُّكَ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ — "Do they await anything but that the angels should come to them, or your Lord should come, or some of your Lord’s signs should come?" (6:158). This is a rhetorical question of denunciation (استفهام إنكاري) in a situation where neither admonition benefits nor a call succeeds. Thus, the matters mentioned in the verse are inevitably things that are accompanied only by the judgment between them with justice and the decisive decree (الحكم الفصل) by their removal and the purification of the earth from their filth.

The necessity of this context is that the intended meaning of the coming of the angels is their descent with the sign of punishment, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ ، لَوْ ما تَأْتِينا بِالْمَلائِكَةِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ ، ما نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَما كانُوا إِذاً مُنْظَرِينَ — "They say, ‘O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy! Why do you not bring us the angels, if you are truthful?’ We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they would not be granted any respite" (15:6-8).

The intended meaning of the coming of the Lord is the Day of Encounter (يوم اللقاء), which is the complete disclosure of the sign of monotheism (آية التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — such that no veil remains over it, as is the nature of the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) which is characterized by the removal of the covering. What justifies the application of "coming" (الإتيان) to that is the manifestation after concealment and presence after absence; exalted is His majesty (جل شأنه) above being described with the attributes of bodies.

It is sometimes said that the intended meaning is the coming of the command of the Lord, and its likeness has already passed in His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ فِي ظُلَلٍ مِنَ الْغَمامِ — "Do they await anything but that Allah should come to them in the shades of the clouds" (2:210), in the second volume of this book.

The intended meaning of the coming of some of the signs of the Lord is the coming of a sign that necessitates the transformation of the realm of life (نشأة الحياة) for them, such that there is no way to return to the expanse of choice (فسحة الاختيار). This is like the sign of death, which transforms the realm of action (نشأة العمل) into the realm of the intermediate recompense (جزاء البرزخي) — the state between death and resurrection — or it necessitates the settling of the disposition (ملكة) of disbelief and denial in their souls with a permanence that makes it impossible for them to submit to monotheism (التوحيد) and turn their hearts toward the truth, except for what might be on their tongues out of fear of the encompassing wrath and punishment. This is perhaps indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَخْرَجْنا لَهُمْ دَابَّةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ تُكَلِّمُهُمْ أَنَّ النَّاسَ كانُوا بِآياتِنا لا يُوقِنُونَ — "When the word falls against them, We shall bring out for them a monster from the earth who shall speak to them that the people had no faith in Our signs" (27:82).

Likewise is His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْفَتْحُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ ، قُلْ يَوْمَ الْفَتْحِ لا يَنْفَعُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِيمانُهُمْ وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ — "They say, ‘When will this judgment be, if you are truthful?’ Say, ‘On the day of judgment their faith will not benefit the faithless, nor will they be granted any respite’" (32:29-30). For it is apparent that the intended meaning of "the judgment" (الفتح) is the victory for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by the decree between him and his community with justice, as Allah the Exalted narrated from Shu‘ayb (عليه‌السلام) in his saying: رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْفاتِحِينَ — "Our Lord! Judge between us and our people with justice, and You are the best of judges!" (7:89), and as He narrated from His messengers in His saying: وَاسْتَفْتَحُوا وَخابَ كُلُّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ — "They sought victory, and every obstinate tyrant was disappointed" (14:15).

Or it necessitates a might (بأسا) from Allah the Exalted that cannot be turned back or escaped, so Allah compels them to faith so they may protect themselves from the painful punishment, but that does not benefit them. For faith (الإيمان) does not benefit except what was out of choice, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا قالُوا آمَنَّا بِاللهِ وَحْدَهُ وَكَفَرْنا بِما كُنَّا بِهِ مُشْرِكِينَ ، فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنْفَعُهُمْ إِيمانُهمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا سُنَّتَ اللهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ فِي عِبادِهِ وَخَسِرَ هُنالِكَ الْكافِرُونَ — "Then, when they sighted Our punishment, they said, ‘We believe in Allah alone, and we disbelieve in what we used to ascribe as partners to Him.’ But their faith did not benefit them when they sighted Our punishment—Allah’s precedent that has passed among His servants—and there the faithless were losers" (40:84-85).

So these things—namely the coming of the angels, or the coming of the Lord, or the coming of some of His signs—are matters that accompany the decree between them with justice. Since no proof (حجة) affects them and no admonition (موعظة) benefits them, they await nothing but that, even if they are heedless of it; for what lies before them is a reality, whether they know it or ignore it.

It is sometimes said that the interrogation is for mockery (التهكم), as they used to propose to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he should bring down angels upon them, or that they should see their Lord, or that he should bring them a sign as the former ones were sent. So it is as if it were said: These people do not want a proof; they are only waiting for the things they proposed.

This view is not far-fetched regarding the beginning of the verse, but its end—namely His saying: يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ (the day when some of your Lord’s signs come)—does not suit it with such harmony, for mockery does not usually extend into the explanation of realities and the detailing of effects.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ to the end of the verse, explains the specific characteristic of the day of the appearance of these signs. In reality, it is a characteristic of the signs themselves: that faith (الإيمان) does not benefit a soul that did not believe before that day with a faith of obedience and choice, or believed before it but had not earned any good in its faith and did not perform righteous deeds (صالحا), but rather was immersed in evil deeds and acts of disobedience. This is because there is no repentance (توبة) for such a person. The Exalted said: وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ حَتَّى إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ الْآنَ — "But repentance is not for those who go on committing misdeeds until, when death visits one of them, he says, ‘I repent now’" (4:18). Thus, the soul that did not believe previously with a faith of obedience and pleasure, or believed in Allah but denied the signs of Allah and paid no heed to any of the laws of Allah, and indulged in destructive sins and earned nothing of righteous work (صالح العمل) when it was required of it, then witnessed the divine might and was driven by necessity to faith to repel the might of Allah the Exalted—that did not benefit it, nor did it repel any might from it; and His might is not repelled from the guilty people.

In the verse, there is a marvelous composition and subtle context in that He repeated the word "your Lord" (رَبُّكَ) three times. This is only to support the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) against his adversaries, the polytheists (المشركون), as they used to boast of their lords and take pride in their idols. This was so he might find strength in his Lord, his heart might be firm through Him, and his courage might be bolstered in his call—if it succeeds, otherwise by the decisive decree (القضاء الفصل) with which his Lord judges between him and his adversary. Then He emphasized that and increased the reassurance of his soul by saying at the conclusion of the verse: قُلِ انْتَظِرُوا إِنَّا مُنْتَظِرُونَ — "Say, ‘Wait! We too are waiting’" (6:158). That is: wait for what they are waiting for, and inform them that you are in expectation of it, and command them to wait for it, for it is the decisive matter and not a jest.

From here it becomes clear that the verse contains a serious threat, not a formal scaring. By this, the invalidity of what some mentioned in refuting the one who said the interrogation in the verse is for mockery becomes apparent. He said: "These three signs are what they, like others, await in reality, so it is not correct that this 'some' refers to anything they proposed; because the granting of signs proposed to the messengers necessitates, in the sunnah of Allah, the destruction of the community by an exterminating punishment if they do not believe in it, and Allah does not destroy the community of the Prophet of Mercy." [End of quote].

The flaw in this is that the indication of the Quranic verses that this community will be encompassed by the decree between them with justice and the decisive judgment (الحكم الفصل) is something that cannot be hidden, such as His saying: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ ، وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ ، قُلْ لا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللهُ لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ — until He said — وَيَسْتَنْبِئُونَكَ أَحَقٌّ هُوَ قُلْ إِي وَرَبِّي إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ — "Every nation has an apostle. So when their apostle comes, judgement is passed between them with justice, and they are not wronged. They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ Say, ‘I have no power to bring myself any benefit or harm, except what Allah may wish. There is a time for every nation... They ask you, ‘Is it true?’ Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord! It is indeed true, and you cannot frustrate [Him]’" (10:47-53).

The verse has been used as evidence that faith (الإيمان) has no effect if it is not coupled with action, and this is true in general (في الجملة), not absolutely. For the verse is in the position of explaining that whoever had it in his power to believe in Allah but did not believe, or had it in his power to believe and perform righteous deeds but believed and did not perform righteous deeds until the severe divine might overtook him and compelled him to that, then he does not benefit from his faith. As for the one who believed willingly but death overtook him and his term did not grant him respite to perform righteous deeds and earn good in his faith, the verse does not address his condition. Rather, the verse is not devoid of an intimation or indication that what is beneficial is only faith when it is out of willingness and has not been encompassed by sin nor corrupted by evil deeds.

In His saying: لا يَنْفَعُ نَفْساً إِيمانُها لَمْ تَكُنْ آمَنَتْ — "its faith shall not benefit any soul that had not believed" (6:158), there is a separation between the described (الموصوف) and the description (الوصف) by the subject of the verb, which is "its faith" (إيمانها). It seems this is to avoid a long separation between the verb and its subject, and the convergence of "in its faith" (فِي إِيمانِها) and "its faith" (إيمانُها) in wording.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكانُوا شِيَعاً لَسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ — "Indeed those who split up their religion and became sects, you have nothing to do with them" (6:159) etc. Although the preceding discourse was with the polytheists (المشركين) who were afflicted with splitting the primordial religion (الدين الحنيف), and the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) also had a share of the discourse and were perhaps alluded to, and the necessity of that is that His saying: "those who split up their religion and became sects" applies to the polytheists, rather to them and to the Jews and Christians due to the commonality of all in splitting and differing in the divine religion.

However, the connection of the discourse to the verses explaining the general divine laws—which begin with the prohibition of polytheism (الشرك) and end with the prohibition of splitting from the way of Allah—requires that His saying: "those who split up their religion and became sects" be a subject for explaining the state of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with whoever possesses this description. Thus, the use of the past tense in His saying: "split up their religion" (فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ) is to explain the principle of occurrence, whether in the past, present, or future, not the occurrence of the act in the past time only.

It is well known that the distinction of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his exclusion from those who differ in religion and are split into sects—each sect following a leader who guides them—is only because he is a messenger calling to the word of truth and the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد). He is a perfect example who represents Islam by his existence and calls to it by his action. Thus, the meaning of His saying: "you have nothing to do with them" (لَسْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ) returns to the fact that they are not upon your religion to which you call, nor upon the level of your path which you tread.

So the meaning of the verse is that those who split their religion through differences that inevitably arise from knowledge (العلم)—"and those who were given it did not differ except out of envy among themselves"—and through sectarian divisions, are not upon your path which is built upon the unity of the word and the negation of division. Their matter in this splitting is only with their Lord; nothing of them adheres to you. Then He will inform them on the Day of Resurrection of what they used to do and disclose to them the reality of their deeds for which they are held hostage.

It has become clear from what preceded that there is no reason to restrict the verse to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) clearance from the polytheists, or from them and the Jews and Christians, or from those who differ in schools of thought (المذاهب) and innovations (البدع) from this community. Rather, the verse is general and encompasses everyone.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها وَمَنْ جاءَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ فَلا يُجْزى إِلَّا مِثْلَها وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like, and whoever brings an evil deed shall be requited only with its like, and they shall not be wronged" (6:160). The verse is complete in itself, disclosing a divine favor (منة إلهية) that He bestows upon His servants: that He rewards a righteous deed (الحسنة) with ten times its like, and does not reward an evil deed (السيئة) except with its like. That is, He counts the righteous deed as ten and the evil deed as one, and He is not unjust in the fulfillment, so He does not decrease from the former nor increase in the latter. If it is possible that He increases the reward of the righteous deed, He increases it beyond ten, as indicated by His saying: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوالَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنْبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنابِلَ فِي كُلِّ سُنْبُلَةٍ مِائَةُ حَبَّةٍ وَاللهُ يُضاعِفُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ — "The parable of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is that of a grain which grows seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains. Allah enhances severalfold whomever He wishes" (2:261). And it is possible that He pardons the evil deed, so He does not count the right of the single like.

However, by its connection to what preceded it and its arrangement with it in a single context, it yields another meaning. It is as if it were said after the discourse in the previous verses regarding agreement and gathering upon the truth and splitting therein: These are two traits, a righteous one and an evil one, in which what is similar to them is rewarded, and there is no injustice, for the reward is similar to the deed. So whoever brings the righteous deed, for him is its like and it is multiplied for him; and whoever brings the evil deed—which is the prohibited division—he shall not be rewarded except with an evil deed like it, and let him not covet a good reward. The meaning returns to something similar to what was understood from His saying: وَجَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها — "The recompense of an evil deed is an evil deed like it" (42:40), that the intended meaning is to explain the similarity of the reward of the evil deed to it in being an evil that is not desired, not to establish unity and negate multiplication.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them) regarding His saying: يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ لا يَنْفَعُ نَفْساً إِيمانُها — "the day when some of your Lord’s signs come, its faith shall not benefit any soul" (6:158). He said: "The rising of the sun from the west, the emergence of the Beast (الدابة) and the Smoke (الدخان). And the man who was persistent [in sin] and did not perform the work of faith—then the signs come, so his faith does not benefit him."

I say: His saying: "the man who was persistent..." etc., is an explanation of His saying: أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً — "or earned some good in its faith" (6:158) according to what we presented, and it is indicated by the following narration.

In the same source, from Abu Basir from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them) regarding His saying: أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً — "or earned some good in its faith" (6:158). He said: "The sinning believer (المؤمن العاصي) whose many sins and few righteous deeds stood between him and his faith—so he did not earn any good in his faith."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, my father narrated to me from Safwan from Ibn Muskan from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ — "the day when some of your Lord’s signs come" (6:158). He said: "When the sun rises from its west—then everyone who believes on that day, his faith will not benefit him."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad and ‘Abd ibn Humayd in his Musnad, al-Tirmidhi, Abu Ya‘la, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying: يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ — "the day when some of your Lord’s signs come" (6:158). He said: "The rising of the sun from its west."

I say: It is apparent that the narration is of the nature of "application" (الجري), and likewise the preceding narrations, or it could be from the commentary (التفسير). In any case, it is a day in which the divine seizure (البطشة الإلهية) appears, which compels people to faith, and it does not benefit them. The rising of the sun from its west has been mentioned in very many traditions from the paths of the Shi‘a from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) and from the paths of the Sunnis from a group of the companions such as Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, Ibn Mas‘ud, Abu Hurayrah, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar, Hudhayfah, Abu Dharr, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abbas, ‘Abdullah ibn Abi Awfa, Safwan ibn ‘Assal, Anas, ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn ‘Awf, Mu‘awiyah, Abu Umamah, ‘A’ishah, and others, even if they differ in their contents significantly.

Scientific views today do not prevent the change of the earth's motion contrary to what it is today of eastern motion, or the change of the two poles by the northern becoming southern and vice versa, either gradually as explained by astronomical observations or suddenly due to a total atmospheric event. All of this is if the word is not a symbol (رمز) pointing to a secret among the secrets of realities.

Among those signs, the narrations have counted the emergence of the Beast of the Earth (دابة الأرض), the Smoke (الدخان), and the emergence of Gog and Magog (يأجوج ومأجوج), and these are matters spoken of by the Noble Quran. They also counted other things such as the emergence of the Mahdi (peace be upon him), the descent of Jesus son of Mary, the emergence of the Dajjal, and others. Although these are from the events of the end of times, it is not clear that they are among those by which the door of repentance (باب التوبة) is closed.

In al-Burhan, from al-Barqi with his chain from ‘Abdullah ibn Sulayman al-‘Amiri from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him). He said: "The earth has never been except that Allah has a proof (حجة) in it—through whom the lawful and the prohibited are known, and who calls to the way of Allah. The proof is never cut off from the earth—except for forty days before the Day of Resurrection. So when the proof is raised and the door of repentance is closed—its faith shall not benefit any soul—that had not believed before the proof was raised. Those are the worst of Allah's creation, and they are the ones upon whom the Resurrection will occur."

I say: It was also narrated by Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari in the book Manaqib Fatima with another chain from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from his father from al-Nadr from al-Halabi from Mu‘alla ibn Khunays from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكانُوا شِيَعاً — "Indeed those who split up their religion and became sects" (6:159). He said: "By Allah, the people departed from their religion."

I say: Meaning by the difference of schools of thought (المذاهب). The tradition regarding the community splitting into seventy-three sects has already passed.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse. He said: "Ali (peace be upon him) used to recite it: 'they departed from their religion' (فارقوا دينهم)."

I say: This recitation is narrated from him (peace be upon him) from some Sunni paths as well, as in al-Durr al-Manthur and others.

In al-Burhan, from al-Barqi from his father from al-Nadr from Yahya al-Halabi from Ibn Muskan from Zurarah. He said: "Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked while I was sitting—about the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها — 'Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like' (6:160). Does it apply to those who do not recognize this matter [the Imamate]?" He said: "It is only for the believers (المؤمنين) specifically." I said to him: "May Allah set you right, what do you say of one who fasts and prays—and avoids the prohibited and has good piety (ورع) among those who do not recognize [the Imamate] nor show enmity?" He said: "Indeed, Allah will enter those into Paradise by His mercy."

I say: The narration indicates that the reward is in proportion to the recognition (المعرفة). Regarding this meaning, there are narrations reported from the paths of both groups.

There are many narrations regarding the meaning of His saying: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like" (6:160) etc., narrated by both groups and cited in the commentary of the verse. However, they are reported regarding the identification of instances (المصاديق) such as fasting, prayer, and others; we have omitted their citation for that reason.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنَّنِى هَدَىٰنِى رَبِّىٓ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ دِينًا قِيَمًا مِّلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۚ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٦١)
Say, ‘Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path, the upright religion, the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists.’ (161)
قُلْ إِنَّ صَلَاتِى وَنُسُكِى وَمَحْيَاىَ وَمَمَاتِى لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦٢)
Say, ‘Indeed my prayer and my worship, my life and my death are for the sake of Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (162)
لَا شَرِيكَ لَهُۥ ۖ وَبِذَٰلِكَ أُمِرْتُ وَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (١٦٣)
He has no partner, and I have been commanded [to follow] this [creed], and I am the first of those who submit [to Allah].’ (163)
قُلْ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِى رَبًّا وَهُوَ رَبُّ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ ۚ وَلَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ إِلَّا عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُم مَّرْجِعُكُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ (١٦٤)
Say, ‘Shall I seek a Lord other than Allah, while He is the Lord of all things?’ No soul does evil except against itself, and no bearer shall bear another’s burden; then to your Lord will be your return, whereat He will inform you concerning that about which you used to differ. (164)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَكُمْ خَلَـٰٓئِفَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَرَفَعَ بَعْضَكُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَـٰتٍ لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِى مَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْعِقَابِ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌۢ (١٦٥)
It is He who has made you successors on the earth, and raised some of you in rank above others so that He may test you in respect to what He has given you. Indeed your Lord is swift in retribution, and indeed, He is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (165)

(Commentary)

The verses conclude the Surah and contain a summary of the purpose of his (the Prophet’s) صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله call within the Surah, affirming that he is actively engaged in practicing what he calls toward. They also contain a summary of the arguments established therein to invalidate the creed of polytheism (الشرك).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدَانِي رَبِّي إِلَى صِرَاطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — "Say, ‘Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path’" until the end of the two verses. The word qiyam (القيم) — with a kasrah then a fathah — is a lightened form of qiyam (القيام). The religion (الدين) is described by it to emphasize its standing (قيامه) over the interests of the servants. It is also said that it is a description meaning "the sustainer" (القيم) over the affair.

Allah, the Glorified, commands him to inform them that his Lord, whom he calls toward, has guided him through divine guidance (هداية إلهية) to a straight path (صراط مستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — and a clear way that sustains those who traverse it, wherein there is neither deviation nor discrepancy. It is a religion standing (قائماً) upon the interests of this world and the Hereafter in the best possible manner—because it is built upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. It is the creed (ملة) of Abraham, a hanif (حنيفاً) — one who inclines away from the extremes of polytheism toward the moderation of monotheism — and he was not of the polytheists. The clarification of these meanings has already preceded in the exegesis of the previous verses of the Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّ صَلَاتِي وَنُسُكِي وَمَحْيَايَ وَمَمَاتِي لِلَّهِ — "Say, ‘Indeed my prayer, my rites, my life and my death are for Allah...’" until His saying: أَوَّلُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ — "the first of those who submit." Rites (النسك) refers to worship (العبادة) in general, though its usage became frequent regarding slaughter or the sacrificial animal offered as a means of proximity to Allah, the Glorified.

He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded a second time to inform them that he is a practitioner of what Allah has guided him toward and is fully immersed in it, just as he is commanded to do so. This is to be further from their suspicion and closer to their acceptance; for indeed, a sign of truthfulness is that a person acts according to what he advocates, and that his actions correspond to his words.

Thus He said: Say: Indeed, I have made my prayer and my worship in general—and prayer was specifically mentioned independently due to the increased divine concern for it—and my life (محياي) with all its affairs pertaining to me, including actions, attributes, deeds, and omissions; and my death (مماتي) with all its matters that return to me, which are the aspects that relate back from it to life—as it is said: "As you live, so shall you die"—I have made them all for Allah, the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين), without associating anyone with Him therein. Thus, I am a servant in all my affairs, in my life and my death, to Allah alone. I have turned my face toward Him; I do not intend anything nor abandon anything except for Him, and I do not travel the path of my life nor encounter my death except for Him, for He is the Lord of all the worlds, Who owns everything and manages their affairs.

And I have been commanded with this manner of servitude (العبودية), and I am the first of those who submit (أول المسلمين) to Allah in what He has willed of complete servitude in every chapter and aspect.

From here it becomes clear that the intent of His saying: إِنَّ صَلَاتِي وَنُسُكِي وَمَحْيَايَ وَمَمَاتِي لِلَّهِ is the manifestation of servile sincerity (الإخلاص العبودي) or its initiation regarding what pertains to him of the affairs of worship, life, and death, rather than merely informing about sincerity in worship and the belief that the Owner of death and life is Allah. The evidence for what we have mentioned is His saying: وَبِذَلِكَ أُمِرْتُ — "And with that I have been commanded." It is apparent that he was commanded to make everything for Allah, the Glorified, in a single sense, not to make the first two for Him out of sincerity and submission while merely believing that the latter two belong to Him, except through strained interpretation.

In His saying: وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ — "and I am the first of those who submit," there is an indication that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is the first of people in terms of the degree and station of Islam (الإسلام) — the total submission to the will of Allah — for before him in time there were other Muslims. Allah, the Glorified, has narrated this from Noah when he said: وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ — "and I have been commanded to be of those who submit" (10:72); and from Abraham in his saying: أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ — "I submit to the Lord of all the worlds" (2:131); and from him and his son Ishmael in their saying: رَبَّنَا وَاجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ — "Our Lord, make us both submissive to You" (2:128); and from Lot in His saying: فَمَا وَجَدْنَا فِيهَا غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ — "but We did not find in it any but one house of those who submit" (51:36); and from the Queen of Sheba in His saying: وَأُوتِينَا الْعِلْمَ مِنْ قَبْلِهَا وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ — "and we were given knowledge before her and we had submitted" (27:42) — if her intent was submission to Allah. And her saying: وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمَانَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ — "and I submit with Solomon to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (27:44). No one in the Quran was described as "the first of those who submit" except what is found in this verse regarding the command to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to inform his people of that, and what is in Surah al-Zumar in His saying: قُلْ إِنِّي أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللَّهَ مُخْلِصًا لَهُ الدِّينَ * وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ — "Say, ‘I am commanded to worship Allah, making religion pure for Him, and I am commanded to be the first of those who submit’" (39:11-12).

It is sometimes said that the intent is the first of the Muslims from this nation (الأمة), for Abraham was the first of the Muslims and those after him followed him in Islam. However, there is no evidence for this restriction. As for Abraham being the first of the Muslims, this is refuted by the previously cited verses.

As for the saying of the Exalted narrating from Abraham and Ishmael in their supplication: وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُسْلِمَةً لَكَ — "and from our offspring a nation submissive to You" (2:128), and His saying: مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْرَاهِيمَ هُوَ سَمَّاكُمُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ — "the faith of your father, Abraham. He named you ‘Muslims’" (22:78), there is no indication in them regarding this matter.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَغَيْرَ اللَّهِ أَبْغِي رَبًّا وَهُوَ رَبُّ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Say, ‘Shall I seek a Lord other than Allah, while He is the Lord of all things?’" etc. This verse and the one following it contain three arguments which are the summaries of the arguments mentioned in the Surah for monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. These are: the argument from the way of the beginning of creation (بدء الخلقة), the argument from the way of its return (عودها), and the argument from the state of man while he is between them. In other words, the argument from the realm of the lower life (نشأة الحياة الدنيا) and the realms before and after it.

The argument from the way of the beginning is what is in His saying: أَغَيْرَ اللَّهِ أَبْغِي رَبًّا وَهُوَ رَبُّ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ. It is well known that if He is the Lord of everything, then everything is a subject (مربوب) to Him; thus, there is absolutely no lord other than Him who is fit to be worshipped.

The argument from the way of the return is what is contained in His saying: وَلَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ إِلَّا عَلَيْهَا — "No soul commits [evil] but to its own detriment" until the end of the verse. That is, every soul does not perform an action nor earn anything except that it is held against it, and no bearer shall bear the burden (وزر) of another, such that what one soul earned is carried by another. Then the return is to Allah, and to Him belongs the recompense by disclosing the realities of the servants' deeds. Since there is no escape from the recompense, and He is the Master of the Day of Judgment, then He is the One whose worship is mandatory, not anyone else who possesses nothing.

The argument from the way of the worldly realm is what is in His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلَائِفَ — "It is He who has made you successors..." etc. Its summary is that this wondrous system (النظام) that governs your livelihood in the lower life—which is built upon your succession (خلافة) on the earth and the variation of your states in terms of age, strength, weakness, masculinity, femininity, wealth, poverty, leadership, subordination, knowledge, ignorance, and others—even if it is a conventional system (نظام اعتباري), it arises from the act of creation (التكوين) and terminates in it. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is its organizer. He only did that to test and try you (لامتحانكم وابتلائكم). Therefore, He is the Lord who manages the affair of your felicity (سعادة), bringing whoever obeys Him to the felicity decreed for him and leaving the wrongdoers (الظالمين) therein on their knees. Thus, He is the One whose worship is rightful.

It has become clear from what has passed that the combination of the two sentences: وَلَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ إِلَّا عَلَيْهَا وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَى was brought to convey a single meaning: that what a soul earns is binding upon it and does not transcend it. This is the meaning of His saying: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ — "Every soul is a hostage to what it has earned" (74:38).

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلَائِفَ الْأَرْضِ — "It is He who has made you successors on the earth." Khala'if (الخلائف) is the plural of khalifah (خليفة), meaning that you succeed one another, or He made you successors for Himself on the earth. Discussion regarding the meaning of this succession (الخلافة) has already passed in the exegesis of His saying: إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً — "I am indeed going to set a viceroy on the earth" (2:30) in the first volume of the book. The meaning of the verse is clear from the preceding explanation. The Surah concludes with forgiveness (المغفرة) and mercy (الرحمة).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Muskan, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah: حَنِيفاً مُسْلِماً — "a hanif, a Muslim." He said: "Pure and sincere, with nothing of the worship of idols within it."

I say: It was narrated in al-Burhan by al-Barqi with his chain from Ibn Muskan from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and in it: "Pure and sincere, with nothing mixed into it." This is a clarification of the intent, not an exegesis by meaning.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, he said: "One should not say: 'one degree'; indeed Allah says: 'degrees, some above others.' The people only excel one another through deeds."

I say: This is a narration of the verse by its meaning, for the verse is thus: وَرَفَعَ بَعْضَكُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَاتٍ — "and raised some of you above others in rank" and in another place: وَرَفَعْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَاتٍ — "and We have raised some of them above others in rank" (43:32). It appears that his saying: "some above others" is from his (the Imam's) عليه‌السلام own speech. The hadith was only narrated in the exegesis of verses like His saying: هُمْ دَرَجَاتٌ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ — "They have [various] degrees with Allah," not in the exegesis of the verse we are currently discussing. Its inclusion under this verse is a mistake by the narrator, because his saying at the end: "The people only excel one another through deeds" does not correspond to the verse, as is not hidden.

Completed, and praise be to Allah.
